Research Hub بحث

All research documents, experiments, and findings for the Alif Arabic learning project.

42
Research Documents
10
Categories
11
Days of Research
Feb 8–21
Date Range, 2026
42 docs

Key Research Findings

Three-Phase Lifecycle
Encountered → Acquiring (Leitner 3-box) → FSRS-6. Bridges the gap between "seen once" and "scheduled for review."
Morphological Awareness #1
Root+pattern decomposition is the single strongest predictor of Arabic reading comprehension. Explicit decomposition > implicit exposure.
8–12 Encounters Needed
Nation's research: words need 8-12 meaningful encounters before acquisition. Sleep consolidation matters — space across days.
85% Comprehension Sweet Spot
Krashen's i+1 operationalized: sessions at ~85% known words optimize the balance of challenge and acquisition.
Sentence-Centric SRS
Reviewing words in sentence context is more effective than isolated flashcards. Greedy set cover maximizes due-word coverage.
Semantic Clustering Hurts
Introducing similar/related words together causes interference. Space root family members 2-4 days apart, not simultaneously.
10–20 Words/Day Max
Sustainable introduction rate consensus: 10-20 new words/day. The "10x rule" means 10 new = 100 daily reviews within weeks.
High-Productivity Patterns
3 patterns show robust priming: fa'il (doer), maf'ul (object), masdar (verbal noun). Low-productivity patterns stored as whole words.

Research Timeline

Feb 8
Project Inception
Hosting research, initial architecture decisions
Feb 9
First Production Analysis
Day 1: 59 sentence reviews, 390 selections, 217 FSRS reviews
Feb 10
Early Metrics
481 word reviews, 85 textbook imports, 107 words at 30d+ stability
Feb 11
Sentence Diversity Audit
Found 24.2% of sentences start with hal. 2075 total sentences.
Feb 12
Algorithm Redesign
8-agent deep research. Three-phase lifecycle designed. Learner profile captured.
Feb 13
Sentence Benchmark
213 sentences, 3 models x 6 strategies. Gemini Flash selected.
Feb 14
Story Benchmark
32 stories, 4 models x 4 strategies. Opus selected for production.
Feb 17
Data Quality Crisis
Lemma mapping audit: false al-prefix matching, 24+ wrong assignments found and fixed.
Feb 19
LLM Cost Migration
Background tasks switched to Claude CLI (free). Two-tier architecture deployed.
Feb 20
Production Analytics
13 days of data analyzed. 209 known words (28.8%), acquisition rates validated.
Feb 21
Morphological Patterns
Wazn system implemented. Pattern decomposition in UI. 83% backfill coverage.

Summary Comparison Table\n\n### Morphological Analysis / NLP Toolkits\n\n| Tool | Type | Cost | Auth | Key Features |\n|------|------|------|------|-------------|\n| **Farasa** | REST API | Free (academic) | API key | Segmentation, lemma, POS, NER, diacritize, parse |\n| **CAMeL Tools** | Python lib | Free (MIT) | None | Morphology, POS, lemma, diacritize, NER, sentiment, DID |\n| **Stanza** | Python lib | Free (Apache 2.0) | None | Tokenize, POS, lemma, dependency parse, NER |\n| **Tashaphyne** | Python lib | Free (GPL) | None | Light stemming, segmentation |\n| **Qutuf** | Java lib | Free (Apache 2.0) | None | Morphological analysis, POS tagging |\n| **AraMorph** | Perl/Java/Python | Free | None | Classic Buckwalter morphological analysis |\n\n### Diacritization\n\n| Tool | Type | Cost | Accuracy | Notes |\n|------|------|------|----------|-------|\n| **Farasa** | REST API | Free | High | Part of full NLP suite |\n| **Mishkal** | REST API / Python | Free (GPL) | Good | Self-hostable, rule-based |\n| **RDI Tashkeel** | REST API | Paid (trial available) | High | On-premises option available |\n| **Zyla API** | REST API | $0.016/req (7-day trial) | Unknown | Hosted marketplace |\n| **Sadeed** | HF Model | Free | High | 1.5B param model, self-hosted |\n\n### Translation\n\n| Service | Free Tier | Auth | Notes |\n|---------|-----------|------|-------|\n| **Azure Translator** | 2M chars/month | Azure key | Best free tier |\n| **Google Translate API** | 500K chars/month | API key | Never expires |\n| **DeepL** | 500K chars/month | API key | High quality |\n| **MyMemory** | 5K-50K chars/day | None/email | No signup needed |\n| **LibreTranslate** | Unlimited (self-hosted) | Optional | AGPL-3.0, run your own |\n| **Lingvanex** | Sign-up bonus | API key | $5/M chars after |\n\n### Text-to-Speech\n\n| Service | Free Tier | Arabic Variant | Notes |\n|---------|-----------|----------------|-------|\n| **Google Cloud TTS** | 1M chars/month (WaveNet) | ar-XA (MSA) | Best free tier, multiple voices |\n| **Azure Speech** | 500K chars/month | ar-SA, ar-EG, etc. | Neural voices |\n| **Klaam** | Unlimited (self-hosted) | MSA | Open source, FastSpeech2 |\n\n### Cloud NLP (Entity/Sentiment/Syntax)\n\n| Service | Free Tier | Arabic Support | Features |\n|---------|-----------|----------------|----------|\n| **Google NL API** | 5K units/month | Yes | Entity, sentiment, syntax, classification |\n| **AWS Comprehend** | 50K units/month (12 mo) | Yes | Entity, sentiment, key phrases, topics |\n| **Azure Text Analytics** | 5K txn/month | Yes | Sentiment, NER, key phrases |\n\n---\n\n## Recommended Stack for an Arabic Learning App\n\nFor a web/mobile application focused on MSA, here is a practical combination of free services:\n\n1. **Morphology & root extraction:** CAMeL Tools (self-hosted Python) or Farasa API\n2. **Diacritization:** Mishkal (self-hosted, free) or Farasa API\n3. **Verb conjugation:** Qutrub API (`http://qutrub.arabeyes.org/api`)\n4. **Translation:** Azure Translator (2M chars/month free) or Google Translate (500K chars/month)\n5. **Text-to-speech:** Google Cloud TTS (1M WaveNet chars/month free)\n6. **Dictionary lookup:** Ejtaal.net data (GitHub) + Hans Wehr app data\n7. **Sentiment / NER:** CAMeLBERT models on HuggingFace or AWS Comprehend\n8. **Transliteration:** Yamli API (free widget)\n9. **Text utilities:** PyArabic for normalization, diacritics stripping, etc.\n", "date": null, "category": "nlp", "status": "reference", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/arabic-apis-services.md"}, "arabic-children-content-2026-02-21": {"title": "Arabic Children's Rhymes, Lullabies, and Simple Stories for Language Learning", "content": "# Arabic Children's Rhymes, Lullabies, and Simple Stories for Language Learning\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-21\n**Purpose**: Reference catalog of simple, memorable Arabic content for potential import into Alif as reading material.\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Classic Nursery Rhymes and Lullabies](#1-classic-nursery-rhymes-and-lullabies)\n2. [Simple Children's Songs (Educational)](#2-simple-childrens-songs-educational)\n3. [Simple Folk Tales and Fables](#3-simple-folk-tales-and-fables)\n4. [Juha Stories](#4-juha-stories)\n5. [Modern Children's Content](#5-modern-childrens-content)\n6. [Graded Readers and Curated Resources](#6-graded-readers-and-curated-resources)\n7. [Pedagogical Assessment for Adult A1-A2 Learners](#7-pedagogical-assessment-for-adult-a1-a2-learners)\n8. [Implementation Recommendations for Alif](#8-implementation-recommendations-for-alif)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Classic Nursery Rhymes and Lullabies\n\n### 1.1 ماما زمانها جاية (Mama Zamanha Gaya) — \"Mommy Is Almost Here\"\n**Origin**: Egypt. By Mohamed Fawzi.\n**Dialect**: Egyptian Arabic (widely understood across Arab world).\n\n**Arabic Text** (opening):\n> ماما زمانها جاية / جاية بعد شوية / جايبة العاب وحاجات / وعلبة فيها كتاكيت / بطة ووزة وديك / واللي بيعمل كاك كاك / واللي بيعمل واك واك\n\n**Transliteration**: Mama zamanha gaya / gaya baedeh shiwaya / gayba al'ab wa hagat / wa 'olba fiha katakit / batta wi wizza wi dik / willi biy'mil kak kak / willi biy'mil wak wak\n\n**Translation**: Mommy is coming / She is almost here / She is bringing toys and gifts / And a box with chicks / A duck and a goose and a rooster / And the one that goes \"kak kak\" / And the one that goes \"wak wak\"\n\n**Vocabulary**: mama (mom), gaya (coming), al'ab (toys), batta (duck), wizza (goose), dik (rooster)\n**Note**: The song continues with a story about a boy named Adel who learns to drink milk. Very catchy, widely known across the Arab world. Egyptian dialect but comprehensible to MSA learners.\n\n---\n\n### 1.2 ذهب الليل طلع الفجر (Thahaba al-Laylu Tala'a al-Fajr) — \"The Night Has Gone, Dawn Has Come\"\n**Origin**: Egypt. By Mohamed Fawzi.\n**Dialect**: Mix of Egyptian and near-MSA.\n\n**Arabic Text** (opening):\n> ذهب الليل طلع الفجر / والعصفور صوصو / فات على بسبس قاله / هات حتة جبنة يا نونو\n\n**Transliteration**: Thahaba al-layl tala'a al-fajr / wal-'usfur sawsaw / fat 'ala bisbis qal-lu / hat hitta gibna ya nunu\n\n**Translation**: The night has gone, the dawn has come / And the bird Sawsaw / Passed by the cat Bisbis and said / Give me a piece of cheese, Nunu\n\n**Vocabulary**: layl (night), fajr (dawn), 'usfur (bird), gibna (cheese)\n**Note**: Classic Egyptian children's song with animal characters. Teaches animal vocabulary and onomatopoeia. Used as learning material on LingQ for intermediate Arabic.\n\n---\n\n### 1.3 يلا تنام ريما (Yalla Tnam Rima) — \"Come Sleep, Rima\"\n**Origin**: Lebanon. Performed by Fairuz. From the film \"Bint El Haress.\"\n**Dialect**: Lebanese Arabic.\n\n**Arabic Text** (opening):\n> يلا تنام ريما / يلا يجيها النوم / تكبر تحب الصلا / وتكبر تحب الصوم / يا رب خليها / كل يوم بعافية / يلا تنام وأنا أطبخلها / حمامة مشوية\n\n**Transliteration**: Yalla tnam Rima / yalla yijiha an-nom / tikbar thib as-sala / w tikbar thib as-som / ya rabb khalliha / kill yom b'afiye / yalla tnam w ana atbakhilha / hamame mashwiye\n\n**Translation**: Come on Rima, sleep / May sleep come to her / May she grow to love prayer / And grow to love fasting / Oh God keep her / Healthy every day / Come sleep and I'll cook for her / A roasted pigeon\n\n**Vocabulary**: tnam (sleep), tikbar (grow up), salat (prayer), rabb (God/Lord), hamame (pigeon)\n**Note**: The most famous Arabic lullaby. Fairuz's voice makes it iconic. The lyrics humorously reassure a pigeon that the cooking is just a trick to make Rima sleep. Lebanese dialect but culturally universal.\n\n---\n\n### 1.4 هالسيسان (Hal Sisan) — \"These Chicks\"\n**Origin**: Levant (Syria/Lebanon).\n**Dialect**: Levantine Arabic.\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n> هالسيسان شو حلوين / عم يدوروا حول أمهن / مبسوطين / شربوا ماي / قالوا خي / رفعوا راسهن / شكروا ربهن / مبسوطين\n\n**Transliteration**: Ha-l-sisan sho hilwin / 'am yiduru hawl immhon / mabsutin / shirbu may / qalu khai / rafa'u rashon / shakaru rabhon / mabsutin\n\n**Translation**: These chicks, how sweet / Walking around their mother / Happily / They drank water / They said \"yum\" / They lifted their heads / They thanked their Lord / Happily\n\n**Vocabulary**: sisan (chicks), hilwin (sweet/cute), yiduru (walk around), umm (mother), mabsutin (happy), may/ma' (water), ras (head), rabb (Lord)\n**Note**: Very short (under 20 words), highly repetitive. Perfect for absolute beginners. Accompanied by rhythmic clicking. Teaches animal, emotion, and body vocabulary. One of the most widely taught nursery rhymes in the Levant.\n\n---\n\n### 1.5 يا باح يا باح (Ya Bah Ya Bah) — Syrian Tickle Game\n**Origin**: Syria/Levant.\n**Dialect**: Levantine Arabic.\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n> يا باح يا باح / يا عرق التفاح / يا أصابع اللولو / يا إيدين الحلوين / المناح / إجا العصفور يشرب / من هوني / هادا سقا وهادا طعما / وهادا غطّا وهادا دفّا / وهادا غفّا / هاد زند السوارة / هاد كوع السلة / هاد كتف الجرة / وغر غر غر\n\n**Translation**: Hey, reveal, hey, reveal! / An apple branch / Little pearl fingers / Pretty little hands / Of good nature / A sparrow came to drink / From here / This one gave water, this one fed / This one covered, this one warmed / This one slept / This is the bracelet wrist / This is the basket elbow / This is the jar shoulder / And ger ger ger!\n\n**Vocabulary**: tuffah (apple), asabi' (fingers), 'usfur (sparrow), yad/eid (hand), zand (wrist), katif (shoulder)\n**Note**: Interactive fingerplay game. Each line involves touching a different finger/body part. Excellent for body part vocabulary. Words vary by region within Syria.\n\n---\n\n### 1.6 حمامة نودي نودي (Hamama Nudi Nudi) — \"Dove, Fly, Fly\"\n**Origin**: Gulf region / widely known.\n\n**Arabic Text** (opening):\n> حمامه نودي نودي / سلمي على عبودي / عبودي طالع للبر / وأمونه لبسه الأحمر / عبودي يبغى فنجال / والفنجال يبغى الدلة / والدلة فوق الجمرة / وأمونه تبغى تمرة / والتمرة ما شفناها / إلا مع ضو القمرة\n\n**Translation**: Dove, fly, fly / Greet Aboudi / Aboudi went to the desert / And Amouna wore red / Aboudi wants a cup / And the cup wants the coffee pot / And the pot is on the ember / And Amouna wants a date / And the date we only saw / In the moonlight\n\n**Vocabulary**: hamama (dove), nudi (fly/sway), barr (desert/outside), ahmar (red), finjal (cup), dalla (coffee pot), jamra (ember), tamra (date), qamar (moon)\n**Note**: Chain/cumulative structure. Each verse builds on the last. Rich in everyday object vocabulary. Multiple regional variants exist.\n\n---\n\n### 1.7 عصفور طل من الشباك ('Usfur Tall min ash-Shubbak) — \"A Sparrow Peeked from the Window\"\n**Origin**: Levant.\n**Dialect**: Levantine Arabic.\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n> عصفور طل من الشباك / قالي يا نونو خبيني / عندك خبيني دخلك يا نونو / قلتله إنت من وين / قالي من حدود السما / قلتله جاي من وين / قالي من بيت الجيران / قلتله خايف من مين / قالي من القفص هربان / قلتله ريشاتك وين / قالي فرفطها الزمان\n\n**Translation**: A sparrow peeked from the window / He told me \"Nunu, hide me / Hide me please, Nunu\" / I asked him \"Where are you from?\" / He said \"From the edge of the sky\" / I asked \"Where did you come from?\" / He said \"From the neighbors' house\" / I asked \"Who are you afraid of?\" / He said \"I escaped from the cage\" / I asked \"Where are your feathers?\" / He said \"Time scattered them\"\n\n**Vocabulary**: 'usfur (sparrow), shubbak (window), khabbini (hide me), sama (sky), jiran (neighbors), qafas (cage), rish (feathers), zaman (time)\n**Note**: Beautiful, slightly melancholic children's song. Dialogue structure makes it very useful for conversational patterns (asking/answering questions). The sparrow as metaphor for freedom is deeply embedded in Arabic culture.\n\n---\n\n### 1.8 آ شتا تاتا تاتا (Ashta Ta Ta Ta) — Rain Chant\n**Origin**: Morocco.\n**Dialect**: Moroccan Arabic (Darija).\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n> أشتا تا تا تا تا / أولاد الحراثة / المعلم بوزكري / طيبلي خبزي بكري / باش نعشي وليداتي / وليداتي عند القاضي / والقاضي ما جاب خبار\n\n**Translation**: Oh rain, rain, rain / Oh peasants' children / Oh Mr. Bouzekri / Bake my bread early / So I can feed my children / My children are at the judge's house / But the judge doesn't know\n\n**Note**: Children chant this whenever rain starts falling. Very regional (Moroccan). Less useful for MSA learners due to heavy Darija, but culturally interesting.\n\n---\n\n### 1.9 انا البطة الشلبية (Ana al-Batta ash-Shalabiyya) — \"I Am the Pretty Duck\"\n**Origin**: Egypt.\n**Dialect**: Egyptian Arabic.\n\n**Arabic Text** (opening):\n> أنا البطة الشلبية / أمشي وأتميلي / وبعمل كده وكده / ومحدش قدي\n\n**Transliteration**: Ana al-batta ash-shalabiyya / amshi wa atmayyali / wa ba'mil kida wa kida / wa mahadish addi\n\n**Translation**: I am the pretty duck / I walk and I sway / And I do this and that / And no one is like me\n\n**Note**: Humorous song about a duck who loses her ID card in the water when the police ask for it. Very catchy, teaches animal vocabulary and everyday expressions.\n\n---\n\n### 1.10 سنة حلوة يا جميل (Sana Hilwa Ya Jamil) — \"Happy Birthday\"\n**Origin**: Pan-Arab.\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n> سنة حلوة يا جميل / سنة من عمر طويل / سنة حلوة يا حبيبي / سنة حلوة يا جميل\n\n**Translation**: Happy year, beautiful one / A year from a long life / Happy year, my darling / Happy year, beautiful one\n\n**Vocabulary**: sana (year), hilwa (sweet/happy), jamil (beautiful), 'umr (life/age), habib (darling)\n**Note**: The Arabic equivalent of \"Happy Birthday.\" Universally known. Very simple, highly repetitive. Good for basic adjective and endearment vocabulary.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Simple Children's Songs (Educational)\n\n### 2.1 الواحد عصاية (Al-Wahid 'Asaya) — Palestinian Number Shape Rhyme\n**Origin**: Palestine.\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n> الواحد عصاية / والاثنين بتطخ / والثلاثة مسننة / والأربعة بتهتز / والخمسة مدورة / والستة زعلانة / والسبعة بتحمد الله / والثمانية بتصلي / والتسعة عكازة / والعشرة نقطة\n\n**Transliteration**: El-wahad 'asaye / w-ithnen btetokh / w-thalathi msannane / w-arba'a btihtzz / w-khamse mdawwra / w-sitte za'lane / w-sab'a btihmid allah / w-tmane btisalli / w-tis'a 'ikkaze / w-'ashara nuq'ta\n\n**Translation**: One is a stick / And Two is shooting / And Three has teeth / And Four shakes / And Five is round / And Six is sad / And Seven thanks God / And Eight prays / And Nine is a crutch / And Ten has a dot\n\n**Vocabulary**: wahid-'ashara (1-10), 'asaya (stick), msannane (with teeth), mdawwra (round), za'lane (sad), 'ikkaze (crutch)\n**Note**: Teaches Arabic numerals (Eastern Arabic: ١٢٣٤٥٦٧٨٩٠) by describing their visual shapes. Brilliant pedagogical concept for children ages 2-3 learning to recognize written numbers. For adult learners, useful for number vocabulary and adjectives.\n\n---\n\n### 2.2 Numbers Counting Song\n\n**Arabic Text** (common version):\n> وقف الواحد ليصلي / ركع الاثنان لربه / والثلاثة واحد واثنان / والأربعة اثنان واثنان / والخمسة كعكة بسكر / والستة وقفت لتفكر / والسبعة تنظر للنجمة / والثمانية عكس سبعة\n\n**Translation**: One stood to pray / Two bowed to his Lord / And three is one and two / And four is two and two / And five is a cake with sugar / And six stopped to think / And seven looks to the stars / And eight is the opposite of seven\n\n**Vocabulary**: salla (pray), raka'a (bow), ka'ka (cake), sukkar (sugar), fakkar (think), najma (star)\n**Note**: MSA counting rhyme. Teaches numbers with character descriptions. More MSA-friendly than the Palestinian version above.\n\n---\n\n### 2.3 Thematic Educational Songs (by topic)\n\nThe following topics are covered by various Arabic children's song creators (Adam wa Mishmish, AlifBee Kids, Little Thinking Minds, Lali Kids):\n\n| Topic | Key Vocabulary | MSA Suitability |\n|-------|---------------|-----------------|\n| Arabic Alphabet (Alif Ba Ta) | All 28 letters + example words | Excellent - universal |\n| Colors (الألوان) | ahmar, azraq, asfar, akhdar, abyad, aswad | Excellent |\n| Animals (الحيوانات) | asad, qitt, kalb, 'usfur, samak, arnab | Excellent |\n| Numbers 1-10 (الأرقام) | wahid through 'ashara | Excellent |\n| Body Parts (أعضاء الجسم) | ra's, yad, rijl, 'ayn, udhun, anf | Excellent |\n| Days of the Week | al-ahad through as-sabt | Excellent |\n| Months of the Year | yanayir through disambir | Good |\n| Four Seasons (الفصول) | rabi', sayf, kharif, shita' | Excellent |\n| Fruits (الفواكه) | tuffah, mawz, burtuqal, 'inab | Good |\n| Vegetables (الخضراوات) | batata, tamata, khiyar, jazar | Good |\n| Family Members (العائلة) | ab, umm, akh, ukht, jadd, jadda | Excellent |\n| Greetings (التحيات) | salam, marhaba, sabah al-khayr | Excellent |\n| Professions (المهن) | tabib, mu'allim, muhandis, shurta | Good |\n\n---\n\n## 3. Simple Folk Tales and Fables\n\n### 3.1 الأرنب والسلحفاة (The Hare and the Tortoise)\n\n**Full Arabic Text (MSA)**:\n\nفي يَوْمٍ مِنَ الأَيَّامِ، في غابَةٍ كَثيفَةٍ، كانَ هُناكَ أَرْنَبٌ سَريعٌ وَسُلَحْفاةٌ بَطيئَةٌ. كانَ الأَرْنَبُ فَخورًا بِسُرْعَتِهِ وَكانَ يَتَفاخَرُ بِذلِكَ أَمامَ الحَيَواناتِ الأُخْرى. وَكانَ يُحِبُّ أَنْ يَسْخَرَ مِنَ السُّلَحْفاةِ لِبُطْئِها.\n\nفي أَحَدِ الأَيَّامِ، شَعَرَتِ السُّلَحْفاةُ بِالضَّجَرِ مِنْ تَفاخُرِ الأَرْنَبِ، فَتَحَدَّتْهُ في سِباقٍ. ظَنَّ الأَرْنَبُ أَنَّ هذا أَمْرٌ مُضْحِكٌ، فَوافَقَ عَلى التَّحَدّي. اخْتارَ المُتَسابِقانِ مِضْمارًا لِلسِّباقِ، وَجاءَتْ جَميعُ الحَيَواناتِ في الغابَةِ لِمُشاهَدَتِهِ.\n\nعِنْدَما بَدَأَ السِّباقُ، انْطَلَقَ الأَرْنَبُ بِسُرْعَةٍ تارِكًا السُّلَحْفاةَ بَعيدَةً وَراءَهُ. عِنْدَما رَأى الأَرْنَبُ مَدى بُطْءِ السُّلَحْفاةِ، شَعَرَ بِالثِّقَةِ في فَوْزِهِ. فَكَّرَ قائِلًا: \"لَدَيَّ الكَثيرُ مِنَ الوَقْتِ، يُمْكِنُني أَنْ آخُذَ قَيْلولَةً قَصيرَةً\"، وَنامَ تَحْتَ شَجَرَةٍ.\n\nلكِنَّ السُّلَحْفاةَ اسْتَمَرَّتْ في التَّقَدُّمِ بِبُطْءٍ وَثَباتٍ. لَمْ تَهْتَمَّ بِمَدى سُرْعَةِ الأَرْنَبِ، بَلْ رَكَّزَتْ فَقَطْ عَلى الوُصولِ إِلى النِّهايَةِ.\n\nبَيْنَما كانَ الأَرْنَبُ نائِمًا، اقْتَرَبَتِ السُّلَحْفاةُ بِبُطْءٍ مِنْ خَطِّ النِّهايَةِ. وَعِنْدَما اسْتَيْقَظَ الأَرْنَبُ وَرَأى أَنَّ السُّلَحْفاةَ كانَتْ عَلى وَشْكِ الوُصولِ، كانَ الوَقْتُ قَدْ فاتَ. رَكَضَ بِأَقْصى سُرْعَةٍ مُمْكِنَةٍ، وَلكِنَّ السُّلَحْفاةَ كانَتْ قَدْ وَصَلَتْ بِالفِعْلِ وَفازَتْ بِالسِّباقِ.\n\nاِحْتَفَلَتِ الحَيَواناتُ في الغابَةِ بِفَوْزِ السُّلَحْفاةِ. لَقَدْ أَظْهَرَتْ لَهُمْ أَنَّ الثَّباتَ وَعَدَمَ الاسْتِسْلامِ يُمْكِنُ أَنْ يُؤَدِّيَ إِلى النَّجاحِ.\n\nبَعْدَ ذلِكَ، تَوَقَّفَ الأَرْنَبُ عَنِ الاعْتِقادِ بِأَنَّهُ أَفْضَلُ مِنَ الآخَرينَ وَلَمْ يَعُدْ يَسْخَرُ مِنْهُمْ.\n\n**Translation**: In a dense forest, there was a fast hare and a slow tortoise. The hare was proud of his speed and liked to mock the tortoise. One day the tortoise challenged him to a race. The hare agreed, thinking it was funny. When the race began, the hare ran far ahead but felt so confident he took a nap under a tree. The tortoise kept moving slowly and steadily. While the hare slept, the tortoise approached the finish line. When the hare woke up, it was too late. The tortoise had won. The animals celebrated, learning that persistence leads to success.\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: ghaba (forest), arnab (hare), sulahfa (tortoise), sari' (fast), bati' (slow), sibaq (race), qailula (nap), shajara (tree), fawz (victory), najah (success)\n\n**Pedagogical Value**: HIGH. Pure MSA. Simple narrative structure. Past tense verbs throughout. Rich in common adjectives (fast, slow, happy, proud). Moral lesson at end. ~180 words total.\n\n---\n\n### 3.2 الأسد والفأر (The Lion and the Mouse)\n\n**Summary in MSA**: A lion sleeps in the forest. A small mouse runs over him. The angry lion catches the mouse and plans to eat him. The mouse begs for mercy, promising to help someday. The lion laughs -- how could a tiny mouse help a mighty lion? -- but lets him go. Later, the lion falls into a hunter's net. He roars for help. The mouse hears him, comes running, and gnaws through the ropes to free the lion.\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: asad (lion), fa'r (mouse), ghaba (forest), nawm (sleep), ghadib (angry), rahma (mercy), wa'd (promise), shabaka (net), sayyad (hunter), hurr (free)\n\n**Pedagogical Value**: HIGH. Universal story. Simple cause-and-effect narrative. Great for animal vocabulary and size-related adjectives (kabir/saghir, qawi/da'if).\n\n---\n\n### 3.3 الثعلب والغراب (The Fox and the Raven)\n\n**Summary in MSA**: A raven sits on a high tree holding a piece of cheese in its beak. A cunning fox sees the cheese and wants it. The fox praises the raven's beauty and asks to hear its \"beautiful voice.\" When the raven opens its mouth to sing, the cheese falls. The fox snatches it and runs away.\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: tha'lab (fox), ghurab (raven), shajara (tree), jubna (cheese), minqar (beak), maki (cunning), sawt (voice), jamil (beautiful)\n\n**Pedagogical Value**: HIGH. Very short. Teaches adjectives of flattery, deception vocabulary, and animal names. Clear moral lesson about not trusting flattery.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Juha Stories (نوادر جحا)\n\nJuha (also spelled Joha) is the beloved folk trickster of Arabic culture -- a wise fool whose stories have been told for centuries. His tales are short, humorous, and use everyday vocabulary, making them ideal for language learners.\n\n### 4.1 جحا وابنه والحمار (Juha, His Son, and the Donkey)\n\n**Arabic Text**:\n\nرَكِبَ جُحا حِمارًا وَمَشى ابْنُهُ خَلْفَهُ وَمَرَّ أَمامَ جَماعَةٍ فَقالوا: أُنْظُروا إِلى هذا الرَّجُلِ الَّذي خَلا قَلْبُهُ مِنَ الشَّفَقَةِ يَرْكَبُ هُوَ وَيَتْرُكُ ابْنَهُ يَمْشي. فَنَزَلَ جُحا وَمَشى وَأَرْكَبَ ابْنَهُ. وَمَرَّ عَلى جَماعَةٍ فَقالوا: أُنْظُروا إِلى هذا الغُلامِ غَيْرِ المُؤَدَّبِ يَرْكَبُ الحِمارَ وَيَتْرُكُ أَباهُ يَمْشي.\n\nفَرَكِبَ جُحا وَابْنُهُ مَعًا. وَمَرّا عَلى جَماعَةٍ فَقالوا: أُنْظُروا إِلى هذَيْنِ لا يَرْحَمانِ الحِمارَ المِسْكينَ. فَنَزَلا وَمَشَيا مَعَ الحِمارِ. وَمَرّا عَلى جَماعَةٍ فَقالوا: أُنْظُروا إِلى هذَيْنِ الأَحْمَقَيْنِ يَمْشِيانِ وَالحِمارُ فارِغٌ.\n\nفَحَمَلَ جُحا الحِمارَ عَلى ظَهْرِهِ. فَضَحِكَ النّاسُ وَقالوا: أُنْظُروا إِلى هذا المَجْنونِ يَحْمِلُ الحِمارَ.\n\n**Translation**: Juha rode his donkey while his son walked behind. People said: \"Look at this merciless man riding while his son walks!\" So Juha got off and put his son on. Next group: \"Look at this rude boy riding while his father walks!\" So they both rode. Next group: \"Look at them, no mercy for the poor donkey!\" So they both walked. Next group: \"Look at these two fools walking with an empty donkey!\" Finally, Juha carried the donkey on his back. People laughed: \"Look at this madman carrying a donkey!\"\n\n**Moral**: لا يُمْكِنُكَ أَنْ تُرْضِيَ جَميعَ النّاسِ (You cannot please everyone.)\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: himar (donkey), rakiba (ride), masha (walk), jama'a (group), shafaqa (mercy), mu'addab (polite), miskin (poor), majnun (crazy)\n\n**Pedagogical Value**: VERY HIGH. Pure story form. Repetitive structure (5 variations of the same scenario). Teaches verb conjugations in natural context. Clear moral. Universal humor.\n\n---\n\n### 4.2 جحا يربح حمارًا (Juha Wins a Donkey)\n\n**Summary**: Juha buys ten donkeys. While riding one of them home, he counts them and only gets nine -- because he forgets to count the one he's riding! He dismounts to count again and gets ten. He rides again -- nine! He concludes that walking saves him one donkey.\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: 'ashara (ten), tis'a (nine), 'adda (count), himar (donkey), rakiba (ride), nazala (dismount), masha (walk)\n\n---\n\n### 4.3 جحا والبخيل (Juha and the Miser)\n\n**Summary**: Traveling through the desert, Juha encounters his stingy neighbor Salih eating food. Despite Juha praising Salih's dog, wife, and son, Salih refuses to share any food or water. The story satirizes stinginess and false flattery.\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: bakhil (miser), sahra' (desert), ta'am (food), ma' (water), jari (neighbor), madh (praise)\n\n---\n\n### 4.4 مسمار جحا (Juha's Nail)\n\n**Summary**: Juha sells his house but inserts a clause in the contract: he retains ownership of one nail in the wall. He then uses this nail as an excuse to visit the house anytime, hang things on it, and generally make the new owner's life miserable until the buyer gives up and returns the house.\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: mismar (nail), bayt (house), ba'a (sell), 'aqd (contract), shart (condition), za'aja (annoy)\n\n**Note**: This story is used as a proverb in Arabic culture -- \"mismar Juha\" (Juha's nail) refers to someone using a flimsy excuse to get what they want.\n\n---\n\n### 4.5 جحا وحمار الملك (Juha and the King's Donkey)\n\n**Summary**: The king orders Juha to teach his donkey to read within twenty years, or face execution. Juha agrees calmly. When asked why, he says: \"In twenty years, either I will die, or the king will die, or the donkey will die.\"\n\n**Key Vocabulary**: malik (king), himar (donkey), qira'a (reading), sana (year), mawt (death)\n\n---\n\n### 4.6 فقد جحا حماره (Juha Lost His Donkey)\n\n**Summary**: Juha loses his donkey in a crowded market. Instead of being upset, he appears happy. People ask why. He says: \"If I had been riding the donkey, I would be lost too!\"\n\n---\n\n## 5. Modern Children's Content\n\n### 5.1 YouTube Channels\n\n| Channel | Language | Content Type | Best For |\n|---------|----------|-------------|----------|\n| **Adam wa Mishmish** | MSA | Animated vocabulary songs (colors, numbers, greetings, animals) | A1 vocabulary |\n| **Learn with Zakaria** | MSA | Alphabet, numbers, animals, nasheed songs | A1 basics |\n| **AlifBee Kids** | MSA | Alphabet, colors, numbers, professions, seasons | A1 vocabulary |\n| **Lali Kids** | MSA | Original songs (days of week, months, fish, farmer, seasons) | A1-A2 thematic vocabulary |\n| **Arabic Blooms** (Ms. Radwa) | MSA | Short stories, reading practice, grammar | A1-A2 reading |\n| **Kalam Kids** | MSA + dialects | Basic emotions, words, toddler vocabulary | Pre-A1 |\n| **My Arabic Class** | MSA | Basic phrases, vocabulary, songs, animations | A1 |\n| **Little Thinking Minds** | MSA | Nursery rhyme collections (3 volumes, 50+ songs) | A1 |\n\n### 5.2 Lali Kids Song Collection (with downloadable lyrics in Arabic + English)\n- For Every Child (title track)\n- Graduation Day\n- The Music Band\n- I am Travelling\n- We the Fish\n- Days of the Week\n- Fou Fou the Elephant\n- Months of the Year\n- The Grocer\n- The Four Seasons\n- The Farmer's Big Dream\n- Because I am Special\n\n### 5.3 Little Thinking Minds Nursery Rhyme Collections\n**Volume 1** (18 songs): These Chicks, Colors All Around, The Shapes Song, This is How Birds Fly, I am an Artist, I am the Square, The Rabbit and the Deer, If I Were a Blue Butterfly, and more.\n**Volume 2**: Numbers 1-10, My Arabic Alphabet (Parts 1 & 2), and more.\n**Volume 3**: At the Zoo with Nour and Fares, Animals Under the Red Sea, My Home with Fares and Nour.\n\n### 5.4 Children's Book Publishers\n- **Dar al-Yasmine** (Jordan): 90+ children's books, ages 2-16. Notable: \"That Fish in My Stomach\", \"Don't Let Judy Go Without a Coat\"\n- **Dar al-Saqi / Saqi Kids** (Lebanon): ~15 titles/year. Notable: \"Where Do I Put You?\" by Fatima Sharafeddine\n- **Al Salwa Books**: Publishers of the famous \"32 Arabic Nursery Rhymes\" DVD/CD collection\n\n---\n\n## 6. Graded Readers and Curated Resources\n\n### 6.1 Lingualism — One Thousand and One Nights (Elementary MSA)\n- **Level**: A2 (Elementary)\n- **Content**: 10 simplified classic tales from 1001 Nights\n- **Features**: Full tashkeel (diacritics), slow native audio, comprehension questions, vocabulary lists\n- **Format**: Paperback + audio\n- **Why it's good**: Controlled vocabulary, deliberate repetition, neutral MSA pronunciation, familiar stories\n\n### 6.2 Olly Richards — Short Stories in Arabic for Intermediate Learners (MSA)\n- **Level**: B1 (Intermediate)\n- **Content**: Multiple genre stories (sci-fi, fantasy, crime, thriller)\n- **Features**: Vocabulary lists, comprehension questions\n- **Format**: Book + Audible audio\n\n### 6.3 First Arabic Reader for Beginners (Saher Ahmed Salama)\n- **Level**: A1-A2\n- **Content**: Parallel Arabic-English texts about real-life situations (meeting people, jobs, studying)\n- **Features**: Bilingual format, graded difficulty\n\n### 6.4 Tashkil.net\n- **Level**: A1-A2+\n- **Free online stories**: The Rabbit and the Turtle, Adam and the Rooster, A Gift to the Future, Thirsty Man's Kindness to a Dog, The Sheikh's Favorite Student, In Unity is Strength, The Lion and the Mouse, Sindbad the Sailor (complete book)\n- **Features**: Full tashkeel, flashcards, PDF downloads, audio narration, translation exercises\n- **Why it's good**: Free, interactive, with vocabulary support built in\n\n### 6.5 Ibnulyemen Arabic\n- **Free online short stories** in MSA with diacritics, based on Kamil Keilany's adaptations\n- **Stories available**: Juha and the Miser, Juha and the Hypocrite, and others\n- **Level**: High-beginner to pre-intermediate\n- **Features**: Vocabulary quizzes, reading comprehension questions\n\n### 6.6 Tales from Kalila wa Dimna (Munther Younes)\n- **Level**: Intermediate to Advanced\n- **Content**: Simplified animal fables from the classic Arabic collection\n- **Why it's relevant**: These 8th-century Arabic animal fables are the grandfather of all Arabic storytelling. Simplified versions exist for learners.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Pedagogical Assessment for Adult A1-A2 Learners\n\n### Tier 1: HIGHEST VALUE (MSA, simple, repetitive, broadly useful vocabulary)\n\n| Content | Language | Word Count | Key Skills |\n|---------|----------|-----------|------------|\n| **هالسيسان** (These Chicks) | Near-MSA | ~20 words | Animals, emotions, verbs of motion |\n| **الأرنب والسلحفاة** (Hare & Tortoise) | Pure MSA | ~180 words | Past tense narrative, adjectives, animals |\n| **الأسد والفأر** (Lion & Mouse) | Pure MSA | ~200 words | Past tense, size adjectives, cause/effect |\n| **جحا وابنه والحمار** (Juha & Son & Donkey) | Near-MSA | ~150 words | Repetitive structure, opinions, transport verbs |\n| **Tashkil.net stories** | MSA with tashkeel | Varies | Reading practice, vocabulary in context |\n| **الواحد عصاية** (Number Shape Rhyme) | Near-MSA | ~30 words | Numbers 1-10, adjectives |\n| **Numbers counting song** | MSA | ~40 words | Numbers, simple verbs |\n| **سنة حلوة يا جميل** (Happy Birthday) | Universal | ~15 words | Basic adjectives, endearments |\n\n### Tier 2: HIGH VALUE (mostly comprehensible, some dialect)\n\n| Content | Language | Key Skills |\n|---------|----------|------------|\n| **ماما زمانها جاية** (Mama is Coming) | Egyptian | Family, gifts, animal sounds |\n| **ذهب الليل** (Night Has Gone) | Egyptian/MSA | Time of day, animals, food |\n| **طيري يا عصفورة** (Fly Little Bird) | Lebanese/MSA | Bird/nature vocabulary, freedom |\n| **يلا تنام ريما** (Fairuz lullaby) | Lebanese | Sleep, prayer, cooking, family |\n| **حمامة نودي نودي** (Dove Fly) | Gulf | Chain narrative, everyday objects |\n| **عصفور طل من الشباك** (Sparrow at Window) | Levantine | Question/answer dialogue, fear, freedom |\n| **Juha stories** (all) | Near-MSA | Humor, daily life, moral lessons |\n| **الثعلب والغراب** (Fox & Raven) | Pure MSA | Adjectives of flattery, animal names |\n\n### Tier 3: MODERATE VALUE (heavy dialect or very specific)\n\n| Content | Language | Notes |\n|---------|----------|-------|\n| **يا باح يا باح** (Tickle Game) | Levantine | Body parts, but heavily dialectal |\n| **انا البطة الشلبية** (Pretty Duck) | Egyptian | Funny but dialectal |\n| **آ شتا تاتا** (Rain Chant) | Moroccan Darija | Too dialectal for MSA learners |\n\n### Why Children's Content Works for Adult A1-A2 Learners\n\nResearch supports using children's songs and stories for adult language acquisition:\n\n1. **Repetition**: Songs use natural repetition patterns that reinforce vocabulary without feeling like drilling.\n2. **Clear pronunciation**: Children's content features slower, clearer articulation -- valuable for training the ear.\n3. **Controlled vocabulary**: Limited word pools match A1-A2 capacity (~300-600 words).\n4. **Narrative structure**: Fables follow predictable patterns (problem -> attempt -> resolution), making comprehension easier.\n5. **Cultural integration**: Learning the songs and stories that every Arab child knows creates genuine cultural connection.\n6. **Memory anchoring**: Melodies create strong neural connections for vocabulary retention.\n7. **Comprehensible input**: Short texts with high-frequency vocabulary provide ideal i+1 input.\n\n### Caveats\n\n1. **Dialect mixing**: Many classic nursery rhymes are dialectal (Egyptian, Lebanese, Levantine). This is actually a feature, not a bug -- adult learners benefit from early dialect exposure alongside MSA.\n2. **Some vocabulary is childish**: Words like \"nunu\" (baby), \"bisbis\" (kitty) are fun but not critical for adult communication.\n3. **Cultural sensitivity**: Some adults feel silly learning children's content. Frame it as \"cultural literacy\" rather than \"children's material.\"\n\n---\n\n## 8. Implementation Recommendations for Alif\n\n### Short-term: Import as Story Mode content\nThe following could be imported as short stories in Alif's story mode:\n\n1. **الأرنب والسلحفاة** — Full MSA text with tashkeel available (see section 3.1)\n2. **الأسد والفأر** — MSA text widely available online\n3. **الثعلب والغراب** — Short MSA fable\n4. **جحا وابنه والحمار** — Near-MSA, repetitive structure\n5. **مسمار جحا** — Simple vocabulary, cultural reference\n6. **Tashkil.net stories** — Already have tashkeel, designed for learners\n\n### Medium-term: Nursery rhyme reading cards\nSimple rhymes could become reading-mode material:\n- **هالسيسان** (20 words, very simple)\n- **الواحد عصاية** (numbers + adjectives)\n- **سنة حلوة يا جميل** (birthday song, 15 words)\n\n### External resources to recommend to users\n- **Tashkil.net** — Free MSA stories with full diacritics\n- **Lingualism 1001 Nights** — Elementary MSA graded reader\n- **Adam wa Mishmish** (YouTube) — MSA vocabulary songs\n- **Arabic Blooms** (YouTube) — MSA short stories for reading practice\n\n---\n\n## Sources\n\n### Lyrics and Text Sources\n- [Mama Lisa's World - Arabic Children's Songs](https://www.mamalisa.com/?lang=Arabic&t=el)\n- [LyricsTranslate - Arabic Children's Songs Collection](https://lyricstranslate.com/en/arabic-childrens-songs-lyrics.html)\n- [A Crafty Arab - These Chicks Nursery Rhyme](https://acraftyarab.com/2011/03/arabic-these-chicks-nursery-rhyme-resource/)\n- [FableReads - Arabic Fables](https://www.fablereads.com/ar/ar-fable/the-tortoise-and-the-hare)\n- [WorldStories - Juha Stories in Arabic](https://worldstories.org.uk/reader/juha-stories/arabic/937)\n- [Transparent Language - Juha and the Donkey](https://blogs.transparent.com/arabic/juha-his-son-and-the-donkey-%D8%AC%D8%AD%D8%A7-%D9%88%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%86%D9%87-%D9%88%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AD%D9%85%D8%A7%D8%B1/)\n- [Ibnulyemen Arabic - Juha and the Miser](https://www.ibnulyemenarabic.com/arabic-language/arabic-short-story-3/)\n- [Simple Arabic For Learners - Juha's Nail](https://arabicwritings.substack.com/p/547)\n\n### Modern Content and YouTube\n- [Adam wa Mishmish](https://www.adamwamishmish.com/)\n- [Al Fusaic - Adam wa Mishmish Review](https://www.alfusaic.net/blog/recommendations/adam-wa-mishmish)\n- [AlifBee Kids Blog - 15 Songs for Kids in Arabic](https://blog.alifbeekids.com/songs-for-kids-in-arabic/)\n- [Lali Kids / Arabic Songs for Kids](https://arabicsongsforkids.com/)\n- [eArabic - 17 Arabic Educational Songs](https://earabic.io/blog/best-17-arabic-educational-songs-for-kids)\n- [Welcome2Jordan - 5 Famous Arabic Children's Songs](https://welcome2jordan.com/arabic-childrens-songs/)\n- [In The Playroom - 10 Arabic Songs and Rhymes](https://intheplayroom.co.uk/10-arabic-songs-and-rhymes-for-children/)\n\n### Graded Readers and Learning Resources\n- [Lingualism - One Thousand and One Nights (Elementary MSA)](https://lingualism.com/products/one-thousand-and-one-nights-elementary-msa)\n- [Tashkil.net - Arabic Stories with Diacritics](https://www.tashkil.net/)\n- [Learning Arabic with Angela - Free Short Stories](https://www.learningarabicwithangela.com/post/free-online-arabic-short-stories-for-pdf-download-stage-1-children-and-beginners)\n- [Fluent Arabic - Books with Harakat](https://fluentarabic.net/arabic-books-with-harakat/)\n- [Olly Richards - Short Stories in Arabic (Intermediate)](https://us.teachyourself.com/products/short-stories-in-arabic-for-intermediate-learners-msa)\n\n### Publishers\n- [ArabKidLitNow - Contemporary Arabic Children's Literature](https://arabkidlitnow.com/)\n- [ArabLit - 10 Arabic Children's Literature Publishers](https://arablit.org/2023/09/19/arabic-literature-for-young-readers-10-publishers-to-know/)\n- [Dar al-Yasmine](https://arabkidlitnow.com/tag/dar-al-yasmine/)\n- [Al Salwa Books - Arabic Nursery Rhymes](https://alsalwabooks.com/en/shop/arabic-childrens-books/board-books/arabic-nursery-rhymes-toddlers/arabic-nursery-rhymes-cd-2/)\n\n### Research / Pedagogical\n- [Fairuz Yalla Tnam Rima - Lebanese Lullaby Analysis](https://lebanese-arabic.com/lullaby/)\n- [Lebanese Arabic Blog on Lullabies](http://www.lullabyabc.com/international_lullabies/arabic/index.html)\n- [Arabic Vocabulary by Level A1-C1](https://earabiclearning.com/blog/2026/01/arabic-vocabulary-by-level/)\n- [Research on Songs in Arabic Language Teaching](https://jurnaledukasia.org/index.php/edukasia/article/view/1427)\n- [Kalila wa Dimna - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kal%C4%ABla_wa-Dimna)\n- [Kalila wa Dimna for Students of Arabic (Munther Younes)](https://www.amazon.com/Tales-Kalila-Wa-Dimna-Students/dp/0879501316)\n", "date": "Feb 21", "category": "education", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/arabic-children-content-2026-02-21.md"}, "arabic-datasets-corpora": {"title": "Arabic Language Learning & NLP: Datasets and Corpora", "content": "# Arabic Language Learning & NLP: Datasets and Corpora\n\nA comprehensive survey of freely (and some paid) available datasets, corpora, dictionaries, and tools for Arabic language learning and NLP, with a focus on Modern Standard Arabic (MSA / fusha).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-02-08\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Meta-Catalogues](#1-meta-catalogues)\n2. [Word Frequency Lists](#2-word-frequency-lists)\n3. [Machine-Readable Dictionaries](#3-machine-readable-dictionaries)\n4. [Root-Word Databases](#4-root-word-databases)\n5. [Morphological Databases & Tools](#5-morphological-databases--tools)\n6. [Diacritized (Vowelized) Corpora](#6-diacritized-vowelized-corpora)\n7. [Parallel Corpora (Arabic-English)](#7-parallel-corpora-arabic-english)\n8. [Treebanks](#8-treebanks)\n9. [Large MSA Text Corpora](#9-large-msa-text-corpora)\n10. [Graded / Readability-Classified Resources](#10-graded--readability-classified-resources)\n11. [Semantic Resources (WordNet / Ontology)](#11-semantic-resources-wordnet--ontology)\n12. [Quranic & Classical Arabic Corpora](#12-quranic--classical-arabic-corpora)\n13. [Diacritization & Morphological Tools (Software)](#13-diacritization--morphological-tools-software)\n14. [Dialect Resources](#14-dialect-resources)\n15. [Wiktionary & Wikipedia Derived Data](#15-wiktionary--wikipedia-derived-data)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Meta-Catalogues\n\nBefore diving into individual resources, these catalogues index hundreds of Arabic NLP datasets.\n\n### Masader (ARBML)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** The largest public catalogue for Arabic NLP and speech datasets. 600+ datasets, each annotated with 25+ metadata attributes (language variety, domain, task, license, size, etc.)\n- **Access:** Web interface, or programmatic via HuggingFace: `load_dataset('arbml/masader')`\n- **License:** GPL-3.0 (the catalogue itself)\n\n### NNLP-IL Arabic Resources List\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Comprehensive curated list of Arabic NLP resources, tools, datasets, and corpora.\n\n### Awesome Arabic NLP\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Extensive collection of resources, tools, datasets, and best practices for Arabic NLP.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Word Frequency Lists\n\n### CAMeL Lab Arabic Frequency Lists\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Frequency lists derived from the pretraining data of CamelBERT models. Massive scale: 17.3 billion tokens total, 16.1M unique word types.\n- **MSA file:** `MSA_freq_lists.tsv.zip` -- 11.4M unique MSA word types with frequencies\n- **Format:** TSV (word in Arabic script + frequency count)\n- **Other varieties:** Classical Arabic (2.4M types), Dialectal Arabic (6.7M types), Mixed (16.1M types)\n- **Quality:** Very high quality; derived from curated pretraining datasets. Undiacritized.\n- **License:** Check repository LICENSE.txt\n\n### KELLY Project Arabic Word List\n\n- **URL:** \n- **Leeds mirror:** \n- **What:** Pedagogically-oriented frequency lists for language learners, developed for 9 languages including Arabic. Words ranked by frequency (instances per million) and tagged with suggested CEFR levels (A1-C2).\n- **Format:** Excel (.xlsx)\n- **License:** CC BY-NC-SA 2.0\n- **Quality:** Small but high-quality curated list aimed at learners. One of the few resources that maps Arabic words to CEFR levels.\n\n### Wiktionary Frequency Lists for Arabic\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Links to 50K+ word frequency lists based on OpenSubtitles (CC BY-SA-4.0) and lists from Leeds University's Centre for Translation Studies (CC BY-2.5).\n- **Quality:** Useful starting point, but no morphological or diacritical annotations.\n\n### Buckwalter & Parkinson: A Frequency Dictionary of Arabic\n\n- **Internet Archive:** \n- **Publisher:** Routledge (2011)\n- **What:** 5,000 most frequent MSA words + dialect coverage. Originally a paid book, but full text available on Internet Archive.\n- **Format:** Text (scanned/OCR)\n- **Quality:** Excellent pedagogical resource. Frequency counts from a balanced corpus.\n- **Note:** The digital scan may require OCR cleanup for machine use.\n\n### Abu El-Khair Corpus Word Frequency Lists\n\n- **URL:** \n- **HuggingFace:** \n- **What:** Top 30,000 word forms for each of 10 news domains + master frequency list, derived from 5M+ articles / 1.5B words.\n- **Format:** Excel (.xlsx)\n- **License:** Free for research\n\n### Top 50,000 Arabic Words (modernstandardarabic.com)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** 50,000 most common Arabic words ranked by frequency in media/publications, with English translations.\n- **Note:** Premium/paid resource for the full list.\n\n### ArTenTen (Lexical Computing / Sketch Engine)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Frequency lists generated from ArTenTen, a large web corpus from Arab-country websites. Enriched with POS tags.\n- **Note:** Free sample lists available; full access requires Sketch Engine subscription.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Machine-Readable Dictionaries\n\n### Kaikki.org Arabic Dictionary (Wiktionary Extract)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **Raw data:** \n- **What:** Complete machine-readable extraction of all Arabic entries from English Wiktionary.\n- **Size:** 57,592 distinct words, 384.8 MB download\n- **Format:** JSONL (one JSON object per line per sense)\n- **Fields:** Word form, definitions/glosses, part of speech, etymology, pronunciations, translations, inflections, examples, etc.\n- **License:** CC BY-SA 3.0 (inherited from Wiktionary)\n- **Updates:** Weekly re-extraction from latest Wiktionary dumps\n- **Tool:** Generated by [wiktextract](https://github.com/tatuylonen/wiktextract)\n- **Quality:** Excellent breadth. Covers both MSA and dialects. Includes roots, verb forms, plurals. Variable depth per entry.\n\n### Arramooz (Arabic Dictionary for Morphological Analysis)\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **SourceForge:** \n- **What:** Open-source Arabic dictionary designed for morphological analysis. Covers verbs, nouns, and stop words. Derived from Ayaspell Arabic spellchecker.\n- **Format:** Tab-separated text, SQL database, XML, StarDict, Python+SQLite\n- **Python API:** \n- **License:** GPL-2.0\n- **Quality:** Good for NLP integration. Includes word frequency data (`data/nouns/wordfreq.csv`).\n\n### Ejtaal / Arabic Almanac (Hans Wehr, Lane's Lexicon, etc.)\n\n- **Web app:** \n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Compilation of major Arabic-English dictionaries searchable by root: Hans Wehr (4th ed.), Lane's Lexicon, al-Mawrid, Hava, Hinds/Badawi (Egyptian). Page images with root-based lookup.\n- **Format:** HTML/JavaScript app (downloadable as zip for offline use). Dictionary pages are scanned images, not structured data.\n- **License:** Varies by dictionary. The web interface code is open source.\n- **Quality:** Gold-standard dictionaries but stored as page images, not structured text. Best for human lookup, not programmatic extraction.\n\n### ARALEX (Lexical Database for MSA)\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **URL:** Historically at `www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk:8081/aralex.online/login.jsp` (may be down)\n- **What:** Lexical database based on a 40M word MSA corpus. Provides token/type frequencies for roots, word patterns, bigrams, trigrams.\n- **License:** GNU-like license\n- **Quality:** Excellent for psycholinguistic research. Useful frequency data organized by root patterns (wazn). Status of online availability uncertain.\n\n### Buckwalter Arabic Morphological Analyzer (BAMA)\n\n- **v1.0 (free):** (also on SourceForge as AraMorph: )\n- **v2.0 (paid):** \n- **SAMA v3.1 (paid):** \n- **What:** The foundational Arabic morphological analyzer. Contains lexicons with ~83,000 entries of Arabic prefixes, suffixes, and stems. Perl + data files.\n- **License:** v1.0 is GPL-2.0 (free). v2.0 and SAMA require LDC membership (paid).\n- **Quality:** Industry standard. The underlying lexicon data is extremely valuable for root/pattern analysis.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Root-Word Databases\n\n### Arabic Roots and Derivatives (SourceForge)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Database of Arabic roots and their derivatives (voweled + unvoweled) with stems. Extracted from the classical dictionary \"Taj al-Arus\" (تاج العروس من جواهر القاموس).\n- **Size:** 142,000+ records, 10,000+ roots\n- **Format:** MySQL database dump (`KhorsiCorpus.sql.bz2`, 1.3 MB compressed)\n- **License:** CC BY-SA 3.0\n- **Quality:** Beta stage (last updated 2013). Classical Arabic focus. Very useful for root-to-derivative mapping.\n\n### Root Words of Quran\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Every word in the Quran mapped to its root, with frequency counts.\n- **Quality:** Complete for Quranic vocabulary. Limited to ~1,700 unique roots appearing in the Quran.\n\n### Aratools Dictionary\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Arabic-English dictionary with root-based lookup. Each entry includes English translation, word class, and Arabic root.\n- **Format:** Web interface (no bulk download apparent)\n\n### Living Arabic Project\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Database searchable by Arabic word, English word, and Arabic root. Covers classical Arabic and dialects.\n- **Format:** Web interface\n\n---\n\n## 5. Morphological Databases & Tools\n\n### CAMeL Tools\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **Paper:** \n- **What:** Full Arabic NLP Python toolkit from NYU Abu Dhabi. Includes morphological analysis, disambiguation, POS tagging, NER, sentiment analysis, dialect identification, tokenization, diacritization.\n- **Morphological DBs included:** `calima-msa-r13` (MSA) and `calima-egy-r13` (Egyptian Arabic)\n- **License:** MIT\n- **Requirements:** Python 3.8-3.12 (64-bit), Rust compiler\n- **Quality:** State of the art. Actively maintained. The `calima-msa` database is effectively a modernized Buckwalter lexicon.\n\n### KALIMAT Corpus\n\n- **What:** 20,291 MSA documents from Omani newspaper Al Watan, with summaries, named entities, POS tags, and morphological analysis.\n- **Quality:** Well-annotated MSA news corpus.\n\n### OSIAN (Open Source International Arabic News)\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **What:** 3.5M articles, 37M sentences, ~1B tokens from 32 international Arabic news sources (CNN Arabic, Al Jazeera, DW, RT, etc.). Each word annotated with lemma and POS.\n- **Format:** XML with metadata per article\n- **License:** CC BY-NC 4.0\n- **Access:** Via CLARIN infrastructure\n- **Quality:** Large-scale, lemmatized, POS-tagged MSA corpus. Used for training AraBERT.\n\n### Khaleej-2004 and Watan-2004 (Arabic Corpus on SourceForge)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Khaleej-2004 (5,690 docs, 4 topics) + Watan-2004 (20,291 docs, 6 topics). Useful for text classification.\n- **License:** Free for research\n\n---\n\n## 6. Diacritized (Vowelized) Corpora\n\n### Tashkeela\n\n- **SourceForge:** \n- **Kaggle:** \n- **Paper:** \n- **What:** ~75 million fully vocalized Arabic words from 97 books. The largest freely available diacritized corpus.\n- **Breakdown:** 98.85% classical/Islamic texts (from Shamela Library), 1.15% MSA (Al Jazeera, al-kalema.org, etc.)\n- **Format:** Plain text and XML files organized by source\n- **License:** Free / open\n- **Quality:** Excellent for training diacritization models. Heavy classical bias. The MSA portion is small (~868K words) but still useful.\n\n### Tashkeela Benchmark (Cleaned Subset)\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Cleaned benchmark extracted from Tashkeela: 55K lines, ~2.3M words. Standard train/dev/test splits.\n- **Format:** Text files\n- **Quality:** Well-preprocessed. Standard benchmark for diacritization research.\n\n### Tanzil Quran Text\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** The complete Quran text in multiple formats, fully diacritized. Version 1.1 (Feb 2021).\n- **Format:** Plain text, XML, SQL. Multiple transcription styles.\n- **License:** Free (with attribution)\n- **Quality:** Gold-standard diacritization. ~77K words. Classical Arabic, not MSA.\n\n### Sadeed Tashkeela (HuggingFace)\n\n- **HuggingFace:** \n- **What:** A curated diacritized Arabic dataset on HuggingFace.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Parallel Corpora (Arabic-English)\n\n### United Nations Parallel Corpus (UNPC) v1.0\n\n- **Official download:** \n- **OPUS mirror:** \n- **Paper:** Ziemski et al. (2016), LREC\n- **What:** Manually translated UN documents (1990-2014) for all 6 official UN languages.\n- **Size:** ~20 million Arabic-English sentence pairs\n- **License:** Free with attribution to the United Nations\n- **Quality:** Very high quality (professional human translation). Formal/legal register.\n\n### OPUS Collection\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** The largest collection of freely available parallel corpora. Key Arabic-English resources include:\n - **OpenSubtitles:** ~30M+ Arabic-English sentence pairs from movie/TV subtitles. Informal register.\n - **MultiUN:** ~20M sentence pairs from UN documents.\n - **CCAligned / CCMatrix:** Web-crawled parallel data using LASER embeddings. Very large but noisier.\n - **WikiMatrix:** Parallel sentences mined from Wikipedia.\n - **Tatoeba:** Small but high-quality sentence pairs.\n - **QED:** Educational video subtitles.\n- **Format:** Various (TMX, Moses format, JSONL). Downloadable via OpusTools Python package.\n- **License:** Varies by sub-corpus (mostly CC or public domain)\n- **Quality:** Ranges from excellent (UN, Tatoeba) to noisy (web-crawled).\n\n### MADAR Parallel Corpus\n\n- **Download:** \n- **Internet Archive:** \n- **What:** Parallel sentences covering dialects of 25 Arab cities + MSA + English + French. Travel domain.\n- **License:** Non-commercial research use\n- **Quality:** Unique resource for dialect-MSA-English comparison.\n\n---\n\n## 8. Treebanks\n\n### UD Arabic-PADT (Universal Dependencies)\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **UD page:** \n- **What:** Based on the Prague Arabic Dependency Treebank. 7,664 sentences, 282,384 tokens. Newswire domain. Full morphological and syntactic annotation in UD format.\n- **Format:** CoNLL-U\n- **License:** CC BY-NC-SA 3.0\n- **Quality:** High. Standard UD annotations. Freely downloadable. The most accessible Arabic treebank.\n\n### UD Arabic-NYUAD\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Based on the Penn Arabic Treebank. Annotations released without underlying text due to LDC licensing.\n- **Note:** Requires separate purchase of PATB data from LDC to reconstruct full treebank.\n\n### UD Arabic-PUD\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Parallel Universal Dependencies treebank. 1,000 sentences translated from English.\n- **License:** CC BY-SA 4.0\n\n### Penn Arabic Treebank (PATB) -- PAID\n\n- **LDC:** (Part 3), and other parts\n- **What:** The gold standard Arabic treebank. Multiple parts covering newswire. Full constituency parse trees + morphological annotation.\n- **License:** LDC membership required (paid)\n- **Quality:** Highest quality. Used extensively in Arabic NLP research.\n\n### Prague Arabic Dependency Treebank (PADT) 1.0\n\n- **Download:** \n- **LDC:** \n- **What:** ~212,500 tokens of MSA newswire with multi-level annotation (morphology, analytical syntax, tectogrammatical).\n- **License:** Free via LINDAT/CLARIN repository\n- **Quality:** Very high. The basis for UD Arabic-PADT.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Large MSA Text Corpora\n\n### Abu El-Khair Corpus (1.5 Billion Words)\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **HuggingFace:** \n- **What:** 5M+ newspaper articles from 10 major Arabic news sources across 8 countries, collected over 14 years.\n- **Size:** 1.5 billion words, ~3M unique word forms\n- **Format:** UTF-8 and Windows CP-1256, marked up in SGML and XML\n- **License:** Free for research\n- **Quality:** The largest freely available MSA corpus. Good variety across countries and time periods.\n\n### OSIAN Corpus\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **Size:** ~1 billion tokens, 3.5M articles, 37M sentences\n- **License:** CC BY-NC 4.0\n- (See Section 5 for details)\n\n### Hindawi Foundation Arabic E-Book Corpus\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** 1,745 books (81.5M words) published by Hindawi Foundation (2008-2024). Covers non-fiction, novels, children's literature, poetry, plays.\n- **License:** Freely available (Hindawi publishes all content openly)\n- **Quality:** High-quality edited literary and non-fiction MSA. Excellent for building diverse reading corpora.\n\n### Shamela Corpus\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **KITAB download (Zenodo):** See KITAB project: \n- **What:** ~1 billion words across 14 centuries of Arabic texts (6,000+ texts). Drawn from Al-Maktaba Al-Shamela.\n- **Size:** KITAB release: 1,859 authors, 4,288 titles, 755M words\n- **License:** Open access (via Zenodo)\n- **Quality:** Primarily classical Arabic. Cleaned and processed with morphological analyzer. Some texts are diacritized.\n\n### Arabic Gigaword -- PAID\n\n- **LDC:** (5th edition)\n- **What:** Comprehensive archive of Arabic newswire text. Multiple editions.\n- **License:** LDC membership required\n- **Quality:** Gold standard for large-scale MSA news text.\n\n### TALAA Corpus\n\n- **What:** Free general Arabic corpus from daily newspaper websites. 14M+ words, 57,827 articles.\n- **License:** Free\n\n---\n\n## 10. Graded / Readability-Classified Resources\n\n### Arabic Vocabulary Profile (AVP) -- A1 CEFR\n\n- **URL:** \n- **Authors:** Familiar, L.; Atanassova, G.; Soliman, R. (2025)\n- **What:** 1,625 lexical items across 11 grammatical categories (nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs, particles, pronouns, etc.) with English translations, validated by 71 expert teachers of Arabic as a second language.\n- **CEFR coverage:** A1 only (A2 in progress)\n- **Format:** Interactive web app with embedded data (extractable from page source)\n- **Quality:** The only rigorously validated CEFR-profiled Arabic vocabulary list available.\n\n### KELLY Project Arabic List (with CEFR levels)\n\n- (See Section 2 above)\n- **Includes:** CEFR level annotations (A1-C2) for each word\n- **Quality:** Corpus-based assignment of CEFR levels using pedagogical criteria\n\n### BAREC (Balanced Arabic Readability Evaluation Corpus)\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **What:** 69,441 sentences (1M+ words) annotated across 19 readability levels (kindergarten to postgraduate). The largest Arabic readability corpus.\n- **Annotation:** Manually annotated at sentence level. High inter-annotator agreement (81.8% QWK).\n- **Levels:** 19 fine-grained levels that map to 7, 5, and 3-level collapsed versions. Aligned to school grades and Arabi21 guidelines.\n- **Quality:** State of the art. Published 2025.\n\n### SAMER Arabic Text Simplification Corpus\n\n- **Paper:** \n- **What:** First manually annotated Arabic parallel corpus for text simplification. 159K words from 15 Arabic fiction novels (mostly 1865-1955). Includes readability level annotations at document and word levels, plus two simplified parallel versions targeting different learner levels.\n- **License:** Publicly available\n- **Quality:** Unique resource combining readability assessment with actual simplified text versions.\n\n### DARES 2.0\n\n- **What:** Concept-based readability training dataset for Saudi educational texts, grades 1-12.\n- **Quality:** Curriculum-aligned. Good for educational applications.\n\n### Arabi21 Framework\n\n- **What:** The Arab Thought Foundation funded the leveling of 9,000+ children's books. The Arabi21 framework provides Arabic-specific CEFR-like level descriptors.\n- **Note:** Framework documentation exists; not clear how much of the actual leveled book data is freely downloadable.\n\n---\n\n## 11. Semantic Resources (WordNet / Ontology)\n\n### Arabic Ontology (Birzeit University)\n\n- **Portal:** \n- **Paper:** \n- **What:** A formal Arabic wordnet with ontologically clean content. ~1,800 well-investigated concepts + 16,000 partially validated concepts. 150 Arabic-multilingual lexicons.\n- **Mappings:** Fully mapped to Princeton WordNet, Wikidata\n- **Access:** Searchable web portal. Data availability for bulk download unclear -- contact SinaLab.\n- **Quality:** Ontologically rigorous. Best Arabic WordNet available.\n\n### Arabic WordNet (SourceForge)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Multi-lingual concept dictionary mapped to Princeton WordNet v2.0. Supports Arabic-English search.\n- **License:** Free download\n- **Quality:** Older project. Less actively maintained than Birzeit's Arabic Ontology.\n\n### SinaLab NLP Resources\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Collection of Arabic NLP resources from Birzeit University including the Arabic Ontology, corpora, and tools.\n\n---\n\n## 12. Quranic & Classical Arabic Corpora\n\n### Quranic Arabic Corpus\n\n- **URL:** \n- **Download:** \n- **What:** Complete morphological annotation of all 77,430 words in the Quran. Each word tagged with part of speech and multiple morphological features. Also includes syntactic treebank and semantic ontology.\n- **Format:** Downloadable data (requires email for access)\n- **License:** GNU General Public License\n- **Quality:** Gold standard. Every word manually annotated. Invaluable for studying Arabic morphology.\n\n### Quran Morphology (GitHub)\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **What:** Morphological data from the Quranic Arabic Corpus in a more accessible format.\n\n### Tanzil Quran Text\n\n- (See Section 6)\n\n### Shamela Library / KITAB Corpus\n\n- (See Section 9)\n- **Note:** The Shamela library itself () contains 10,000+ classical Arabic texts browsable online.\n\n---\n\n## 13. Diacritization & Morphological Tools (Software)\n\nThese are NLP tools (not datasets), but they contain or depend on valuable lexical databases and can generate annotated data.\n\n### CAMeL Tools\n\n- (See Section 5)\n- **Diacritization:** Includes diacritization module for MSA and Egyptian\n\n### Farasa\n\n- **URL:** \n- **Python wrapper:** \n- **What:** Fast Arabic text processing toolkit from QCRI. Segmentation, lemmatization, POS tagging, diacritization, dependency parsing, NER, spell-checking.\n- **License:** Free for academic/research use. RESTful API available.\n- **Quality:** State of the art in Arabic segmentation. Diacritization module available.\n\n### Mishkal\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **PyPI:** \n- **What:** Open-source Arabic text diacritizer (rule-based). Uses Arramooz dictionary and Qalsadi morphological analyzer. Console, desktop, and web interfaces.\n- **License:** Open source (GPL)\n- **Quality:** Rule-based, so less accurate than neural approaches (DER ~13.78%), but transparent and customizable.\n\n### Tashaphyne (Arabic Light Stemmer)\n\n- **GitHub:** \n- **PyPI:** Available on PyPI\n- **What:** Arabic light stemmer and segmentor. Supports prefix/suffix removal, root extraction. Customizable affix lists.\n- **License:** Open source\n- **Quality:** Good for lightweight stemming without requiring full morphological analysis.\n\n### Shakkala (Deep Learning Diacritizer)\n\n- **What:** Character-level deep learning diacritization system using bidirectional LSTM.\n- **Quality:** DER ~2.88%, WER ~6.37% -- significantly better than rule-based systems.\n\n### Stanza (Stanford NLP)\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Stanford's Python NLP library with Arabic support. Tokenization, POS tagging, lemmatization, dependency parsing.\n- **License:** Apache 2.0\n\n---\n\n## 14. Dialect Resources\n\n### MADAR Parallel Corpus\n\n- (See Section 7)\n- **Covers:** 25 city dialects + MSA + English + French\n\n### NADI Shared Tasks\n\n- **URL:** \n- **What:** Annual shared task for Arabic dialect identification and processing. Releases dialect-labeled datasets each year.\n\n### Palm Dataset\n\n- **HuggingFace:** \n- **What:** 10 Arabic dialects + MSA, built by 44 native speakers from 15 countries.\n\n### AraDiCE\n\n- **HuggingFace:** \n- **What:** Arabic dialect capabilities benchmark. Accepted at COLING 2025.\n\n---\n\n## 15. Wiktionary & Wikipedia Derived Data\n\n### Kaikki.org / Wiktextract\n\n- (See Section 3 for full details)\n- **Arabic entries:** 57,592 distinct words\n- **Format:** JSONL, 384.8 MB\n- **How to use:** Download JSONL, parse with any JSON library. Each entry contains definitions, POS, etymology, inflections, translations.\n\n### Arabic Wikipedia Dumps\n\n- **Wikimedia downloads:** \n- **GitHub tool:** (Arabic Corpus Generator from Wikipedia)\n- **GitHub tool:** (Arabic Wikipedia text extracts)\n- **Kaggle:** \n- **Gensim:** `gensim.corpora.wikicorpus` can process Arabic Wikipedia dumps directly\n- **What:** Arabic Wikipedia contains 1M+ articles. Can be processed into plain text corpus.\n- **Quality:** Good MSA with some dialect mixing. Useful for building frequency lists, training language models, etc.\n\n### Wiktionary Frequency Lists\n\n- (See Section 2)\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table: Quick Reference\n\n| Resource | Type | Size | Free? | Format | MSA? |\n|----------|------|------|-------|--------|------|\n| CAMeL Frequency Lists | Frequency | 11.4M MSA types | Yes | TSV | Yes |\n| KELLY Arabic | Frequency + CEFR | ~9K words | Yes | Excel | Yes |\n| Kaikki.org Arabic | Dictionary | 57.6K words | Yes | JSONL | Yes |\n| Arramooz | Dictionary | Verbs+Nouns+Stops | Yes | SQL/XML/TSV | Yes |\n| Buckwalter v1 | Morph. Analyzer | 83K entries | Yes (GPL) | Perl+data | Yes |\n| Arabic Roots & Derivs | Root DB | 142K records, 10K roots | Yes (CC) | MySQL | Classical |\n| Tashkeela | Diacritized | 75M words | Yes | Text/XML | Mostly Classical |\n| Tanzil Quran | Diacritized | 77K words | Yes | Text/XML/SQL | Classical |\n| UNPC | Parallel | 20M sent. pairs | Yes | Various | Yes |\n| OPUS OpenSubtitles | Parallel | 30M+ sent. pairs | Yes | Various | Mixed |\n| UD Arabic-PADT | Treebank | 282K tokens | Yes (CC) | CoNLL-U | Yes |\n| Abu El-Khair | Text corpus | 1.5B words | Yes | SGML/XML | Yes |\n| OSIAN | Text corpus | 1B tokens | CC BY-NC | XML | Yes |\n| Hindawi E-Books | Text corpus | 81.5M words | Yes | Text | Yes |\n| Shamela/KITAB | Text corpus | 755M words | Yes | Various | Classical |\n| Quranic Corpus | Morphological | 77K words | Yes (GNU) | Various | Classical |\n| AVP A1 | CEFR vocab | 1,625 items | Yes | Web/JS | Yes |\n| BAREC | Readability | 69K sentences | TBD | Text | Yes |\n| SAMER | Simplification | 159K words | Yes | Text | Yes |\n| Arabic Ontology | WordNet | 16K+ concepts | Web access | Various | Yes |\n| CAMeL Tools | NLP toolkit | -- | Yes (MIT) | Python | Yes |\n| Farasa | NLP toolkit | -- | Academic | Java/API | Yes |\n| Masader | Catalogue | 600+ datasets | Yes | Web/HF | -- |\n\n---\n\n## Recommended Starting Points\n\nFor building an Arabic language learning application, the most immediately useful freely available resources are:\n\n1. **Dictionary data:** Kaikki.org Wiktionary extract (57K entries in JSONL with definitions, POS, inflections)\n2. **Frequency data:** CAMeL Lab MSA Frequency Lists (11.4M types from 17.3B tokens)\n3. **Root mapping:** Arabic Roots and Derivatives on SourceForge (142K entries, 10K roots)\n4. **Morphological analysis:** CAMeL Tools (Python, MIT license, includes MSA lexicon)\n5. **Diacritization training data:** Tashkeela corpus (75M diacritized words)\n6. **CEFR vocabulary:** KELLY Arabic word list (frequency + CEFR levels) + AVP A1 dataset\n7. **Parallel text:** UN Parallel Corpus (20M sentence pairs, high quality)\n8. **Treebank/syntax:** UD Arabic-PADT (282K tokens, free, CoNLL-U format)\n9. **Readability levels:** BAREC corpus (69K sentences across 19 difficulty levels)\n10. **Literary texts:** Hindawi E-Book Corpus (81.5M words across genres)\n\n---\n\n## Notes on LDC Resources (Paid)\n\nThe Linguistic Data Consortium (LDC) at University of Pennsylvania hosts many high-quality Arabic resources that require membership fees:\n\n- **Arabic Gigaword** (5th ed.) -- Large-scale newswire corpus\n- **Penn Arabic Treebank** (Parts 1-3) -- Gold-standard syntactic annotation\n- **Buckwalter Morphological Analyzer v2.0 / SAMA v3.1** -- Enhanced morphological lexicons\n- **Arabic Broadcast News/Conversations** -- Speech corpora\n\nLDC membership costs vary; some universities have institutional access. For free alternatives, the resources listed above cover most of the same ground.\n", "date": null, "category": "nlp", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21", "vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/arabic-datasets-corpora.md"}, "arabic-diacritization": {"title": "Arabic Diacritization (Tashkeel) - Comprehensive Research", "content": "# Arabic Diacritization (Tashkeel) - Comprehensive Research\n\n> Research compiled February 2026 for language learning app development.\n> Focus: Modern Standard Arabic (MSA / fusha) diacritization tools and models.\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Background](#background)\n2. [Evaluation Metrics](#evaluation-metrics)\n3. [Accuracy Benchmark Summary](#accuracy-benchmark-summary)\n4. [Deep Learning Models](#deep-learning-models)\n5. [Rule-Based and Hybrid Systems](#rule-based-and-hybrid-systems)\n6. [Production-Ready Libraries](#production-ready-libraries)\n7. [LLM-Based Diacritization](#llm-based-diacritization)\n8. [Partial / Selective Diacritization](#partial--selective-diacritization)\n9. [Evaluation and Verification Tools](#evaluation-and-verification-tools)\n10. [Datasets and Corpora](#datasets-and-corpora)\n11. [API Services](#api-services)\n12. [Practical Recommendations for a Language Learning App](#practical-recommendations-for-a-language-learning-app)\n\n---\n\n## Background\n\nArabic text is typically written without short vowels (harakat). Diacritization (tashkeel) is the process of adding these marks back:\n\n| Mark | Name | Arabic | Transliteration |\n|------|------|--------|-----------------|\n| َ | Fatha | فَتْحَة | a |\n| ُ | Damma | ضَمَّة | u |\n| ِ | Kasra | كَسْرَة | i |\n| ْ | Sukun | سُكُون | (no vowel) |\n| ّ | Shadda | شَدَّة | (gemination) |\n| ً | Tanwin Fath | تَنْوِين فَتْح | -an |\n| ٌ | Tanwin Damm | تَنْوِين ضَمّ | -un |\n| ٍ | Tanwin Kasr | تَنْوِين كَسْر | -in |\n\nFor a language learning app, diacritization is critical because learners cannot correctly pronounce or understand words without vowel marks. Native speakers infer vowels from context, but learners need explicit marks.\n\n---\n\n## Evaluation Metrics\n\nAll systems are evaluated using two standard metrics:\n\n- **DER (Diacritization Error Rate)**: Percentage of characters with incorrectly predicted diacritics. Lower is better.\n- **WER (Word Error Rate)**: Percentage of words with at least one diacritization error. Lower is better.\n\nBoth metrics are reported in two modes:\n- **With case ending (CE)**: Includes the final vowel of each word (i3rab), which is the hardest part.\n- **Without case ending**: Excludes the last character's diacritic; this is easier and more practical for many applications.\n\nThe standard evaluation library is `diacritization-evaluation` on PyPI:\n```bash\npip install diacritization-evaluation\n```\nRepository: https://github.com/almodhfer/diacritization_evaluation\n\n---\n\n## Accuracy Benchmark Summary\n\nThis table consolidates results across multiple published benchmarks. Numbers vary by dataset and evaluation protocol; treat as approximate guides. All values are percentages (lower is better).\n\n### On the Tashkeela (Fadel) Test Set\n\n| System | Type | DER (w/ CE) | WER (w/ CE) | DER (no CE) | WER (no CE) |\n|--------|------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|\n| **SukounBERT.v2** | BERT-based | 0.92 | 1.91 | -- | -- |\n| **Fine-Tashkeel (ByT5)** | Seq2Seq Transformer | 0.95 | 2.49 | -- | -- |\n| **Sadeed (corrected)** | Decoder-only LM (1.5B) | 1.24 | 2.94 | 0.76 | 1.74 |\n| **SUKOUN** | BERT-based | 1.16 | 3.34 | 0.96 | 1.96 |\n| **D3 (Deep Diacritization)** | Hierarchical RNN | 1.83 | 5.34 | 1.48 | 3.11 |\n| **Shakkala** | B-LSTM (Keras) | 2.88 | 6.37 | -- | -- |\n| **Shakkelha** | RNN/FFNN ensemble | ~3.0 | ~7.0 | -- | -- |\n| **CBHG** | Tacotron-encoder | ~3.5 | ~8.0 | -- | -- |\n| **Mishkal** | Rule-based | 13.78 | 21.92 | -- | -- |\n| **Farasa** | SVM + dictionary | 21.43 | 58.88 | -- | -- |\n| **MADAMIRA** | Morphological | ~12 | ~30 | -- | -- |\n\n### On the WikiNews Test Set\n\n| System | DER (w/ CE) | WER (w/ CE) | DER (no CE) | WER (no CE) |\n|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|\n| **CATT ED** | 6.07 | -- | 3.74 | -- |\n| **CATT EO** | 5.43 | -- | 3.11 | -- |\n| **FRRNN (Darwish)** | 3.7 | 6.0 | 0.9 | 2.9 |\n| **Sadeed** | 5.25 | 14.64 | 3.11 | 8.44 |\n| **CATT (Alasmary)** | 5.96 | 20.06 | 3.63 | 11.31 |\n\n### On SadeedDiac-25 Benchmark (Mixed MSA + Classical)\n\n| System | DER (w/ CE) | WER (w/ CE) | Hallucination Rate |\n|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|\n| **Claude 3.7 Sonnet** | 1.39 | 4.67 | 0.82% |\n| **GPT-4** | 3.86 | 5.27 | 1.02% |\n| **Sadeed** | 7.29 | 13.74 | 7.19% |\n| **Aya-8B** | 25.63 | 47.49 | 5.78% |\n| **ALLaM-7B** | 50.36 | 70.34 | 36.51% |\n\n**Important caveat on benchmarks**: The Sadeed paper (2025) revealed that 34.6% of the Fadel test samples are fully present in common training sets, and 68.12% exhibit similarity > 0.5. This means many published DER/WER numbers on the Fadel benchmark are inflated by data leakage. The SadeedDiac-25 benchmark was created to address this.\n\n---\n\n## Deep Learning Models\n\n### 1. CATT (Character-based Arabic Tashkeel Transformer)\n\n**The current state-of-the-art open-source model for Arabic diacritization.**\n\n- **Paper**: \"CATT: Character-based Arabic Tashkeel Transformer\" (ArabicNLP 2024)\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/abjadai/catt\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 (commercial use allowed)\n- **Architecture**: Two variants:\n - Encoder-Only (EO): 6 layers, 512 dim, 16 heads -- faster inference\n - Encoder-Decoder (ED): 3+3 layers -- higher accuracy\n- **Base**: Pretrained character-based BERT + Noisy-Student training\n- **Installation**: `pip install catt-tashkeel`\n- **ONNX export**: Supported via `export_to_onnx.py`\n\n```python\nfrom catt_tashkeel import CATTEncoderDecoder, CATTEncoderOnly\n\neo = CATTEncoderOnly()\ned = CATTEncoderDecoder()\n\ntext = \"وقالت مجلة نيوزويك الامريكية في تقريرها\"\nprint(eo.do_tashkeel(text, verbose=False))\nprint(ed.do_tashkeel_batch([text], verbose=False))\n```\n\n**Strengths**: Best open-source accuracy, Apache 2.0 license, pip-installable, ONNX support.\n**Weaknesses**: Relatively new, smaller community. Performance on MSA text that differs from training data can vary.\n\n---\n\n### 2. Sadeed\n\n- **Paper**: \"Sadeed: Advancing Arabic Diacritization Through Small Language Model\" (2025)\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/misraj-ai/Sadeed\n- **HuggingFace datasets**: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Misraj/Sadeed_Tashkeela\n- **Architecture**: Fine-tuned decoder-only LM from Kuwain 1.5B\n- **Model size**: ~1.5B parameters\n- **Training**: 3 epochs on 53M words, 8x A100 GPUs\n- **Benchmark**: Introduced SadeedDiac-25 (1,200 paragraphs, 50% MSA / 50% Classical)\n- **License**: Not clearly stated in repository\n\n**Reported accuracy** (Fadel corrected): DER 1.24%, WER 2.94% (with CE).\n\n**Strengths**: Strong accuracy, introduced important new benchmark, addresses data leakage issues in prior work.\n**Weaknesses**: Large model (1.5B params), model weights may not be publicly downloadable yet, heavy compute requirements for inference.\n\n---\n\n### 3. Fine-Tashkeel (ByT5-based)\n\n- **Paper**: \"Fine-Tashkeel: Finetuning Byte-Level Models for Accurate Arabic Text Diacritization\" (2023)\n- **HuggingFace**: https://huggingface.co/basharalrfooh/Fine-Tashkeel\n- **Architecture**: ByT5 (byte-level T5 transformer, seq2seq)\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Training**: Fine-tuned on Tashkeela for 13,000 steps\n- **Framework**: PyTorch / HuggingFace Transformers\n\n```python\nfrom transformers import AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM, AutoTokenizer\n\nmodel_name = \"basharalrfooh/Fine-Tashkeel\"\ntokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)\nmodel = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained(model_name)\n\ntext = \"كيف الحال\"\ninput_ids = tokenizer(text, return_tensors=\"pt\").input_ids\noutputs = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=128)\nprint(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))\n```\n\n**Reported accuracy**: DER 0.95%, WER 2.49% (on Tashkeela test set, likely with data leakage concerns).\n**Strengths**: MIT license, on HuggingFace, simple API, handles unseen words at >76% accuracy.\n**Weaknesses**: ByT5 models are slow for inference (byte-level processing), model size can be large, Tashkeela benchmark numbers may be inflated.\n\n---\n\n### 4. Deep Diacritization (D2/D3)\n\n- **Paper**: \"Deep Diacritization: Efficient Hierarchical Recurrence for Improved Arabic Diacritization\" (WANLP 2020)\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/BKHMSI/deep-diacritization\n- **License**: AGPL-3.0\n- **Architecture**: Two-level recurrence hierarchy (word + character level) with cross-level attention\n- **D3 variant**: Accepts partially diacritized input as priors (unique feature)\n- **Accuracy**: DER 1.83%, WER 5.34% (with CE, Fadel set)\n\n**Strengths**: D3's partial diacritization support is unique and valuable for language learning (teacher can add some diacritics, model completes the rest). Relatively efficient architecture.\n**Weaknesses**: AGPL license restricts commercial use without open-sourcing your app. Pretrained weights via Google Drive links.\n\n---\n\n### 5. Shakkala\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/Barqawiz/Shakkala\n- **License**: MIT (must credit \"Shakkala\" as base model)\n- **Architecture**: RNN/LSTM, Keras/TensorFlow\n- **Installation**: `pip install shakkala`\n- **Framework**: TensorFlow 2.9.3\n\n```python\nfrom shakkala import Shakkala\nsh = Shakkala(version=2)\ninput_int = sh.prepare_input(input_text)\nmodel, graph = sh.get_model()\nlogits = model.predict(input_int)[0]\npredicted_harakat = sh.logits_to_text(logits)\nfinal_output = sh.get_final_text(input_text, predicted_harakat)\n```\n\n**Reported accuracy**: DER 2.88%, WER 6.37%.\n**Strengths**: Mature project, pip-installable, MIT license, well-documented.\n**Weaknesses**: Older architecture (LSTM), TensorFlow dependency, not state-of-the-art accuracy.\n\n**PyTorch/ONNX ports available**:\n- PyTorch: https://github.com/nipponjo/arabic-vocalization\n- ONNX: https://github.com/nipponjo/arabic_vocalizer (MIT license)\n\n```python\n# ONNX version - very lightweight\nfrom arabic_vocalizer import vocalize\nresult = vocalize(input_text, model='shakkala')\n```\n\n---\n\n### 6. Shakkelha\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/AliOsm/shakkelha\n- **Paper**: \"Neural Arabic Text Diacritization: State-of-the-Art Results\" (EMNLP-IJCNLP 2019)\n- **Architecture**: Multiple models (RNN, CRF-RNN, FFNN variants)\n- **Also available as ONNX**: https://github.com/nipponjo/arabic_vocalizer\n\n---\n\n### 7. 2SDiac\n\n- **Paper**: \"Take the Hint: Improving Arabic Diacritization with Partially-Diacritized Text\" (Interspeech 2023)\n- **Architecture**: BiLSTM + self-attention with Guided Learning\n- **Model size**: <5M parameters (very small)\n- **Accuracy**: 3.3% WER (no CE), 31% relative improvement over prior work\n- **Key innovation**: Accepts partial diacritics as hints, improving all predictions\n\n**Strengths**: Tiny model (<5M params), fast inference, low latency, supports partial input diacritics.\n**Weaknesses**: No public GitHub repository found. Published at Interspeech but code availability unclear.\n\n---\n\n### 8. CBHG / Arabic_Diacritization\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/almodhfer/Arabic_Diacritization\n- **Architecture**: 4 models (Baseline BiLSTM, Seq2Seq+Attention, Tacotron-based, CBHG)\n- **Framework**: PyTorch\n- **License**: Not specified\n- **Best model**: CBHG achieves the best WER and DER\n\n---\n\n### 9.
