Hareef\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/mush42/hareef\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Architecture**: Two models implemented:\n - **Sarf**: Deep GRU + transformer encoder layers\n - **CBHG**: Based on published CBHG architecture\n- **Framework**: PyTorch Lightning\n- **Features**: ONNX export, standardized evaluation, config-driven training\n- **Requirements**: Python 3.10+\n\n**Strengths**: Clean codebase, MIT license, ONNX export, used as basis for libtashkeel.\n\n---\n\n### 10. SukounBERT\n\n- **Paper**: \"BERT-Based Arabic Diacritization\" (Expert Systems with Applications, 2024)\n- **Architecture**: BERT-based, multi-phase approach\n- **Accuracy**: DER 0.92%, WER 1.91% (morphological), DER 1.14%, WER 3.34% (syntactic)\n- **Training data**: Sukoun Corpus -- 5.2M lines, 71M tokens\n- **SukounBERT.v2**: >55% relative DER/WER reduction over leading models\n\n**Critically**: Data and model are \"available on request\" only. Not fully open-source. No public GitHub repository found.\n\n---\n\n### 11. Ad-Dabit-Al-Lughawi (PTCAD)\n\n- **Paper**: \"Arabic Text Diacritization In The Age Of Transfer Learning\" (2024)\n- **Architecture**: Two-phase (pre-finetuning + finetuning) token classification on BERT\n- **Accuracy**: 20-30% WER reduction over prior SOTA; outperforms GPT-4 on ATD tasks\n- **Key innovation**: Pre-finetuning on Classical Arabic, POS tagging, segmentation as MLM tasks\n\n**Status**: Published in Expert Systems with Applications journal. Code availability unclear.\n\n---\n\n## Rule-Based and Hybrid Systems\n\n### Mishkal\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/linuxscout/mishkal\n- **PyPI**: `pip install mishkal` (v0.4.1, last updated July 2021)\n- **License**: GPL-3.0\n- **Approach**: Multi-stage rule-based pipeline:\n 1. Morphological analysis (Qalsadi analyzer + Arramooz dictionary)\n 2. Word frequency weighting\n 3. Syntactic analysis (ArAnaSyn)\n 4. Semantic analysis (Asmai)\n 5. Probability-based selection\n- **Accuracy**: DER 13.78%, WER 21.92% (Fadel benchmark)\n\n```python\nimport mishkal.tashkeel\nvocalizer = mishkal.tashkeel.TashkeelClass()\nresult = vocalizer.tashkeel(\"تطلع الشمس صباحا\")\n# Output: 'تَطْلُعُ الشَّمْسُ صَبَاحًا'\n```\n\n**Features**:\n- `--reduced` flag for reduced/partial diacritization (adds only essential marks)\n- `--strip` to remove all diacritics\n- `--syntax` / `--semantic` to toggle analysis modules\n- Web server mode, JSON API, GUI, console interfaces\n\n**Strengths**: No ML model needed, no GPU, deterministic, supports reduced tashkeel mode, comprehensive Arabic morphology stack. Great for understanding *why* a diacritic was chosen.\n**Weaknesses**: GPL-3.0 license (copyleft), significantly lower accuracy than neural models, not maintained actively.\n\n---\n\n### Farasa\n\n- **Website**: https://farasa.qcri.org/\n- **Developer**: Qatar Computing Research Institute (QCRI)\n- **Approach**: SVM-ranking segmentation + dictionary lookups\n- **Accuracy**: DER 21.43%, WER 58.88% (Fadel benchmark) -- poor for diacritization specifically\n- **License**: Free API (registration required), not fully open-source\n- **API**: RESTful, free, with Python/Java/JS/curl examples\n\n**Strengths**: Very fast, comprehensive Arabic NLP toolkit (segmentation, NER, POS, etc.).\n**Weaknesses**: Diacritization accuracy is notably lower than neural approaches. The diacritizer is not the strongest component. API-dependent.\n\n---\n\n### CAMeL Tools\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/camel_tools\n- **Developer**: CAMeL Lab, NYU Abu Dhabi\n- **PyPI**: `pip install camel-tools`\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Approach**: Hybrid neuro-symbolic. Morphological analyzer generates candidate analyses, classifier ranks them.\n- **Features**: Morphological analysis/generation, dialect ID, NER, sentiment, transliteration\n- **Requirements**: Python 3.8-3.12, Rust compiler, CMake\n\n```python\nfrom camel_tools.disambig.mle import MLEDisambiguator\n\nmle = MLEDisambiguator.pretrained()\nsentence = 'الطفلان أكلا الطعام'.split()\ndisambig = mle.disambiguate(sentence)\ndiacritized = [d.analyses[0].analysis['diac'] for d in disambig]\nprint(' '.join(diacritized))\n```\n\n**Data download**: `camel_data -i light` (morphology only) or `camel_data -i all`\n\n**Strengths**: MIT license, comprehensive NLP suite, morphological databases for MSA (calima-msa-r13) and Egyptian (calima-egy-r13), well-maintained, academic backing.\n**Weaknesses**: Diacritization accuracy is not as strong as dedicated neural models. Complex installation (Rust compiler needed). Heavier than pure ML approaches.\n\n---\n\n### Pipeline-Diacritizer\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/Hamza5/Pipeline-diacritizer\n- **PyPI**: `pip install pipeline-diacritizer`\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Paper**: \"Multi-components system for automatic Arabic diacritization\" (ECIR 2020)\n- **Approach**: Deep learning + rule-based + statistical corrections pipeline\n- **Framework**: TensorFlow\n\n---\n\n## Production-Ready Libraries\n\n### libtashkeel\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/mush42/libtashkeel\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Language**: Rust core with Python, C/C++, WASM bindings\n- **Model**: ONNX (derived from Hareef project, trained on MSA)\n\n```python\nfrom pylibtashkeel import tashkeel\nresult = tashkeel(\"إن روعة اللغة العربية لا تتبدى إلا لعشاقها\")\n# Output: 'إِنَّ رَوْعَةَ اللُّغَةِ الْعَرَبِيَّةِ لَا تَتَبَدَّى إِلَّا لِعُشَّاقِهَا'\n```\n\n**Installation** (requires Rust):\n```bash\ncd pylibtashkeel\npython3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate\npip install maturin && maturin build --release --strip\npip install ./target/wheels/pylibtashkeel*.whl\n```\n\n**Strengths**: MIT license, cross-platform (Rust/Python/C++/WASM), ONNX inference, used by Piper TTS for Arabic. Ideal for production deployment where you need a lightweight, compiled library.\n**Weaknesses**: Requires Rust toolchain to build, no pip install (yet), accuracy not benchmarked publicly.\n\n---\n\n### arabic_vocalizer (ONNX)\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/nipponjo/arabic_vocalizer\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Models**: Shakkala and Shakkelha ported to ONNX format\n- **Installation**: `pip install git+https://github.com/nipponjo/arabic_vocalizer.git`\n\n```python\nfrom arabic_vocalizer import vocalize\nresult = vocalize(\"السلام عليكم\", model='shakkala')\n```\n\n**Strengths**: Extremely simple API, ONNX (no TF/PyTorch needed), MIT license, lightweight.\n**Weaknesses**: Uses older Shakkala/Shakkelha models, not state-of-the-art accuracy.\n\n---\n\n### Rababa\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/interscript/rababa\n- **License**: Open source (part of Interscript)\n- **Languages**: Python and Ruby bindings\n- **Supports**: Arabic and Hebrew diacritization\n- **Architecture**: Neural network models with ONNX inference\n\n**Strengths**: Supports both Arabic and Hebrew, Ruby bindings (unique), integrates with Interscript transliteration.\n\n---\n\n## LLM-Based Diacritization\n\n### Evaluation of General-Purpose LLMs\n\nBased on the Kentoseth evaluation (March 2025) and the Sadeed paper (2025):\n\n| Model | Typical DER Range | Notes |\n|-------|-------------------|-------|\n| **Claude 3.7 Sonnet** | 1.4% | Best on SadeedDiac-25 benchmark |\n| **Gemini 2.0 Pro** | 0-40% | Highly variable; excellent on some texts, poor on others |\n| **GPT-4** | 3.9-20% | Moderate; prone to hallucination on some texts |\n| **Mistral Saba** | 1.7-40% | Surprisingly good on classical texts |\n| **LLama 3.3 70B** | 53-74% | Very poor; often worse than no diacritization |\n| **Aya-8B** | 25.6% | Moderate open model |\n| **ALLaM-7B** | 50.4% | Poor; high hallucination rate (36.5%) |\n\n**Key findings**:\n- LLMs are highly inconsistent across text types and genres\n- Proprietary models (Claude, GPT-4) significantly outperform open LLMs\n- Hallucination is a real problem: LLMs sometimes change words, not just add diacritics\n- LLMs are too expensive and slow for production diacritization at scale\n- Specialized models consistently outperform general LLMs for this task\n\n---\n\n## Partial / Selective Diacritization\n\nFor a language learning app, partial diacritization is valuable: you may want to diacritize only ambiguous words, or only vocabulary the learner is studying.\n\n### Tools with Partial Diacritization Support\n\n| Tool | Partial Mode | Details |\n|------|-------------|---------|\n| **Mishkal** | `--reduced` flag | Adds only essential marks, skipping obvious ones |\n| **D3 (Deep Diacritization)** | Input priors | Accepts partially diacritized text and improves it |\n| **2SDiac** | Guided Learning | Trained to leverage partial input diacritics at various masking levels |\n| **CAMeL Tools** | Per-word control | Disambiguate individual words, giving you control over which to diacritize |\n\n### Implementing Selective Diacritization\n\nNo existing tool does exactly \"diacritize only ambiguous words\" out of the box. Strategies:\n\n1. **Full diacritization + selective display**: Diacritize everything, then strip marks from \"easy\" words in the UI. Use word frequency lists to determine which words a learner likely knows.\n\n2. **CAMeL Tools morphological analysis**: Analyze each word to see how many valid diacritized forms exist. Words with only one form are unambiguous; show diacritics only on multi-form words.\n\n3. **D3/2SDiac approach**: Pre-diacritize known words, let the model fill in the rest. The model uses the given diacritics as context clues.\n\n4. **Mishkal reduced mode**: Use the `--reduced` flag which already implements a form of \"minimal necessary\" diacritization.\n\n---\n\n## Evaluation and Verification Tools\n\n### diacritization-evaluation\n\n- **PyPI**: `pip install diacritization-evaluation`\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/almodhfer/diacritization_evaluation\n- **Calculates**: DER and WER, with and without case endings\n\n```python\nfrom diacritization_evaluation import der, wer\n\n# From text strings\nder_score = der.calculate_der(reference_text, hypothesis_text)\nwer_score = wer.calculate_wer(reference_text, hypothesis_text)\n\n# Options: with_case_ending=True/False\n```\n\n### Comparison Approach\n\nFor a language learning app, you can build a verification pipeline:\n1. Diacritize text with your primary model\n2. Cross-check with a second model (e.g., primary=CATT, secondary=Mishkal)\n3. Where they disagree, flag for human review or use a morphological analyzer to check validity\n4. Use CAMeL Tools morphological analyzer to verify each diacritized form exists in the dictionary\n\n---\n\n## Datasets and Corpora\n\n### Tashkeela Corpus\n\n- **Source**: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/linuxscout/tashkeela\n- **Also**: https://sourceforge.net/projects/tashkeela/\n- **Size**: 75.6M words (97 books from Shamela Library + web-crawled MSA)\n- **Composition**: 98.85% classical Arabic (Islamic texts), 1.15% MSA\n- **Download**: ~183 MB compressed\n- **Creator**: Taha Zerrouki (linuxscout)\n\n### Tashkeela Processed (Fadel Benchmark)\n\n- **Repository**: https://github.com/AliOsm/arabic-text-diacritization\n- **Split**: 50K train / 2.5K val / 2.5K test lines (~2.3M words total)\n- **Selection**: Lines with >80% diacritic-to-character ratio\n- **Warning**: 34.6% of test samples found in common training sets (data leakage)\n\n### SadeedDiac-25\n\n- **HuggingFace**: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Misraj/Sadeed_Tashkeela\n- **Size**: 1,200 paragraphs (40-50 words each)\n- **Composition**: 50% MSA + 50% Classical Arabic\n- **Quality**: Expert-reviewed diacritization\n- **Purpose**: Fairer evaluation across diverse genres and complexity\n\n### Arabic Treebank (ATB-3)\n\n- **Source**: LDC (Linguistic Data Consortium) -- requires license\n- **Used by**: Many research systems for MSA diacritization\n- **Quality**: High-quality linguist-annotated MSA text\n\n### Sukoun Corpus\n\n- **Size**: 5.2M lines, 71M tokens\n- **Sources**: Classical Arabic, MSA, dictionaries, poetry, contextual sentences\n- **Availability**: \"On request\" from authors\n\n---\n\n## API Services\n\n| Service | Type | Cost | Notes |\n|---------|------|------|-------|\n| **Farasa API** | REST | Free (registration required) | https://farasa.qcri.org/ |\n| **RDI Tashkeel** | REST + On-premise | Paid (enterprise) | https://rdi-eg.ai/arabic-tashkeel-online/ |\n| **Zyla Arabic Diacritization API** | REST | Paid (per-request) | Bearer token auth |\n| **Sahehly** | Web | Freemium (1500 chars/day free) | Spelling + grammar + tashkeel |\n| **Tashkil.net** | Web | Free | Online diacritization tool |\n\n---\n\n## Practical Recommendations for a Language Learning App\n\n### Recommended Architecture\n\n**Primary approach**: Use CATT (Apache 2.0) as the main diacritization engine.\n\n```\nUser Text Input\n |\n v\n[CATT Encoder-Decoder] -- primary diacritization\n |\n v\n[Confidence check / morphological validation via CAMeL Tools]\n |\n v\n[Cache diacritized text in database]\n |\n v\n[Selective display layer in UI -- show/hide marks per learner level]\n```\n\n### Why CATT?\n\n1. **License**: Apache 2.0 (commercial-friendly)\n2. **Accuracy**: State-of-the-art among truly open-source models\n3. **Ease of use**: `pip install catt-tashkeel`, simple Python API\n4. **ONNX export**: Can deploy without PyTorch in production\n5. **Batch inference**: Can process multiple texts efficiently\n\n### Alternative Tiers\n\n| Scenario | Recommended Tool | Reason |\n|----------|-----------------|--------|\n| Best accuracy, cost no object | Claude API / GPT-4 | Best on SadeedDiac-25 but expensive |\n| Best open-source accuracy | CATT (Apache 2.0) | State-of-the-art, commercial-friendly |\n| Lightweight / edge deployment | libtashkeel (Rust/ONNX) or arabic_vocalizer | Small footprint, fast inference |\n| HuggingFace integration | Fine-Tashkeel (ByT5) | Standard transformers API, MIT license |\n| Morphological understanding | CAMeL Tools + Mishkal | Know *why* a form was chosen |\n| Partial diacritization | D3 or 2SDiac approach | Accept hints from teachers/learners |\n| No ML dependency | Mishkal | Pure rule-based, deterministic |\n\n### Deployment Considerations\n\n1. **Latency**: For real-time typing, use CATT EO (faster) or ONNX models. For batch processing (e.g., preparing lesson content), use CATT ED (more accurate).\n\n2. **Model size vs. accuracy tradeoff**:\n - 2SDiac: <5M params (if code becomes available)\n - Shakkala/ONNX: ~10-50MB\n - CATT: ~100-200MB (estimated from architecture)\n - Fine-Tashkeel: ~300MB+ (ByT5-small)\n - Sadeed: ~3GB+ (1.5B params)\n\n3. **Caching strategy**: Arabic vocabulary is finite. Cache diacritized forms of common words/phrases in a database. For a language learning app with curated content, you can pre-diacritize all lesson materials and only need real-time diacritization for user-generated content.\n\n4. **Quality assurance for learning content**: For educational content, always have a human Arabic linguist review critical materials. Use automated diacritization as a first pass, then human review. The morphological analyzer in CAMeL Tools can flag potentially incorrect forms.\n\n5. **Case endings (i3rab)**: For language learners, case endings are important for grammar understanding but are the hardest to predict. Consider a setting that lets learners toggle case ending display. Most native Arabic content omits case endings even when other diacritics are present.\n\n### Key Risk: Benchmark Inflation\n\nMany published DER/WER numbers are inflated due to data leakage between training and test sets (as documented by the Sadeed paper). Real-world performance on novel, diverse MSA text will be worse than published numbers. Always test on your own representative data before committing to a model.\n\n---\n\n## License Summary\n\n| Tool | License | Commercial Use |\n|------|---------|----------------|\n| CATT | Apache 2.0 | Yes |\n| Fine-Tashkeel | MIT | Yes |\n| Shakkala | MIT (with attribution) | Yes |\n| arabic_vocalizer | MIT | Yes |\n| libtashkeel | MIT | Yes |\n| Hareef | MIT | Yes |\n| Pipeline-Diacritizer | MIT | Yes |\n| CAMeL Tools | MIT | Yes |\n| Deep Diacritization (D3) | AGPL-3.0 | Restricted |\n| Mishkal | GPL-3.0 | Restricted (copyleft) |\n| Farasa | Free API, proprietary | API use only |\n| SukounBERT | On request | Unknown |\n| Sadeed | Not stated | Unknown |\n", "date": null, "category": "nlp", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/arabic-diacritization.md"}, "arabic-education-research-2026-02-21": {"title": "Arabic Language Education: Research Bibliography & Annotated Sources", "content": "# Arabic Language Education: Research Bibliography & Annotated Sources\n\n**Compiled: 2026-02-21**\n\nThis document collects academic research, international assessment data, and authoritative reports on Arabic language education quality, literacy outcomes, and effective teaching methods. Sources are organized thematically with full citations, key findings, and URLs where available.\n\n**Related Alif research:**\n- [arabic-teaching-worldwide-2026-02-21.md](arabic-teaching-worldwide-2026-02-21.md) — Teaching quality/approaches by country\n- [arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21.md](arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21.md) — Digital textbook sources\n- [deep-research-compilation-2026-02-12.md](deep-research-compilation-2026-02-12.md) — Arabic-specific learning research (Section 3)\n- [cognitive-load-language-learning.md](cognitive-load-language-learning.md) — Cognitive load in language learning\n- [vocabulary-acquisition-research.md](vocabulary-acquisition-research.md) — General vocabulary acquisition research\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [International Assessments (PIRLS, PISA, TIMSS)](#1-international-assessments)\n2. [Learning Poverty and Literacy Statistics](#2-learning-poverty-and-literacy-statistics)\n3. [The Arabic Literacy Crisis: Major Reports](#3-the-arabic-literacy-crisis-major-reports)\n4. [Diglossia and Its Impact on Education](#4-diglossia-and-its-impact-on-education)\n5. [Arabic Orthography, Morphology, and Reading Acquisition](#5-arabic-orthography-morphology-and-reading-acquisition)\n6. [Arabic as a Foreign Language (AFL) Research](#6-arabic-as-a-foreign-language-afl-research)\n7. [Heritage Language Learners](#7-heritage-language-learners)\n8. [Technology in Arabic Education](#8-technology-in-arabic-education)\n9. [Spaced Repetition and Vocabulary Acquisition](#9-spaced-repetition-and-vocabulary-acquisition)\n10. [Key Scholars and Reference Works](#10-key-scholars-and-reference-works)\n\n---\n\n## 1. International Assessments\n\n### 1.1 PIRLS 2021 (Progress in International Reading Literacy Study)\n\n**Citation:** Mullis, I.V.S., von Davier, M., Foy, P., Fishbein, B., Reynolds, K.A., & Wry, E. (2023). *PIRLS 2021 International Results in Reading*. Boston College, TIMSS & PIRLS International Study Center.\n\n**URL:** https://pirls2021.org/results/achievement/overall/\n\n**Full report PDF:** https://pirls2021.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/files/PIRLS-2021-International-Results-in-Reading.pdf\n\n**What it measures:** Reading comprehension achievement in 4th-grade students (age 9-10) across 57 countries and 8 benchmarking entities. The PIRLS scale centerpoint is 500 (set to the 2001 international mean), with a standard deviation of 100.\n\n**Arab country scores (PIRLS 2021):**\n\n| Country | Score | Relation to Centerpoint (500) |\n|---------|-------|-------------------------------|\n| Qatar | 485 | Below (but close) |\n| UAE (national) | 483 | Below |\n| Dubai (benchmarking) | 552 | Significantly above |\n| Abu Dhabi (benchmarking) | 440 | Significantly below |\n| Bahrain | 458 | Below |\n| Saudi Arabia | 449 | Below |\n| Oman | 429 | Significantly below |\n| Jordan | 381 | Significantly below |\n| Egypt | 378 | Significantly below |\n| Morocco | 372 | Significantly below |\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Every Arab country that participated scored below the PIRLS centerpoint of 500, except Dubai as a benchmarking entity (552).\n- Egypt and Morocco scored at the very bottom of all participating countries globally, more than 120 points below the centerpoint.\n- The gulf between Dubai private schools (566) and national averages in Egypt/Morocco (~375) represents nearly 200 scale points -- roughly two standard deviations.\n- Girls outperformed boys in reading across all participating Arab countries.\n- Some countries (Bahrain, UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia) administered the test in fall 2021 rather than spring, which may affect comparability.\n\n**Notes on the UAE:** The UAE result is complex. The national average (483) masks enormous variation: Dubai private schools scored 566 (top 10 globally, comparable to Finland), while Abu Dhabi scored 440. Private schools in Sharjah approached scores of Germany and France. This reflects the stratification between well-resourced private education serving expatriate populations and national public education.\n\n---\n\n### 1.2 PISA 2022 (Programme for International Student Assessment)\n\n**Citation:** OECD (2023). *PISA 2022 Results (Volume I): The State of Learning and Equity in Education*. OECD Publishing.\n\n**URL:** https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/pisa-2022-results-volume-i_53f23881-en.html\n\n**What it measures:** Reading, mathematics, and science performance of 15-year-olds. OECD average is the reference point.\n\n**Arab country results (PISA 2022):**\n\n| Country | Reading | Math | Science | OECD Average |\n|---------|---------|------|---------|--------------|\n| UAE | 417 | 431 | 432 | 476 / 472 / 485 |\n| Saudi Arabia | 383 | 389 | 390 | 476 / 472 / 485 |\n| Qatar | ~402 | ~414 | ~432 | 476 / 472 / 485 |\n| Jordan | ~342 | ~361 | ~373 | 476 / 472 / 485 |\n| Morocco | ~339 | ~365 | ~365 | 476 / 472 / 485 |\n\n**Key findings:**\n- All participating Arab countries scored significantly below OECD averages in all three domains.\n- Saudi Arabia scored 93 points below the OECD average in reading -- equivalent to roughly 3 years of schooling.\n- Only 51% of UAE students attained Level 2 proficiency in mathematics, vs. 69% OECD average.\n- UAE reading scores declined between 2018 and 2022.\n- Girls outperformed boys in reading by 33-45 score points across Arab countries.\n\n**Country notes:**\n- UAE: https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/pisa-2022-results-volume-i-and-ii-country-notes_ed6fbcc5-en/united-arab-emirates_74e92cf9-en.html\n- Saudi Arabia: https://gpseducation.oecd.org/CountryProfile?primaryCountry=SAU&treshold=5&topic=PI\n\n---\n\n### 1.3 TIMSS 2023 (Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study)\n\n**Citation:** IEA (2024). *TIMSS 2023 International Results in Mathematics and Science*. TIMSS & PIRLS International Study Center, Boston College.\n\n**URL:** https://timss2023.org/results/\n\n**Arab country participation:** Bahrain, Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman, Palestine, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE.\n\n**Key findings:**\n- TIMSS measures math and science (not reading directly), but provides context on overall education system quality.\n- The UAE showed gains in TIMSS 2023.\n- Most Arab countries performed below the TIMSS scale centerpoint of 500.\n- Arab countries collectively participate more actively in TIMSS than in PIRLS or PISA, providing the broadest dataset on MENA education system quality.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Learning Poverty and Literacy Statistics\n\n### 2.1 World Bank Learning Poverty Data\n\n**Citation:** World Bank (2022). *The State of Global Learning Poverty: 2022 Update*. Washington, DC: World Bank Group.\n\n**URL:** https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2022/06/23/70-of-10-year-olds-now-in-learning-poverty-unable-to-read-and-understand-a-simple-text\n\n**Definition:** \"Learning poverty\" = the share of 10-year-old children who cannot read and understand a simple, age-appropriate text.\n\n**Key statistics:**\n- **MENA regional learning poverty rate: ~60% pre-COVID** (59% was the widely cited pre-pandemic figure).\n- Post-COVID estimates suggest this has risen by at least 10 percentage points, potentially reaching 70%.\n- Globally, 70% of 10-year-olds are now in learning poverty (up from 57% pre-COVID).\n- MENA performs worse than the global average despite higher income levels than Sub-Saharan Africa or South Asia.\n\n**Country learning poverty briefs:** https://www.worldbank.org/en/topic/education/brief/country-learning-poverty-briefs\n\n**Significance:** The 60% learning poverty figure means that in a typical MENA classroom, 6 out of 10 children cannot read and understand a basic text at the end of primary school. This is remarkable given that MENA countries spend a higher share of GDP on education than many other developing regions.\n\n---\n\n### 2.2 Adult Literacy in the Arab World\n\n**Citation:** UNESCO Institute for Statistics. Literacy rate data. Accessed via World Bank Open Data.\n\n**URL:** https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SE.ADT.LITR.ZS?locations=1A\n\n**Additional source:** DVV International. \"Illiteracy in the Arab World.\" *Adult Education and Development*, No. 66, 2006.\n\n**URL:** https://www.dvv-international.de/en/adult-education-and-development/editions/aed-662006/education-for-all-and-literacy/illiteracy-in-the-arab-world\n\n**Key statistics:**\n- Arab world adult literacy rate: approximately 75-80% (compared to 86% global average).\n- In the early 2000s, literacy was only 62.2% for the population aged 15+.\n- 60-65 million adult illiterates in the Arab region (as of 2008 estimates), of whom approximately 18 million lived in Egypt alone.\n- Over 70% of illiterates in Yemen, Libya, Jordan, and Palestine are women.\n- 10 million children in the Arab world still have no schooling at all (AHDR 2003 figure).\n\n**Additional source:** Magin, P. \"Illiteracy in the Arab Region: A Meta Study.\" *Global Issues in Language Education*, Vol. 4, No. 2.\n\n**URL:** https://www.diu.edu/documents/gialens/Vol4-2/Magin-Arab-Illiteracy.pdf\n\n---\n\n## 3. The Arabic Literacy Crisis: Major Reports\n\n### 3.1 World Bank: Advancing Arabic Language Teaching and Learning (2021)\n\n**Citation:** Gregory, L., & Blom, A. (2021). *Advancing Arabic Language Teaching and Learning: A Path to Reducing Learning Poverty in the Middle East and North Africa*. Washington, DC: World Bank Group.\n\n**Full report PDF:** https://documents1.worldbank.org/curated/en/909741624654308046/pdf/Advancing-Arabic-Language-Teaching-and-Learning-A-Path-to-Reducing-Learning-Poverty-in-the-Middle-East-and-North-Africa.pdf\n\n**Press release:** https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2021/06/28/new-path-to-address-mena-learning-crisis-through-advancing-arabic-language-teaching-and-learning\n\n**This is the single most important recent report on Arabic education.** Key findings:\n\n1. **The core problem:** More than half of MENA children cannot read and understand an age-appropriate text by age 10.\n\n2. **Why Arabic teaching fails -- the report identifies these structural issues:**\n - Children arrive at school with minimal MSA exposure. Parents in MENA are less likely to read to young children or play word games compared to parents in other regions.\n - Enrollment in formal early childhood education (preschool) is lower in MENA than in other regions.\n - Children are taught to read and write \"in a rigid way with a focus on rules, grammar, and accuracy\" with \"a lack of playfulness and inquiry.\"\n - Many Arabic language teachers \"are often the product of ineffective Arabic language education and are not comfortable using it as a medium of instruction.\"\n - There is no explicit strategy for bridging the gap between spoken dialects and MSA.\n\n3. **The 9-step framework for improvement:**\n - Develop national literacy strategies with quantifiable goals\n - Increase early exposure to MSA\n - Ensure language-rich environments\n - Apply the science of reading to Arabic instruction\n - Maximize overlap between MSA and colloquial varieties\n - Improve teacher education and professional development\n - Develop better curriculum materials\n - Strengthen assessment and early intervention\n - Support schools with resources and accountability\n\n4. **Country progress:** Jordan, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE have prepared specific strategies for improving Arabic literacy with ambitious learning targets. Morocco piloted a \"Teaching at the Right Level\" approach showing substantial improvements in Arabic, Mathematics, and French.\n\n**Follow-up publication:** Blom, A. (2023). \"The World Bank's Approach to Reducing Learning Poverty in the Middle East and North Africa: Advancing Arabic Language.\" *Arab Journal of Applied Linguistics*.\n\n**URL:** https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1410089.pdf\n\n---\n\n### 3.2 UNDP Arab Human Development Reports (2002-2005)\n\n**Citation:** UNDP (2002). *Arab Human Development Report 2002: Creating Opportunities for Future Generations*. New York: United Nations Publications.\n\n**URL:** https://www.undp.org/arab-states/publications/arab-human-development-report-2002\n\n**Citation:** UNDP (2003). *Arab Human Development Report 2003: Building a Knowledge Society*. New York: United Nations Publications.\n\n**URL:** https://hdr.undp.org/content/arab-human-development-report-2003\n\n**Full PDF (2003):** https://files.acquia.undp.org/public/migration/arabstates/ahdr2003e.pdf\n\n**Overview of all reports:** https://www.europarl.europa.eu/meetdocs/2004_2009/documents/dv/overview_/overview_en.pdf\n\n**These reports were landmark publications that defined the discourse on Arab development deficits.**\n\n**AHDR 2002 -- The Three Deficits:**\nThe first report identified three major \"development deficits\" holding the Arab region back:\n1. **Knowledge** -- a growing knowledge gap with the rest of the world\n2. **Women's empowerment** -- gender inequality constraining development\n3. **Freedom** -- governance deficits limiting human potential\n\n**AHDR 2003 -- Building a Knowledge Society (key statistics):**\n- The number of books translated into Arabic increased from 175/year (1970-1975) to 330/year -- but this was still one-fifth of what Greece alone translated.\n- The aggregate total of all books translated into Arabic from the 9th century Abbasid era (al-Ma'mun) to the present day amounts to approximately 10,000 books -- equivalent to what Spain translates in a single year.\n- Books translated per million people: Arab world 4.4, Hungary 519, Spain 920.\n- 65 million adults in the Arab region were illiterate, almost two-thirds of them women.\n- The quality of education \"has deteriorated severely.\"\n- There is a \"severe mismatch between the labour market and the education system.\"\n\n**Strategic vision (five pillars):**\n1. Guaranteeing key freedoms\n2. Disseminating quality education\n3. Embedding science\n4. Shifting towards knowledge-based production\n5. Developing an enlightened Arab knowledge model\n\n---\n\n### 3.3 Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Foundation: Arab Reading Index (2016)\n\n**Citation:** MBRF & UNDP (2016). *Arab Reading Index*. Dubai: Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Foundation.\n\n**URL:** https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/arab-reading-index-results-place-arab-countries-on-the-global-reading-map-610103315.html\n\n**Foundation URL:** https://mbrf.ae/en\n\n**Methodology:** Survey of 148,000 participants across Arab countries.\n\n**Key findings:**\n- The average Arab reader reads 11 Arabic books and 6 foreign-language books per year.\n- Average reading time: 35 hours/year, divided into 20 hours in academic institutions and 15 hours outside.\n- 16 hours spent reading print material, 19 hours on electronic platforms.\n- UAE leads the GCC: 51 hours of reading and 24 books per person on average, scoring 82%.\n- Qatar was second among GCC countries.\n- The Index \"dispelled widespread inaccurate statistics, which placed reading levels in the region much lower than they really are.\"\n\n**Context:** This report was somewhat controversial as it painted a more positive picture than the AHDR statistics. The discrepancy may partly reflect different methodologies (self-reported survey vs. analysis of publishing data), different time periods, and the inclusion of digital reading in the 2016 index.\n\n---\n\n### 3.4 ALECSO (Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization)\n\n**Citation:** ALECSO. *Developing Arabic-language curricula and teaching methods in the first three grades of general education*. Tunis: ALECSO.\n\n**URL:** https://www.alecso.org/nsite/en/mn-education-2/projects/developing-arabic-language-curricula-and-teaching-methods-in-the-first-three-grades-of-general-education,-establishing-a-standardized-arabic-language-curriculum,-and-promoting-book-reading-in-arabic\n\n**Citation:** ALECSO (2025). *A Unified Framework for Arabic Language Teaching*. Presented at Sharjah.\n\n**URL:** https://sharjah24.ae/en/Articles/2025/09/18/mm03\n\n**About ALECSO:** Founded in Cairo on July 25, 1970, ALECSO is a Tunis-based specialized institution of the League of Arab States focused on education, culture, and sciences. Its Khartoum International Institute for Arabic Language focuses on teaching Arabic to non-native speakers.\n\n**Key initiatives:**\n- Developing standardized Arabic language curricula for the first three grades\n- Creating the Reference Framework for Teaching Arabic to Non-Native Speakers (the first achievement of its kind for Arabic)\n- Promoting book reading in Arabic\n- Action Plan 2005-2010 priorities: literacy teaching, closing the digital gap, enhancing educational systems with modern techniques\n\n---\n\n## 4. Diglossia and Its Impact on Education\n\n### 4.1 Queen Rania Foundation Systematic Review (2021)\n\n**Citation:** Queen Rania Foundation (2021). *A Report on the Effect of Arabic Language Diglossia on Teaching and Learning*. Amman: QRF.\n\n**Full report PDF:** https://qrf.org/sites/default/files/2023-11/report_on_the_effect_of_arabic_language_diglossia_on_teaching_and_learning_0.pdf\n\n**Summary page:** https://www.qrf.org/en/research-reports/diglossia-research\n\n**Blog posts on the review:**\n- https://qrf.org/en/media-center/our-latest-blogs/raising-our-heads-documents-sharing-some-mid-term-findings-our\n- https://www.qrf.org/en/media-center/our-latest-blogs/significance-diglossia\n- https://www.qrf.org/en/media-center/our-latest-blogs/arabic-language-diglossia-systematic-review\n\n**This is the most comprehensive systematic review of diglossia research to date.**\n\n**Key findings:**\n- **The evidence is contradictory and inconclusive**: \"There is no clear understanding about the impact diglossia has on teaching and learning that is fully based on evidence.\"\n- Difficulty in learning MSA diminishes with increased exposure and grade advancement, with improvement evident around Grade 4.\n- Early literacy activities at home sometimes provide students with baseline MSA knowledge before formal schooling.\n- The distance between MSA and local dialects creates measurable challenges, compounded by socioeconomic factors.\n- Using the \"Simple View of Reading\" framework, skilled decoders may understand letter-sounds but struggle with comprehension when dialect-MSA gaps are substantial.\n\n**Recommended bridging strategies:**\n- Early MSA exposure through multimedia\n- Diglossic-awareness activities\n- Explicit vocabulary instruction\n- Parental/community involvement\n- Adapted methodologies for non-native speakers\n\n**Context:** Lebanon and Jordan contributed the majority of diglossia research studies. The review was conducted by researchers at Zayed University (UAE).\n\n---\n\n### 4.2 Saiegh-Haddad: Linguistic Distance and Reading Acquisition (2003)\n\n**Citation:** Saiegh-Haddad, E. (2003). Linguistic distance and initial reading acquisition: The case of Arabic diglossia. *Applied Psycholinguistics*, 24(3), 431-451.\n\n**URL:** https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/applied-psycholinguistics/article/abs/linguistic-distance-and-initial-reading-acquisition-the-case-of-arabic-diglossia/9E475E6D4A634E683830D1FCE0E501AB\n\n**ResearchGate:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/231894994_Linguistic_Distance_and_Initial_Reading_Acquisition_The_Case_of_Arabic_Diglossia\n\n**Author:** Elinor Saiegh-Haddad, Full Professor at Bar-Ilan University, is the leading researcher on Arabic diglossia and reading acquisition.\n\n**Scholar profile:** https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=Z976BOwAAAAJ&hl=en\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Examined two diglossic variables -- phoneme and word syllabic structure -- by testing children's phoneme isolation and pseudoword decoding skills.\n- Both diglossic variables interfered with children's performance on both tasks in both grades tested.\n- Linguistic distance between spoken Arabic and MSA directly impacts children's phonological processing, phonological and morphological awareness, and Standard Arabic word reading.\n- This paper laid the theoretical and methodological grounds for studying the psycholinguistics of diglossia.\n\n**Why this matters:** This foundational paper empirically demonstrated what had been theoretically suspected: that diglossia is not merely a sociolinguistic curiosity but has measurable cognitive consequences for reading acquisition. Children must essentially learn to read in what is functionally a second language.\n\n---\n\n### 4.3 Saiegh-Haddad: Diglossic Features and Reading Comprehension (2025)\n\n**Citation:** Saiegh-Haddad, E. (2025). Diglossic and Orthographic Features of Reading Comprehension in Standard Arabic: The Primacy of the Spoken Language. *Reading Research Quarterly*.\n\n**URL:** https://ila.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/rrq.598\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Recent work continues to confirm the primacy of spoken language features in reading comprehension.\n- Diglossic distance has lasting effects beyond initial acquisition, affecting comprehension even in later grades.\n\n---\n\n### 4.4 Frontiers in Education: Non-Deficit Model (2025)\n\n**Citation:** (2025). Arabic diglossia: Advocating for a non-deficit model in comparative analysis of reading and language acquisition. *Frontiers in Education*, 10, 1518728.\n\n**URL:** https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1518728/full\n\n**Key argument:**\n- Challenges the prevailing \"deficit model\" that treats diglossia as inherently problematic.\n- Argues for comparative analysis that recognizes diglossia as a linguistic reality requiring adapted pedagogy rather than viewing it as a disorder.\n- Diglossia has a negative impact on \"phonological awareness, morphological awareness, phonological representation, word decoding, reading fluency, and letter naming.\"\n- This negative impact is greater for families of lower socioeconomic standing.\n\n---\n\n### 4.5 Egyptian Textbook Analysis\n\n**Citation:** (Referenced in QRF systematic review and other studies)\n\n**Key finding:** In Egyptian textbooks for kindergarten-aged children, only 40% of the words overlap with the Egyptian dialect, with the rest showing varying degrees of differences -- from phonological differences to being completely different words.\n\n**Significance:** This quantifies the \"foreign language\" experience that Arab children have when they begin formal schooling. A 60% mismatch between textbook language and home language would be considered catastrophic in any other educational context.\n\n---\n\n### 4.6 Diglossia and Executive Functions (2024)\n\n**Citation:** (2024). The Impact of Diglossia on Executive Functions and on Reading in Arabic. *PMC*.\n\n**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11505870/\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Diglossia affects not just linguistic processing but broader executive functions.\n- The cognitive load of processing diglossic text has measurable neuropsychological effects.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Arabic Orthography, Morphology, and Reading Acquisition\n\n### 5.1 Orthographic Depth and Arabic\n\n**Citation:** Abu-Rabia, S. (2016). Deep and shallow in Arabic orthography: New evidence from reading performance of elementary school native Arab readers.\n\n**URL:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/294891709_Deep_and_shallow_in_Arabic_orthography_New_evidence_from_reading_performance_of_elementary_school_native_Arab_readers\n\n**Full text PDF:** https://zai.zu.ac.ae/zairesources/651362/26e740a5-8021-4b0d-a0b4-f5be4c718a22.pdf\n\n**Key concepts:**\n- Arabic has a unique **dual orthographic system**: vowelized (shallow/transparent) and unvowelized (deep/opaque).\n- Unlike Roman scripts where each written language has one depth, Arabic operates at two depths simultaneously.\n- **Vowelized Arabic** (with tashkeel/diacritics) is a transparent orthography: each phoneme maps predictably to a grapheme. This is used for beginning readers and religious texts.\n- **Unvowelized Arabic** (without tashkeel) is a deep orthography: readers must infer short vowels from context, morphological knowledge, and syntactic parsing.\n- The transition from vowelized to unvowelized text (typically around end of elementary school) creates a second major hurdle in reading development.\n- Unvowelized Arabic produces a \"high amount of homographs\" -- the reader must rely on context to determine both pronunciation and meaning.\n\n**Practical relevance:** This dual nature is why tashkeel fading (gradually removing diacritics as proficiency increases) is pedagogically sound. It mirrors the natural transition Arab children make during schooling.\n\n---\n\n### 5.2 Role of Diacritics (Tashkeel) in Reading\n\n**Citation:** Abu-Rabia, S. Reading in Arabic: New Evidence for the Role of Vowel Signs.\n\n**URL:** https://zai.zu.ac.ae/zairesources/651362/eaeca72f-d0d9-415d-9e15-85fa681a51fa.pdf\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Diacritical marks significantly affect reading difficulty.\n- Vowelized Arabic is considerably more transparent and easier for beginning readers.\n- As readers gain proficiency, they increasingly rely on consonantal roots and morphological patterns rather than vowel marks.\n- The ability to read unvowelized text is a key marker of Arabic reading proficiency.\n\n---\n\n### 5.3 Root-Based Morphology and Word Recognition\n\n**Citation:** Boudelaa, S., & Marslen-Wilson, W.D. (Various). Reading in a root-based-morphology language: The case of Arabic.\n\n**URL:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/227774899_Reading_in_a_root-based-morphology_language_The_case_of_Arabic\n\n**Citation:** (2020). Early Sensitivity to Morphology in Beginning Readers of Arabic. *Frontiers in Psychology*, 11, 552315.\n\n**URL:** https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.552315/full\n\n**Citation:** (Development of root awareness measure). *Applied Psycholinguistics*.\n\n**URL:** https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/applied-psycholinguistics/article/abs/development-of-a-measure-of-root-awareness-to-account-for-reading-performance-in-the-arabic-language-a-development-and-validation-study/787A072025D7A6E39508978A620BD68E\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Arabic word structure is \"highly morphemic\" -- almost all words derive from combining at least two bound morphemes (roots and patterns) simultaneously.\n- Consonants play a \"much greater role than vowels in word recognition.\"\n- When reading unvowelized Arabic, readers focus on consonant structure and rely on morphological knowledge and sentence context.\n- **Root awareness emerges earlier than word-pattern (wazn) awareness** in children's morphological development.\n- Children read faster and more accurately when pseudowords are composed of frequent morphemes.\n- Phonological awareness AND morphological awareness are both strong predictors of Arabic reading performance.\n- Morphological awareness correlates significantly with both word-level reading and reading comprehension.\n\n**Practical relevance:** This research supports pedagogical approaches that emphasize root-pattern awareness (wazn) as a strategy for vocabulary acquisition and reading fluency. Teaching learners to recognize root consonants and common patterns accelerates word recognition.\n\n---\n\n### 5.4 What Predicts Word Reading in Arabic?\n\n**Citation:** (2023). What predicts word reading in Arabic? *Frontiers in Psychology*, 14, 1077643.\n\n**URL:** https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1077643/full\n\n**Key finding:** Multiple factors predict Arabic word reading, including phonological awareness, morphological awareness, and orthographic processing. The relative importance varies by age and reading experience level.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Arabic as a Foreign Language (AFL) Research\n\n### 6.1 FSI Language Difficulty Classification\n\n**Citation:** Foreign Service Institute, U.S. Department of State. Language difficulty rankings and training time estimates.\n\n**URL (FSI):** https://www.state.gov/national-foreign-affairs-training-center/foreign-language-training\n\n**URL (overview):** https://www.languagetesting.com/how-long-does-it-take\n\n**URL (detailed):** https://www.fsi-language-courses.org/blog/fsi-language-difficulty/\n\n**Key facts:**\n- Arabic is classified as **Category IV** (or Category V in some taxonomies that split the hardest tier), alongside Chinese (Mandarin), Cantonese, Japanese, and Korean.\n- Expected time to reach S-3/R-3 (ILR Level 3, roughly CEFR B2/C1): **88 weeks / 2,200 class hours** of intensive study.\n- For comparison, Category I languages (Spanish, French, Italian) require 24-30 weeks / 600-750 hours.\n- Arabic requires roughly **3x the study time** of a Romance language to reach the same proficiency level.\n- The State Department provides incentive pay: 5% salary for 2/2 rating, 10% for 3/3, 15% for 4/4.\n\n**National Academies review:** A Principled Approach to Language Assessment: Considerations for the U.S. Foreign Service Institute. https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/25748/chapter/4\n\n**Why Arabic is so difficult for English speakers:**\n1. Non-Latin script with right-to-left directionality\n2. Root-and-pattern morphology (fundamentally different from English affixation)\n3. Diglossia -- learners must decide which variety to study and navigate between them\n4. Complex verb conjugation (10+ verb forms/patterns)\n5. Case system (largely absent in spoken Arabic but present in MSA)\n6. Phonemes that do not exist in English (pharyngeals, emphatics)\n7. Orthographic depth when diacritics are absent\n\n---\n\n### 6.2 ACTFL Proficiency Guidelines for Arabic\n\n**Citation:** American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL). (2012). ACTFL Proficiency Guidelines (Revised). Alexandria, VA: ACTFL.\n\n**URL:** https://www.actfl.org/resources/actfl-proficiency-guidelines-2012/arabic\n\n**Key information:**\n- Four major levels: Novice, Intermediate, Advanced, Superior (each with Low/Mid/High subdivisions).\n- Distinguished level added in 2012.\n- The Arabic-specific guidelines acknowledge the unique challenges of diglossia and script.\n- Most university Arabic programs in the US produce students at Intermediate-Low to Intermediate-Mid after 2-3 years of study.\n- Research from Cambridge suggests a ~40% failure rate in achieving target proficiency within recommended study periods for Category IV/V languages.\n\n---\n\n### 6.3 Ryding: Teaching and Learning Arabic as a Foreign Language\n\n**Citation:** Ryding, K.C. (2013). *Teaching and Learning Arabic as a Foreign Language: A Guide for Teachers*. Georgetown University Press.\n\n**URL:** https://press.georgetown.edu/Book/Teaching-and-Learning-Arabic-as-a-Foreign-Language\n\n**About the author:** Karin C. Ryding is Sultan Qaboos bin Said Professor Emerita of Arabic linguistics at Georgetown University. She headed Arabic training at the Foreign Service Institute from 1980 to 1986 and is a former president of the American Association of Teachers of Arabic.\n\n**Key contributions:**\n- Discusses issues at the core of Arabic teaching effectiveness: pronunciation, speaking, reading, listening, writing.\n- Addresses teaching mixed-level classes, creative classroom organization, corrective feedback.\n- Revisits the 2012 ACTFL language-proficiency guidelines.\n- Frames discussions with SLA (Second Language Acquisition) literature.\n- Also serves as a resource for teachers of other Less Commonly Taught Languages (LCTLs).\n\n---\n\n### 6.4 Wahba, Taha, & England: Handbook for Arabic Language Teaching Professionals\n\n**Citation:** Wahba, K.M., Taha, Z.A., & England, L. (Eds.). (2014). *Handbook for Arabic Language Teaching Professionals in the 21st Century*. New York: Routledge.\n\n**Volume II:** https://www.routledge.com/Handbook-for-Arabic-Language-Teaching-Professionals-in-the-21st-Century/Wahba-England-Taha/p/book/9781138934771\n\n**About the editors:**\n- Kassem M. Wahba: Professor, Department of Near Eastern Studies, Cornell University.\n- Zeinab A. Taha: Associate Professor, Department of Applied Linguistics, American University in Cairo.\n\n**Key content:** Comprehensive treatment of teaching and research in Arabic as a second and foreign language worldwide. Covers pedagogy, assessment, curriculum design, and SLA theory applied to Arabic.\n\n---\n\n### 6.5 Azaz: Instructed Second Language Acquisition of Arabic\n\n**Citation:** Azaz, M. (2021). *Instructed Second Language Acquisition of Arabic: Contextualized Input*. Routledge.\n\n**URL:** https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781003055600/instructed-second-language-acquisition-arabic-mahmoud-azaz\n\n**Key focus:** Applies instructed SLA theory specifically to Arabic learning contexts, with emphasis on contextualized input approaches.\n\n---\n\n### 6.6 Enhancing Arabic Language Acquisition (2024)\n\n**Citation:** (2024). Enhancing Arabic Language Acquisition: Effective Strategies for Addressing Non-Native Learners' Challenges. *Education Sciences*, 14(10), 1116.\n\n**URL:** https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7102/14/10/1116\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Conventional teacher-centered methods (rote memorization, grammar-translation) are \"frequently insufficient.\"\n- Arabic instructors should focus on improving feedback practices, developing customized curricula, and incorporating technology.\n- The ideal teaching approach should be \"eclectic and student-centered.\"\n- Non-native speakers can organize their L2 Arabic lexicons similarly to native speakers, showing root-based priming effects despite typological distance.\n\n---\n\n### 6.7 Arabic Reading Comprehension Strategies\n\n**Citation:** Mokhtari, K., & Reichard, C.A. (2004). Metacognitive awareness of reading strategy use in Arabic as a second language. *Reading in a Foreign Language*, 16(2).\n\n**URL:** https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ994855.pdf\n\n**Key findings:**\n- A positive relationship exists between metacognitive strategy awareness and reading comprehension in Arabic.\n- Successful readers use more reading strategies than unsuccessful ones.\n- The Arabic writing system is a significant obstacle for non-native readers, particularly those whose L1 uses a different script.\n\n**Citation:** (2022). Investigating Arabic teachers' perceptions of extensive reading practices in higher education. *Cogent Education*.\n\n**URL:** https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/2331186X.2022.2162701\n\n**Key findings on extensive reading:**\n- Extensive reading is \"as efficient and better than intensive reading\" in improving reading comprehension, reading speed, and vocabulary acquisition for Arabic learners.\n- Benefits include enhanced knowledge of spelling, grammar, text structure, and vocabulary, increased knowledge of the world, and greater enjoyment of reading.\n\n---\n\n### 6.8 Comprehensible Input and Arabic (Yale)\n\n**Citation:** Yale University. *Read, Learn and Play: K-16 Online Interactive Arabic Reading Materials*.\n\n**URL:** https://campuspress.yale.edu/readlearnplayarabic/\n\n**Key information:**\n- Materials designed according to Comprehensible Input (CI) theory and the Teaching Proficiency Through Reading and Storytelling (TPRS) method.\n- Provides graded Arabic reading materials for learners from kindergarten to college level.\n- Demonstrates practical application of CI theory to Arabic instruction.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Heritage Language Learners\n\n### 7.1 Arabic as a Heritage Language: A Scoping Review\n\n**Citation:** Visona, M.W., & Plonsky, L. (2020). Arabic as a heritage language: A scoping review. *International Journal of Bilingualism*, 24(4).\n\n**URL:** https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/1367006919849110\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Most Arabic heritage language learners (HLLs) have higher proficiencies in their dialect than in MSA.\n- Many Arabic HLLs may have no experience with MSA prior to entering college.\n- Vocabulary knowledge is often mostly passive.\n- Arabic HLLs need practice and further exposure to retrieve lexical items quickly.\n\n---\n\n### 7.2 Arabic Heritage Learners in Islamic Schools\n\n**Citation:** (Various). Heritage Language Learners of Arabic in Islamic Schools: Opportunities for Attaining Arabic Language Proficiency.\n\n**URL:** https://www.academia.edu/64050028/Heritage_Language_Learners_of_Arabic_in_Islamic_Schools_Opportunities_for_Attaining_Arabic_Language_Proficiency\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Studies reveal \"a sense of general discontent towards the results of learning Arabic at community schools.\"\n- The push for monoculturalism and monolingualism plus political issues \"conspire against language maintenance.\"\n- Arabic heritage speakers are often reluctant to use Arabic outside their local community.\n\n---\n\n### 7.3 Arabic as a Heritage Language in the US\n\n**Citation:** Bale, J. (2010). Arabic as a Heritage Language in the United States. *International Multilingual Research Journal*, 4(2).\n\n**URL:** https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/19313152.2010.499041\n\n**Additional source:** CUNY Graduate Center. *Heritage Arabic eBook*. City University of New York.\n\n**URL:** https://cilc.commons.gc.cuny.edu/heritage-arabic-ebook/arabic-as-a-heritage-language/\n\n**Key context:**\n- Arabic language efforts in the US are embedded within \"a larger sociopolitical and sociohistorical context of conflict.\"\n- Despite growing interest among younger Arab Americans motivated by desire to reconnect with cultural roots, significant structural and social barriers persist.\n\n---\n\n## 8. Technology in Arabic Education\n\n### 8.1 Systematic Review: Technology Integration in Arabic Curriculum (2025)\n\n**Citation:** (2025). Integrating technology into the Arabic language curriculum: A systematic review of trends, strategies and cultural dimensions. *System*, 2025, 107028.\n\n**URL:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291125007028\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Digital tools (AI, gamified applications, LMS) enhance learner engagement and independent learning.\n- Pedagogical strategies including infographic modules, multimedia scaffolds, translanguaging, and problem-based learning improve comprehension and motivation.\n- Key challenges: lack of tailored Arabic-specific resources, technical limitations related to Arabic language compatibility (RTL text, morphological complexity).\n\n---\n\n### 8.2 Technology Integration in Arabic: Literature Review (2024)\n\n**Citation:** (2024). Technology integration in Arabic language learning: A literature review on the effectiveness of e-learning and mobile applications.\n\n**URL:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/385263128_Technology_integration_in_arabic_language_learning_A_literature_review_on_the_effectiveness_of_e-learning_and_mobile_applications\n\n**Key findings:**\n- A significant positive relationship between technology use and Arabic language skills: \"every additional hour of technology use per week increasing Arabic language skill scores by 3.679 points.\"\n- Technology integration can improve grammar, vocabulary, and speaking competence.\n- Mobile learning significantly impacts learning effectiveness through content creation, communication, and collaboration.\n- Tools include interactive chatbots, speech recognition, assistive technologies, virtual assistants, and language learning platforms.\n\n---\n\n### 8.3 Gamification and Language Learning Apps\n\n**Citation:** Shortt, M., Tilak, S., Kuznetcova, I., et al. (2021). Gamification in mobile-assisted language learning: A systematic review of Duolingo literature from public release of 2012 to early 2020. *Computer Assisted Language Learning*, 36(3).\n\n**URL:** https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/09588221.2021.1933540\n\n**Citation:** (2023). The Effectiveness of Gamified Tools for Foreign Language Learning (FLL): A Systematic Review. *PMC*.\n\n**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10135444/\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Mixed effectiveness: \"some bringing positive changes, others negative changes, and some showing no differences.\"\n- Gamification has a \"hugely positive effect\" on user retention and engagement.\n- Some gamification characteristics \"create anxiety, pressure, and frustration among users.\"\n- No major studies focus specifically on Arabic gamification effectiveness, though general findings apply across languages.\n\n---\n\n### 8.4 Arabic NLP for Education Applications\n\n**Citation:** (2025). Arabic Natural Language Processing (NLP): A Comprehensive Review of Challenges, Techniques, and Emerging Trends. *Computers*, 14(11), 497.\n\n**URL:** https://www.mdpi.com/2073-431X/14/11/497\n\n**Conferences:**\n- ArabicNLP 2024/2025 (ACL Special Interest Group on Arabic NLP): https://arabicnlp2025.sigarab.org/\n- AbjadNLP 2026: https://wp.lancs.ac.uk/abjad/\n\n**Key research areas relevant to education:**\n- Morphological analysis and tokenization (critical for vocabulary tools)\n- OCR and handwriting recognition (digitizing Arabic texts)\n- Speech recognition and synthesis (pronunciation feedback, TTS)\n- Text classification and readability assessment\n- Question answering systems\n- Dialect identification and MSA-dialect translation\n\n**Challenges:** Arabic's rich morphology, orthographic ambiguity (especially without diacritics), dialectal variation, and limited availability of high-quality annotated resources.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Spaced Repetition and Vocabulary Acquisition\n\n### 9.1 SRS for Arabic Vocabulary: University Case Study\n\n**Citation:** (2024). Introducing Spaced Repetition Software (SRS) for vocabulary acquisition in a university-level Arabic language course: A case study. *Journal of Language Teaching*.\n\n**URL:** https://jlt.ac/home/article/view/99\n\n**Full text:** https://jlt.ac/home/article/download/99/83/325\n\n**Key findings:**\n- Twenty-two beginning Arabic students were required to use SRS for vocabulary acquisition.\n- Over 100 years of research confirms spaced repetition as superior to massed practice for long-term retention.\n- Recommended pace: 5-10 new Arabic words per day, with the \"compounding effect\" meaning 50-100 reviews daily within a month.\n- Students need to adhere to the rigorous schedule SRS requires.\n- Self-monitoring of flashcard reviews helps students recognize the link between effort and outcome.\n\n**Practical note:** Anki remains the gold standard for serious learners due to its algorithm and offline flexibility. Lingualism (https://lingualism.com/) provides pre-built Arabic Anki decks for MSA and various dialects.\n\n---\n\n## 10. Key Scholars and Reference Works\n\n### 10.1 Key Researchers in Arabic Education\n\n| Scholar | Affiliation | Focus Area |\n|---------|-------------|------------|\n| Elinor Saiegh-Haddad | Bar-Ilan University | Diglossia, reading acquisition, psycholinguistics |\n| Salim Abu-Rabia | University of Haifa | Arabic orthography, reading difficulties |\n| Karin C. Ryding | Georgetown University (Emerita) | Arabic pedagogy, FSI, ACTFL guidelines |\n| Kassem M. Wahba | Cornell University | Arabic as a foreign language |\n| Zeinab A. Taha | American University in Cairo | Applied linguistics, Arabic SLA |\n| Mahmoud Azaz | University of Arizona | Instructed SLA of Arabic |\n| Laura Gregory | World Bank | Arabic learning poverty, MENA education policy |\n| Andreas Blom | World Bank | MENA education, Arabic literacy |\n\n### 10.2 Key Reference Works\n\n1. **Ryding, K.C.** (2005). *A Reference Grammar of Modern Standard Arabic*. Cambridge University Press. -- The standard reference grammar for MSA.\n\n2. **Ryding, K.C.** (2013). *Teaching and Learning Arabic as a Foreign Language*. Georgetown University Press. -- The pedagogical reference.\n\n3. **Wahba, K.M., Taha, Z.A., & England, L.** (Eds.). *Handbook for Arabic Language Teaching Professionals in the 21st Century* (Vol. I & II). Routledge. -- Comprehensive professional handbook.\n\n4. **World Bank** (2021). *Advancing Arabic Language Teaching and Learning*. -- The policy reference for MENA education reform.\n\n5. **UNDP** (2002-2005). *Arab Human Development Report* series. -- The foundational diagnosis of Arab development deficits.\n\n### 10.3 Key Journals\n\n- *Arab Journal of Applied Linguistics* (AJAL): https://www.arjals.com/\n- *Applied Psycholinguistics* (Cambridge University Press)\n- *Reading Research Quarterly* (Wiley/ILA)\n- *Frontiers in Education* and *Frontiers in Psychology*\n- *Journal of Language Teaching*\n- *Computer Assisted Language Learning* (Taylor & Francis)\n\n---\n\n## Summary of Key Takeaways\n\n1. **The scale of the problem is enormous.** 60% of MENA children cannot read an age-appropriate text by age 10 (pre-COVID). International assessments (PIRLS, PISA) consistently show Arab countries scoring significantly below international averages in reading.\n\n2. **Diglossia is a structural challenge with no consensus solution.** Children effectively learn to read in a language they do not speak at home. The QRF systematic review found contradictory evidence about whether this is a primary cause of poor outcomes or a manageable factor.\n\n3. **Teaching methods are a major part of the problem.** The World Bank identifies rigid, grammar-focused, accuracy-obsessed approaches that lack playfulness and inquiry. Many Arabic teachers are themselves products of ineffective education.\n\n4. **Arabic's orthographic system creates a unique double challenge.** Vowelized text is transparent and learnable, but the transition to unvowelized text requires deep morphological knowledge and context awareness. This transition point is a critical vulnerability in the education pipeline.\n\n5. **Root-pattern morphology is both a challenge and an opportunity.** Research shows that morphological awareness (root and wazn awareness) is a strong predictor of reading success. Pedagogies that leverage this structure rather than treating Arabic as if it were English can be more effective.\n\n6. **For foreign learners, Arabic is objectively one of the hardest languages.** The FSI's 2,200-hour estimate (vs. 600 for Spanish) is well-established. The combination of script, morphology, diglossia, and phonology creates compounding difficulty.\n\n7. **Technology shows promise but lacks Arabic-specific research.** Most studies on language learning apps and gamification are not Arabic-specific. The technical challenges of Arabic NLP (morphological richness, orthographic ambiguity, dialectal variation) remain partially unsolved.\n\n8. **Spaced repetition works for Arabic vocabulary** but requires discipline. The compounding review load is a common attrition factor. Sentence-context review (rather than isolated word cards) may improve retention and transfer.\n\n---\n\n*This bibliography was compiled on 2026-02-21 from web searches of academic databases, institutional reports, and scholarly publications. URLs were verified at time of compilation. Some paywalled articles are cited with open-access alternatives where available.*\n", "date": "Feb 21", "category": "education", "status": "active", "cross_refs": ["arabic-teaching-worldwide-2026-02-21", "arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21", "deep-research-compilation-2026-02-12", "cognitive-load-language-learning", "vocabulary-acquisition-research"], "backlinks": ["arabic-teaching-worldwide-2026-02-21", "arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/arabic-education-research-2026-02-21.md"}, "arabic-learning-architecture": {"title": "Arabic Learning App: Architecture Research", "content": "# Arabic Learning App: Architecture Research\n\nResearch compiled 2026-02-08. This document covers existing open source tools, NLP libraries, spaced repetition systems, text difficulty assessment, word knowledge tracking, and -- critically -- architectural patterns for combining LLM generation with deterministic vocabulary validation.\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Existing Open Source Arabic Learning Tools](#1-existing-open-source-arabic-learning-tools)\n2. [Arabic NLP Libraries for Root/Morphology Analysis](#2-arabic-nlp-libraries-for-rootmorphology-analysis)\n3. [Spaced Repetition Systems](#3-spaced-repetition-systems)\n4. [Text Difficulty Assessment](#4-text-difficulty-assessment)\n5. [Word Knowledge Tracking Data Model](#5-word-knowledge-tracking-data-model)\n6. [Arabic Morphology and the Root System](#6-arabic-morphology-and-the-root-system)\n7. [Frequency Lists and CEFR-Graded Vocabulary](#7-frequency-lists-and-cefr-graded-vocabulary)\n8. [Sentence Corpora and Datasets](#8-sentence-corpora-and-datasets)\n9. [LLM + Deterministic Tools Architecture](#9-llm--deterministic-tools-architecture)\n10. [Sentence Generation with Constraints](#10-sentence-generation-with-constraints)\n11. [Recommended Architecture](#11-recommended-architecture)\n12. [Implementation Roadmap](#12-implementation-roadmap)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Existing Open Source Arabic Learning Tools\n\n### ArabicDialectHub\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/saleml/arabic-dialect-hub\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Paper**: [ArabicDialectHub: A Cross-Dialectal Arabic Learning Resource and Platform](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.22987)\n- **Stack**: React 18 + TypeScript, Netlify CDN\n- **What it does**: 552 phrases across 6 Arabic varieties (Moroccan Darija, Lebanese, Syrian, Emirati, Saudi, MSA). Phrases were LLM-generated and validated by native speakers. Features include translation exploration, adaptive quizzing with algorithmic distractor generation, cloud-synchronized progress tracking, and cultural context cards.\n- **Relevance**: Demonstrates LLM-generated content validated by humans, adaptive quiz generation, progress tracking. Good reference for quiz UI patterns.\n\n### OpenArabic (edenmind)\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/edenmind/OpenArabic\n- **License**: Open source\n- **Stack**: Microservice mesh providing language services (tashkeel, text-to-speech, lemmatization) to a backend API serving frontend clients. GitOps deployment with Flux2/Flagger.\n- **What it does**: Reading platform with short bilingual texts and vocabulary quizzes. Focuses on MSA through Islamic texts (Quran, Hadith, Fiqh, Tafsir, etc.).\n- **Relevance**: Microservice architecture for Arabic NLP services is directly applicable. Shows how to decompose tashkeel, TTS, and lemmatization into independent services.\n\n### ArabEngo\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/michaelsboost/ArabEngo\n- **Status**: Discontinued (2018)\n- **What it does**: Simple open source language learning template for English speakers learning Arabic.\n- **Relevance**: Limited -- discontinued and basic, but shows early open source attempts.\n\n### LangSeed\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/simedw/langseed\n- **Live**: https://langseed.com\n- **Stack**: Elixir/Phoenix, Gemini API\n- **What it does**: Defines new words using only vocabulary the learner already knows, with emojis bridging semantic gaps. Generates fill-in-the-blank and yes/no questions at the learner's level. Supports Chinese, Swedish, English.\n- **Relevance**: Directly relevant architecture pattern. The core concept -- LLM constrained to use only known vocabulary -- is exactly the \"i+1\" generation problem. Does not support Arabic yet, but the architecture is transferable.\n\n### Clozemaster\n\n- **Site**: https://www.clozemaster.com/languages/learn-arabic-online\n- **License**: Proprietary (but uses open data)\n- **What it does**: Cloze deletion exercises from the Tatoeba dataset (8.5M sentence pairs). Sentences ordered by word frequency. Arabic is supported.\n- **Relevance**: Demonstrates frequency-ordered cloze testing at scale. Their approach of sourcing sentences from Tatoeba and ordering by frequency is a proven pattern.\n\n### Wordpecker\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/baturyilmaz/wordpecker-app\n- **What it does**: Personalized language learning combining Duolingo-style lessons with user-curated vocabulary lists. Users add words from books, articles, or videos and revisit them through interactive quizzes and LLM-generated lessons.\n- **Relevance**: Shows the pattern of LLM-generated lessons personalized to user vocabulary.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Arabic NLP Libraries for Root/Morphology Analysis\n\nThese are essential for the deterministic side of the architecture: given a word in a generated sentence, determine its root, lemma, and whether the user \"knows\" it.\n\n### CAMeL Tools (Primary Recommendation)\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/camel_tools\n- **Docs**: https://camel-tools.readthedocs.io\n- **Paper**: [CAMeL Tools: An Open Source Python Toolkit for Arabic NLP](https://aclanthology.org/2020.lrec-1.868.pdf)\n- **Developed by**: CAMeL Lab, NYU Abu Dhabi\n- **License**: MIT\n- **Install**: `pip install camel-tools` then `camel_data -i light` (for morphology only)\n\n**Key capabilities for this project:**\n\n```python\nfrom camel_tools.morphology.database import MorphologyDB\nfrom camel_tools.morphology.analyzer import Analyzer\n\ndb = MorphologyDB.builtin_db()\nanalyzer = Analyzer(db, backoff='NOAN_PROP')\n\n# Analyze a word -- returns list of possible analyses\nanalyses = analyzer.analyze('كتب')\n# Each analysis contains:\n# root -> 'ك.ت.ب'\n# lex -> lemma\n# pos -> part of speech\n# prc0/prc1/prc2/prc3 -> proclitics\n# enc0 -> enclitic\n# form_gen, form_num -> gender, number\n# ... full morphological feature set\n```\n\n**Why it is the best choice:**\n- Handles MSA and multiple dialects (Egyptian, Gulf, Levantine, Maghrebi)\n- Returns root, lemma, POS, full morphological decomposition\n- Has disambiguation (picks the most likely analysis in context)\n- Has a morphological generator (given features, generate the word form)\n- Active development by a major NLP lab\n- Includes tokenization, diacritization, transliteration\n\n### Qalsadi\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/linuxscout/qalsadi\n- **What it does**: Morphological analysis using a lexical database. Handles vocalized/unvocalized text. Returns root, lemma, POS, morphological features.\n- **Relevance**: Alternative to CAMeL Tools, lighter weight. Part of a family of Arabic NLP tools by the same author (Taha Zerrouki).\n\n### Qutuf\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/Qutuf/Qutuf\n- **What it does**: Arabic Morphological Analyzer (root extraction, stemming) and POS tagger implemented as an expert system.\n- **Relevance**: Rule-based rather than statistical, which can be more predictable for deterministic checking.\n\n### wordfreq\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/rspeer/wordfreq\n- **Install**: `pip install wordfreq`\n- **Arabic support**: Yes, with 5 large frequency lists from Wikipedia, subtitles, news, books, web content.\n- **Note**: Data is a snapshot through ~2021, no longer being updated.\n\n```python\nfrom wordfreq import word_frequency, zipf_frequency\nzipf_frequency('كتاب', 'ar') # Returns Zipf-scale frequency\n```\n\n### CAMeL Arabic Frequency Lists\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/Camel_Arabic_Frequency_Lists\n- **What it does**: Frequency lists from the same lab that makes CAMeL Tools.\n- **Relevance**: Can be used to assign frequency ranks to words, which correlates with difficulty level.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Spaced Repetition Systems\n\n### FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) -- Recommended\n\n- **Organization**: https://github.com/open-spaced-repetition\n- **Python library**: https://github.com/open-spaced-repetition/py-fsrs (`pip install fsrs`)\n- **Algorithm paper**: Based on DSR (Difficulty, Stability, Retrievability) model\n- **Also available in**: JavaScript, Rust, Go, Elixir, Swift, and more\n- **Used by**: Anki (via fsrs4anki plugin), Mochi Cards\n\n```python\nfrom fsrs import Scheduler, Card, Rating\n\nscheduler = Scheduler()\ncard = Card()\n\n# After reviewing a card:\ncard, review_log = scheduler.review_card(card, Rating.Good)\n\n# Check retrievability (probability of recall):\nretrievability = scheduler.get_card_retrievability(card)\n\n# Cards have states: Learning, Review, Relearning\n# Cards and ReviewLogs are JSON-serializable via to_json()/from_json()\n```\n\n**Why FSRS over SM-2 (Anki's legacy):**\n- Empirically shown to be more efficient (fewer reviews for same retention)\n- Adapts to individual memory patterns\n- Supports advance/delayed reviews\n- Parameters can be optimized from user's own review history via `Optimizer` class\n- Open source with implementations in every major language\n\n### What to Track per Card\n\nFor Arabic specifically, cards should track knowledge at multiple granularity levels:\n\n| Level | Example | Rationale |\n|-------|---------|-----------|\n| **Root** | ك.ت.ب | Knowing a root gives partial knowledge of all derived words |\n| **Lemma** | كِتَاب (book) | The dictionary form -- core vocabulary unit |\n| **Word form** | كُتُب (books) | Specific inflection (broken plural in this case) |\n| **Verb form/wazn** | Form II كَتَّبَ | Verb pattern -- knowing the pattern gives predictability |\n| **Conjugation** | كَتَبْتُ (I wrote) | Specific person/tense/mood combination |\n\n### Existing Anki Decks for Arabic\n\n- **Lingualism MSA Verb Conjugation Drills**: 25 verbs x 60 conjugations = 1,500 cards, each with audio. Available in Arabic-English and English-Arabic (3,000 total cards).\n- **Lingualism Arabic Learner's Dictionary**: Flashcards organized by root.\n- **Lingualism Egyptian Arabic Verb Conjugation Drills**: Multiple sets covering common Egyptian verbs.\n- **Frequency-based decks**: Available on https://anki-decks.com/anki-decks/arabic/\n\n---\n\n## 4. Text Difficulty Assessment\n\n### BAREC Corpus and Shared Task\n\n- **Dataset**: https://huggingface.co/datasets/CAMeL-Lab/BAREC-Shared-Task-2025-sent\n- **Paper**: [A Large and Balanced Corpus for Fine-grained Arabic Readability Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.13520)\n- **What it is**: 69,441 sentences, 1M+ words, manually annotated across 19 readability levels (from kindergarten to postgraduate). Inter-annotator agreement: 81.8% QWK.\n- **Level schemes**: Available in 19-level, 7-level, 5-level, and 3-level granularity.\n- **License**: Publicly available.\n\nThe 19 levels use Abjad-order naming: 1-alif, 2-ba, 3-jim, ... 19-qaf.\n\n### SAMER Lexicon\n\n- **Paper**: [The SAMER Arabic Text Simplification Corpus](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.18615v1)\n- **What it is**: A 40K-lemma lexicon with a 5-level readability scale (L1=easy to L5=specialist). Includes a Google Docs add-on for word-level difficulty visualization.\n- **Levels**: L1 (Grade 1, age 6), L2 (Grade 2-3, age 7-8), L3 (Grade 4-5, age 9-10), L4 (Grade 6-8, age 11-14), L5 (specialist, age 15+).\n- **Relevance**: Can be used to tag each word in a generated sentence with its difficulty level. Essential for the constraint checker.\n\n### OSMAN Readability Tool\n\n- **Repo**: https://github.com/drelhaj/OsmanReadability\n- **What it does**: Open source tool for computing Arabic text readability scores.\n- **Relevance**: Can score entire texts or passages for difficulty.\n\n### Approaches to Arabic Readability\n\nResearch ([Strategies for Arabic Readability Modeling](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.03032v1)) shows that effective Arabic readability assessment combines:\n\n1. **Lexical features**: Word frequency, word length, rare word ratio\n2. **Syntactic features**: Sentence length, clause depth, coordination ratio\n3. **Morphological features**: Type-token ratio, verb complexity\n4. **ML classifiers**: Trained on BAREC or SAMER data to predict level\n\nFor this app, a practical approach is:\n- Use SAMER lexicon for word-level difficulty\n- Use word frequency (from wordfreq or CAMeL frequency lists) as a proxy for difficulty\n- Combine with sentence-level heuristics (length, number of unknown words)\n\n---\n\n## 5. Word Knowledge Tracking Data Model\n\n### Core Data Model\n\n```\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│ USER │\n│ user_id, created_at, settings │\n└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘\n │ 1:N\n ▼\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│ USER_ROOT_KNOWLEDGE │\n│ user_id, root (e.g. 'ك.ت.ب'), familiarity_score, │\n│ first_seen, last_reviewed, review_count │\n│ ────────────────────────────────────────────────── │\n│ Tracks: does the user recognize this root family? │\n└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘\n │ 1:N\n ▼\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│ USER_LEMMA_KNOWLEDGE │\n│ user_id, lemma_id, root, knowledge_state, │\n│ fsrs_card (JSON), last_reviewed, times_seen, │\n│ times_correct, source (explicit_study | encountered) │\n│ ────────────────────────────────────────────────── │\n│ knowledge_state: new | learning | known | lapsed │\n│ ────────────────────────────────────────────────── │\n│ Tracks: does the user know this specific word? │\n└──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘\n │ 1:N\n ▼\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│ USER_FORM_KNOWLEDGE │\n│ user_id, lemma_id, form_type (e.g. 'verb_form_II'), │\n│ inflection (e.g. 'past_3ms'), knowledge_state, │\n│ fsrs_card (JSON) │\n│ ────────────────────────────────────────────────── │\n│ Tracks: does the user know this specific inflection? │\n└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│ LEMMA (reference) │\n│ lemma_id, lemma_ar, lemma_en, root, pos, │\n│ frequency_rank, difficulty_level (SAMER L1-L5), │\n│ verb_form (I-X for verbs), cefr_estimate │\n└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│ ROOT_FAMILY (reference) │\n│ root, core_meaning_en, word_count, │\n│ common_lemmas[] (ordered by frequency) │\n└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### Knowledge State Transitions\n\n```\n ┌─────┐ study/encounter ┌──────────┐ FSRS review ┌───────┐\n │ new ├────────────────────►│ learning ├──────────────────►│ known │\n └─────┘ └─────┬─────┘ └───┬───┘\n │ │\n │ fail fail │\n ▼ ▼\n ┌──────────┐ ┌─────────┐\n │ new │◄─────────────────┤ lapsed │\n └──────────┘ after timeout └─────────┘\n```\n\n### Key Design Decisions\n\n1. **Root-level knowledge propagation**: When a user learns a word with root ك.ت.ب, their familiarity_score for that root increases. When generating sentences, words sharing a known root can be treated as \"partially known\" (the user can likely guess the meaning from context + root knowledge).\n\n2. **Implicit vs explicit knowledge**: Track whether a word was explicitly studied (flashcard) or implicitly encountered (appeared in a sentence and was understood). Both contribute to knowledge but with different confidence levels.\n\n3. **FSRS card per lemma**: Each lemma the user is actively studying has an FSRS Card object (JSON-serialized). The scheduler determines when it needs review.\n\n4. **Efficient \"is this word known?\" lookup**: For the sentence validator, you need a fast set lookup. Maintain a materialized set of known lemma IDs per user (updated on each review). Given a sentence, morphologically analyze each word to get its lemma, then check set membership. This must be fast (sub-100ms for a sentence).\n\n---\n\n## 6.
