Arabic Diacritics and L2 Reading](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/modl.12642) -- Landmark study on diacritics and L2 learner performance\n- [L2 Arabic Learners' Processing of Garden-Path Sentences (2024)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2024.1333112/full) -- Word order processing in L2 Arabic\n- [Arabic Emphatic Consonants as Produced by English Speakers (PMC 2023)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9932739/) -- Phonological challenges for L2 learners\n- [Learnability and Generalization of Arabic Broken Plural Nouns (PMC 2014)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4192858/) -- Pattern vs. rote learning of broken plurals\n- [Enhancing Arabic Language Acquisition (MDPI 2024)](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7102/14/10/1116) -- Strategies for addressing non-native learner challenges\n- [Native English Speakers' Perception of Arabic Emphatic Consonants (2016)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/flan.12217) -- Vowel context effects on emphatic consonant perception\n- [Analyzing Word Order Variation in Standard Arabic (2023)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/23311983.2023.2268920) -- VSO/SVO agreement asymmetry\n- [Arabic Diglossia: Non-Deficit Model (Frontiers 2025)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2025.1518728/full) -- Diglossia and reading acquisition\n\n### Arabic Linguistics Resources\n- [Arabic Verbs - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_verbs) -- Comprehensive overview of verb system\n- [The 10 Arabic Verb Forms - Arabic for Nerds](https://arabic-for-nerds.com/arabic-verb-forms/) -- Learner-oriented verb form guide\n- [Arabic Verb Forms - Desert Sky](https://arabic.desert-sky.net/g_vforms.html) -- Detailed verb form reference\n- [Idafa Construction - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I%E1%B8%8D%C4%81fah) -- Construct state explanation\n- [Sun and Moon Letters - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun_and_moon_letters) -- Classification and rules\n- [Hamza Rules - adamiturabi](https://adamiturabi.github.io/hamza-rules/) -- Detailed orthographic rules\n- [I'rab - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%CA%BEI%CA%BFrab) -- Case system overview\n\n### Teaching and Methodology\n- [Al-Kitaab Arabic Language Program](https://alkitaabtextbook.com/) -- Georgetown textbook series\n- [Critique of Al-Kitaab](https://www.arabamerica.com/the-good-and-the-bad-of-al-kitaab/) -- Analysis of strengths and weaknesses\n- [Arabic Root System and Vocabulary Acquisition](https://lingua-learn.qa/how-arabic-root-system-enhances-vocabulary-acquisition/) -- Root-based teaching research\n- [Learning Arabic Through Living Roots (QFI)](https://www.qfi.org/blog/when-words-connect/) -- Root-based pedagogy\n- [LANDSTARZ Mnemonic for Sun/Moon Letters](https://www.academia.edu/40408561/LANDSTARZ_A_Mnemonic_for_Teaching_the_Arabic_Sun_and_Moon_Letters) -- Teaching methodology\n\n### Graded Readers\n- [Sahlawayhi Series](https://us.amazon.com/Sahlawayhi-Arabic-Reading-Comprehension-Khorshid/dp/1797977156) -- Graded stories for beginners\n- [Lingualism Arabic Readers](https://lingualism.com/lingualism-news/new-and-revised-arabic-readers/) -- Multi-level, multi-dialect readers\n\n### Arabic NLP and Tools\n- [CJKI Arabic Verb Conjugator](http://cjki.org/arabic/cave/cavehelp.htm) -- 130+ affirmative forms per verb, 240 total per subtype\n- [Broken Plural Patterns in MSA](https://www.academia.edu/42530084/Broken_Plural_in_Modern_Standard_Arabic) -- Academic analysis of pattern inventory\n- [Arabic Broken Plural Patterns - Amin Academy](https://aminacademy.org/arabic-broken-plurals-patterns/) -- Learner reference\n", "date": null, "category": "science", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/arabic-learning-challenges.md"}, "arabic-morphology-tools": {"title": "Arabic Morphological Analysis: Open Source Tools & Libraries", "content": "# Arabic Morphological Analysis: Open Source Tools & Libraries\n\n*Comprehensive survey -- last updated February 2026*\n\nThis document covers open-source tools for Arabic morphological analysis, root extraction, lemmatization, conjugation generation, and stemming. Focus is on **Modern Standard Arabic (MSA / fusha)**.\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Comprehensive Toolkits (Multi-feature)](#1-comprehensive-toolkits)\n2. [Root Extraction](#2-root-extraction)\n3. [Morphological Analysis](#3-morphological-analysis)\n4. [Lemmatization](#4-lemmatization)\n5. [Conjugation Generation](#5-conjugation-generation)\n6. [Stemming](#6-stemming)\n7. [Supporting Libraries & Resources](#7-supporting-libraries--resources)\n8. [Transformer-Based Models](#8-transformer-based-models)\n9. [Quality & Accuracy Comparisons](#9-quality--accuracy-comparisons)\n10. [Summary Recommendation Matrix](#10-summary-recommendation-matrix)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Comprehensive Toolkits\n\nThese are full-stack toolkits that cover multiple aspects of Arabic morphological processing.\n\n### CAMeL Tools\n\n**The most actively maintained and comprehensive Arabic NLP toolkit available today.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/camel_tools\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** MIT\n- **Stars:** ~522\n- **Latest Version:** v1.5.7 (September 2025)\n- **Python:** 3.8 - 3.12 (also requires Rust compiler)\n- **Install:** `pip install camel-tools` + `camel_data -i all`\n- **Status:** Actively maintained by CAMeL Lab at NYU Abu Dhabi\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Morphological analysis and disambiguation (using CALIMA databases)\n- Morphological generation (word form generation from features)\n- Lemmatization\n- POS tagging\n- Dialect identification (MSA + dialects)\n- Named entity recognition\n- Sentiment analysis\n- Diacritization\n- Tokenization and segmentation\n- Transliteration (Buckwalter, Safe Buckwalter, etc.)\n\n**Morphological databases included:**\n- `calima-msa-r13` -- Modern Standard Arabic\n- `calima-egy-r13` -- Egyptian Arabic\n\n**Quality:** State-of-the-art for MSA morphological analysis. The underlying CALIMA Star database extends SAMA with 40K+ lemmas. Consistently outperforms other tools on several benchmarks (Nafis dataset: 0.68 accuracy, Sharaye: 0.81 accuracy in segmentation comparison studies).\n\n**Notes:** This is the spiritual successor to MADAMIRA. The CAMeL Lab actively publishes research improving the toolkit. Requires downloading ~2-3 GB of model data.\n\n---\n\n### CamelMorph (companion to CAMeL Tools)\n\n**The largest open-source MSA morphological analyzer and generator.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/camel_morph\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** MIT (code), CC-BY 4.0 (data)\n- **Stars:** ~15\n- **Status:** Active (LREC-COLING 2024 release)\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Morphological analysis (input word -> all possible analyses)\n- Morphological generation (features -> word forms)\n- Reinflection\n- 100K+ lemmas\n- ~1.45 billion possible analyses, ~535 million unique diacritizations\n- ~36% less out-of-vocabulary rate than SAMA on a 10-billion-word corpus\n\n**Quality:** Order-of-magnitude larger than SAMA. Integrates seamlessly with CAMeL Tools.\n\n---\n\n### SinaTools\n\n**Strong recent contender with high accuracy on lemmatization and POS tagging.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/SinaLab/sinatools\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** MIT\n- **Stars:** ~31\n- **Install:** `pip install sinatools` (requires Python 3.11.11)\n- **Status:** Active (published at ACLING 2024)\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Lemmatization (90.5% accuracy)\n- POS tagging (93.8% accuracy)\n- Root tagging\n- Named entity recognition\n- Corpus processing\n- Diacritic-aware word matching\n- Text stripping methods\n\n**Quality:** Claims to outperform all similar tools on lemmatization and POS tagging benchmarks. Processing speed: 33K tokens/sec. Published evaluation shows strong results.\n\n**Related tool -- Alma:** The lemmatizer/POS tagger component of SinaTools, also available separately at https://sina.birzeit.edu/alma/. Achieved 88% F1 on LDC Arabic Treebank and 90% F1 on Salma corpus. Outperformed Farasa, MADAMIRA, and Camelira in both lemmatization and POS tagging.\n\n---\n\n### Farasa\n\n**Fast and accurate Arabic NLP toolkit from QCRI.**\n\n- **URL:** https://farasa.qcri.org/\n- **GitHub (Python wrapper):** https://github.com/MagedSaeed/farasapy\n- **Alternative wrapper:** https://github.com/OpenITI/pyFarasa\n- **Language:** Java (core), Python wrapper available\n- **License:** MIT (Python wrapper); **research use only** (core Java toolkit)\n- **Stars:** ~139 (farasapy)\n- **Install:** `pip install farasapy` (requires Java installed)\n- **Status:** Maintained, but core toolkit license restricts commercial use\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Word segmentation (98%+ accuracy)\n- Stemming\n- Lemmatization\n- POS tagging\n- Diacritization\n- Named entity recognition\n- Dependency parsing\n- Spell checking\n\n**Quality:** Segmentation accuracy on par with or better than MADAMIRA and Stanford. Processes 1 billion words in < 5 hours. However, lemmatization accuracy is lower than Alma/SinaTools in benchmarks (e.g., 0.59 accuracy on Nafis dataset). Very fast but sacrifices some accuracy for speed.\n\n**Important caveat:** The core Farasa binaries are **restricted to research/academic use**. The Python wrapper is MIT but wraps proprietary Java JARs. Not suitable for commercial applications without permission from QCRI.\n\n---\n\n### Qalsadi\n\n**Rule-based Arabic morphological analyzer and lemmatizer ecosystem.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/linuxscout/qalsadi\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** GPL-2.0+\n- **Stars:** ~42\n- **Latest Version:** 0.5.1 (July 2025)\n- **Install:** `pip install qalsadi`\n- **Status:** Actively maintained by Taha Zerrouki\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Full morphological analysis (vocalized and unvocalized text)\n- Lemmatization\n- Word frequency data for modern Arabic\n- Verb conjugation analysis (via Qutrub integration)\n- Multiple output formats (table, CSV, JSON, XML)\n- Caching support (memory, pickle, pickledb, CodernityDB)\n\n**Quality:** Production/stable status. Rule-based approach means consistent behavior. Part of a larger ecosystem of tools by the same author (PyArabic, Tashaphyne, Qutrub, Mishkal, Arramooz).\n\n---\n\n### Stanza (Stanford NLP)\n\n**General-purpose multilingual NLP toolkit with Arabic support.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/stanfordnlp/stanza\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** Apache 2.0\n- **Stars:** ~7,700\n- **Latest Version:** v1.11.0 (October 2025)\n- **Install:** `pip install stanza`\n- **Status:** Very actively maintained\n\n**Capabilities for Arabic:**\n- Tokenization and sentence segmentation\n- Lemmatization\n- POS tagging\n- Morphological feature tagging\n- Dependency parsing\n- Named entity recognition\n- Multi-word token expansion\n\n**Quality:** Neural pipeline trained on Universal Dependencies Arabic-PADT treebank. Good general accuracy but not specialized for Arabic morphology. Does not provide root extraction or detailed morphological decomposition. Best used when you need a multilingual pipeline rather than deep Arabic-specific analysis.\n\n---\n\n### SAFAR Framework\n\n**Large-scale Java-based Arabic NLP platform.**\n\n- **URL:** http://arabic.emi.ac.ma/safar/\n- **Language:** Java\n- **License:** Open source (specific license varies by component)\n- **Status:** Academic project, 50+ integrated tools\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Morphological analysis (wraps Alkhalil, BAMA, MADAMIRA)\n- Stemming (multiple algorithms)\n- Lemmatization\n- Morphological generation\n- Syntactic analysis\n- Semantic analysis\n\n**Quality:** More of a framework/platform than a single tool. Integrates many third-party analyzers behind a unified Java API. Useful for researchers who want to compare different approaches.\n\n---\n\n### MADAMIRA\n\n**The classic reference tool, now largely superseded by CAMeL Tools.**\n\n- **URL:** Previously distributed via Columbia University\n- **Language:** Java\n- **License:** Restricted (academic license required)\n- **Status:** No longer actively developed; superseded by CAMeL Tools\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Morphological analysis and disambiguation\n- Lemmatization\n- POS tagging\n- Diacritization\n- Tokenization\n- NER\n- Base-phrase chunking\n\n**Quality:** Was the gold standard for years. Combined MADA + AMIRA systems. Still cited in many papers but CAMeL Tools is the recommended successor.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Root Extraction\n\n### Tashaphyne\n\n**Light stemmer that uniquely provides both stemming and root extraction simultaneously.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/linuxscout/tashaphyne\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** GPL-3.0\n- **Stars:** ~103\n- **Install:** `pip install tashaphyne`\n- **Status:** Maintained by Taha Zerrouki\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Light stemming + root extraction in a single pass\n- Word segmentation (all possible decompositions)\n- Word normalization\n- Customizable affix lists\n- Data-independent (no external dictionary required)\n- Finite state automaton-based\n\n**Quality:** Tashaphyne 0.4 outperformed Khoja, ISRI, Motaz/Light10, Farasa, and Assem stemmers in comparative studies. Distinctive in providing root extraction alongside stemming, which most light stemmers do not do.\n\n---\n\n### Qutuf\n\n**Expert-system-based morphological analyzer with root extraction.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/Qutuf/Qutuf\n- **Language:** Python (65%), HTML (35%)\n- **License:** Not specified\n- **Stars:** ~132\n- **Latest Release:** Qutuf 2.0 (June 2019)\n- **Status:** Not recently updated (last release 2019)\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Root extraction (heavy stemming)\n- Light stemming\n- POS tagging (rule-based expert system)\n- Cliticization parsing via finite state automata\n- Uses AlKhalil Morpho Sys database\n- JSON, XML, HTML output formats\n\n**Quality:** Well-documented academic project. The expert-system approach provides explainable results. However, has not been updated since 2019.\n\n---\n\n### CAMeL Tools (Morphological Analyzer)\n\nCAMeL Tools' morphological analyzer provides root information as part of its full morphological analysis output. See [CAMeL Tools](#camel-tools) above.\n\n---\n\n### Buckwalter Arabic Morphological Analyzer (BAMA)\n\n**The foundational analyzer that many modern tools build upon.**\n\n- **URL (v1):** https://catalog.ldc.upenn.edu/LDC2002L49\n- **URL (v2):** https://catalog.ldc.upenn.edu/LDC2004L02\n- **Open-source reimplementation:** https://sourceforge.net/projects/aramorpher/\n- **Language:** Perl (original), C++ (Aramorpher)\n- **License:** GPL v2 (v1.0); LDC membership required (v2.0)\n- **Status:** Historical/foundational -- not actively developed\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Morphological analysis producing root, pattern, POS, gloss\n- Concatenative morphology decomposition\n\n**Quality:** The original reference standard. Version 1.0 is freely available under GPL. All modern tools (SAMA, CALIMA, CAMeL Tools) descend from this work. The Aramorpher reimplementation on SourceForge provides a C++ interface.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Morphological Analysis\n\n### AlKhalil Morpho Sys 2\n\n**High-coverage Arabic morphosyntactic analyzer.**\n\n- **URL:** https://sourceforge.net/projects/alkhalil/\n- **Language:** Java\n- **License:** Open source (via SourceForge)\n- **Status:** Available, academic project\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Processes vocalized, partially vocalized, and unvocalized text\n- Provides all possible vowelized forms\n- Outputs: vocalization, grammatical category, roots, patterns, proclitics, enclitics\n- Root database + vocalized patterns + clitic tables\n\n**Quality:** Won a competition for Arabic morphological systems in 2010. Version 2 exceeds 99% word coverage. Database used by Qutuf and other tools.\n\n---\n\n### AraComLex\n\n**Finite-state morphological transducer for MSA.**\n\n- **URL:** https://sourceforge.net/projects/aracomlex/ and https://github.com/mohammedattia/AraComLex\n- **Language:** XFST / foma (finite state)\n- **License:** GPL v3\n- **Status:** Academic project, version 2.0 available\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Full morphological analysis via finite state transducers\n- Generation from morphological specifications\n- Corpus-based: tuned to contemporary MSA usage\n- Generated a 9-million-word spell-checking dictionary\n\n**Quality:** Built from a corpus of 1+ billion words. Eliminates archaic Classical Arabic entries not used in modern MSA. Requires foma or Xerox xfst compiler.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Lemmatization\n\n### Alma\n\n**Currently the fastest and most accurate open-source Arabic lemmatizer.**\n\n- **URL:** https://sina.birzeit.edu/alma/\n- **Language:** Python (part of SinaTools)\n- **License:** MIT (as part of SinaTools)\n- **Status:** Active (2024)\n\n**Performance:**\n- 88% F1 on LDC Arabic Treebank (339K tokens)\n- 90% F1 on Salma corpus (34K tokens)\n- POS accuracy: 93.8%\n- Speed: 32K tokens/sec (339K tokens in 10 seconds)\n- Outperforms Farasa, MADAMIRA, and Camelira\n\n**Distinctive feature:** Returns unambiguous (fully diacritized) lemmas, unlike many tools that strip diacritics. Uses Qabas lexicographic database with frequency-ordered morphological solutions.\n\n---\n\n### CAMeL Tools Lemmatizer\n\nUses CALIMA Star and CamelMorph databases. See [CAMeL Tools](#camel-tools).\n\n---\n\n### Farasa Lemmatizer\n\nAvailable via farasapy. See [Farasa](#farasa). Reported 97.32% accuracy in its own benchmarks, though independent evaluations show lower numbers.\n\n---\n\n### Stanza Arabic Lemmatizer\n\nNeural lemmatizer trained on UD treebanks. See [Stanza](#stanza-stanford-nlp).\n\n---\n\n## 5. Conjugation Generation\n\n### Qutrub\n\n**The primary open-source Arabic verb conjugator.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/linuxscout/qutrub\n- **PyPI library:** `pip install libqutrub`\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** GPL\n- **Stars:** ~98\n- **Status:** Maintained by Taha Zerrouki\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Conjugates Arabic verbs across all tenses: past, present, future, imperative\n- Active and passive voice\n- Subjunctive and jussive moods\n- Confirmed (energetic) forms\n- All persons and numbers (14 pronoun forms)\n- Transitive / intransitive distinction\n- Six primary conjugation modes (Bab Tasrif) using diacritical patterns\n- Multiple output formats: HTML, CSV, TeX, XML, JSON, GUI tables\n\n**Quality:** The most established open-source Arabic verb conjugator. Generic approach (does not rely on classified verb models), which means it works with any verb but may not handle every irregular case perfectly. Web demo available at https://tahadz.com/qutrub.\n\n**Limitations:** Does not explicitly enumerate Forms I-X in the way a classical grammar textbook does. It conjugates based on the vocalized verb input rather than root + form number.\n\n---\n\n### arabic-conjugation (robaleman)\n\n**Programmatic verb form generation from triliteral roots.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/robaleman/arabic-conjugation\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** Not specified\n- **Status:** Small project, limited maintenance\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Generate conjugations from a root (e.g., \"k-t-b\") + form number\n- Supports Forms I-XV\n- All tenses, persons, genders, numbers, moods, voices\n- `generate()` for specific forms, `generate_all()` for complete paradigm\n\n**Limitations:** Currently only supports \"sound\" roots (no glottal stops or semi-vowels). Form I requires manual stem vowel specification. Small project without wide adoption.\n\n---\n\n### CamelMorph Generator\n\nCAMeL Tools' morphological generator can produce word forms from feature specifications. See [CamelMorph](#camelmorph-companion-to-camel-tools).\n\n---\n\n### ACON (Arabic Conjugator)\n\n- **URL:** http://acon.baykal.be/\n- **Status:** Web-only tool, not a downloadable library\n- **Quality:** Automated conjugator applying Arabic grammar rules. Useful as a reference but not embeddable.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Stemming\n\n### ISRI Stemmer (via NLTK)\n\n**The most widely used Arabic stemmer, included in NLTK.**\n\n- **URL:** https://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.stem.isri.html\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** Apache 2.0 (NLTK)\n- **Install:** `pip install nltk` then `from nltk.stem.isri import ISRIStemmer`\n- **Status:** Stable, maintained as part of NLTK\n\n**Approach:** Does NOT use a root dictionary. Falls back to normalization if root cannot be found.\n\n**Quality:** Good for information retrieval. Performs better than Khoja on shorter title queries. Widely used due to NLTK integration. Light/aggressive stemming without dictionary lookup means some errors on irregular words.\n\n```python\nfrom nltk.stem.isri import ISRIStemmer\nstemmer = ISRIStemmer()\nstemmer.stem(\"يكتبون\") # -> كتب\n```\n\n---\n\n### Khoja Stemmer\n\n**Classical root-based Arabic stemmer.**\n\n- **Language:** Java (original), Python ports available\n- **License:** Open source\n- **Status:** Historical -- original implementation from early 2000s\n\n**Approach:** Uses a root dictionary. Aggressive stemming (85% reduction rate).\n\n**Quality:** High reduction rate (85-90.9% with Lucene variant) but can be too aggressive. Errors on some word types. The basis for many subsequent stemmers.\n\n---\n\n### Tashaphyne\n\nSee [Tashaphyne under Root Extraction](#tashaphyne). Provides light stemming + root extraction. Outperforms Khoja, ISRI, Farasa, and Assem stemmers in comparative studies.\n\n---\n\n### Apache Lucene Arabic Analyzer\n\n**Light stemmer built into Lucene/Solr/Elasticsearch.**\n\n- **URL:** Built into Apache Lucene\n- **Language:** Java\n- **License:** Apache 2.0\n- **Status:** Actively maintained (part of Lucene)\n\n**Approach:** Light stemming based on \"Light Stemming for Arabic Information Retrieval\" by Larkey et al. Removes common prefixes/suffixes but does NOT extract roots.\n\n**Third-party root-based extension:** https://github.com/msarhan/lucene-arabic-analyzer (uses AlKhalil database for root extraction, Apache 2.0 license)\n\n**Quality:** Well-tested for IR use cases. The built-in light stemmer is conservative and safe. The root-based extension provides deeper analysis.\n\n---\n\n### Light10 / Motaz Light Stemmer\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/motazsaad/arabic-light-stemming-py (Python), also https://github.com/motazsaad/arabic-light-stemmer (command line)\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** Open source\n- **Status:** Available but not frequently updated\n\n**Quality:** A well-known light stemming algorithm. Removes definite articles, conjunctions, and common suffixes while maintaining minimum word length.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Supporting Libraries & Resources\n\n### PyArabic\n\n**Foundational Python library for Arabic text manipulation.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/linuxscout/pyarabic\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** GPL-3.0\n- **Stars:** ~475\n- **Install:** `pip install pyarabic`\n- **Status:** Maintained by Taha Zerrouki\n\n**Capabilities:**\n- Arabic letter classification and detection\n- Text tokenization\n- Diacritics removal (all, except Shadda, tatweel, last_haraka)\n- Letter normalization (ligatures, hamza variants)\n- Number-to-word conversion\n- Numerical phrase extraction\n\n**Note:** This is NOT a morphological analyzer. It provides the text-processing primitives that tools like Qalsadi and Mishkal build upon.\n\n---\n\n### Arramooz\n\n**Open-source Arabic dictionary for morphological analysis.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/linuxscout/arramooz\n- **Language:** SQLite database + Python\n- **License:** GPL-2.0\n- **Stars:** ~147\n- **Status:** Maintained by Taha Zerrouki\n\n**Contents:**\n- Stop words database\n- Verb database (with conjugation patterns)\n- Noun database\n- Generated from manually collected data (Ayaspell spellchecker)\n\nUsed by Qalsadi, Mishkal, and other tools in the linuxscout ecosystem.\n\n---\n\n### Mishkal (Arabic Diacritizer)\n\n**Rule-based Arabic text vocalization using morphological analysis.**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/linuxscout/mishkal\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** GPL-3.0\n- **Stars:** ~302\n- **Install:** `pip install mishkal`\n- **Status:** Maintained by Taha Zerrouki\n\nUses Qalsadi for morphological analysis, Arramooz dictionary, ArAnaSyn for syntax, and Asmai for semantics to determine correct diacritization. Relevant here because it demonstrates the full morphological analysis pipeline in action.\n\n---\n\n### arabic-reshaper\n\n**Text rendering library (NOT morphological analysis).**\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/mpcabd/python-arabic-reshaper\n- **Language:** Python\n- **License:** MIT\n- **Install:** `pip install arabic-reshaper`\n\nReshapes Arabic characters for display in applications that lack Arabic support. Handles RTL text, ligatures, and diacritics. Not related to morphological analysis but commonly encountered in Arabic NLP tool lists.\n\n---\n\n## 8. Transformer-Based Models\n\n### AraBERT\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/aub-mind/arabert\n- **Hugging Face:** https://huggingface.co/aubmindlab/bert-base-arabert\n- **License:** Apache 2.0\n- **Status:** Active\n\nPre-trained BERT model for Arabic. Can be fine-tuned for morphological tasks. AraBERT v1 & v2 use Farasa segmentation as preprocessing. Useful as a base model for building custom morphological classifiers.\n\n---\n\n### CAMeLBERT\n\n- **URL:** https://huggingface.co/CAMeL-Lab/bert-base-arabic-camelbert-da\n- **License:** Apache 2.0\n- **Status:** Active\n\nBERT models from the CAMeL Lab pre-trained on different Arabic varieties (MSA, dialectal, classical). Can be fine-tuned for morphological disambiguation tasks.\n\n---\n\n### AraT5\n\n- **URL:** https://github.com/UBC-NLP/araT5\n- **Hugging Face:** https://huggingface.co/UBC-NLP/AraT5-base\n- **License:** Apache 2.0\n- **Status:** Active\n\nText-to-text transformer for Arabic. Can be fine-tuned for lemmatization as a sequence-to-sequence task. Recent research shows promising results using AraT5 for transformer-based lemmatization.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Quality & Accuracy Comparisons\n\n### Lemmatization Accuracy\n\n| Tool | Dataset | Metric | Score |\n|------|---------|--------|-------|\n| Alma/SinaTools | LDC ATB (339K tokens) | F1 | 88% |\n| Alma/SinaTools | Salma corpus (34K tokens) | F1 | 90% |\n| Farasa | Self-reported | Accuracy | 97.3% |\n| MADAMIRA | Various | -- | Reference standard |\n| CAMeL Tools | Various | -- | On par with MADAMIRA |\n\nNote: Direct comparisons are difficult due to inconsistent task definitions and lemma representation (especially regarding diacritization).\n\n### Segmentation Accuracy\n\n| Tool | Speed | Quality |\n|------|-------|---------|\n| Farasa | ~200M words/hour | 98%+ segmentation accuracy |\n| MADAMIRA | ~2M words/hour | 98%+ segmentation accuracy |\n| CAMeL Tools | Moderate | Competitive with MADAMIRA |\n\n### Stemming Comparison\n\n| Stemmer | Reduction Rate | Root Dictionary | Quality Notes |\n|---------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|\n| Khoja | 85-91% | Yes | Too aggressive for some tasks |\n| ISRI (NLTK) | ~80% | No | Good for IR, no dictionary needed |\n| Tashaphyne 0.4 | ~75% | No | Best in comparative study vs 6 others |\n| Lucene Light | ~60% | No | Conservative, safe for IR |\n| Farasa | ~70% | No | Fast but less accurate |\n\n### Morphological Analysis Coverage\n\n| Tool | Coverage | Vocabulary |\n|------|----------|-----------|\n| CamelMorph MSA | 99%+ | 100K+ lemmas, 1.45B analyses |\n| AlKhalil 2 | 99%+ | Large rule-based database |\n| SAMA | ~95% | 40K+ lemmas |\n| Buckwalter v2 | ~90% | Historical reference |\n\n---\n\n## 10. Summary Recommendation Matrix\n\n### For a new project in 2026, which tool should you use?\n\n| Need | Recommended Tool | Why |\n|------|-----------------|-----|\n| **Full morphological analysis (MSA)** | CAMeL Tools | Most comprehensive, actively maintained, MIT license, largest database |\n| **Fast lemmatization** | SinaTools / Alma | Best accuracy/speed tradeoff, MIT license |\n| **Root extraction** | CAMeL Tools or Tashaphyne | CAMeL for accuracy, Tashaphyne for lightweight/no-data approach |\n| **Verb conjugation generation** | Qutrub (libqutrub) | Only mature open-source conjugator, GPL |\n| **Stemming for IR** | ISRI (NLTK) or Tashaphyne | ISRI for simplicity, Tashaphyne for better quality |\n| **Quick integration (any language)** | Stanza | Apache 2.0, multilingual, easy setup |\n| **Commercial use** | CAMeL Tools (MIT) or SinaTools (MIT) | Avoid Farasa (research only) and GPL tools |\n| **Java projects** | AlKhalil Morpho Sys 2 | Best open-source Java morphological analyzer |\n| **Morphological generation** | CamelMorph + CAMeL Tools | Only tool generating 1.45B+ word forms |\n| **Lightweight, no external data** | Tashaphyne | No dictionary required, customizable affixes |\n\n### The Taha Zerrouki (linuxscout) Ecosystem\n\nTaha Zerrouki has built an interconnected set of tools that work together. If you use one, you benefit from the whole ecosystem:\n\n- **PyArabic** -- text primitives (GPL-3.0)\n- **Tashaphyne** -- stemming + root extraction (GPL-3.0)\n- **Arramooz** -- morphological dictionary (GPL-2.0)\n- **Qalsadi** -- morphological analyzer (GPL-2.0+)\n- **Qutrub / libqutrub** -- verb conjugation (GPL)\n- **Mishkal** -- diacritization (GPL-3.0)\n\nAll are GPL-licensed, which is a consideration for commercial projects.\n\n### Key Observations\n\n1. **CAMeL Tools is the clear leader** for comprehensive Arabic morphological analysis in Python. It is well-funded (NYU Abu Dhabi), actively maintained, MIT-licensed, and has the largest morphological database available.\n\n2. **SinaTools/Alma is the accuracy leader** for lemmatization specifically, with strong published benchmarks.\n\n3. **Farasa is fast but restrictive** -- the core toolkit is limited to research use, which rules it out for commercial applications.\n\n4. **The linuxscout ecosystem** provides the most tools but everything is GPL, which limits commercial use.\n\n5. **Verb conjugation generation is underserved** -- Qutrub is the only mature option, and it does not explicitly support the traditional Form I-X paradigm by form number. The robaleman/arabic-conjugation project does support form numbers but only handles sound roots.\n\n6. **Root extraction as a standalone feature is rare** -- most tools provide it as part of broader morphological analysis. Tashaphyne is the best standalone option.\n\n7. **For production systems**, the combination of **CAMeL Tools** (analysis, lemmatization, generation) + **Qutrub** (conjugation) provides the most complete coverage.\n\n---\n\n## Appendix: All Tools at a Glance\n\n| Tool | Language | License | Last Active | GitHub Stars | Primary Use |\n|------|----------|---------|-------------|-------------|-------------|\n| [CAMeL Tools](https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/camel_tools) | Python | MIT | 2025 | 522 | Full NLP toolkit |\n| [CamelMorph](https://github.com/CAMeL-Lab/camel_morph) | Python | MIT / CC-BY 4.0 | 2024 | 15 | Morphological DB |\n| [SinaTools](https://github.com/SinaLab/sinatools) | Python | MIT | 2024 | 31 | Lemmatization, POS |\n| [Farasa](https://farasa.qcri.org/) / [farasapy](https://github.com/MagedSaeed/farasapy) | Java/Python | Research only / MIT | 2024 | 139 | Segmentation, NLP |\n| [Qalsadi](https://github.com/linuxscout/qalsadi) | Python | GPL-2.0+ | 2025 | 42 | Morphological analysis |\n| [Tashaphyne](https://github.com/linuxscout/tashaphyne) | Python | GPL-3.0 | Active | 103 | Stemming, root extraction |\n| [Qutrub](https://github.com/linuxscout/qutrub) | Python | GPL | Active | 98 | Verb conjugation |\n| [PyArabic](https://github.com/linuxscout/pyarabic) | Python | GPL-3.0 | 2023 | 475 | Text primitives |\n| [Arramooz](https://github.com/linuxscout/arramooz) | Python/SQL | GPL-2.0 | Active | 147 | Morphological dictionary |\n| [Mishkal](https://github.com/linuxscout/mishkal) | Python | GPL-3.0 | Active | 302 | Diacritization |\n| [Qutuf](https://github.com/Qutuf/Qutuf) | Python | Unspecified | 2019 | 132 | Morphological analysis |\n| [Stanza](https://github.com/stanfordnlp/stanza) | Python | Apache 2.0 | 2025 | 7,700 | Multilingual NLP |\n| [NLTK ISRIStemmer](https://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.stem.isri.html) | Python | Apache 2.0 | Stable | -- | Stemming |\n| [AlKhalil Morpho Sys](https://sourceforge.net/projects/alkhalil/) | Java | Open source | Stable | -- | Morphological analysis |\n| [AraComLex](https://github.com/mohammedattia/AraComLex) | XFST/foma | GPL-3.0 | Stable | -- | FST morphology |\n| [AraNLP](https://github.com/Maha-J-Althobaiti/AraNLP) | Java | Open source | 2014 | -- | Arabic text processing |\n| [SAFAR](http://arabic.emi.ac.ma/safar/) | Java | Open source | Active | -- | NLP framework |\n| [Buckwalter/Aramorpher](https://sourceforge.net/projects/aramorpher/) | Perl/C++ | GPL-2.0 | Historical | -- | Morphological analysis |\n| [Lucene Arabic](https://lucene.apache.org/) | Java | Apache 2.0 | Active | -- | IR stemming |\n| [arabic-conjugation](https://github.com/robaleman/arabic-conjugation) | Python | Unspecified | Small project | -- | Verb conjugation |\n| [AraBERT](https://github.com/aub-mind/arabert) | Python | Apache 2.0 | Active | -- | Transformer model |\n| [AraT5](https://github.com/UBC-NLP/araT5) | Python | Apache 2.0 | Active | -- | Transformer model |\n| [Stanford CoreNLP](https://github.com/stanfordnlp/CoreNLP) | Java | GPL-2.0 | Active | -- | NLP toolkit |\n", "date": null, "category": "nlp", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/arabic-morphology-tools.md"}, "arabic-sentence-corpora-2026-02-21": {"title": "Arabic Sentence Corpora for Language Learning", "content": "# Arabic Sentence Corpora for Language Learning\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-21\n**Purpose**: Survey of large-scale Arabic corpora that could supply pre-made sentences for Alif, reducing dependence on LLM generation.\n\n---\n\n## 1. Free/Open Arabic Corpora (100k+ sentences)\n\n### 1.1 OSIAN (Open Source International Arabic News Corpus)\n\n- **Size**: ~3.5 million articles, 37+ million sentences, ~1 billion tokens\n- **Source**: International Arabic news websites (crawled from 22 Arabic countries)\n- **License**: CC BY-NC 4.0 (non-commercial)\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: No (monolingual Arabic)\n- **Text quality**: Formal MSA news text\n- **NLP research**: Yes, integrated into CLARIN infrastructure\n- **Download**: Via [Leipzig Corpora Collection](https://wortschatz.uni-leipzig.de/en/download/) (subset: 3.3M sentences for Tunisia alone) and [CLARIN](https://aclanthology.org/W19-4619/)\n- **Format**: XML with metadata per article\n- **Alif relevance**: HIGH. Enormous MSA sentence pool. News text is formal but grammatically clean. No diacritics is a downside but we can add via auto-tashkeel models. CC BY-NC is fine for personal use.\n\n### 1.2 Abu El-Khair Corpus\n\n- **Size**: 5+ million newspaper articles, 1.5 billion words, ~3 million unique words\n- **Source**: Arabic newspapers spanning 14 years (pre-2016)\n- **License**: Research use (check terms)\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Text quality**: Formal MSA news\n- **NLP research**: Yes, widely cited (arXiv:1611.04033)\n- **Download**: [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/abuelkhair-corpus/arabic_billion_words) and [author's site](http://www.abuelkhair.net/)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM. Massive but news-heavy. Sentences tend to be long and complex. Would need heavy filtering for learner-appropriate content.\n\n### 1.3 Arabic Wikipedia\n\n- **Size**: ~4.4 million sentences (2021 dump), ~75M tokens. Raw dump ~800MB compressed\n- **Source**: Arabic Wikipedia articles\n- **License**: CC BY-SA 3.0 (free)\n- **Diacritics**: Occasional (inconsistent)\n- **Translations**: No (but parallel via WikiMatrix, see below)\n- **Text quality**: Formal MSA, encyclopedic. Quality varies; some articles are machine-translated stubs\n- **NLP research**: Extensively used\n- **Download**: [Wikimedia dumps](https://dumps.wikimedia.org/arwiki/) + extraction via [WikiExtractor](https://github.com/attardi/wikiextractor) or Gensim. Pre-extracted on [Leipzig Corpora Collection](https://corpora.uni-leipzig.de/en?corpusId=ara_wikipedia_2021)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM. Good vocabulary coverage but encyclopedic style is dry for learners. Best filtered for shorter, simpler sentences.\n\n### 1.4 WikiMatrix (Arabic-English parallel from Wikipedia)\n\n- **Size**: Part of 135M mined parallel sentences across 1620 language pairs. Arabic-English pair likely millions of sentences.\n- **Source**: Automatically mined from Arabic + English Wikipedia using LASER embeddings\n- **License**: CC BY-SA (Wikipedia license)\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: YES (English parallel)\n- **Text quality**: Mixed quality. Top-scored pairs are good; bottom pairs are noisy. Quality degrades rapidly below margin threshold ~1.04\n- **NLP research**: Yes (Facebook/Meta FAIR, EACL 2021)\n- **Download**: [GitHub LASER repo](https://github.com/facebookresearch/LASER/blob/main/tasks/WikiMatrix/README.md)\n- **Alif relevance**: HIGH for translated pairs. We could filter by margin score (>1.06 for high quality) to get reliable Arabic-English sentence pairs. Would need quality filtering.\n\n### 1.5 UN Parallel Corpus v1.0\n\n- **Size**: ~20 million sentence pairs per language pair. Arabic-English: ~20M aligned sentences\n- **Source**: Official UN documents (1990-2014), manually translated\n- **License**: Free / public domain (UN documents)\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: YES (English, plus French, Spanish, Russian, Chinese)\n- **Text quality**: Formal MSA, UN/diplomatic register. Very formal, often legalistic\n- **NLP research**: Yes, standard MT benchmark\n- **Download**: [UN DGACM](https://www.un.org/dgacm/en/content/uncorpus/download) — plain-text bitexts available\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW for direct use. UN language is too formal/specialized for a language learner. Could extract simple sentences but the register is wrong. Translation quality is excellent though.\n\n### 1.6 OpenSubtitles (via OPUS)\n\n- **Size**: Arabic is one of 67 languages. Estimated several million Arabic sentences (exact count unavailable from search; total corpus is 3.35 billion sentence fragments across all languages)\n- **Source**: Movie and TV show subtitles from opensubtitles.org\n- **License**: Free for research (subtitles community-contributed)\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: YES (parallel with English and many other languages via subtitle alignment)\n- **Text quality**: MIXED — some MSA, much dialectal Arabic (Egyptian, Gulf, Levantine). Colloquial, conversational. Short sentences typical of subtitles.\n- **NLP research**: Extensively used\n- **Download**: [OPUS OpenSubtitles v2024](https://opus.nlpl.eu/OpenSubtitles/corpus/version/OpenSubtitles)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM. Short conversational sentences are great for learners, BUT heavy dialect mix is problematic for MSA-focused app. Would need dialect filtering. Translations available are a plus.\n\n### 1.7 Tanzil / Quranic Arabic Corpus\n\n- **Size**: 6,236 verses (Quran), ~77,000 words. The Quranic Arabic Corpus adds morphological annotation for every word.\n- **Source**: Quran text\n- **License**: Free (Tanzil: no restrictions for personal/research use)\n- **Diacritics**: YES (fully vowelized)\n- **Translations**: YES (multiple English translations available)\n- **Text quality**: Classical Arabic, not MSA. Highly literary/religious register\n- **NLP research**: Yes (morphological treebank, semantic ontology)\n- **Download**: [Tanzil](https://tanzil.net/download/) (multiple formats: UTF-8 text, XML), [Quranic Arabic Corpus](https://corpus.quran.com/download/)\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW for direct sentence use (Classical Arabic, not MSA). However, the fully diacritized text with morphological analysis could be useful for roots/patterns research. Many MSA learners are motivated by Quranic literacy, so could be a feature later.\n\n### 1.8 Tashkeela Corpus\n\n- **Size**: 75 million fully vocalized words from 97 books\n- **Source**: Shamila Library (98.85%) + MSA texts (1.15%, ~868K words)\n- **License**: Free / open source\n- **Diacritics**: YES (fully vowelized — this is the main feature)\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Text quality**: Mostly Classical Arabic (Islamic texts from Shamila). Small MSA portion.\n- **NLP research**: Yes, standard benchmark for auto-diacritization\n- **Download**: [Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/linuxscout/tashkeela), [SourceForge](https://sourceforge.net/projects/tashkeela/)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM-HIGH for diacritics. Even though content is mostly classical, the diacritized text is valuable for training/validating our tashkeel models. The MSA portion (~868K words) could yield diacritized sentences directly.\n\n### 1.9 Tatoeba\n\n- **Size**: ~67,464 Arabic sentences (as of 2026)\n- **Source**: Community-contributed example sentences\n- **License**: CC BY 2.0 FR\n- **Diacritics**: Some (inconsistent, contributor-dependent)\n- **Translations**: YES (many translated to English and other languages)\n- **Text quality**: Mixed MSA/dialect, generally simple and conversational. Community quality varies.\n- **NLP research**: Used by Helsinki-NLP Tatoeba Challenge\n- **Download**: [Tatoeba downloads](https://tatoeba.org/en/downloads) (TSV format, bulk download available)\n- **Alif relevance**: HIGH. These are exactly the kind of sentences we need: short, example-style, with translations. 67K is a solid base. Already used by Clozemaster. Main concern: quality is inconsistent, some sentences are dialectal, diacritics are spotty.\n\n### 1.10 KSUCCA (King Saud University Corpus of Classical Arabic)\n\n- **Size**: 50 million tokens\n- **Source**: Classical Arabic texts (pre-Islamic era to 4th Hijri century)\n- **License**: Free (downloadable from SourceForge)\n- **Diacritics**: Partial (classical texts often have diacritics)\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Text quality**: Classical Arabic only\n- **NLP research**: Yes, includes POS-tagged annotated version\n- **Download**: [SourceForge](https://sourceforge.net/projects/ksucca-corpus/)\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW. Classical Arabic is too far from MSA for our learner use case.\n\n### 1.11 Arabic Gigaword (LDC)\n\n- **Size**: Fifth edition has ~4 billion words from 9 Arabic news sources\n- **Source**: Arabic newswire (AFP, Al-Ahram, Al-Hayat, etc.)\n- **License**: LDC membership required ($$$). NOT free.\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Text quality**: Formal MSA news\n- **NLP research**: Standard NLP resource\n- **Download**: [LDC catalog](https://catalog.ldc.upenn.edu/LDC2011T11)\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW (paywall). Same domain as OSIAN/Abu El-Khair which are free.\n\n### 1.12 Penn Arabic Treebank (PATB)\n\n- **Size**: ~19,738 sentences, ~739K tokens\n- **Source**: AFP newswire\n- **License**: LDC membership required\n- **Diacritics**: YES (vocalized verb forms in recent versions; partial overall)\n- **Translations**: English glosses per word\n- **Text quality**: Formal MSA news, fully parsed (POS, morphology, syntax trees)\n- **NLP research**: Foundational resource for Arabic NLP\n- **Download**: [LDC](https://catalog.ldc.upenn.edu/LDC2010T13) (not free)\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW for sentence mining (too small, behind paywall). But the morphological annotations are gold-standard quality.\n\n### 1.13 CCMatrix / CCAligned\n\n- **Size**: CCMatrix: 4.5 billion parallel sentences total (661M aligned with English). Arabic-English pair likely tens of millions. CCAligned: 392M URL pairs across 137 languages.\n- **Source**: Automatically mined from Common Crawl web data\n- **License**: Free for research\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: YES (parallel English)\n- **Text quality**: Very mixed (web-crawled). Needs heavy filtering. Quality varies enormously by margin score.\n- **NLP research**: Yes (Facebook FAIR)\n- **Download**: [CCMatrix on OPUS](https://opus.nlpl.eu/), [CCAligned](https://www.statmt.org/cc-aligned/)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM. Enormous volume but quality is the concern. Would need aggressive filtering by margin score and sentence length. Could yield millions of usable Arabic-English pairs after filtering.\n\n### 1.14 AMARA Corpus (TED Talk Subtitles)\n\n- **Size**: Arabic-English: ~2.6M Arabic words, ~3.9M English words\n- **Source**: Community-translated TED talk subtitles\n- **License**: Free for research\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: YES (English + 19 other languages)\n- **Text quality**: Spoken MSA/semi-formal. Educational content. Good register for learners.\n- **NLP research**: Yes (IWSLT 2013)\n- **Download**: Via OPUS or [research paper](https://aclanthology.org/2013.iwslt-papers.2/)\n- **Alif relevance**: HIGH. Educational content with natural conversational MSA. TED talks cover diverse topics at accessible level. Translations available. Sentence quality should be good (human-translated subtitles).\n\n### 1.15 Leipzig Corpora Collection (Arabic)\n\n- **Size**: Multiple Arabic corpora available. Wikipedia 2021: 4.4M sentences. News corpora: up to 3.3M sentences each. Available in 10K, 30K, 100K, 300K, 1M sentence packs.\n- **Source**: Wikipedia dumps + news crawls\n- **License**: Free for research\n- **Diacritics**: No\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Text quality**: Clean MSA (pre-processed, sentence-split)\n- **NLP research**: Yes\n- **Download**: [Leipzig Wortschatz](https://wortschatz.uni-leipzig.de/en/download/) — look under language selection. Also [Corpora portal](https://corpora.uni-leipzig.de)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM-HIGH. Pre-processed into clean sentences with co-occurrence statistics. Good starting point for sentence mining. The 1M-sentence packs are convenient.\n\n### 1.16 ANT Corpus (Arabic News Texts)\n\n- **Size**: Large-scale Arabic news corpus\n- **Source**: Arabic news websites\n- **License**: Free\n- **Download**: [antcorpus.github.io](https://antcorpus.github.io/)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM. Another news corpus; useful as supplement.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Graded/Educational Arabic Text Collections\n\n### 2.1 Hindawi Foundation / Arabic E-Book Corpus\n\n- **Size**: 1,745 books, 81.5 million words\n- **Source**: Hindawi Foundation publications (2008-2024)\n- **Genres**: Fiction, non-fiction, children's literature, plays, poetry\n- **License**: Unrestricted licenses only (free)\n- **Diacritics**: Varies by book (children's books more likely vowelized)\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Format**: HTML + plain text, 420 MB total\n- **Download**: [Swedish National Data Service](https://researchdata.se/en/catalogue/dataset/2024-145)\n- **Alif relevance**: HIGH. Diverse genres including children's literature (simpler language). Fiction is more engaging than news for learners. Free and well-structured with metadata.\n\n### 2.2 Leveled Reading Corpus of Modern Standard Arabic\n\n- **Size**: 7 million tokens total: 1.4M tokens from UAE K-12 textbooks + 5.6M tokens from 129 fiction works\n- **Source**: UAE curriculum (grades 1-12) + unabridged Arabic fiction\n- **Levels**: Annotated with reading levels from Grade 1 to Post-secondary\n- **License**: Academic (check paper for access)\n- **Diacritics**: Likely yes for lower grades (UAE textbooks for young children are vowelized)\n- **Translations**: No\n- **Download**: Contact authors (NYU Abu Dhabi). Paper: [ACL Anthology](https://aclanthology.org/L18-1366/)\n- **Alif relevance**: VERY HIGH. This is the gold standard for graded Arabic text. Grade-leveled content from actual textbooks + fiction, with reading level annotations. If we can access it, this enables direct difficulty-based sentence selection. The K-3 textbook content would be fully diacritized.\n\n### 2.3 BAREC (Balanced Arabic Readability Evaluation Corpus)\n\n- **Size**: 69,441 sentences, 1+ million words\n- **Source**: 30 resources (textbooks, articles, literature)\n- **Levels**: 19 readability levels (kindergarten to postgraduate)\n- **License**: Open (fair use/public domain sources)\n- **Diacritics**: Unknown\n- **NLP research**: Yes (CAMeL Lab, NYU Abu Dhabi, Shared Task 2025)\n- **Download**: [barec.camel-lab.com](http://barec.camel-lab.com) and [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/CAMeL-Lab/BAREC-Shared-Task-2025-sent)\n- **Alif relevance**: VERY HIGH. Sentence-level readability annotations across 19 levels is exactly what we need for difficulty-graded sentence selection. Available on Hugging Face. This could replace or supplement our comprehensibility gate heuristic with empirically-graded sentences.\n\n### 2.4 SAMER (Simplification of Arabic Masterpieces for Extensive Reading)\n\n- **Size**: 159K words from 15 Arabic fiction novels, with two simplified versions per text\n- **Readability lexicon**: 40K lemmas with 5-level readability scale (L1-L5)\n- **Source**: Arabic fiction masterpieces\n- **License**: Academic\n- **Diacritics**: Word-level readability annotations\n- **Download**: [SAMER Project](https://sites.google.com/nyu.edu/samer)\n- **Alif relevance**: HIGH. The 40K-lemma readability lexicon is extremely valuable for our word difficulty estimation. Could map our lemmas to SAMER levels. The simplified parallel texts model (original + 2 simplified versions) is interesting for adaptive content.\n\n### 2.5 Internet Archive Arabic Collections\n\n- **Size**: 20M+ freely downloadable books/texts; Arabic Collections Online (ACO): 10,042 volumes\n- **Source**: Multiple institutions (NYU, Princeton, Cornell, Columbia, AUC, AUB)\n- **License**: Public domain / free access\n- **Diacritics**: Varies (older texts more likely vowelized)\n- **Download**: [Internet Archive Arabic](https://archive.org/details/booksbylanguage_arabic)\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW-MEDIUM. Raw book scans would need OCR + processing. ACO is better curated. Useful for specific genre mining (children's books, textbooks) but high processing cost.\n\n### 2.6 Arabic Learner Corpus (ALC)\n\n- **Size**: 282,732 words in 1,585 materials from 942 students\n- **Source**: Written essays and spoken recordings by Arabic learners in Saudi Arabia\n- **License**: LDC (not free)\n- **Diacritics**: No (learner-produced text)\n- **Download**: [LDC](https://catalog.ldc.upenn.edu/LDC2015S10)\n- **Alif relevance**: LOW. This is text produced BY learners, not FOR learners. Not useful for sentence mining.\n\n### 2.7 Multidialectal Parallel Arabic Corpus (2025)\n\n- **Size**: ~50,010 unique parallel sentences across MSA + 5 dialects (Saudi, Egyptian, Iraqi, Levantine, Moroccan)\n- **Source**: LLM-generated, human-validated\n- **License**: Open source (Zenodo)\n- **Diacritics**: Unknown\n- **Translations**: Parallel across dialects (MSA + 5 dialects)\n- **Alif relevance**: MEDIUM. The MSA portion could be useful. Dialect parallels interesting for future dialect support.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Diacritics (Tashkeel) Summary\n\nSources with full diacritics are rare and precious for Alif:\n\n| Corpus | Diacritized? | Size | Register |\n|--------|-------------|------|----------|\n| Tanzil/Quran | Full | 77K words | Classical |\n| Tashkeela | Full | 75M words | 99% Classical, 1% MSA |\n| PATB (LDC) | Partial (verbs) | 739K tokens | MSA news |\n| UAE Textbooks (Leveled Corpus) | Likely (K-3) | ~500K tokens | Educational MSA |\n| Children's books (Hindawi) | Likely | Unknown | Simple MSA |\n\n**Key insight**: There is NO large, freely available, fully-diacritized MSA corpus. The best options are: (a) Tashkeela's small MSA portion (~868K words), (b) auto-diacritization of undiacritized corpora using models trained on Tashkeela, (c) children's/educational texts that are naturally vowelized.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Existing Systems for Corpus-Based Sentence Selection\n\n### 4.1 Tatoeba\n\n- **URL**: [tatoeba.org](https://tatoeba.org)\n- **Arabic**: 67,464 sentences\n- **Approach**: Community-contributed example sentences with translations\n- **Selection**: Manual curation, no automatic grading\n- **License**: CC BY 2.0 FR\n- **Integration**: Powers Clozemaster. Bulk download available.\n\n### 4.2 Clozemaster\n\n- **URL**: [clozemaster.com](https://www.clozemaster.com)\n- **Arabic**: ~50,000 sentences (sourced from Tatoeba)\n- **Approach**: Cloze deletion (fill-in-the-blank) from corpus sentences, ordered by word frequency\n- **Selection**: Frequency-based ordering using word frequency lists. \"Fluency Fast Track\" orders sentences from most common to least common missing words.\n- **Strengths**: Simple, effective vocabulary acquisition through context\n- **Limitations**: Tatoeba quality issues, no difficulty grading beyond frequency, no morphological awareness\n\n### 4.3 LingQ\n\n- **URL**: [lingq.com](https://www.lingq.com/en/learn-arabic-online/)\n- **Arabic**: Mini Stories (Standard Arabic, Egyptian, Levantine, Palestinian), plus user-imported content\n- **Approach**: Import-any-text model. Users highlight unknown words (\"LingQs\"), track vocabulary across texts.\n- **Selection**: User-driven. Platform provides starter content but the model is \"bring your own content.\"\n- **Strengths**: Any text becomes a lesson. Vocabulary tracking across all content.\n- **Limitations**: No automatic sentence selection. Limited built-in Arabic content. Dialect mixing.\n\n### 4.4 ML_for_SLA (Research Project)\n\n- **URL**: [GitHub](https://github.com/JonathanLaneMcDonald/ML_for_SLA)\n- **Approach**: Neural network trained to identify \"comprehensible input\" sentences from unstructured text. Selects sentences where context strongly disambiguates unknown words.\n- **Language**: Japanese (but approach is language-agnostic)\n- **Results**: Extracted ~1.1M \"high-quality\" example sentences from 20K word lexicon. Top 100 examples per word for words in the 80th-99th frequency percentile.\n- **Selection criteria**: Fewer unknowns = better. Richer context = better. Targets i+1 (single new word strongly contextualized by known words).\n- **Alif relevance**: VERY HIGH conceptually. This is essentially what our `build_session()` comprehensibility gate does, but with a neural scoring model instead of heuristic. Could adopt similar approach with a corpus.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Academic Research on Sentence Selection for Vocabulary Learning\n\n### 5.1 \"Candidate Sentence Selection for Language Learning Exercises\" (Pilan, Volodina, Borin, 2016)\n\n- **Paper**: [arXiv:1706.03530](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03530), [ACL Anthology](https://aclanthology.org/2016.tal-3.4/)\n- **Key findings**:\n - Hybrid system combining heuristics + machine learning for selecting pedagogically suitable sentences from L1 corpora\n - Two fundamental selection criteria: **linguistic complexity** and **context dependence** (whether the sentence is self-contained)\n - Teacher evaluation: top-ranked model selections performed at the **same level as dictionary examples**\n - Best model significantly outperformed both random corpus selections AND dictionary examples\n - 73% of selected sentences deemed understandable by evaluators\n - Logistic regression on linguistic features (sentence length, vocabulary level, syntactic complexity)\n- **Alif relevance**: Direct template for building a sentence selector. Their criteria map well to our needs.\n\n### 5.2 \"NLP-based Approaches to Sentence Readability for L2 Learning\" (Pilán et al.)\n\n- **Key findings**: Semi-automatic sentence selection from native corpora for L2 learners. Lexical and syntactic factors most influence selection quality. CEFR-level annotation enables grading.\n- **Alif relevance**: Could use similar readability features (sentence length, avg word frequency, syntactic depth) to filter corpus sentences.\n\n### 5.3 Comprehensible Input Research (Krashen / Laufer)\n\n- **Key threshold**: Learners need to understand **95-98%** of words in a text for effective acquisition (Laufer 1989). Alif currently uses 60% known scaffold, but the research suggests higher is better.\n- **i+1 principle**: Optimal learning happens when exactly one new element is embedded in otherwise comprehensible context.\n- **Alif relevance**: Our comprehensibility gate (60%) is well below the research-recommended 95%. This is because we're selecting FOR the unknown word, not for general reading. But for corpus sentence selection, we should target 90-95% known vocabulary.\n\n### 5.4 GenAI vs. Corpus-Based Sentences for L2 Learning\n\n- **Finding**: L2 learners found GenAI-based sentences more suitable than corpus-based sentences in 265 out of 400 pairwise comparisons.\n- **Alif relevance**: Validates our LLM generation approach. But corpus sentences are free and instant, while LLM generation costs time/money. A hybrid approach (corpus first, LLM fallback) could be optimal.\n\n### 5.5 BAREC / Fine-grained Arabic Readability (CAMeL Lab, 2025)\n\n- **Paper**: [arXiv:2502.13520](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.13520)\n- **Key contribution**: 69K Arabic sentences with 19-level readability annotations. Sentence-level (not just document-level) difficulty scoring.\n- **Alif relevance**: Could directly use these graded sentences OR train a readability classifier on this data to score any Arabic sentence.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Practical Recommendations for Alif\n\n### Tier 1: Immediate Value (should integrate)\n\n1. **Tatoeba** (67K sentences with translations) — direct import, filter for MSA, use translations as glosses\n2. **BAREC** (69K sentences with readability levels) — Hugging Face download, use readability levels for difficulty-based selection\n3. **AMARA/TED** (educational content with translations) — natural MSA, diverse topics\n\n### Tier 2: High Value with Processing\n\n4. **WikiMatrix** (Arabic-English parallel, filtered by margin score >1.06) — millions of translated pairs, needs quality filtering\n5. **Hindawi E-Book Corpus** (81.5M words, fiction/children's lit) — extract sentences, classify by difficulty\n6. **OSIAN** (37M sentences, MSA news) — filter for short/simple sentences, no translations\n7. **Leipzig Corpora** (pre-processed sentence packs) — convenient 1M-sentence downloads\n\n### Tier 3: Research/Future Value\n\n8. **Tashkeela** (75M diacritized words) — valuable for tashkeel model training, small MSA portion usable\n9. **Leveled Reading Corpus** (UAE textbooks, graded K-12) — gold standard if accessible\n10. **SAMER readability lexicon** (40K lemmas with difficulty levels) — map to our lemma database\n11. **CCMatrix** (billions of parallel sentences) — massive but needs aggressive quality filtering\n12. **OpenSubtitles** (conversational, parallel) — needs dialect filtering\n\n### Hybrid Pipeline Proposal\n\n```\n1. Load corpus sentences (Tatoeba + BAREC + AMARA)\n2. For each sentence:\n a. Tokenize and match words against user's vocabulary\n b. Calculate comprehensibility score (% known words)\n c. If readability level available (BAREC), use directly\n d. If translation available, store as English gloss\n3. For a given target word:\n a. Find sentences containing the word with comprehensibility >= 90%\n b. Rank by: readability level, sentence length (shorter better), translation availability\n c. If no corpus sentence meets criteria → fall back to LLM generation\n4. Benefits: instant (no LLM latency), free, deterministic, reproducible\n```\n\n### Estimated Usable Sentence Pool\n\n| Source | Raw sentences | After MSA filter | After length filter (<20 words) | With translations |\n|--------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|\n| Tatoeba | 67K | ~50K | ~45K | ~40K |\n| BAREC | 69K | 69K (all MSA) | ~50K | No |\n| AMARA/TED | ~100K est. | ~80K | ~60K | Yes |\n| WikiMatrix (filtered) | ~5M est. | ~4M | ~2M | Yes |\n| OSIAN (filtered) | 37M | 37M | ~10M | No |\n| **Total usable** | | | **~12M** | **~2.1M** |\n\nFor comparison, Alif currently has ~600 active sentences (LLM-generated). Even the smallest corpus (Tatoeba, 45K filtered) would be a 75x increase.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Key URLs and Downloads\n\n| Resource | URL |\n|----------|-----|\n| Tatoeba downloads | https://tatoeba.org/en/downloads |\n| BAREC on Hugging Face | https://huggingface.co/datasets/CAMeL-Lab/BAREC-Shared-Task-2025-sent |\n| OSIAN (Leipzig) | https://corpora.uni-leipzig.de |\n| Hindawi E-Book Corpus | https://researchdata.se/en/catalogue/dataset/2024-145 |\n| Tashkeela (Kaggle) | https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/linuxscout/tashkeela |\n| Tanzil Quran | https://tanzil.net/download/ |\n| UN Parallel Corpus | https://www.un.org/dgacm/en/content/uncorpus/download |\n| WikiMatrix | https://github.com/facebookresearch/LASER |\n| Abu El-Khair (HF) | https://huggingface.co/datasets/abuelkhair-corpus/arabic_billion_words |\n| KSUCCA | https://sourceforge.net/projects/ksucca-corpus/ |\n| SAMER Project | https://sites.google.com/nyu.edu/samer |\n| Masader (500+ datasets catalog) | https://arbml.github.io/masader/ |\n| OPUS (all corpora) | https://opus.nlpl.eu/ |\n| OpenSubtitles | https://opus.nlpl.eu/OpenSubtitles/corpus/version/OpenSubtitles |\n| Internet Archive Arabic | https://archive.org/details/booksbylanguage_arabic |\n| Arabic Collections Online | https://dlib.nyu.edu/aco/ |\n\n---\n\n## Sources\n\n- [OSIAN paper (ACL Anthology)](https://aclanthology.org/W19-4619/)\n- [Abu El-Khair Corpus (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1611.04033)\n- [Tashkeela paper (ScienceDirect)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352340917300112)\n- [WikiMatrix paper (EACL 2021)](https://aclanthology.org/2021.eacl-main.115/)\n- [CCMatrix paper (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.04944)\n- [UN Parallel Corpus paper (LREC 2016)](https://aclanthology.org/L16-1561/)\n- [AMARA Corpus paper (IWSLT 2013)](https://aclanthology.org/2013.iwslt-papers.2/)\n- [Candidate Sentence Selection (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03530)\n- [BAREC paper (arXiv)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.13520)\n- [SAMER project](https://sites.google.com/nyu.edu/samer)\n- [Leveled Reading Corpus (ACL Anthology)](https://aclanthology.org/L18-1366/)\n- [Hindawi E-Book Corpus (ScienceDirect)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S235234092500188X)\n- [Arabic Readability Assessment survey (ACM)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3571510)\n- [Masader catalog (ACL Anthology)](https://aclanthology.org/2022.lrec-1.681/)\n- [ML_for_SLA (GitHub)](https://github.com/JonathanLaneMcDonald/ML_for_SLA)\n- [Comprehensible Input for Arabic (ResearchGate)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/374634011)\n- [Arabic NNLP Resources (GitHub)](https://github.com/NNLP-IL/Arabic-Resources)\n", "date": "Feb 21", "category": "generation", "status": "active", "cross_refs": ["https:/github.com/facebookresearch/LASER/blob/main/tasks/WikiMatrix/README"], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/arabic-sentence-corpora-2026-02-21.md"}, "arabic-teaching-worldwide-2026-02-21": {"title": "Arabic Language Teaching Worldwide: A Comprehensive Research Report", "content": "# Arabic Language Teaching Worldwide: A Comprehensive Research Report\n\n*February 2026*\n\n---\n\n## Executive Summary\n\nArabic is spoken by over 400 million people and holds official status in 25 countries, yet the teaching of Arabic -- both as a first language in the Arab world and as a foreign/heritage language elsewhere -- faces a set of deeply entrenched structural problems. The 2021 World Bank report \"Advancing Arabic Language Teaching and Learning\" identified that **more than half of children in MENA countries experience \"learning poverty\"** -- they cannot read and understand an age-appropriate text by age 10. In lower- and middle-income Arab countries, this figure rises to **63 percent**. These outcomes persist even in wealthy Gulf states, where spending on education is substantial.\n\nThis report examines Arabic language instruction across four broad contexts: native-language education in Arab countries, Arabic education in non-Arab Muslim-majority countries, foreign-language and heritage-language programs in Western countries, and the cross-cutting methodological debates that connect them all.\n\n**Related Alif research:**\n- [arabic-education-research-2026-02-21.md](arabic-education-research-2026-02-21.md) — Annotated bibliography of academic papers and reports\n- [arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21.md](arabic-textbook-repositories-2026-02-21.md) — Digital textbook sources from 17+ countries\n- [deep-research-compilation-2026-02-12.md](deep-research-compilation-2026-02-12.md) — Arabic-specific learning research (Section 3)\n- [vocabulary-acquisition-research.md](vocabulary-acquisition-research.md) — General vocabulary acquisition research\n\n---\n\n## 1. Arab Countries Where Arabic Is L1\n\n### 1.1 General Curricular Framework\n\nAcross the Arab world, education systems teach Modern Standard Arabic (MSA, or *al-fusha*) as the medium of instruction from primary school onward. MSA is the language of textbooks, exams, official documents, and most printed matter. However, no child grows up speaking MSA at home -- all children arrive at school speaking a regional dialect (*ammiyya* / *darija*) that differs from MSA in phonology, morphology, syntax, and vocabulary to varying degrees.\n\nThis creates a situation that the World Bank (2021) describes as follows: \"Children are taught to read and write in a rigid way with a focus on rules, grammar, and accuracy. Children also experience a lack of playfulness and inquiry that are needed to fully engage them in literacy learning.\"\n\n### 1.2 Egypt\n\nEgypt's Arabic curriculum has been widely criticized. The gap between Egyptian colloquial Arabic (ECA) and MSA is significant, with **only approximately 40% vocabulary overlap** between Egyptian textbooks and the Egyptian dialect (Al-Fanar Media, 2022). Children in public schools who only speak Arabic at home are known to have difficulty reading and writing -- MSA functions as a partially foreign register.\n\nKey problems include:\n- Vocabulary introduced in textbooks does not match words children recently learned or use at home\n- Heavy emphasis on rote memorization, where vocabulary retention ends at the exam\n- Curriculum lacks connection to students' lived experiences\n- Pedagogical best practice -- starting with words close to both MSA and dialect -- is rarely followed\n\nEgypt's PIRLS 2021 reading score of approximately **378** (on a 500-point centerpoint scale) places it among the lowest-performing participating countries worldwide. The World Bank found that **69% of Egyptian children** are below minimum reading proficiency.\n\n### 1.3 Saudi Arabia\n\nSaudi Arabia's Vision 2030 has driven extensive education reform, but much of the focus has been on English-language instruction and STEM subjects rather than Arabic pedagogy per se. Arabic instruction hours have actually been *reduced* to make room for English from the first grade (previously starting at grade 4) and new subjects like critical thinking.\n\nSaudi Arabia scored approximately **449** on PIRLS 2021, well below the international average of 500 but above several other Arab countries. The OECD's 2020 review of Saudi education identified fragmented teacher preparation and content-heavy assessment systems as ongoing challenges.\n\nThe Khbrat program sends Saudi teachers abroad for professional development, but these partnerships focus primarily on English-speaking university programs. A national curriculum framework emphasizing skills and competencies has been developed but is still being rolled out.\n\n### 1.4 United Arab Emirates\n\nThe UAE presents a paradox: it is one of the wealthiest Arab countries and invests heavily in education, yet faces a crisis in Arabic-language proficiency among its own citizens. **Over 70% of Emirati children** now enroll in English-medium instruction (EMI) schools, perceived as offering superior educational outcomes. The result is a generational linguistic shift: students are becoming more competent in English and less fluent in Arabic.\n\nDespite UAE Vision 2021's goal for 90% Arabic literacy, **95% of Emirati students entering universities require remedial Arabic courses** -- a staggering gap between policy aspiration and educational reality. The UAE scored approximately **483** on PIRLS 2021 (among higher-performing Arab states, but still below the 500 international average).\n\nIn response, the UAE has increased Arabic instruction time in kindergartens, mandated frameworks for Arabic/Islamic education in private schools, and extended project-based learning to all Cycle 2 students. In 2025, AI was introduced as a compulsory subject from kindergarten through Grade 12. These reforms address the structural problem but do not directly tackle Arabic pedagogy.\n\n### 1.5 Jordan\n\nJordan has a learning poverty rate **exceeding 60%**, with over half of 10-year-olds unable to read and comprehend age-appropriate Arabic texts. Jordan scored approximately **381** on PIRLS 2021.\n\nJordan stands out for having launched a **National Arabic Literacy Strategy 2023-2028**, developed by the Ministry of Education with support from the Queen Rania Foundation (QRF). The strategy explicitly aims to shift from content-driven curriculum to competency-based instruction focused on literacy and numeracy.\n\nHowever, implementation faces structural barriers: fragmented teacher training, overcrowded classrooms, content-heavy assessment systems, and resource constraints. The QRF's \"Let's Read Fluently!\" intervention, grounded in cognitive science and adapted for the Jordanian context, has shown preliminary positive impact on oral reading fluency, syllable identification, and word decoding.\n\n### 1.6 Lebanon\n\nLebanon's education system is distinctive for its trilingual policy, adopted in 1997: Arabic, French, and English are all taught from early years, with English or French serving as mandatory media of instruction for mathematics and sciences.\n\nThis creates a complex linguistic environment:\n- Children speak Lebanese Arabic (a dialect) at home\n- They learn MSA as a \"high\" register in school\n- They learn most technical subjects in French or English\n\nResearchers voice concern that this multilingual arrangement may produce students \"who may not have any real proficiency levels in any of the languages\" and that Arabic is losing ground compared to the foreign languages. Code-switching between Lebanese, MSA, French, and English is pervasive in both public and private sectors.\n\n### 1.7 Qatar\n\nQatar launched its \"Education for a New Era\" reform in 2001-2004, one of the most ambitious in the region. The reform established a system of Independent Schools, developed national curriculum standards in four core subjects (Arabic, English, mathematics, science), and invested heavily in teacher professional development.\n\nQatar scored approximately **485** on PIRLS 2021. While this is among the higher Arab-country scores, it remains below the international average of 500 and far behind top performers like Singapore (587) or Ireland (577).\n\n---\n\n## 2. Diglossia: The Foundational Challenge\n\n### 2.1 The Nature of the Problem\n\nArabic diglossia -- the coexistence of MSA (\"high\" variety) and spoken dialects (\"low\" variety) within the same speech community -- is arguably the single most important factor in Arabic education outcomes worldwide.\n\nKey research findings (summarized from Al-Fanar Media, 2022; Frontiers in Education, 2025; World Bank, 2021):\n\n- **In five wealthy Gulf states** (Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE), **36.6%** of students cannot grasp simple text by fourth grade -- compared to **2.7%** in economically comparable non-Arab nations\n- **Palestinian children** possess just **21.2% shared vocabulary** with MSA\n- Diglossia negatively impacts \"phonological awareness, morphological awareness, phonological representation, word decoding, reading fluency, and letter naming\"\n- The negative impact is **disproportionately greater for families of lower socioeconomic status**\n- International assessments (PISA 2018, PIRLS 2016 and 2021) consistently show Arab children scoring below average, even in wealthy countries\n\n### 2.2 The Maghreb: An Extreme Case\n\nThe Maghreb countries (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia) present the most extreme form of Arabic diglossia, compounded by multilingualism.\n\n**Morocco** scored approximately **372** on PIRLS 2021 -- one of the lowest scores globally. The World Bank found that **67% of grade 2 children in Morocco** could not answer a single reading comprehension question correctly. Moroccan Darija diverges dramatically from MSA, incorporating substantial French, Berber, and Spanish loanwords. The country's Arabization policy, launched after independence in 1956, replaced French with Arabic in public education, but:\n- Many math and science teachers of the transitional generation were educated in French and struggle to teach in MSA\n- Wealthy families bypass the system through private schools (often French-medium), exacerbating socioeconomic inequality\n- French continues to dominate at secondary and postsecondary levels in technical fields\n- After 30+ years of Arabization, language education challenges \"have yet to be fully addressed\"\n\n**Algeria** faced an even more dramatic post-independence transition. In 1962, approximately 18,000 of 23,500 teachers (60%) and 1,400 of 2,000 professors (70%) left the country. The gap was filled by staff from Arab, European, and Asian countries \"who often did not meet the educational levels required.\" By the 1980s, Arabization was largely complete in government and education, but quality concerns persist.\n\n**Tunisia**, once praised for its education system, has faced declining outcomes. The diglossia challenge is compounded by the historical role of French in technical and scientific education.\n\n### 2.3 Resistance to Addressing Diglossia\n\nMany Arab policymakers resist acknowledging diglossia as a factor in education outcomes. Some frame dialect-inclusive education as threatening Arab nationalism or Islamic values, since MSA is closely associated with the Quran. Researchers argue that dialects should serve \"as a steppingstone to reach al-Fusha\" rather than being treated as corruptions to be eliminated -- but this remains politically charged.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Non-Arab Muslim-Majority Countries\n\n### 3.1 Overview\n\nApproximately **85% of the world's 1.8+ billion Muslims live in non-Arab countries**. In these countries, Arabic instruction is primarily motivated by religious literacy -- the ability to read the Quran, understand Islamic jurisprudence, and access classical religious texts. Communicative competence in spoken Arabic is rarely a goal.\n\n### 3.2 Turkey\n\nTurkey's Imam Hatip schools form the largest institutional framework for Arabic instruction outside the Arab world. These vocational high schools allocate approximately **25% of curriculum time** to religious courses including Quran, Arabic, biography of the Prophet, hadith, jurisprudence, and Quranic interpretation.\n\nArabic is taught alongside Turkish as a working language for religious study. However, the focus is on reading comprehension of classical texts rather than communicative ability. Imam Hatip enrollment has expanded significantly under the AKP government, but the Arabic taught remains largely textual and grammatical. Mainstream Turkish schools offer two hours of compulsory religious education per week but minimal Arabic content.\n\n### 3.3 Iran\n\nIn Iran's *hawza* (seminary) system, Arabic occupies a central place. The Qom Seminary curriculum includes Arabic grammar (*sarf* and *nahw*), rhetoric, and classical literature as prerequisites for advanced study of jurisprudence, theology, and Quranic sciences.\n\nThe hawza curriculum takes students from foundational studies to advanced mastery, with rigorous training in classical Arabic throughout. However, this is elite religious education -- the general Iranian school system teaches Arabic as a subject but does not aim for communicative proficiency. Arabic study in mainstream Iranian schools focuses on Quranic vocabulary and basic grammar, not spoken fluency.\n\n### 3.4 Pakistan\n\nPakistan's madrasa system, largely following the Nizami curriculum adopted by Deoband seminary in 1867, teaches Arabic as a component of approximately twenty subjects divided into *al-uloom an-naqliya* (transmitted sciences: Quran, hadith, jurisprudence) and *al-uloom al-aqliya* (rational sciences: grammar, rhetoric, logic, philosophy).\n\nIn Deobandi schools, Urdu, Arabic, and Persian are taught; up to 8th grade, government textbooks are used; after that, traditional Arabic and Persian texts replace them. The curriculum is described as \"very old and inflexible\" and focused on grammatical analysis of classical texts rather than communicative skills. Few Pakistani madrasa graduates can conduct a conversation in Arabic despite years of study.\n\n### 3.5 Indonesia\n\nIndonesia has the world's largest Muslim population and a substantial Arabic education infrastructure:\n\n- **Pesantren** (traditional Islamic boarding schools): Approximately 27,000 pesantren serve millions of students. They prioritize Quranic memorization and study of classical Islamic texts. The grammar-translation method and rote memorization remain the prevailing instructional models, \"often at the expense of communicative competence.\"\n- **National curriculum**: Indonesia has institutionalized Arabic education by integrating it into national curricula under the Ministry of Religious Affairs. Madrasah schools offer Arabic alongside secular subjects.\n- **Modern pesantren**: Some modern pesantren (notably Gontor) have adopted more communicative approaches, blending grammar-translation with communicative language teaching. However, research shows Indonesian language interference remains a significant barrier.\n\nIndonesia is developing a **Competency-Based Arabic Curriculum** aligned with the *Merdeka Belajar* (\"Freedom to Learn\") framework, though implementation remains uneven.\n\n### 3.6 Malaysia\n\nMalaysia's **j-QAF program** (Jawi, Quran, Arabic, Fard Ayn), introduced in 2005, represents one of the most systematic state-level efforts to teach Arabic in a non-Arab country. Four teaching models were developed: Jawi Remedial Class, Khatam Quran, Arabic Language Communication, and Smart Prayer Camp.\n\nArabic is taught in both primary and secondary schools as part of the national curriculum, with specialized religious secondary schools offering more intensive Arabic instruction. However, survey data shows teachers report being dissatisfied with lesson time allocation (58.6% dissatisfied), and Arabic proficiency outcomes remain modest for most students.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Western Countries\n\n### 4.1 United States\n\n#### University Programs\n\nArabic study in U.S. higher education grew dramatically after September 11, 2001, but has since declined. Key data from the **MLA Language Enrollment Survey**:\n- Arabic enrollments **declined by over 27%** between 2016 and 2021\n- **80 fewer institutions** reported Arabic programs between 2016 and 2021\n- Enrollments declined in all three most recent census periods (2013, 2016, 2021)\n- This is part of a broader 16.6% decline in all non-English language enrollment\n\nDespite declining enrollment, several elite programs remain influential:\n\n- **Georgetown University**: Home to the oldest undergraduate Arabic major in the U.S. and the Al-Kitaab textbook series (published by Georgetown University Press), which is used in the majority of American university Arabic programs.
