Allowing R to drop to 80-85% could produce larger stability gains per review.\n\n### 3.9 Metacognitive Illusions\n\n- Foresight bias: learners overestimate performance by ~15pp for difficult items (Koriat & Bjork 2006)\n- Processing fluency ≠ learning (understanding in context ≠ knowing the word)\n- Lower-proficiency learners overestimate more (Dunning-Kruger)\n- Students' predictions uncorrelated with actual performance (Karpicke & Roediger 2008)\n\n### 3.10 The 85% Rule\n\nWilson et al. 2019 (Nature Communications):\n- Optimal error rate for learning: ~15.87% (accuracy ~84.13%)\n- Training at this rate produces exponential improvements in learning speed\n- Too easy (>95%): nothing left to learn\n- Too hard (<70%): approaching chance, no learning signal\n\n---\n\n## 4. Arabic-Specific Learning Research {#arabic-specific}\n\n### 4.1 Key Numbers\n\n| Metric | Value |\n|--------|-------|\n| FSI Category V (hardest) | 2,200 hours to proficiency |\n| 79% text coverage | ~1,000 lemmas |\n| 89% coverage | ~5,000 lemmas |\n| 95% coverage | ~9,000 lemmas |\n| 98% coverage | ~14,000 lemmas |\n| Trilateral roots (Hans Wehr) | 2,967 |\n| Roots for 80% daily vocab | ~500 |\n| AVP A1 validated list | 1,750 items |\n| MSA-dialect lexical overlap | 33-63% |\n\n### 4.2 Diacritics: Always Show\n\n**Midhwah (2020, Modern Language Journal, n=54)**: Vowelized textbook groups outperformed unvowelized at ALL proficiency levels on speed, accuracy, AND comprehension. No evidence diacritics hinder later unvowelized reading. **Alif's always-show policy is strongly supported.**\n\n### 4.3 Root Awareness\n\n- Root awareness accounts for substantial variance in reading outcomes\n- L2 learners rely on roots in 87.5% of encounters with unknown words\n- Even non-native speakers organize L2 Arabic lexicons around root morphemes\n- Root effects are \"reliably present throughout the recognition process\"\n\n**However**: Introducing multiple root siblings simultaneously causes interference (semantic clustering effect, Section 6).\n\n### 4.4 Arabic Morphological Complexity\n\n- Form I verbs: ~60-70% of all verb usage\n- Recommended form learning order: I → II/IV → V/VIII → X/III → VI/VII/IX\n- Morphological density independently impacts reading comprehension beyond vocabulary coverage\n- Arabic's clitic system means \"known\" words can be hard to recognize with attached clitics\n\n### 4.5 Listening\n\n- Listening anxiety is a separate phenomenon from general FL anxiety (Elkhafaifi 2005)\n- Pre-listening vocabulary preview significantly improves comprehension\n- All participants improved after second exposure to listening passages\n- Alif's listening-ready filter (times_seen ≥ 3, stability ≥ 7d) is appropriate scaffolding\n\n### 4.6 CAMeL Tools Validation\n\nBenchmark (Noor-Ghateh 2023): CAMeL achieves highest accuracy across datasets (0.68-0.81), outperforming Farasa (0.59-0.81). Correct choice for Alif.\n\n### 4.7 Arabic Frequency Lists\n\nBest available for MSA learners:\n- **CAMeL MSA corpus** (12.6B tokens) — currently used in Alif\n- **Aralex** (Boudelaa & Marslen-Wilson 2010) — 40K entries with psycholinguistic variables\n- **Kelly Project** — CEFR-aligned, currently used in Alif\n- **AVP A1** (2025) — newest, expert-validated by 71 teachers, 1,750 items\n\n---\n\n## 5. Session Design & Optimization {#session-design}\n\n### 5.1 Optimal Session Length\n\n- **Sweet spot: 10-20 items, 10-20 minutes**\n- Below 5 items: insufficient within-session spacing\n- Above 30 items/30 minutes: cognitive fatigue degrades encoding\n- Baddeley & Longman (1978): shorter, distributed sessions dramatically more efficient\n- Anki community: 20 new/day → ~200 reviews/day is upper limit of sustainability\n\n### 5.2 Micro-Sessions (2-5 items)\n\n- Effective IF they include re-quiz with intervening items\n- Duolingo found making lessons shorter actually hurt learning metrics\n- Minimum viable: enough items for temporal spacing between retrievals of same item\n- A 2-item session with no re-quiz = massed practice (worst)\n\n### 5.3 Session Ordering (Serial Position Effect)\n\n- Items at beginning (primacy) and end (recency) recalled best\n- Middle items get least effective encoding\n- **Alif's easy-bookend ordering is well-supported:**\n 1. Easy items first (warm-up, build self-efficacy)\n 2. Hard items in middle (focused attention, not yet fatigued)\n 3. Easy items last (recency effect, positive emotional state)\n\n### 5.4 Within-Session Retrieval Spacing\n\n**Karpicke & Roediger 2007**: Expanding retrieval (1→2→4→7) vs equal spacing (3→3→3→3):\n- Expanding: better immediate recall\n- Equal: better on 2-day delayed test\n- **Key finding**: Placement of FIRST retrieval matters more than schedule type\n\n**Nakata 2015** (L2 specifically): Limited but significant advantage for expanding spacing.\n\n**Nakata 2017**: 3-5 retrievals per session optimal. 1 retrieval most time-efficient. Diminishing returns after 3.\n\n**Pimsleur within-session**: 5s → 25s → 2min → 10min (first 4 intervals within one lesson)\n\n### 5.5 New:Review Ratios\n\n- No research directly testing optimal ratio\n- Ratio is emergent from: available time, retention target, queue depth\n- **Anki default**: 20 new/day (generates ~200 reviews)\n- Nakata & Webb (2016): Spacing matters more than set size\n- **Practical**: 15 minutes → prioritize reviews. 45 minutes → can afford new items.\n\n### 5.6 Interleaving\n\n- **For established words**: Interleaving (mixing categories) outperforms blocking on delayed tests\n- **For new words**: Initial blocking helps, then interleave (Hwang 2025)\n- **Semantically similar** words benefit most from interleaving\n- Arabic root siblings = high semantic similarity → benefit from interleaving AFTER initial learning\n\n### 5.7 Adaptive Difficulty\n\nNo direct research on increasing vs decreasing difficulty within sessions. Best model:\n1. **Warm-up** (easy, build momentum)\n2. **Peak** (hardest items, attention highest)\n3. **Cool-down** (easy, recency effect, positive exit)\n\n### 5.8 Gamification\n\nSailer & Homner 2020 meta-analysis:\n- Cognitive learning outcomes: g=0.49 (real learning effect, not just engagement)\n- Novelty effect wears off at ~week 4, partially recovers weeks 6-10\n- Streaks: 2.3x more likely to engage daily after 7+ day streak\n- **Tie rewards to mastery, not participation**\n\n---\n\n## 6. Sentence-Centric SRS Research {#sentence-centric}\n\n### 6.1 No Published Sentence-Level SRS Studies\n\nNo rigorous studies directly compare sentence-level to word-level SRS. The \"sentence mining\" community operates on i+1 principle (one unknown per sentence) but without controlled studies.\n\n**Webb 2007**: Word pairs more effective for form-meaning connection, but sentences provide additional grammar/collocation/usage knowledge.\n\n### 6.2 The Comprehension-Retrieval Paradox\n\n**Van den Broek (2018)**: \"Context enhances comprehension but retrieval enhances retention.\"\n- Informative sentence context → better immediate understanding but worse long-term retention\n- Uninformative context → forces retrieval → better retention\n- **In narrative context**: Informative story context led to higher posttest results\n\n**Implication**: Alif's sentence-first model (read Arabic → self-assess) is comprehension-first. Adding retrieval (e.g., recall meaning before reveal) would strengthen retention.\n\n### 6.3 Text Coverage & Unknown Word Density\n\n- **98%** coverage (1 unknown per 50 words): adequate comprehension (Hu & Nation 2000)\n- **95%** (1 unknown per 20): minimally acceptable\n- **80%** (1 unknown per 5): comprehension inadequate\n- Sentence mining consensus: exactly 1 unknown element per sentence (i+1)\n- For 10-word Arabic sentence: 0-1 unknown words ideal\n\n### 6.4 Sentence Re-Reading vs New Sentences\n\n- Diverse contexts → better generalization to new contexts (Norman 2023)\n- Same context → better for familiar contexts\n- **Optimal**: Different sentences on different reviews when word is being learned; only recycle misunderstood sentences\n\n### 6.5 Noticing Hypothesis (Schmidt)\n\n- \"Subliminal language learning is impossible\" — conscious attention required\n- Tap-to-lookup = explicit act of noticing → strongly supported\n- Click-to-translate glosses: large effect (d=1.40) on vocabulary learning\n\n### 6.6 Collateral Word Learning\n\n- Giving FSRS credit (rating 3) to all unmarked words is a reasonable heuristic\n- For already-known words: reading them in context IS a legitimate review\n- For brand-new words: creating FSRS cards is more aggressive than research strictly supports\n- The credit_type metadata distinction is useful for future analysis\n\n### 6.7 Self-Assessment Accuracy\n\n- Ternary self-assessment (understood/partial/no_idea) is appropriately simple\n- Beginners will over-rate comprehension\n- Word-tapping catches specific gaps that global self-assessment misses\n- \"no_idea\" may be the most accurately calibrated rating (total confusion is hard to mistake)\n\n---\n\n## 7. Leech Management & Interference {#leech-management}\n\n### 7.1 Leech Detection\n\n- **Anki default**: 8 lapses. Experienced users recommend **4-6**.\n- FSRS developer: Use lapses, NOT difficulty parameter, for leech detection\n- **2.5% of material can consume 50% of study time** (SuperMemo data)\n- Leeches take ~10x as many repetitions as normal cards\n\n**Recommended for Alif**: 5+ lapses OR accuracy < 50% over last 10 reviews\n\n### 7.2 Root-Family Interference\n\n**Critical finding**: Semantic clustering (learning related words together) IMPEDES learning (Tinkham 1993/97, replicated multiple times).\n\n- Root siblings are inherently semantically related\n- Interference worsened with more repetitions for related words\n- **Thematic clustering** (words connected by scenario) HELPS — unlike semantic clustering\n- Root siblings should be taught within thematic contexts (stories), not as semantic groups\n\n**Optimal spacing for root siblings**: Wait until first sibling has FSRS stability > 7 days before introducing next. Alif's word_selector root familiarity score (30%, peaking at 30-60%) is well-aligned.\n\n### 7.3 Error-Driven Learning\n\n**Kornell, Hays & Bjork (2009)**: Attempted-and-failed retrieval → better later performance than just studying.\n\n- Even items NOT successfully retrieved on pretest showed learning benefit\n- Mechanism: failed retrieval activates semantic network, priming correct answer\n- Retrieval effort hypothesis: difficult, failed retrieval may provide MORE learning than easy success\n- **\"no_idea\" ratings have genuine learning value** — don't over-penalize\n\n### 7.4 Productive Failure (Kapur)\n\nMeta-analysis (Sinha & Kapur 2021): PF outperforms instruction-first for conceptual understanding (d=0.36). Higher fidelity → d=0.58.\n\nMaps to \"no_idea\" scenario: struggle → see answer → better encoding.\n\n### 7.5 Leech Treatment Pipeline\n\n1. **Detect**: 5+ lapses OR accuracy < 50% over last 10 reviews\n2. **Diagnose**: Interference (confusable pair)? Missing knowledge? Bad context?\n3. **Treat**:\n - Interference: Suspend weaker word, teach other first\n - Missing knowledge: Add morphological/root context\n - Bad context: Generate new sentences\n4. **Reintroduce**: Set due date 3-7 days after treatment, preserve FSRS history (don't reset card)\n5. **Monitor**: If recurs, escalate (different mnemonic, different context, explicit root teaching)\n\n### 7.6 Confusable Pairs\n\n- **Contrasting** similar words eventually helps but only for skilled readers (Baxter 2021)\n- Strategy: Suspend one → master the other → reintroduce with explicit discrimination\n- Track which words are confused with each other (within-session error patterns)\n\n### 7.7 Morphological Awareness as Leech Prevention\n\n- Root awareness reduces arbitrary form-meaning associations\n- Understanding root + pattern creates durable, connected memory traces\n- Arabic's root system is especially suited to explicit morphological instruction for adults\n- **Root prediction feature is well-supported** by research\n\n### 7.8 Vocabulary Size and Interference Thresholds\n\n| Words Known | Interference Level | Focus |\n|-------------|-------------------|-------|\n| 0-500 | Low | High-frequency vocabulary |\n| 500-1,000 | Moderate | Similar roots begin overlapping |\n| 1,000-2,000 | High (the \"plateau\") | Leech management critical |\n| 2,000+ | Dominant | Discrimination strategies essential |\n\n---\n\n## 8. Experimental Design (N-of-1) {#experimental-design}\n\n### 8.1 Between-Item Alternating Treatments Design\n\nBest design for comparing SRS algorithms in a single-user app:\n- **Unit of randomization**: Individual vocabulary items (lemma_id)\n- **Both conditions run simultaneously** on different words\n- No washout needed, no temporal carryover\n- Natural within-subject control\n\n### 8.2 Bayesian Beta-Binomial Analysis\n\n```\nPrior: Beta(0.5, 0.5) (Jeffrey's non-informative)\nAfter k successes in n trials: Beta(0.5 + k, 0.5 + n - k)\nCompare: P(p_A > p_B | data)\nROPE: [-0.05, 0.05] for practical equivalence\n```\n\n### 8.3 Sample Size\n\n- **50 words/condition minimum**, 80-100 recommended\n- With ICC=0.3 and 5 reviews/word: effective n~114 per condition\n- **Detectable effects at 80% power:**\n\n| Difference | Words/condition needed |\n|------------|----------------------|\n| 15pp | ~50 |\n| 10pp | ~100 |\n| 8pp | ~150 |\n| 5pp | ~400+ |\n\n### 8.4 Stopping Rules\n\n1. Minimum: 3 reviews/word in both conditions before analysis\n2. Check weekly\n3. Stop for superiority: P(A>B) > 0.975 AND ≥5 reviews/word\n4. Stop for equivalence: 95% HDI within ROPE [-0.05, 0.05]\n5. Maximum: 6 weeks\n\n### 8.5 Confound Control\n\n- Stratified block randomization by: frequency_rank bin, POS, CEFR level\n- Block-randomize root families (all siblings in same condition)\n- Include time-trend covariate in analysis\n- Balanced introduction schedule (equal new words/condition/week)\n\n### 8.6 Non-Stationarity\n\n- Random between-item assignment is primary defense (both conditions affected equally)\n- Include day/review_number as covariate\n- Counterbalanced introduction batches\n- Accept that cross-item transfer is symmetric with proper randomization\n\n### 8.7 Multiple Experiments\n\n- **Start single-factor** (one experiment at a time)\n- Maximum 2 concurrent experiments on non-overlapping word sets\n- 2×2 factorial possible but reduces words/cell to ~25 (underpowered for interactions)\n\n### 8.8 First Experiment Recommendation\n\n**\"Acquisition phase vs FSRS-only\":**\n- Group A: 3 within-session exposures + forced day-1 review before FSRS\n- Group B: Current approach (straight to FSRS)\n- Primary metric: Accuracy at first review after 7+ days\n- 80-100 words per group, 3-4 weeks\n\n---\n\n## 9. Codebase Change Points {#codebase-analysis}\n\n### 9.1 Key Files and Where Changes Go\n\n| Feature | Primary File(s) | Key Functions | New Schema |\n|---------|-----------------|---------------|------------|\n| **Acquisition phase** | `fsrs_service.py:submit_review()` L108, `word_selector.py:introduce_word()` L276 | New `submit_acquisition_step()`, check before FSRS call | `ULK.acquisition_step`, `acquisition_graduated_at` |\n| **Focus cohort** | `sentence_selector.py:build_session()` L199, `word_selector.py:select_next_words()` L182 | New `get_focus_cohort()`, modify scoring | `ULK.cohort_entered_at`, `cohort_exited_at` |\n| **Session repetition** | `sentence_selector.py` Stage 4 (greedy set cover) L481 | Change `remaining_due -= covered` to decrement-count | Transient (session-scoped) |\n| **OCR reform** | `ocr_service.py:process_textbook_page()` L442 | Remove `submit_review(rating=3)` calls at L529/558/615 | Possibly `ULK.knowledge_state=\"encountered\"` |\n| **Batch sentence gen** | `sentence_generator.py`, `llm.py` | New `generate_multi_target_sentence()` | Multi-target via SentenceWord.is_target_word |\n| **Leech management** | `sentence_review_service.py`, `sentence_selector.py` | Extend struggling detection L240 | `ULK.leech_count`, `is_leech` |\n| **A/B testing** | `fsrs_service.py` L13 (module-level Scheduler) | Per-experiment Scheduler instances | `ReviewLog.experiment_id`, `ULK.experiment_group` |\n\n### 9.2 Critical Code Points\n\n**`sentence_selector.py:build_session()`** (lines 199-605) — 6-stage pipeline:\n- Stage 1 (L219-248): Fetch due words → **INSERT acquisition/cohort filtering here**\n- Stage 3 (L316-479): Score candidates → **INSERT cohort boost factor here**\n- Stage 4 (L481-518): Greedy set cover → **MODIFY for word repetition**\n- Score formula (L471): `(len(due_covered) ** 1.5) * dmq * gfit * diversity * freshness`\n\n**`fsrs_service.py:submit_review()`** (L108-203):\n- L152-163: FSRS scheduling → **INSERT acquisition check before FSRS call**\n- L13: Module-level `scheduler = Scheduler()` → **Must become configurable for A/B testing**\n\n**`ocr_service.py:process_textbook_page()`** (L442-699):\n- L529, 558, 615: `submit_review(rating_int=3)` → **Remove for track-only mode**\n\n### 9.3 Test Coverage Gaps\n\n- No tests for word progression through learning stages\n- No session-level word repetition tests\n- No focus cohort or acquisition phase tests\n- No leech auto-suspension tests\n- No A/B testing infrastructure tests\n- OCR tests validate the WRONG behavior (confirm rating=3 is \"expected\")\n- No full review lifecycle integration test\n\n### 9.4 Migration Needed\n\nNew fields on `UserLemmaKnowledge`:\n- `acquisition_step` (Integer, nullable)\n- `acquisition_graduated_at` (DateTime, nullable)\n- `cohort_entered_at` (DateTime, nullable)\n- `leech_count` (Integer, default 0)\n\nNew fields on `ReviewLog`:\n- `experiment_id` (String, nullable)\n- `is_acquisition` (Boolean, default False)\n\n---\n\n## 10. Synthesized Algorithm Proposal {#synthesis}\n\n### 10.1 Three-Phase Word Lifecycle\n\n```\nENCOUNTERED → ACQUIRING → LEARNING/KNOWN/LAPSED\n ↓ ↓ ↓\n No FSRS Leitner-like FSRS scheduling\n No reviews 3-box system Standard reviews\n Track only 4h → 1d → 3d Expanding intervals\n```\n\n**Phase 1: Encountered** (OCR imports, story words)\n- Lemma exists in DB, no ULK record or ULK with `knowledge_state=\"encountered\"`\n- Appears in Learn mode candidates with appropriate scoring\n- No FSRS card, no reviews\n- Contributes to story readiness and text coverage calculations\n\n**Phase 2: Acquiring** (after user chooses to learn in Learn mode)\n- Leitner-like 3-box system: Box 1 (4h) → Box 2 (1d) → Box 3 (3d)\n- Appears 2-3x per session at expanding positions\n- Forced day-1 review regardless of box position\n- Graduation: 5+ reviews AND accuracy ≥ 60%\n- On graduation: Create FSRS card with initial stability based on acquisition performance\n\n**Phase 3: FSRS** (after graduation from acquisition)\n- Standard FSRS-6 scheduling\n- Focus cohort membership determines review priority\n- Leech detection at 5+ lapses or <50% accuracy\n\n### 10.2 Focus Cohort\n\n- Rolling window of **30-50 words** getting intensive treatment\n- Entry: word introduced via Learn mode (auto-enters cohort)\n- Exit: FSRS stability > 7 days AND 5+ reviews AND accuracy > 60%\n- **Words outside cohort don't appear as due** (except as sentence scaffold)\n- Effect: each word reviewed 2-3x/day instead of once/6-days\n\n### 10.3 Session Template (10 cards)\n\n```\n[1] Easy review (warm-up, bookend)\n[2] ACQUIRING word A — first exposure\n[3] ACQUIRING word B — first exposure\n[4] Due review (cohort word)\n[5] ACQUIRING word A — re-quiz (gap=2)\n[6] ACQUIRING word C — first exposure\n[7] Due review (cohort word)\n[8] ACQUIRING word B — re-quiz (gap=4)\n[9] ACQUIRING word C — re-quiz (gap=2)\n[10] Easy review (positive end, bookend)\n```\n\n### 10.4 Sentence Generation: Multi-Target\n\nInstead of one sentence per target word:\n```\nGenerate 8 sentences. Each must include at least 2 of these focus words:\nكتاب، مدرسة، قرأ، جديد، كبير\nEach word should appear in at least 2 different sentences.\n```\n\n### 10.5 OCR Import Reform\n\nThree modes on import screen:\n1. **\"Track only\"** (new default): Lemma entry, no ULK/FSRS. Words appear in Learn candidates.\n2. **\"Mark as studied today\"**: Current behavior (for pages just studied)\n3. **\"Mark as encountered\"**: ULK with `knowledge_state=\"encountered\"`, no FSRS card\n\n### 10.6 Leech Auto-Management\n\nDetection → Diagnosis → Treatment → Reintroduction → Monitoring\n\n- Detection: 5+ lapses OR <50% accuracy (last 10)\n- Auto-suspend with `leech_suspended_at` timestamp\n- Auto-reintroduce after 14 days with fresh acquisition phase\n- Root-sibling interference guard: don't introduce if sibling failed in last 7 days\n- Escalation: 3+ leech cycles → flag for manual review\n\n### 10.7 Target Accuracy: 85%\n\n- Adjust new word introduction rate to maintain ~85% session accuracy\n- Below 75%: pause new introductions, focus on consolidation\n- Above 92%: increase introduction rate\n- Track rolling 20-review accuracy window\n\n---\n\n## 11. Master Reference List {#references}\n\n### Memory & Cognition\n- Cepeda et al. (2006). Distributed practice in verbal recall tasks. *Psychological Bulletin*. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16719566/)\n- Cepeda et al. (2008). Spacing effects in learning. *Psychological Science*. [PDF](https://laplab.ucsd.edu/articles/Cepeda%20et%20al%202008_psychsci.pdf)\n- Cowan (2001). Magical number 4 in short-term memory. *BBS*. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11515286/)\n- Karpicke & Roediger (2006/2008). Test-enhanced learning. *Science*. [PDF](http://psychnet.wustl.edu/memory/wp-content/uploads/2018/04/Karpicke-Roediger-2008_Sci.pdf)\n- Koriat & Bjork (2006). Illusions of competence. *Memory & Cognition*. [PDF](https://bjorklab.psych.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/13/2016/07/Koriat_Bjork_2006_MC.pdf)\n- Kornell, Hays & Bjork (2009). Unsuccessful retrieval enhances learning. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19586265/)\n- Wilson et al. (2019). The 85% rule. *Nature Communications*. [Link](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-019-12552-4)\n- Bjork & Bjork (1992). A new theory of disuse. [PDF](https://bjorklab.psych.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/13/2016/07/RBjork_EBjork_1992.pdf)\n- Bjork & Kroll (2015). Desirable difficulties in vocabulary learning. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4888598/)\n\n### Vocabulary Learning\n- Uchihara, Webb & Yanagisawa (2019). Repetition and incidental vocabulary learning. *Language Learning*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/lang.12343)\n- Hu & Nation (2000). Unknown vocabulary density and reading comprehension. [PDF](https://www.wgtn.ac.nz/lals/resources/paul-nations-resources/paul-nations-publications/publications/documents/2000-Hu-Density-and-comprehension.pdf)\n- Waring & Takaki (2003). At what rate do learners learn words from graded readers? [ERIC](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ759833.pdf)\n- Van den Broek et al. (2018/2022). Context and retrieval in vocabulary learning. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9285746/)\n- Norman et al. (2023). Context diversity and word learning. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10280660/)\n- Laufer & Hulstijn (2001). Involvement Load Hypothesis. *Language Learning*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/0023-8333.00164)\n- Tinkham (1993/1997). Semantic clustering impedes learning. [SAGE](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1191/026765897672376469)\n- Abraham (2008). Computer-mediated glosses meta-analysis. [Taylor & Francis](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/09588220802090246)\n- Nakata (2015). Expanding vs equal spacing for L2 vocabulary. *SSLA*. [Cambridge](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/studies-in-second-language-acquisition/article/abs/effects-of-expanding-and-equal-spacing-on-second-language-vocabulary-learning/D1D796306985C52F9BE7A1200AC50DB9)\n- Nakata (2017). Within-session repeated retrieval. *SSLA*. [Cambridge](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/studies-in-second-language-acquisition/article/abs/does-repeated-practice-make-perfect-the-effects-of-withinsession-repeated-retrieval-on-second-language-vocabulary-learning/F14BA8A576CD2563D14CEA46E35D842E)\n- Nakata & Webb (2016). Vocabulary set size. *SSLA*. [Cambridge](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/studies-in-second-language-acquisition/article/abs/does-studying-vocabulary-in-smaller-sets-increase-learning/E17B75ABAE1300734AF014C363D59FBC)\n\n### Arabic-Specific\n- Midhwah (2020). Arabic diacritics and L2 reading. *Modern Language Journal*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/modl.12642)\n- Masrai & Milton (2016/2019). Arabic vocabulary size and coverage. [JALLR](http://www.jallr.com/index.php/JALLR/article/view/213)\n- Elkhafaifi (2005). Listening anxiety in Arabic. *Modern Language Journal*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1540-4781.2005.00275.x)\n- Noor-Ghateh (2023). Arabic NLP tool benchmark. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2307.09630)\n- Abu-Rabia. Arabic vowels and reading comprehension. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1023291620997)\n- AVP A1 Dataset. [Link](https://lailafamiliar.github.io/A1-AVP-dataset/)\n\n### SRS Algorithms\n- FSRS-6 Algorithm. [Wiki](https://github.com/open-spaced-repetition/fsrs4anki/wiki/The-Algorithm)\n- Expertium Technical Explanation. [Blog](https://expertium.github.io/Algorithm.html)\n- FSRS Benchmark. [Link](https://expertium.github.io/Benchmark.html)\n- py-fsrs. [GitHub](https://github.com/open-spaced-repetition/py-fsrs)\n- Settles & Meeder (2016). Half-Life Regression. [PDF](https://research.duolingo.com/papers/settles.acl16.pdf)\n- SM-17 Algorithm. [SuperMemo](https://supermemo.guru/wiki/Algorithm_SM-17)\n- WaniKani SRS Stages. [Link](https://knowledge.wanikani.com/wanikani/srs-stages/)\n\n### Session Design\n- Baddeley & Longman (1978). Training postal workers. *Ergonomics*. [PDF](https://gwern.net/doc/psychology/spaced-repetition/1978-baddeley.pdf)\n- Karpicke & Roediger (2007). Expanding retrieval practice. *M&C*. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17576148/)\n- Storm, Bjork & Storm (2010). Expanding vs uniform retrieval. [PDF](https://bjorklab.psych.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/13/2016/07/Storm_Bjork_Storm_2010.pdf)\n- Hwang (2025). Undesirable difficulty of interleaving. *Language Learning*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/lang.12659)\n- Sailer & Homner (2020). Gamification meta-analysis. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-019-09498-w)\n\n### Leech Management\n- Anki Leeches Manual. [Link](https://docs.ankiweb.net/leeches.html)\n- Control-Alt-Backspace: Dealing with Leeches. [Link](https://controlaltbackspace.org/leech/)\n- Sense et al. (2016). Rate of forgetting is stable. *ToCS*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/tops.12183)\n- Zaidi et al. (2020). Adaptive forgetting curves. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7334729/)\n\n### Experimental Design\n- Lillie et al. (2011). The N-of-1 clinical trial. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3118090/)\n- Dallery et al. (2013). Single-case experimental designs. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3636286/)\n- Kruschke (2018). ROPE framework. [SAGE](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/2515245918771304)\n- Oleson (2010). Bayesian credible intervals for N-of-1. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3307549/)\n- Rafferty et al. (2019). MAB in education experiments. [JEDM](https://jedm.educationaldatamining.org/index.php/JEDM/article/view/357)\n- Baayen et al. (2008). Mixed-effects models. [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0749596X07001398)\n", "date": "Feb 12", "category": "algorithm", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["arabic-education-research-2026-02-21", "arabic-teaching-worldwide-2026-02-21", "vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/deep-research-compilation-2026-02-12.md"}, "elevenlabs-arabic-voice": {"title": "ElevenLabs Arabic TTS: Voice, Model & Settings Research", "content": "# ElevenLabs Arabic TTS: Voice, Model & Settings Research\n\nResearch for selecting the best ElevenLabs configuration for Arabic (MSA/fusha) text-to-speech aimed at language learners. Last updated: February 2026.\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Model Comparison](#1-model-comparison)\n2. [Recommended Model](#2-recommended-model)\n3. [Arabic Voices (MSA)](#3-arabic-voices-msa)\n4. [Voice Recommendations](#4-voice-recommendations)\n5. [Optimal Voice Settings](#5-optimal-voice-settings)\n6. [API Configuration](#6-api-configuration)\n7. [Known Issues & Workarounds](#7-known-issues--workarounds)\n8. [Pronunciation Dictionaries](#8-pronunciation-dictionaries)\n9. [Cost Comparison](#9-cost-comparison)\n10. [Testing Plan](#10-testing-plan)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Model Comparison\n\nElevenLabs offers several models that support Arabic. Here is a comparison relevant to our use case (clear MSA pronunciation for learners).\n\n| Model | ID | Arabic Support | Latency | Cost (credits/char) | Quality | Notes |\n|-------|----|---------------|---------|---------------------|---------|-------|\n| **Eleven v3** | `eleven_v3` | Yes (`ara`) | High (~1-3s) | 1 | Highest expressiveness, newest | Not designed for real-time; best for long-form narration. No Speaker Boost. |\n| **Multilingual v2** | `eleven_multilingual_v2` | Yes (`ar-SA`, `ar-AE`) | High | 1 | Best emotional depth, life-like | Best text normalization (numbers, dates). Most battle-tested for non-English. |\n| **Turbo v2.5** | `eleven_turbo_v2_5` | Yes | Medium (~300ms) | 0.5 | Good balance quality/speed | 3x faster than v2 for non-English. Good for conversational/real-time. |\n| **Flash v2.5** | `eleven_flash_v2_5` | Yes | Low (~75ms) | 0.5 | Decent, lowest latency | May skip text normalization. Best for real-time agents. |\n| **Flash v2 / Turbo v2** | `eleven_flash_v2` / `eleven_turbo_v2` | English only | Low/Medium | 0.5 | N/A for Arabic | Do NOT use for Arabic -- will produce English-accented output. |\n\n### Key Differences for Arabic\n\n- **Multilingual v2** has the best text normalization for numbers, dates, and symbols in non-English languages. This matters for Arabic where numeral reading rules are complex.\n- **Eleven v3** supports 70+ languages (expanded from 29 in v2) and has the highest emotional range, but is still in research preview and PVCs are not fully optimized.\n- **Turbo v2.5** is the current best balance for our use case: half the cost, good quality, acceptable latency.\n- **Flash v2.5** is cheapest/fastest but may sacrifice pronunciation accuracy for speed.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Recommended Model\n\n### Primary: `eleven_turbo_v2_5` (current choice in CLAUDE.md)\n\nThis is already configured in the project and remains a solid choice:\n- Half the cost of Multilingual v2 (0.5 vs 1.0 credits/char)\n- Good Arabic pronunciation quality\n- Fast enough for on-demand generation\n- Supports all voice settings (stability, similarity, speed, style)\n\n### Secondary/Upgrade: `eleven_multilingual_v2`\n\nConsider switching to this if:\n- Pronunciation accuracy issues are found with Turbo v2.5\n- Text contains numbers, dates, or mixed content that needs normalization\n- Cost is not a primary concern (doubles the per-character cost)\n\n### Future: `eleven_v3`\n\nMonitor this model as it matures:\n- Currently in research preview (launched June 2025)\n- Supports audio tags for expressiveness (`[whispers]`, `[excited]`, etc.)\n- Not optimized for real-time use -- higher latency\n- PVC support is limited; use IVC or designed voices\n- Stability slider works differently (Creative/Natural/Robust presets)\n- When stable, may become the best option for educational audio\n\n### Avoid\n\n- `eleven_flash_v2` / `eleven_turbo_v2` -- English only, will add English accent to Arabic\n- `eleven_flash_v2_5` -- too aggressive on latency optimization, may skip normalization steps that matter for correct Arabic pronunciation\n\n---\n\n## 3. Arabic Voices (MSA)\n\nThe following voices from the ElevenLabs voice library are tagged as **Modern Standard Arabic** (not dialectal). These are the most relevant for a language learning app.\n\n### MSA Female Voices\n\n| Voice | ID | Description |\n|-------|----|-------------|\n| **Asmaa** | `qi4PkV9c01kb869Vh7Su` | Young female, MSA accent, gentle conversational tone |\n| **Abrar Sabbah** | `VwC51uc4PUblWEJSPzeo` | Female, suitable for multiple content types |\n| **GHIZLANE** | `u0TsaWvt0v8migutHM3M` | Female, smooth, balanced, and tranquil |\n| **Mona** | `tavIIPLplRB883FzWU0V` | Female, middle-aged |\n| **Sana** | `mRdG9GYEjJmIzqbYTidv` | Female, soft quality with upbeat tone |\n\n### MSA Male Voices\n\n| Voice | ID | Description |\n|-------|----|-------------|\n| **Mohamed Ben** | `Qp2PG6sgef1EHtrNQKnf` | Young male, calming and engaging |\n| **Chaouki** | `G1HOkzin3NMwRHSq60UI` | Deep, clear male, neutral Arabic accent. Ideal for documentaries. |\n| **Anas** | `R6nda3uM038xEEKi7GFl` | Middle-aged male, gentle conversational tone |\n| **Mo Wiseman** | `DPd861uv5p8migutHM3M` | Male, YouTube and audiobooks |\n| **HMIDA** | `JjTirzdD7T3GMLkwdd3a` | Male, radio-suitable voice |\n| **Mourad Sami** | `kERwN6X2cY8g1XbfzJsX` | Male, calm tone, good for books and news |\n| **Wahab Arabic** | `ldeGOUQJqLGjlVgYn7YL` | Male, book narration |\n\n### Other Dialect Voices (for reference, NOT recommended for MSA learner app)\n\n| Dialect | Voices |\n|---------|--------|\n| Egyptian | Fathy Hammad, Haytham, Hoda, Hamza Abbas, Masry, Alice, Amr |\n| Gulf | Fares |\n| Kuwaiti | Abu Salem, Hasan |\n| Levantine | Salma |\n| Saudi | Raed |\n| Moroccan | Ghizlane (Moroccan), Hamid |\n\n---\n\n## 4. Voice Recommendations\n\nFor a language learner app prioritizing **clear, slow, standard MSA pronunciation**:\n\n### Top Pick: **Chaouki** (`G1HOkzin3NMwRHSq60UI`)\n- Deep, clear male voice\n- Described as \"neutral Arabic accent\" -- closest to textbook MSA\n- Ideal for documentaries = clear, authoritative, measured pace\n- Good for: sentence reading, vocabulary pronunciation\n\n### Runner-up Male: **Mourad Sami** (`kERwN6X2cY8g1XbfzJsX`)\n- Calm tone, described as good for books and news\n- News-reader style = clear articulation, standard pronunciation\n\n### Top Female Pick: **Asmaa** (`qi4PkV9c01kb869Vh7Su`)\n- Explicitly tagged as \"Arabic Modern Standard accent\"\n- Gentle conversational tone = not too fast, clear\n- Good for: sentence reading, conversational examples\n\n### Runner-up Female: **GHIZLANE** (`u0TsaWvt0v8migutHM3M`)\n- Smooth, balanced, tranquil\n- Good for: calm educational content\n\n### Testing Strategy\n\nAll four voices above should be tested with the same diacritized Arabic sentences before committing to one. Test with:\n1. Simple vocabulary words with all harakat\n2. Sentences with shadda (gemination)\n3. Sentences with tanwin (nunation: -an, -in, -un)\n4. Words with hamza in different positions (initial, medial, final)\n5. Long vowels vs short vowels contrast\n6. Sun letters vs moon letters with definite article\n\n---\n\n## 5. Optimal Voice Settings\n\n### For Language Learner Use Case\n\n```json\n{\n \"stability\": 0.85,\n \"similarity_boost\": 0.75,\n \"style\": 0.0,\n \"speed\": 0.8,\n \"use_speaker_boost\": true\n}\n```\n\n### Parameter Details\n\n**stability** (range: 0.0 - 1.0, default: 0.50)\n- Controls randomness between generations\n- **Set HIGH (0.80-0.90)** for learner content: consistent, predictable pronunciation\n- Lower values add emotional variability -- undesirable for educational use\n- Too high (>0.95) can sound monotone, but for word/sentence pronunciation this is acceptable\n\n**similarity_boost** (range: 0.0 - 1.0, default: 0.75)\n- Controls adherence to original voice characteristics\n- **Keep at 0.75** (default): ensures clear, undistorted output\n- Higher values (>0.85) can introduce audio artifacts\n- Lower values may lose voice clarity\n\n**style** (range: 0.0 - 1.0, default: 0.0)\n- Amplifies speaker's style/expressiveness\n- **Keep at 0.0** for educational content: no style exaggeration\n- Uses extra compute and adds latency\n- Any value above 0 is counterproductive for clear pronunciation\n\n**speed** (range: 0.7 - 1.2, default: 1.0)\n- **Set to 0.8 for sentences** (slow, learner-friendly)\n- **Set to 0.75 for individual words** (extra slow for pronunciation focus)\n- Do not go below 0.7 (minimum, may distort quality)\n- At 1.0, native-speed Arabic is too fast for beginners\n- Consider offering user-adjustable speed in the app\n\n**use_speaker_boost** (boolean, default: true)\n- Enhances overall clarity and similarity\n- **Keep enabled** for maximum clarity\n- Note: NOT available on Eleven v3\n\n### Settings Comparison by Use Case\n\n| Use Case | stability | similarity | style | speed |\n|----------|-----------|------------|-------|-------|\n| Word pronunciation | 0.90 | 0.75 | 0.0 | 0.75 |\n| Sentence reading | 0.85 | 0.75 | 0.0 | 0.80 |\n| Paragraph/story | 0.75 | 0.75 | 0.0 | 0.85 |\n| Conversational example | 0.70 | 0.75 | 0.1 | 0.90 |\n\n---\n\n## 6. API Configuration\n\n### Minimal Request (Python)\n\n```python\nimport httpx\n\nELEVENLABS_API_KEY = \"...\"\nVOICE_ID = \"G1HOkzin3NMwRHSq60UI\" # Chaouki (MSA male)\n\nasync def generate_arabic_tts(text: str, speed: float = 0.8) -> bytes:\n \"\"\"Generate Arabic TTS audio from diacritized text.\"\"\"\n url = f\"https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/text-to-speech/{VOICE_ID}\"\n\n response = await httpx.AsyncClient().post(\n url,\n headers={\n \"xi-api-key\": ELEVENLABS_API_KEY,\n \"Content-Type\": \"application/json\",\n },\n json={\n \"text\": text,\n \"model_id\": \"eleven_turbo_v2_5\",\n \"language_code\": \"ar\",\n \"voice_settings\": {\n \"stability\": 0.85,\n \"similarity_boost\": 0.75,\n \"style\": 0.0,\n \"speed\": speed,\n \"use_speaker_boost\": True,\n },\n \"apply_text_normalization\": \"on\",\n },\n timeout=30.0,\n )\n response.raise_for_status()\n return response.content # binary audio (mp3 by default)\n```\n\n### Important API Parameters\n\n| Parameter | Value | Why |\n|-----------|-------|-----|\n| `model_id` | `eleven_turbo_v2_5` | Best quality/cost balance for Arabic |\n| `language_code` | `ar` | Ensures Arabic text normalization rules apply |\n| `apply_text_normalization` | `on` | Forces normalization (numbers, dates) -- important for Arabic |\n| `output_format` | `mp3_44100_128` | Default, good quality. Use `mp3_22050_32` for smaller files |\n| `seed` | (optional) | Set for reproducible output during testing |\n\n### Output Format Options\n\n| Format | Size | Quality | Use Case |\n|--------|------|---------|----------|\n| `mp3_44100_128` | Large | High | Default, best quality |\n| `mp3_44100_64` | Medium | Good | Production app |\n| `mp3_22050_32` | Small | Acceptable | Offline cache, bandwidth-limited |\n| `pcm_16000` | Large | Lossless | Post-processing |\n\n---\n\n## 7. Known Issues & Workarounds\n\n### Diacritics (Tashkeel) Handling\n\n**Status: Generally works, but imperfect.**\n\n- ElevenLabs models are trained on a mix of diacritized and undiacritized Arabic text\n- When diacritics ARE present, the model generally respects them\n- However, the model may occasionally override diacritics with its own predicted pronunciation, especially for common words where the model has strong priors\n- No formal guarantee that all tashkeel marks will be read as written\n\n**Workaround:** Always provide fully diacritized text. This gives the model the best chance of correct pronunciation. Our pipeline already diacritizes all text via CATT, so this is covered.\n\n### Hamza Issues\n\n- Hamzat al-wasl (connecting hamza, ء) at word beginnings is generally handled correctly\n- Hamzat al-qat' (cutting hamza) is sometimes dropped or softened\n- Hamza on carriers (أ إ ؤ ئ) may be read inconsistently\n- **Workaround:** Test specific problematic words and add to pronunciation dictionary if needed\n\n### Shadda (Gemination)\n\n- Shadda (ّ) marking consonant doubling is usually pronounced correctly\n- Occasionally the gemination is too subtle or skipped entirely on less common words\n- **Workaround:** Ensure shadda is always present in the text. If a specific word consistently fails, use an alias in the pronunciation dictionary (e.g., spelling out the doubled consonant).\n\n### Tanwin (Nunation)\n\n- Tanwin fatHa (ًا), tanwin Damma (ٌ), tanwin kasra (ٍ) are generally handled\n- The distinction between tanwin and actual nun can sometimes be confused\n- **Workaround:** Ensure proper Unicode encoding of tanwin characters\n\n### English Accent Bleed\n\n- Default/premade voices (like \"Rachel\", \"Adam\") are English-native and WILL carry English accent into Arabic\n- ALWAYS use Arabic-native voices from the voice library for Arabic content\n- Using `language_code: \"ar\"` helps but does not fully prevent accent issues if the voice is English-native\n\n### Language Switching\n\n- On longer texts, the model may occasionally switch to reading Arabic text with English phonetics or vice versa\n- More common with mixed-script text (Arabic + English in same request)\n- **Workaround:** Keep requests short (single sentence or phrase). Never mix Arabic and English in the same TTS request.\n\n### Text Normalization Gaps\n\n- `apply_language_text_normalization` is currently only supported for Japanese, NOT Arabic\n- General `apply_text_normalization` helps with numbers and dates but is not Arabic-specific\n- Arabic numbers (Eastern Arabic numerals ٠١٢٣) may not be normalized correctly\n- **Workaround:** Pre-process text to spell out numbers in Arabic words before sending to TTS\n\n### Pronunciation Dictionary Limitations\n\n- SSML phoneme tags (IPA/CMU) only work with English-only models (Flash v2, Turbo v2, English v1)\n- For Arabic, you must use **alias tags** in pronunciation dictionaries instead\n- Aliases replace a word with an alternative spelling that produces the desired pronunciation\n- Up to 3 pronunciation dictionaries can be attached per request\n\n---\n\n## 8. Pronunciation Dictionaries\n\nSince SSML phoneme tags do not work for Arabic, use pronunciation dictionaries with alias rules.\n\n### Creating a PLS Dictionary for Arabic\n\n```xml\n\n\n\n \n \n مسؤول\n مسْئُول\n \n\n \n \n مُعَلِّم\n مُعَلْلِم\n \n\n\n```\n\n### Workflow\n\n1. Upload the .pls file via the ElevenLabs API (`POST /v1/pronunciation-dictionaries/add-from-file`)\n2. Reference the dictionary ID in TTS requests via `pronunciation_dictionary_locators`\n3. Build up the dictionary over time as pronunciation issues are discovered\n4. Maximum 3 dictionaries per request\n\n---\n\n## 9. Cost Comparison\n\nBased on typical Arabic sentence lengths for a learning app.\n\n### Assumptions\n- Average Arabic sentence: ~50 characters\n- Average Arabic word: ~8 characters\n- Starter plan: 30,000 credits/month ($5/month)\n- Scale plan: 100,000 credits/month ($22/month)\n\n### Per-Model Costs\n\n| Model | Credits/char | Sentences/30k credits | Words/30k credits |\n|-------|-------------|----------------------|-------------------|\n| Turbo v2.5 | 0.5 | 1,200 sentences | 7,500 words |\n| Flash v2.5 | 0.5 | 1,200 sentences | 7,500 words |\n| Multilingual v2 | 1.0 | 600 sentences | 3,750 words |\n| Eleven v3 | 1.0 | 600 sentences | 3,750 words |\n\n### Recommendation\n\nAt 0.5 credits/char, **Turbo v2.5 gives 2x the output** of Multilingual v2 for the same cost. For a personal learning app generating a few dozen sentences per day, the Starter plan (30k credits) should be sufficient with Turbo v2.5.\n\n---\n\n## 10. Testing Plan\n\nBefore finalizing the voice and model choice, run these tests:\n\n### Test Sentences (fully diacritized)\n\n```\n1. Basic: ذَهَبَ الطَّالِبُ إِلَى المَدْرَسَةِ\n (The student went to the school)\n Tests: sun letter assimilation (الطّ), kasra on ta marbuta\n\n2. Tanwin: رَأَيْتُ كِتَابًا جَدِيدًا\n (I saw a new book)\n Tests: tanwin fatha, hamza on alif\n\n3. Shadda: المُعَلِّمُ يُعَلِّمُ الأَطْفَالَ\n (The teacher teaches the children)\n Tests: shadda gemination, hamzat al-qat'\n\n4. Hamza positions: سَأَلَ عَنْ شَيْءٍ مَسْؤُولٍ\n (He asked about a responsible thing)\n Tests: hamza in different positions\n\n5. Long vs short vowels: كَتَبَ كَاتِبٌ كِتَابًا كَبِيرًا\n (A writer wrote a big book)\n Tests: short a vs long aa, short i vs long ii\n\n6. Numbers: عِنْدِي ثَلَاثَةُ كُتُبٍ\n (I have three books)\n Tests: number pronunciation\n```\n\n### Testing Procedure\n\n1. Generate each sentence with all 4 shortlisted voices (Chaouki, Mourad Sami, Asmaa, GHIZLANE)\n2. Generate each with both `eleven_turbo_v2_5` and `eleven_multilingual_v2`\n3. Compare at speed 0.8 and speed 1.0\n4. Have an Arabic speaker evaluate pronunciation accuracy\n5. Score each combination on: clarity, correctness, naturalness, pace\n\n### Test Script Location\n\nCreate `backend/scripts/test_tts_voices.py` to automate the above comparisons.\n\n---\n\n## Sources\n\n- [ElevenLabs Models Documentation](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/overview/models)\n- [ElevenLabs Arabic TTS](https://elevenlabs.io/text-to-speech/arabic)\n- [ElevenLabs Voice Settings](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/api-reference/voices/settings/get)\n- [ElevenLabs Best Practices](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/overview/capabilities/text-to-speech/best-practices)\n- [ElevenLabs Pronunciation Dictionaries](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/developers/guides/cookbooks/text-to-speech/pronunciation-dictionaries)\n- [ElevenLabs TTS API Reference](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/api-reference/text-to-speech/convert)\n- [ElevenLabs Troubleshooting](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/resources/troubleshooting)\n- [ElevenLabs Arabic Voices List](https://json2video.com/ai-voices/elevenlabs/languages/arabic/)\n- [ElevenLabs v3 Blog Post](https://elevenlabs.io/blog/eleven-v3)\n- [ElevenLabs Language Tutor Voices](https://elevenlabs.io/voice-library/language-tutor)\n- [Arabic Pronunciation Errors in AI Narration (Research Paper)](https://al-kindipublisher.com/index.php/fcsai/article/download/11517/10251)\n- [ElevenLabs Speed Control](https://elevenlabs.io/docs/agents-platform/customization/voice/speed-control)\n- [ElevenLabs Pricing](https://elevenlabs.io/pricing/api)\n", "date": null, "category": "cost", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/elevenlabs-arabic-voice.md"}, "experiment-log": {"title": "Experiment Log", "content": "# Experiment Log\n\nRunning lab notebook for Alif's learning algorithm. Each entry documents what changed, why, what we expect, and how to verify.\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-21: Restore Features Reverted by Bundled Commit 7ee81cf\n\n### Problem\nCommit `7ee81cf` (\"Add tashkeel fading, root-aware graduation boost, and learning analysis\") was a large bundled commit (24 files, 3256 insertions, 705 deletions) that legitimately added tashkeel fading and root-aware graduation, but silently reverted 10+ features across multiple files.\n\n### Reverted features restored\n1. **`sentence_selector.py`** (most impacted — 160+ lines lost):\n - Reserved intro slots (`INTRO_RESERVE_FRACTION=0.2`)\n - Within-session scaffold diversity (`SESSION_SCAFFOLD_DECAY=0.5`)\n - `compute_sentence_diversity_score()` helper\n - Rescue pass for recency-blocked words\n - Relaxed recency cutoffs (partial 2d→4h, no_idea 4h→30m)\n - `selection_info` logging on each selected sentence\n - `skip_on_demand` parameter (prefetch skips on-demand generation)\n - Function word filter in `_auto_introduce_words()`\n - Fill phase second pass for undersized sessions\n\n2. **`schemas.py`**: `selection_info` field on `SentenceReviewItem`. `InsightsOut` restored `total_sentence_reviews`, `record_intro_day`, `record_graduation_day`; removed 4 phantom fields with no backend computation.\n\n3. **`review.py`**: `skip_on_demand=not prefetch` in `build_session()` call.\n\n4. **`types.ts`**: `selection_info` on `SentenceReviewItem`. Cleaned phantom InsightsData fields.\n\n5. **`index.tsx`**: `selectionInfo` prop passed to `SentenceInfoModal`. Holistic AI explanation prompt (was changed to word-by-word).\n\n6. **`experiment-log.md`**: 10 deleted entries (Feb 18-20) covering function word fix, selection reasoning, recency relaxation, session diversity, story zero-unknown, comprehensibility gate, LLM mapping, function word restoration, LLM audit + Claude CLI migration.\n\n### Approach\nSurgical restoration: copied pre-7ee81cf `sentence_selector.py`, then re-applied tashkeel fading (LearnerSettings, per-word show_tashkeel) and today's comprehensibility gate + SAVEPOINT fixes on top. Other files fixed with targeted edits.\n\n### Verification\n- `python3 -m pytest` — 719 tests pass (including 2 regression tests for encountered/unmapped gate)\n- `selection_info` visible again in SentenceInfoModal\n- Reserved intro slots, scaffold diversity, rescue pass all active again\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-21: Comprehensibility Gate Tightening + DB Lock Resilience\n\n### What Changed\n1. **Encountered words no longer count as known scaffold**: Previously, OCR/book-imported words that were never studied counted as \"passive vocabulary\" in the comprehensibility gate. This let through sentences full of words the learner had never studied. Now only actively studied words (known/learning/lapsed, or acquiring past box 1) count.\n\n2. **Unmapped words count as unknown**: Words with `lemma_id=None` were silently excluded from the scaffold calculation, inflating the comprehensibility score. Now they're included as unknown scaffold — a sentence with 5 unmapped words and 2 known words correctly scores 2/7=29% instead of 2/2=100%.\n\n3. **DB lock resilience**: The lemma_id backfill `db.flush()` during session building now uses a SAVEPOINT. If a concurrent cron task holds the write lock, only the backfill is skipped — the session still loads instead of returning a 500 error.\n\n### Why\nUser was served \"وأسنان مدببة تلمع، ومخالب حادة تخدش، وأفواه ضخمة تزمجر\" — a sentence with ~8 unknown words. Also hit a `database is locked` 500 error during session loading.\n\n### Expected Impact\n- Significantly fewer incomprehensible sentences in review sessions\n- More on-demand generation (since fewer pre-generated sentences will pass the tighter gate)\n- No more session-loading 500 errors from DB lock contention\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-21: Morphological Patterns (Wazn) Foundation + Card Redesign\n\n### What Changed\n1. **Structured wazn data on Lemma model**: Added `wazn` (indexed VARCHAR(30)) and `wazn_meaning` (TEXT) columns. Normalized pattern names (fa'il, maf'ul, form_1-form_10, etc.). Alembic migration `x4e5f6g7h890`.\n\n2. **New API endpoints**: `/api/patterns` (list all patterns with coverage stats), `/api/patterns/{wazn}` (words by pattern), `/api/patterns/roots/{root_id}/tree` (root derivation tree grouped by pattern). Pattern family added to word detail endpoint.\n\n3. **Learn card redesign**: Removed quiz phase entirely (was: pick → quiz → done, now: pick → done). Removed example sentence section. Added pattern decomposition display (`fa'il — doer/agent`, `fa'il + ك.ت.ب (writing)`). Made etymology and mnemonic more prominent (styled info boxes instead of small italic text).\n\n4. **WordInfoCard redesign**: Removed RootGateView (was: show root siblings first, require tap to reveal meaning). Now always shows full info immediately. Removed example sentence section. Removed dimmed/unknown root siblings (only known/learning shown). Added pattern decomposition line.\n\n5. **Backfill script**: `scripts/backfill_wazn.py` — Phase 1 extracts from existing etymology_json.pattern (no LLM), Phase 2 uses LLM for remaining root-bearing lemmas.\n\n### Why\nResearch shows morphological awareness is the #1 predictor of Arabic reading comprehension. Root+pattern decomposition (e.g., مَكْتَبة = مَفْعَلة place + ك.ت.ب writing) builds vocabulary transfer. The quiz phase in Learn mode added friction without reinforcing the right thing — words get proper acquisition reviews through sentence-first review. The root gate similarly delayed useful information without clear learning benefit.\n\n### Expected Effects\n- Pattern decomposition visible during word introduction and review lookup\n- Foundation for pattern-aware word selection (future: prefer pattern diversity when introducing root siblings)\n- Foundation for pattern explorer screen and pattern-family reviews\n\n### Backfill Results\n- Phase 1 (etymology extraction): 1248 lemmas, no LLM cost\n- Phase 2 (LLM classification): 244 lemmas\n- **Total: 1492 / 1789 canonical lemmas (83%)** have wazn data\n- Remaining 297: 209 rootless (loanwords/particles), 88 no recognizable pattern\n\n### Verification\n- `SELECT wazn, COUNT(*) FROM lemmas WHERE wazn IS NOT NULL GROUP BY wazn` — verify backfill coverage\n- Learn mode: introduce a word, verify no quiz step, verify pattern decomposition shows\n- Review: miss a word, verify no root gate, verify info shows immediately with pattern line\n- Backend: `python3 -m pytest` — 717 tests pass\n- Frontend: `npx tsc --noEmit` — clean\n\n### Follow-up Check (~2026-02-24)\n- Are pattern decomposition lines showing up during learn and review? Do they feel helpful?\n- Is removing the root gate OK? Does always-show-meaning feel better or worse?\n- Is removing the quiz from Learn mode fine? Are acquisition reviews picking up the slack?\n- Are root sibling introductions still spaced well (1-3 day clustering)?\n- Check graduation rates — did removing the quiz change acquisition speed?\n- Run `scripts/learning_analysis.py` to compare metrics before/after\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-20: Fix Function Words Stuck in Acquisition\n\n### Problem\nFunction words (في, جدّاً, كلّ, هذا, etc.) were entering acquisition via `select_next_words()` which had no function word filter. Once in acquisition, the review loop's `continue` at `sentence_review_service.py:149` skipped them from all review processing — including `submit_acquisition_review()` — so their `times_seen` never incremented and they could never graduate. في was stuck in box 2 despite being seen 148 times.\n\n### Fix\n1. Added `_is_function_word()` filter to `select_next_words()` candidates (primary)\n2. Added same filter to `_auto_introduce_words()` (belt-and-suspenders)\n3. Data fix: graduated 16 acquiring + 7 encountered function words to \"known\"\n\n### Impact\n- 16 fewer acquiring words competing for session slots\n- ~80 function words now correctly excluded from introduction candidates\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-20: Selection Reasoning Transparency\n\n### Change\nEach `SentenceReviewItem` now includes a `selection_info` dict exposing why the sentence was chosen for the session. The `SentenceInfoModal` (opened by tapping sentence info during review) displays this at the top of the modal.\n\n### Fields\n- `reason`: greedy_cover | acquisition_repeat | on_demand | fill_intro\n- `score`: final weighted score from greedy set cover\n- `order`: pick order (1st, 2nd, 3rd...)\n- `word_reason`: human-readable primary word state (e.g. \"Learning (stability 2d)\", \"Acquiring (box 2)\")\n- `components`: per-factor breakdown (due_coverage, difficulty_match, grammar_fit, diversity, freshness, source_bonus, session_diversity, rescue)\n\n### Purpose\nDebugging and understanding the session builder's decisions. No algorithm change — purely observational.\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-20: Recency Filter Relaxation & Rescue Pass\n\n### Problem\nSessions varied wildly: 7 cards one session, 25 the next. Production diagnostics revealed:\n- 13 of 52 due words (25%) had ALL their sentences blocked by the recency filter\n- The recency cutoffs were too aggressive for failed sentences: `partial` waited 2 days, `grammar_confused` 1 day, `no_idea` 4 hours — even though the learner would benefit from retrying the same sentence sooner\n- Words with only \"understood\" sentences were dropped entirely even when due\n- `MAX_ACQUISITION_EXTRA_SLOTS=15` inflated sessions with many acquiring words to 25 cards\n\n### Changes\n1. **Relaxed non-understood recency cutoffs**: `partial` 2d→4h, `grammar_confused` 1d→2h, `no_idea` 4h→30min. Rationale: if you failed a sentence, seeing it again quickly lets you get a positive review, then ideally see the same word in a different sentence next time.\n2. **Rescue pass for recency-blocked words**: After the main sentence query, any due word with zero fresh sentences gets its stale sentences fetched without recency filter. These participate in the greedy set cover with a 0.3× score penalty (fresh sentences always preferred, but stale beats dropping the word).\n\n### Expected Effect\n- More consistent session sizes (fewer 5-card sessions where words were blocked)\n- Failed sentences re-shown quickly for positive reinforcement\n- No words dropped from session just because their sentences were all understood recently\n\n### Verify\n- Compare session sizes over next 2 days (should see less 5-7 range)\n- Check `sentence_selected` events for rescue sentences (shown_at within last 4 days)\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-19: Session Diversity & Continuous Word Introduction\n\n### Problem\nAnalysis of today's sessions revealed: only 2 words introduced all day (both collateral) despite 93.9% accuracy. Same scaffold words dominated every session: طالب (31/304 sentences), أستاذ (26), لون (24), جميل (24). Known word جميلة reviewed 8 times in one day. Auto-intro never fired because `slots_needed = max(0, limit - due_count)` was always 0 with 60+ due words.\n\nTwo root causes: (1) auto-intro only fires when session is undersized, which never happens when enough words are due; (2) no within-session scaffold diversity tracking, so greedy set cover picks sentences with the same high-frequency scaffold words every time.\n\n### Changes\n1. **Reserved intro slots** (`sentence_selector.py`): `INTRO_RESERVE_FRACTION = 0.2` — always reserves 20% of session (2 of 10 slots) for new word introductions when accuracy allows, independent of due count. New `_get_accuracy_intro_slots()` helper. Backward-compat: still fires when undersized too.\n\n2. **Within-session scaffold diversity** (`sentence_selector.py`): `SESSION_SCAFFOLD_DECAY = 0.5` — tracks scaffold words selected in current session. Each reuse multiplies score by 0.5 (1st→1.0x, 2nd→0.5x, 3rd→0.25x). Prevents same words (طالب, أستاذ) from appearing in every sentence.\n\n3. **Tighter generation diversity** (`sentence_generator.py`): `DIVERSITY_SENTENCE_THRESHOLD` 15→10, `MAX_AVOID_WORDS` 20→30, threshold `median*2`→`median*1.5`. Stronger LLM avoid instruction: \"Do NOT use — will cause rejection\" (was \"try NOT to use\"). Step C in `update_material.py` now passes avoid_words (was missing).\n\n4. **Diversity score logging** (`sentence_selector.py`): `compute_sentence_diversity_score()` logs `diversity_score`, `scaffold_uniqueness`, `scaffold_freshness` per sentence_selected event.\n\n5. **Pipeline cap 300→600** (`material_generator.py`, `update_material.py`): 270 active words but most had only 1 sentence — 300 cap was blocking generation of needed second sentences. 21 words had zero active sentences. Ideal is 2 per word = 540 minimum.\n\n6. **Generate-then-write DB pattern** (`material_generator.py`, `update_material.py`): Refactored `generate_material_for_word()`, `warm_sentence_cache()`, `generate_sentences_for_word()`, and `step_backfill_sentences()` to close DB before LLM calls and reopen briefly for writes. Previously, Claude CLI calls (15-30s) held the DB session open, exceeding the 15s busy_timeout and causing \"database is locked\" errors that blocked user sessions and reviews.\n\n### Expected Effect\n- 2+ new words introduced per session even with full due queue\n- Much less repetition of scaffold words across sentences in a session\n- New sentences generated with better vocabulary diversity\n- Monitoring via diversity_score metrics in interaction logs\n- ~230 additional sentences generated to fill gaps (most words had only 1 sentence)\n\n### Verify\n- Check interaction logs for `auto_introduce` events (should appear now!)\n- Check `sentence_selected` events for diversity_score values\n- Compare scaffold word distribution in next day's sessions vs today's baseline\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-19: Story Generation — Zero Unknown Words + Self-Correction\n\n### Problem\nGenerated stories allowed up to 5 unknown words (MAX_NEW_WORDS_IN_STORY=5) and accepted 70% vocabulary compliance (STORY_COMPLIANCE_THRESHOLD=70%). Stories also included Leitner box 1 words (seen once, not yet reinforced) in the vocabulary pool. This meant stories could contain words the learner barely recognized.\n\nThe retry loop was also wasteful — on compliance failure, it regenerated the entire story from scratch instead of fixing just the offending words.\n\n### Changes\n1. **`_get_known_words()` excludes box 1**: Only acquiring words in Leitner box 2+ (plus learning/known) are eligible for story vocabulary. Box 1 words are too fresh.\n2. **100% compliance required**: Zero unknown words allowed. Compliance checked against the known-words set (not just \"exists in DB\").\n3. **Self-correction loop replaces retry-from-scratch**: Generate once, then iteratively ask the LLM to replace unknown words (up to 3 correction rounds). Correction prompt gets the full vocabulary list (not the 500-word sample) for maximum replacement options. Lower temperature (0.3) for corrections vs generation (0.9).\n4. **Removed `MAX_NEW_WORDS_IN_STORY` and `STORY_COMPLIANCE_THRESHOLD` constants**.\n\n### Expected Effect\n- Stories are fully comprehensible — every content word is one the learner knows\n- Self-correction is more token-efficient than regeneration (only the offending words change)\n- Correction prompt sees full vocab, not just the generation sample, so has more options\n\n### Verification\n- Generate a story, verify `readiness_pct` is ~100% for known words\n- Check logs for correction rounds (should converge in 1-2 rounds typically)\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-19: Tighten Comprehensibility Gate & Fix Book Lemma Mapping\n\n### Problem\n1. Book sentences shown in sessions had too many unknown words (5-7 per sentence). The comprehensibility gate (≥60% known scaffold) was passing these because `encountered` words counted as \"known\" — but the learner had only *seen* them, never studied them.\n2. Sentence 3477 (\"وأسنان مدببة تلمع\") had \"تَلْمَعُ\" (to glisten) incorrectly mapped to lemma \"مَعَ\" (with). NLP stripped the ت-prefix, matched remaining لمع to مع. No lemma for لَمَعَ existed in DB.\n3. Book import pipeline does NOT run LLM mapping verification (`verify_word_mappings_llm()`), unlike the sentence generation pipeline.\n\n### Changes\n1. **Excluded `encountered` from comprehensibility gate** — only `known`, `learning`, `lapsed`, and `acquiring` (box≥2) count as known scaffold. Encountered words are no longer passive vocab in the gate.\n2. **Created lemma 2191** (لَمَعَ, \"to glisten, to gleam\") and remapped sentence_word in sentence 3477.\n\n### Expected Impact\n- Book sentences will only appear when the learner has actively studied ≥60% of scaffold words\n- Slower book progression but much more comprehensible sessions\n\n### Verification\n- `python3 -m pytest` — 704 tests passing\n- Next session should have fewer unknown words per sentence\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-19: LLM Mapping Verification for Book Imports\n\n### Problem\nBook import pipeline (`book_import_service.py`) created sentence_words without LLM verification of lemma mappings. The sentence generation pipeline had this (`verify_word_mappings_llm()`), but book imports and `store_multi_target_sentence()` did not. This caused bad mappings like \"تلمع\" → \"مع\", \"بيدها\" → \"باد\", \"روزي\" → \"روز\".\n\n### Changes\n1. **Added LLM verification to `_create_book_sentences()`** — bad mappings nulled out (not discarded)\n2. **Added LLM verification to `store_multi_target_sentence()`** — discards sentence if bad mappings found\n3. **Scanned existing book sentences** — found and nulled 15 bad mappings across 9 sentences\n\n### Impact\n- All sentence creation paths now have consistent LLM mapping verification\n- ~$0.001 Gemini API cost per sentence verified\n- ~20% false positive rate on conjugated forms (null mapping safer than wrong mapping)\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-19: Function Word Restoration & Book Word Count Dedup\n\n### Problem\nBook page 1 showed 1 remaining word (`بِهِ` → lemma `بِ`, a preposition) that would never be introduced. Additionally, 43 book words across pages 3-20 had lemma_ids but no UserLemmaKnowledge records, making them invisible to the word selector. Story word counts were also inflated (counting every token, not unique lemmas).\n\n### Root Cause\n1. `FUNCTION_WORDS` set was intentionally emptied months ago (\"all words are learnable\"), so `_is_function_word()` always returned False. Function words like بِ, في, من were counted as \"words to learn\" in books.\n2. Book import created lemmas but some ULK records were missing (likely from an older code version).\n3. `_recalculate_story_counts()` counted every token, not unique lemmas.\n\n### Changes\n1. **Populated `FUNCTION_WORDS`** from `FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES` keys (~80 particles, prepositions, pronouns, conjunctions)\n2. **Added lemma-level function word detection** in `_create_story_words()` — checks resolved lemma bare form too (catches cliticized forms like بِهِ → بِ)\n3. **Deduped `_recalculate_story_counts()`** — counts unique lemmas, not tokens\n4. **Created 41 missing ULK records** as \"encountered\" for book words\n5. **Added cron Step G** (book ULK consistency) — safety net that creates missing ULK every 6h\n\n### Impact\n- Book \"Rosie\": total 238→123 unique lemmas, unknown 54→48\n- Function words excluded from: book to-learn counts, page word introduction, FSRS review credit, scaffold diversity checks\n- 94 StoryWords marked as function words across all stories\n- Word selector can now see all book words and will introduce them in strict page order\n\n### Verification\n- `python3 -m pytest` — 704 tests passing\n- Check book page status: all pages should have 0 `no_ulk` content words\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-19: LLM Usage Audit & Task-Type Logging\n\n### Problem\nNo visibility into which LLM tasks consume the most API budget. Also discovered `update_material.py` cron crashing since Feb 17 18:30 due to `None` stability in `fsrs_card_json`.\n\n### Changes\n1. **Bug fix**: `update_material.py:531` — `card.get(\"stability\", 0)` → `(card.get(\"stability\") or 0)` to handle `None` values\n2. **task_type logging**: Added `task_type` parameter to `_log_call()` and `generate_completion()` in `llm.py`. Tagged all 18 callers across 10 service files.\n3. **Audit script**: New `scripts/audit_llm_usage.py` — parses call logs, estimates costs, infers task types for untagged entries.\n\n### Key Findings (Feb 8-19 server data, 15,686 calls)\n\n**Corrected cost estimates** (Gemini 3 Flash: $0.50/M input, $3.00/M output):\n\n| Model | Calls | Est. Cost | Main Use |\n|-------|-------|-----------|----------|\n| Gemini Flash | 12,388 ok / 513 fail | $10-15 | Sentence gen, enrichment, quality |\n| GPT-5.2 | 1,791 ok | $3-5 | One-time scripts (Feb 11-12 redesign) |\n| Haiku (API) | 963 ok / 31 fail | $0.34 | Quality review gate |\n| **Total** | **15,142 ok** | **~$14-20** | |\n\n- **Gemini Flash is ~75% of cost**, not GPT-5.2 as initially thought\n- GPT-5.2 calls were from one-time scripts (`verify_sentences.py`, `generate_sentences.py`) run during Feb 11-12 redesign — they default to `--model openai`\n- Flag evaluation itself: only 9 flags total, ~18 GPT-5.2 calls (negligible)\n- **Ongoing daily cost** (excluding one-time scripts): ~$1-1.50/day for Gemini Flash\n- Enrichment calls (forms, etymology, etc.) are high-volume (6,263) but cheap (<$0.10 total)\n- Sentence gen + quality review are the main ongoing cost drivers\n- 230 OCR vision calls not included in cost estimate (image token pricing)\n- `update_material.py` cron had been crashing since Feb 17 — fixed and deployed\n\n### Phase 2: Benchmark Results (same day)\n\n**Quick wins done**: 4 scripts changed `--model openai` → `gemini`. Audit script pricing fixed.\n\n**Benchmark** (`scripts/benchmark_claude_code.py`): Compared Gemini Flash, Sonnet CLI, Haiku CLI across 4 tasks.\n\n| Task | Winner | Rationale |\n|------|--------|-----------|\n| Sentence gen | Gemini (keep) | 2.8s/word vs 32s. Sonnet 87% vs 73% pass rate, but difference is mostly validator false negatives (conjugated forms not in forms_json) |\n| Quality gate | Haiku API (keep) | 90% approval, good balance. CLI Haiku unexpectedly strict at 60% |\n| Forms | Gemini (keep) | All models 100% match. Gemini 5-10x faster |\n| Memory hooks | **Sonnet CLI (switch)** | Better mnemonics + more cognates. Low volume task. |\n\n**Key insight**: The biggest quality improvement isn't model switching — it's expanding `forms_json` with more conjugation patterns to reduce validator false negatives. Both Gemini and Sonnet produce natural Arabic; the validator rejects valid sentences because it doesn't recognize conjugated forms.\n\n**Sonnet batch mode**: Sending multiple words in one CLI call cuts per-word time from 32s → 16s (50% reduction), but quality matched Gemini instead of exceeding it. Prompt tuning needed.\n\n### Phase 3: Claude CLI Integration (same day, continued session)\n\n**Goal**: Actually switch background LLM work to Claude CLI (free) — the whole point of the investigation.\n\n**Changes**:\n1. **`llm.py`**: Added `_generate_via_claude_cli()` — shells out to `claude -p --output-format json`. New model overrides: `claude_sonnet`, `claude_haiku` dispatched via `CLAUDE_CLI_MODELS` dict. Handles JSON parsing, markdown fence stripping, timeout, logging.\n2. **Sentence gen (cron)**: `update_material.py --model` default changed from `gemini` to `claude_sonnet`. Threaded `model_override` through `step_backfill_sentences()` → `generate_validated_sentences_multi_target()`.\n3. **Quality gate**: `review_sentences_quality()` switched from `anthropic` (paid Haiku API) to `claude_haiku` (free CLI).\n4. **Enrichment**: `lemma_enrichment.py` forms + etymology both switched to `claude_haiku`.\n5. **Memory hooks**: `memory_hooks.py` switched from `anthropic` to `claude_haiku`.\n6. **On-demand paths kept on Gemini**: `generate_material_for_word()` defaults to `\"gemini\"`, `warm_sentence_cache()` defaults to `\"gemini\"` — both are user-facing/latency-sensitive.\n7. **Claude CLI on server**: Authenticated via `claude setup-token` with Max plan.\n\n**Architecture**: Two-tier model strategy. Background/cron → Claude CLI (free, ~15-30s). On-demand → Gemini Flash (fast, ~1-2s, paid).\n\n### Remaining Future Work\n- Expand forms_json validator (reduce false negatives for all models)\n- Switch stories to Opus CLI (already proven via `generate_story_claude.py`)\n- Agentic enrichment prototype (one session for forms+etymology+hooks)\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-18: Fix Prefetch Storm Causing Single-Card Sessions\n\n### Problem\nSessions producing only 1 card despite many due words. Occurred intermittently, especially during active review.\n\n### Root Cause\nTwo compounding issues:\n1. **7 prefetch requests per session cycle**: `deepPrefetchSessions` (2 requests) fired from `getSentenceReviewSession` cache hit, cache miss, and session-complete screen. Plus `prefetchSessions` near end of session. All called `build_session()` with on-demand generation (DB writes).\n2. **No rollback after DB lock failure**: When concurrent writes hit SQLite's write lock (even with 15s busy_timeout), `db.flush()` raised `OperationalError`. The `try/except` in `_with_fallbacks` caught it, but the SQLAlchemy session was left in `PendingRollbackError` state. All subsequent queries (fill phase, auto-intro) crashed, returning whatever items existed before the failure (often 1 card).\n\n### Changes\n1. **Reduced to 1 prefetch per session cycle**: Single `prefetchSessions()` call on session-complete screen only. Removed prefetches from `getSentenceReviewSession` (both cache hit and miss paths) and near-end-of-session. The prefetch does full generation so cached sessions have complete data.\n2. **Added `db.rollback()` after on-demand generation failures** in Phase 1 of `_with_fallbacks()`. Prevents cascading PendingRollbackError.\n3. **Wrapped entire fill phase in try/except with `db.rollback()`**: `_auto_introduce_words` could hit UNIQUE constraint when concurrent requests introduced the same word. This cascaded into PendingRollbackError that crashed the grammar feature check, producing 500 errors.\n4. **Fixed grammar card duplication**: Prefetched sessions built before the user dismissed a grammar card would re-show the same feature. Added module-level `introducedGrammarKeys` Set in the frontend that tracks dismissed features and filters them from cached sessions.\n5. **Removed `deepPrefetchSessions`** from index.tsx imports (no longer called).\n\n### Session Flow (after fix)\n1. Session complete → 1 prefetch fires (full build_session with on-demand gen) → cached in AsyncStorage\n2. User taps \"Next Session\" → cached session returned instantly, no server call\n3. Cache miss (first launch, network failure) → fresh `build_session` call with on-demand gen\n\n### Expected Effects\n- No more single-card sessions from DB lock cascading or UNIQUE constraint crashes\n- No more repeated grammar cards across sessions\n- Reduced server load (1 vs 7 requests per session)\n- Slight delay possible on very first session (no cache), but all subsequent sessions instant\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-18: Fix Shrinking Session Sizes (Cooldown + On-Demand Gate)\n\n### Problem\nSessions shrank from 10-14 cards to 2-5 cards over 2 days. User noticed progressively fewer cards per session, especially when reviewing multiple sessions within a few hours.\n\n### Diagnosis\n1. **16/32 due words had zero sentences passing the recency filter.** The \"understood\" cooldown (7d) combined with only 1-3 active sentences per word meant once shown and rated, words had no eligible sentences left for the rest of the week.\n2. **On-demand generation was gated incorrectly.** `_with_fallbacks()` checked `len(items) < limit`, but items included acquisition repetition extras (up to +15). With 8 set-cover items + 4 acquisition reps = 12 items > limit(10), on-demand generation for uncovered words was skipped entirely.\n3. **Pipeline over cap** at 322/300, blocking warm-cache pre-generation (warm cache skips at 310).\n\n### Data Snapshot (pre-fix)\n- 48/67 candidate sentences failed recency, 19 passed\n- Recency failures: 26 understood (7d cooldown), 22 partial (2d cooldown)\n- Recent sessions: 4, 5, 5, 2, 3, 7 cards (today)\n- 2-day accuracy: 91.6% (auto-intro would grant 7 slots, but no room)\n\n### Changes\n1. **Reduced \"understood\" cooldown: 7d → 4d** (`sentence_selector.py` line 343). Still provides meaningful spacing, frees up sentences shown 4+ days ago.\n2. **Fixed on-demand generation gate** (`sentence_selector.py`): Track `base_item_count` (pre-acquisition-repetition) and use it for on-demand budget in `_with_fallbacks()`. Acquisition repetition no longer blocks on-demand generation for uncovered words.\n3. **Ran sentence rotation + backfill**: Pipeline back to 300 (was 322). 12 new multi-target sentences generated.\n\n### Expected Effects\n- Sessions should consistently produce 10+ items\n- Due words without pre-existing sentences get on-demand coverage\n- Sentence pool stays healthier with shorter cooldown recycling\n\n### What to Check (Feb 20-22)\n- **Session sizes**: Should be consistently 8-12+ items. Check via: `ssh alif \"docker exec alif-backend-1 python3 -c \\\"from app.database import SessionLocal; from app.models import SentenceReviewLog; from sqlalchemy import func; db=SessionLocal(); print(db.query(SentenceReviewLog.session_id, func.count()).filter(SentenceReviewLog.reviewed_at >= '2026-02-18').group_by(SentenceReviewLog.session_id).all())\\\"\"`\n- **On-demand generation**: Check interaction logs for `on_demand_generated` events. If zero, the gate fix may not be reaching production usage.\n- **Pipeline cap**: Should stay ≤300. If creeping up again, the 6h cron (rotate + update_material) may need tuning.\n- **Sentence variety**: With 4d cooldown, sentences cycle back faster. Watch for user fatigue from seeing the same sentences too often. If so, increase MIN_SENTENCES_PER_WORD from 2 to 3.\n- **Accuracy**: Verify 2-day accuracy stays >85%. If the shorter cooldown causes over-reviewing easy sentences, accuracy could inflate artificially.\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-18: Lemma Lookup Two-Pass Fix + LLM Mapping Verification\n\n### Changes\n1. **Two-pass `build_lemma_lookup()`** (`sentence_validator.py`): Pass 1 registers all lemma bare forms, Pass 2 registers forms_json derived forms. Direct bare forms always take priority. Previously, forms_json entries could shadow other lemmas' bare forms depending on iteration order.\n2. **Improved LLM verification prompt** (`sentence_validator.py`): Rewritten to reduce false positives — explicitly allows morphological derivations (conjugations, plurals, possessives) and only flags genuine semantic mismatches. Reduced false positive rate from ~16% to 0%.\n3. **LLM verification now active** (`VERIFY_MAPPINGS_LLM=1` was already in .env): Sentences with any flagged bad mapping are discarded and regenerated.\n\n### Bug Found\nUser reported حَوْلَ (\"around\") showing as حَال (\"to change\") in sentence popup. Root cause: lemma #890 (حال) had `masdar: حَوْل` in forms_json, which was registered in the lookup before lemma #2033 (حول, \"around\") — first-one-wins. 171 total shadow cases found across the DB.\n\n### Data Fixes\n- 4 sentence_words: حَوْلَ remapped from #890 (حال) → #2033 (حول)\n- 3 sentence_words: عالم remapped from #722 (عَلِمَ) → #723 (عَالَم)\n\n### Audit Results (50 recent sentences)\n- Pre-fix LLM verification: 28% flagged (many false positives from old prompt)\n- Post-fix + improved prompt: 10% flagged, 0% false positives\n- Remaining flags are genuine homographs (كَتَبَ/كُتُب, أَكَلَ/أَكْل, بِأَنَّ/بَانَ) that need contextual disambiguation\n\n### Expected Effects\n- No more wrong lemma popups from forms_json shadows\n- ~10% of newly generated sentences will be discarded for homograph mapping issues and regenerated\n- Pipeline throughput effectively unchanged (100+ sentences/day, plenty of headroom)\n\n---\n\n## 2026-02-18: Leitner System Review Enhancements\n\n### Changes\n1. **Scaffold freshness tuning** (`sentence_selector.py`): `FRESHNESS_BASELINE` 8→5 (penalty kicks in sooner) and floor 0.3→0.1 (allows much stronger suppression of over-used words). Before: jameel at 200 reviews → 30% sentence score. After: → 10%.\n2. **`entered_acquiring_at` field** (`models.py` + migration `v2c3d4e5f678`): Tracks when each word entered the acquisition pipeline, enabling flow visualization.\n3. **Pipeline due counts**: `_count_due_cards()` now includes acquiring words (`acquisition_next_due <= now`) in the `due_today` total, giving a true picture of cards waiting.\n4. **Memory hooks model upgrade**: Now uses Claude Haiku (via `model_override=\"anthropic\"`) instead of the default Gemini Flash fallback chain.
