Detects Python automatically.\n\n#### Railway -- ~$10-15/mo\n\nRailway has the smoothest developer experience but is the most expensive option for\nalways-on workloads because RAM is billed at $10/GB/month.\n\n**Pros:**\n- Best DX: `railway up` or git-push deploys\n- Persistent volumes for SQLite ($0.25/GB/mo)\n- Auto-detects Python/FastAPI, no Dockerfile needed\n- Nice dashboard and logs\n\n**Cons:**\n- $5/mo subscription fee regardless of usage\n- RAM billed at $10/GB/month -- a 1 GB app running 24/7 costs ~$10 in RAM alone\n- Total cost for this workload: $5 (sub) + ~$10 (1GB RAM) = ~$15/mo\n- Exceeds budget significantly\n\n### Tier 3: Workable but Compromised\n\n#### Render Starter -- ~$9-10/mo\n\n**Pros:**\n- Git-push deploys, clean UI\n- Persistent disk available on paid plans ($0.25/GB/mo)\n\n**Cons:**\n- Starter plan only has 512 MB RAM -- likely too tight for CAMeL Tools\n- Standard plan ($25/mo) is well over budget\n- Free tier has no persistent disk and spins down after inactivity\n- Cold starts after spin-down would reload 300 MB of model data (slow)\n\n**Verdict:** 512 MB is risky for this workload. Standard plan is too expensive.\n\n#### DigitalOcean -- $6/mo (1 GB)\n\n**Pros:**\n- Simple, well-documented VPS\n- 1 GB droplet at $6/mo with 25 GB SSD\n- Per-second billing (2026)\n- Extensive tutorials for Python deployment\n\n**Cons:**\n- More expensive than Hetzner for less resources\n- Same VPS management burden without Coolify\n- $4/mo droplet (512 MB) is too small for CAMeL Tools\n\n#### AWS Lightsail -- $5-10/mo\n\n**Pros:**\n- Familiar AWS ecosystem\n- Predictable flat pricing\n- Free tier for first 3 months ($5 plan)\n\n**Cons:**\n- $5/mo plan has only 512 MB RAM (too tight)\n- $10/mo plan (1 GB) is at the budget ceiling\n- Less value than Hetzner (1 GB vs 4 GB for similar price)\n- AWS console overhead\n\n### Tier 4: Not Recommended\n\n#### Google Cloud Run\n\nCloud Run's ephemeral containers are fundamentally incompatible with SQLite. Workarounds\nexist (GCS FUSE, NFS via Filestore) but add complexity, latency, and cost. Filestore\nalone starts at $200/mo. Cold starts with 300 MB model data would be painful.\n\n**Verdict:** Wrong tool for this job.\n\n## Recommendation\n\n### Primary: Hetzner CAX11 + Coolify -- ~$4/mo\n\nThis is the clear winner. For EUR 3.79/month you get a full ARM VPS with 4 GB RAM,\n2 vCPUs, and 40 GB storage. Install [Coolify](https://coolify.io) on it for free to\nget a Heroku-like deployment experience with git-push deploys through a web UI.\n\n**Setup (one-time, ~30 minutes):**\n\n```bash\n# 1. Create CAX11 instance on Hetzner Cloud dashboard\n# 2. SSH in and install Coolify\ncurl -fsSL https://cdn.coollabs.io/coolify/install.sh | bash\n\n# 3. Open Coolify web UI (https://your-server-ip:8000)\n# 4. Connect your GitHub repo\n# 5. Configure environment variables\n# 6. Deploy\n```\n\nAfter setup, deploying is just pushing to your configured branch.\n\n**Why this wins:**\n- 4 GB RAM is very comfortable (CAMeL Tools + SQLite + headroom)\n- Persistent filesystem by default (it is a VPS)\n- Coolify gives you git-push deploys, SSL, logs, and monitoring for free\n- $4/mo is well under the $5/mo target\n- Always-on, no cold starts, no spin-downs\n- Full control over the server\n\n### Fallback: Fly.io -- ~$7-8/mo\n\nIf you prefer a managed PaaS and want to avoid any server management, Fly.io is the\nbest PaaS option for SQLite workloads. The `fly deploy` command is simple, and persistent\nvolumes work well. Budget is slightly over the $5 target but within the $10 ceiling.\n\n### Worth a shot: Oracle Cloud Free Tier -- $0/mo\n\nTry to provision an Always Free ARM instance. If it works, you get far more resources\nthan you need for literally zero cost. But have Hetzner as your fallback because\nprovisioning can be unreliable.\n\n## Quick Decision Matrix\n\n| Priority | Choose |\n|---|---|\n| Cheapest possible | Oracle Cloud Free (if you can get it) |\n| Best value with easy deploys | **Hetzner CAX11 + Coolify ($4/mo)** |\n| Zero server management | Fly.io ($7-8/mo) |\n| Absolute simplest DX | Railway ($15/mo, over budget) |\n", "date": null, "category": "cost", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/hosting-options.md"}, "inn-arabic-heritage-language": {"title": "Arabic Heritage Language Education Research at INN University, Hamar", "content": "# Arabic Heritage Language Education Research at INN University, Hamar\n\nResearchers at INN University (Universitetet i Innlandet), Hamar campus, working on Arabic language learning and heritage language education. Potential collaborators or sources of insight for Alif's pedagogical design.\n\n## Researchers\n\n### Jonas Yassin Iversen — Professor of Pedagogy\n- Email: jonas.iversen@inn.no\n- Faculty of Teacher Education and Pedagogy, Hamar\n- Director of the Diversity in Education (DivE) research group\n- Co-coordinator, ECER Network 31 on Language and Education\n- Norwegian editor of journal *Nordand*\n- PhD in Pedagogy (INN, 2020), MA in subject didactics (NTNU)\n- [INN profile](https://www.inn.no/finn-en-ansatt/jonas-iversen.html)\n\n### Lana Amro — PhD Candidate\n- Email: lana.amro@inn.no\n- Campus Hamar, Room 2P328\n- PhD project: \"Arabic-speaking students' translinguistic and transcultural identity development through digital Arabic language learning\"\n- Supervised by Iversen\n- Background as bilingual subject teacher and Arabic heritage language teacher\n- MA in adapted education (INN)\n- [INN profile](https://www.inn.no/english/find-an-employee/lana-amro.html)\n\n## Key Publications (2024–2025)\n\n### 1. Transculturation in Arabic Literacy Education (Iversen, 2025)\n**\"Transculturation in Arabic literacy education within and beyond mainstream education in Norway and Sweden\"**\nEuropean Educational Research Journal. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1177/14749041241235718)\n\n**Method:** 8 weeks of ethnographic fieldwork in each country — a supplementary heritage language school in Norway and three mainstream schools in Sweden (two Arabic heritage language teachers).\n\n**Findings:**\n- Sweden integrates Arabic heritage language education into mainstream schools. This leads Arabic instruction to align closely with official Swedish literacy expectations.\n- Norway organizes it as supplementary (weekend) schools. This preserves more traditional Arabic teaching approaches.\n- The organizational model shapes how transculturation (cultural transformation across boundaries) plays out in the classroom.\n\n**Relevance to Alif:** The tension between traditional Arabic literacy pedagogy (rote, grammar-translation) and modern communicative approaches mirrors our design choices about how to teach vocabulary and reading. Students in the Norwegian supplementary model were more likely to experience a disconnect between how they learn Arabic and how they learn at school.\n\n### 2. Policies, Conditions and Student Experiences in Arabic and Sámi Heritage Language Education (Iversen, 2025)\n**\"Policies, conditions and student experiences in Arabic and Sámi heritage language education\"**\nScandinavian Journal of Educational Research. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1080/00313831.2025.2600956)\n\n**Method:** 10 weeks of ethnographic fieldwork comparing Sámi heritage language education (in public schools) and Arabic heritage language education (in a complementary weekend school) in Norway.\n\n**Findings:**\n- Both Arabic and Sámi heritage language education are marginalized forms of education.\n- Sámi students reported encouragement from peers and pride in their heritage.\n- Arabic students reported **keeping their heritage language education secret** from their mainstream school peers.\n- Heritage language education organized *within* mainstream schooling fosters more positive student attitudes than when organized *outside* it.\n\n**Relevance to Alif:** The stigma finding is striking — Arabic learners hiding their language study from peers. A private, self-directed app like Alif may actually be an advantage here, removing the social visibility issue entirely. Also relevant to how we think about motivation and identity in the learning experience.\n\n### 3. Translanguaging in Transnational Digital Arabic Heritage Language Education (Amro & Iversen, 2025)\n**\"Translanguaging in transnational digital Arabic heritage language education\"**\nEuropean Educational Research Journal. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1177/14749041241311958)\n\n**Method:** 7 months of digital linguistic ethnography — online classroom observations, interviews with teacher and students, document collection. Studied a heritage language teacher of Arabic conducting classes digitally across borders.\n\n**Findings:**\n- The teacher used translanguaging (drawing on students' full multilingual repertoires — Arabic, Norwegian, other languages) as a deliberate pedagogical strategy.\n- Digital format enables transnational heritage language education, connecting Arabic-speaking students across countries.\n- Translanguaging created a \"safe space\" and supported literacy development in Modern Standard Arabic.\n- Students' diverse linguistic resources were treated as assets, not interference.\n\n**Relevance to Alif:** Validates our approach of showing English translations alongside Arabic (a form of translanguaging scaffolding). Also relevant to the question of whether to include transliteration — research here suggests leveraging all available linguistic resources is productive, not a crutch. The digital/transnational aspect is directly relevant since Alif is a digital tool.\n\n### 4. Pre-service Teachers' Agency to Enact Multilingual Pedagogies (Iversen, 2025)\n**\"Pre-service teachers' agency to enact multilingual pedagogies: A longitudinal case study\"**\nEuropean Educational Research Journal. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1177/14749041241290381)\n\n**Method:** Longitudinal case study — interviews with 5 Norwegian pre-service teachers at the start and end of their teacher education.\n\n**Findings:**\n- By graduation, teachers had altered beliefs, gained knowledge, and developed more flexible approaches to multilingual learners.\n- However, the monolingual structures of Norwegian schools still constrain their practical agency.\n- Teachers need both knowledge AND structural conditions to enact multilingual pedagogies.\n\n**Relevance to Alif:** Less directly relevant but confirms that institutional structures shape what's possible in language learning. An independent app can bypass many of these structural constraints.\n\n## Active Research Projects\n\n- **Forest Sámi teachers' perspectives** — teaching about/for Forest Sámi students\n- **DIALOGUES** — Erasmus+ project (2025–2027) on languages, literacies, and learning in a digital age. Project manager: Kristin Vold Lexander.\n- **Dekom** (Lana Amro) — Decentralized competence development in second language pedagogy with Oslo municipality\n\n## Research Groups\n\n- **Diversity in Education (DivE)** — both Iversen and Amro\n- **Language Didactics in Multilingual Contexts**\n- **[Language Teaching and Learning in Multilingual Contexts](https://www.inn.no/english/research/our-research/language-teaching-and-learning/)** — broader faculty group\n\n## Key Themes Relevant to Alif\n\n1. **Organizational model matters:** How heritage language education is structured (mainstream vs. supplementary) fundamentally shapes the learning experience. Alif as a self-directed tool sits outside both models.\n\n2. **Translanguaging is productive:** Using multiple languages in the learning process (L1 English + L2 Arabic) is validated by this research, not seen as a deficit. Our English glosses, transliteration, and root explanations are supported.\n\n3. **Identity and motivation:** Arabic heritage learners face unique identity pressures. Even non-heritage learners of Arabic may benefit from private, low-stakes practice environments.\n\n4. **Digital delivery is viable:** Amro's PhD specifically studies digital Arabic language education and finds it effective for heritage language maintenance.\n\n5. **MSA literacy is the shared goal:** Across diverse Arabic-speaking backgrounds, Modern Standard Arabic literacy is the common target — aligned with Alif's MSA focus.\n", "date": null, "category": "external", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/inn-arabic-heritage-language.md"}, "learner-profile-2026-02-12": {"title": "Learner Profile — Stian", "content": "# Learner Profile — Stian\n\n> Date: 2026-02-12\n> Source: Structured interview, Substack article, observed behavior\n\n---\n\n## Identity & Motivation\n\n- **True beginner** in Arabic (<6 months), but NOT a beginner at learning. Has prior experience building sentence-based language learning tools (Glossarium) and studying other languages.\n- **Builder-learner**: Literally constructing his own learning tool. Wants the algorithm to be *right*, not just \"good enough.\" Will read the research and question decisions.\n- **Patient and self-honest**: No gamification needed. No need for quizzes to verify honesty — \"I'm 100% honest, so I don't need any more obstacles like Duolingo quiz.\" Will show up consistently if the system works.\n- **Research-driven**: Makes decisions based on evidence, not intuition or marketing. Reads papers. Questions whether the algorithm is optimal.\n- **High agency, high motivation**: Willing to do a LOT if he trusts the process. The key is *trust* — which is earned by visible progress.\n\n## The Core Motivation: \"Read the Last Page\"\n\nThe deepest motivational pattern: **uploading a text he cares about (poem, song, story) → doing targeted practice → coming back a week later and reading it fluently**. This is the \"holy grail\" moment. Not abstract progress bars, but the lived experience of comprehension.\n\n> \"That feeling of being able to read the story fluently and enjoy the beauty, no tricks, is the most amazing feeling and motivation I can have.\"\n\nThis maps directly to the Story mode flow: import → learn targeted vocab → read and complete. The algorithm's job is to make this loop reliable and fast.\n\n## What \"Progress\" Means\n\nNOT: \"I reviewed 200 cards today\"\nNOT: \"My streak is 14 days\"\nIS: **\"The number of words I confidently know — that if given a new text, there's a high probability I'd recognize them — is significantly increasing week over week.\"**\n\nHe cares about *functional reading vocabulary growth* — words that are genuinely consolidated, not just \"seen.\" The current system fails this because FSRS thinks 586 words are known when only ~150-200 actually are.\n\n## Trust Breakers\n\n1. **Failing the same words repeatedly** without the system doing something about it. Leeches that keep appearing feel like the system isn't learning.\n2. **No visible progress** — doing hundreds of reviews but the \"known word count\" (real known, not FSRS-inflated) doesn't grow.\n3. NOT a trust breaker: difficulty, struggle, or low session accuracy. He's patient with hard material if he believes it's building toward something.\n\n## Michel Thomas Principle\n\nInspired by Michel Thomas' approach: **\"The teacher manages the memory for you — if you do as I say and put effort into each individual interaction, trust me that you'll learn.\"**\n\nThis means: the algorithm should take FULL responsibility for scheduling, repetition, leech management, and pacing. The user's only job is to show up and engage honestly with each card. \"Automate everything.\"\n\nThe worry with the textbook is: \"I constantly worry that the things I learnt, I will forget.\" Alif should eliminate this worry completely — if it's in the system, it will be reviewed at the right time.\n\n## Learning Style\n\n- **Sentences >>> individual words**. Strong opinion based on prior experience (Glossarium). Sentences provide context anchoring, pattern recognition, multiple-word reinforcement, and feel like real reading.\n- **Analytical**: Wants to understand roots, patterns, morphology — not just memorize. The root prediction feature and morphological display align with this.\n- **Slow, thorough processing**: When facing a hard sentence (3-4 unknowns), works through it slowly using context, then flips to check. Doesn't give up or rush.\n- **Honest self-assessment**: Triple-tap marking is reliable signal from this user. No inflation risk.\n\n## Schedule Pattern\n\n- **Variable**: Some days 5 min (phone pickup), some days 45 min (train ride). Cannot count on consistent session length.\n- The algorithm must handle both micro-sessions (3-4 cards) and deep sessions (30+ cards) gracefully.\n- **Front-loading priority items** is critical — if he only does 3 cards, those 3 must be the highest-impact items.\n\n## Study Context\n\n- Primary: Alif + one textbook (working through linearly)\n- OCR captures textbook pages into Alif to reinforce what's being studied\n- Future OCR imports will be incremental (a few pages at a time), not bulk\n- Wants the textbook vocab to be tracked AND actively learned\n- Story imports for motivational texts (poems, songs, articles)\n\n## Goals (1-2 months)\n\n- **Read a short Arabic paragraph and understand 80%+ without help**\n- Reading is the clear priority. Listening is a future concern.\n- Religious texts (Quran) are a stretch goal, not immediate.\n\n## Algorithm Implications\n\n1. **Metric that matters**: Growth rate of \"genuinely known\" words per week (real stability, not inflated FSRS)\n2. **Story mode is the motivational engine**: Import → learn → read → feel the magic. Make this loop fast and reliable.\n3. **Session design**: Must work for both 3-card and 30-card sessions. Always start with highest-impact items.\n4. **No gamification needed**: Progress visualization yes, badges/streaks/points no.\n5. **Full automation**: User trusts the algorithm. Don't ask for decisions — make them and show the reasoning.\n6. **Leech management must be invisible**: Auto-suspend, auto-reintroduce, auto-escalate. Don't burden the user with \"this word is a leech, what do you want to do?\"\n7. **Textbook sync**: Each OCR capture should seamlessly feed the review pipeline. New pages = new words entering acquisition phase.\n8. **Sentences always**: Even during acquisition phase, use sentence context. Never show isolated word flashcards unless absolutely no sentences are available.\n", "date": "Feb 12", "category": "algorithm", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/learner-profile-2026-02-12.md"}, "learning-algorithm-redesign-2026-02-12": {"title": "Learning Algorithm Redesign — Research & Implementation Plan", "content": "# Learning Algorithm Redesign — Research & Implementation Plan\n\n> Date: 2026-02-12\n> Status: Research phase. No code changes yet.\n> Context: Post-OCR import of ~100 textbook pages created 411 words with inflated FSRS state. Accuracy cratered from 78% to 25%. Need fundamental algorithm rethink.\n\n---\n\n## Part 1: Current State Diagnosis\n\n### Production Data (as of 2026-02-12)\n\n| Metric | Value | Concern |\n|--------|-------|---------|\n| Total tracked words | 592 | — |\n| From textbook scans | 411 (69%) | Bulk import with false \"Good\" signal |\n| From Duolingo | 149 (25%) | Older, better reviewed |\n| From Learn mode | 32 (5%) | Best quality signal |\n| Words seen 0-2 times | **367 (63%)** | Barely any memory trace |\n| Words seen exactly once | 214 | Single encounter = near-zero learning |\n| Words never seen | 11 | Not yet scheduled |\n| FSRS stability 30+ days | **586 (100%)** | ALL words falsely show high stability |\n| Leeches (3+ failures) | 20 | Need intervention |\n\n### Accuracy Trend (Post-OCR)\n\n| Date | Reviews | Accuracy | Context |\n|------|---------|----------|---------|\n| Feb 8 | 180 | 78% | Pre-OCR, organic reviews |\n| Feb 9 | 259 | 63% | Some new words entering |\n| Feb 10 | 1,254 | 99% | **OCR bulk import** (auto Good ratings) |\n| Feb 11 | 48 | **46%** | Real reviews — accuracy halved |\n| Feb 12 | 8 | **25%** | Real reviews — still declining |\n\n### How FSRS State Got Corrupted\n\n1. `process_textbook_page()` calls `submit_review(rating=3)` for every OCR-extracted word\n2. FSRS sets initial stability S₀(Good) = 2.3 days\n3. When these words appear in sentence reviews and user doesn't mark them (reading holistically), they get another Good rating\n4. Stability compounds: 2.3d → 5d → 12d → 30d+ over just a few reviews\n5. Result: FSRS thinks user knows all 586 words. User actually knows ~150-200.\n\n### Session Patterns\n\n- Highly variable: 3 to 35 cards per session\n- Many micro-sessions: 3-4 cards (picking up phone briefly)\n- Some deep sessions: 30-35 cards (train ride)\n- Inter-review gaps within sessions: 0-6 minutes\n- Between sessions: 12-48 hours\n- User reviews 50-200 words per day on active days\n\n---\n\n## Part 2: Literature Review Findings\n\n### 2.1 Encounters Needed for Vocabulary Acquisition\n\n**Sources**: Uchihara et al. 2019 meta-analysis, Laufer & Nation 2012, Conti 2025\n\n- **8-12 meaningful encounters** to establish stable mental representation\n- **<6 encounters → <30% recall after a week**\n- **10+ encounters → 80%+ recall**\n- **20-30 spaced repetitions** for long-term embedding\n- Only **11% of variance** in word learning explained by encounter frequency — context diversity, retrieval effort, and modality also matter\n- Diminishing returns beyond ~20 encounters\n- **The first 2 encounters determine learning to the largest extent** (PMC4277705)\n- A single exposure produces **minimal durable learning**\n\n### 2.2 FSRS Cold-Start Limitations\n\n**Sources**: Expertium blog, FSRS Algorithm wiki, Anki forums\n\n**Default initial stability values (FSRS-6):**\n| Rating | Stability | Meaning |\n|--------|-----------|---------|\n| Again (1) | 0.212 days (~5h) | Complete failure |\n| Hard (2) | 1.293 days | Struggled |\n| Good (3) | 2.307 days | Normal pass |\n| Easy (4) | 8.296 days | Very confident |\n\n**Critical limitation**: FSRS was NOT designed to model short-term memory. Quote from Expertium: \"While neither FSRS-5 nor FSRS-6 have a proper model of short-term memory, they have a crude heuristic.\"\n\n**FSRS has NO native learning phase.** It relies on the host application (e.g., Anki) to handle acquisition via \"learning steps.\" Anki's default learning steps (1m, 10m) happen OUTSIDE FSRS. Our system goes directly from first encounter → FSRS scheduling, skipping this entirely.\n\n**FSRS-5 same-day review stability progression**: 1.87 min → 13.88 min → 6.26 hours → 1.08 days\n\n**Expertium recommends**: A single learning step of 15-30 minutes, then let FSRS determine graduation interval. Multiple same-day learning steps provide negligible benefit.\n\n### 2.3 Spacing and Initial Acquisition\n\n**Sources**: Pimsleur 1967, Smolen et al. 2016, Wozniak (SM-2)\n\n**Pimsleur's memory schedule** (graduated interval recall):\n5 seconds → 25 seconds → 2 minutes → 10 minutes → 1 hour → 5 hours → 1 day → 5 days → 25 days → 4 months → 2 years\n\nKey insight: **the first 4 intervals are within-session** (seconds to minutes). The first inter-session gap is 1 day.\n\n**Reconsolidation research** (Smolen et al.): Inverted-U relationship between spacing and retention. For 7-day retention test, ~3-day spacing optimal. For 35-day test, ~8-day spacing optimal. Spacing too short OR too long relative to desired retention is suboptimal.\n\n**Massed vs distributed for acquisition**:\n- For initial acquisition: massed and distributed produce **equivalent** immediate performance\n- For retention: distributed is **dramatically superior**\n- Practical implication: initial concentrated exposure followed by expanding intervals is optimal\n- At least one **overnight sleep consolidation** should occur between acquisition and first spaced review\n\n**Optimal initial schedule** (synthesized from literature):\n1. Day 0: 3-4 exposures (introduction + within-session retrieval)\n2. Day 1: 1-2 retrieval attempts (**most critical review**)\n3. Day 3-4: 1 retrieval attempt\n4. Day 7-10: 1 retrieval attempt\n5. After this: FSRS takes over\n\n### 2.4 Within-Session Spacing\n\n**Sources**: Karpicke & Bauernschmidt, Storm/Bjork/Storm 2010, Kornell 2009\n\n**Expanding retrieval practice** (Karpicke): lag 0 → lag 1 → lag 5 → lag 9 (gap = number of intervening items)\n\n- **Expanding** (0-1-5-9): Better for short-term retention and initial success\n- **Equal** (5-5-5-5): Better for long-term retention\n- **Massed** (0-0-0-0): Worst for both\n\nWhen initial retrieval is likely to **fail** (brand new vocabulary), expanding spacing loses its advantage. Use slightly compressed initial gaps.\n\n**Kornell 2009**: One large flashcard stack (natural spacing) was more effective than four small stacks (massing). 90% of participants learned more with spacing; 72% **believed** massing was better.\n\n**Optimal session layout for 10 cards with 3 new items**:\n```\nPosition 1: Review card (warm-up)\nPosition 2: NEW CARD A\nPosition 3: Review card\nPosition 4: NEW CARD B\nPosition 5: Review card\nPosition 6: RE-QUIZ A (gap of 3)\nPosition 7: NEW CARD C\nPosition 8: Review card\nPosition 9: RE-QUIZ B (gap of 4)\nPosition 10: Review card (easy, end positive)\n```\n\n### 2.5 Interleaving vs Blocking\n\n**Sources**: Bjork lab, Hwang 2025, Libersky et al. 2025\n\n- **Interleaving helps** for discrimination tasks (distinguishing similar items)\n- **Blocking helps** for initial acquisition of difficult material (especially for weaker learners)\n- **Hybrid approach**: Block during initial encoding (Learn mode), interleave during practice (review sessions)\n- **Arabic-specific risk**: Words from the same root share 3 consonants — high interference. Avoid introducing two root-siblings in the same session.\n\n### 2.6 Retrieval Practice vs Recognition\n\n**Sources**: Karpicke & Roediger 2008, Conti 2025, Stewart et al. 2024\n\n- Active recall produces **150% better long-term retention** than passive re-exposure\n- Dropping items from **test cycles** drastically reduced recall; dropping from **study cycles** had minimal impact\n- Receptive vocabulary is ~40% larger than productive vocabulary\n- Even **multiple-choice** is dramatically better than pure self-assessment\n- For reading-focused apps: recognition IS the target skill, but adding retrieval effort still helps\n\n**Current gap in Alif**: Sentence review is pure passive recognition (see Arabic → self-assess comprehension). No verification of understanding.\n\n### 2.7 Leech Management\n\n**Sources**: Anki manual, Hacking Chinese\n\n- Anki's leech threshold: 8 lapses (we should use 6 for Arabic due to script difficulty)\n- Leeches take **on average 10x** as many repetitions as normal cards\n- Two primary causes: **interference** (similar items competing) and **poor encoding** (insufficient context)\n- Fix for interference: **suspend one, learn the other first**, reintroduce later\n- Fix for poor encoding: add images, mnemonics, more distinctive sentence contexts\n- Arabic interference risk: verb forms I-X from same root, similar letter shapes, vowel pattern differences\n\n### 2.8 A/B Testing with N=1\n\n**Sources**: PMC3118090, PMC6787650\n\n**Feasibility**: Yes, using between-word randomization.\n\n**Design**: Randomly assign each new word to condition A or B at introduction time. Track 7-day retention as primary metric.\n\n**Sample sizes needed**:\n- ~50 words per condition (100 total)\n- 4 weeks of reviews per word to measure 7-day retention\n- Total timeline: 8-12 weeks for one experiment\n- Use Bayesian Beta-Binomial updating: Prior Beta(1,1), stop when P(A>B) > 0.95\n\n**Challenges**:\n- Carryover: can't unlearn something\n- Non-stationarity: learner improves over time\n- Word difficulty confounding: mitigate by randomization\n\n**Best first experiment**: \"Acquisition phase (3 within-session exposures)\" vs \"current approach (single introduction then FSRS)\"\n\n---\n\n## Part 3: Proposed Algorithm Changes\n\n### 3.1 IMMEDIATE: FSRS State Correction (backfill script)\n\n**What**: Reset FSRS cards for textbook_scan words that don't have genuine review evidence.\n\n**Logic**:\n```\nFor each ULK where source = 'textbook_scan':\n Count \"real reviews\" = reviews where review_mode != 'textbook_scan'\n If real_reviews <= 2:\n Reset fsrs_card to fresh Card() with due = now\n Set knowledge_state = 'learning'\n Set stability to 0 (fresh card)\n If real_reviews >= 3 but accuracy < 40%:\n Reset stability to 0.5 days\n Set knowledge_state = 'lapsed'\n```\n\n**Expected impact**: Immediately fixes the false stability problem. ~350 words will become properly \"due\" and enter the acquisition pipeline.\n\n### 3.2 Acquisition Phase (Pre-FSRS Learning Steps)\n\n**What**: A new state between introduction and FSRS scheduling.\n\n**New knowledge_state**: `\"acquiring\"` (added to the existing new/learning/known/lapsed/suspended states)\n\n**Entry criteria**: Any word with < 5 genuine reviews OR accuracy < 50%\n\n**Behavior during acquisition**:\n- Appear 2-3x per session in different sentences\n- Within-session spacing: positions N, N+3, N+7 (expanding retrieval)\n- NOT scheduled by FSRS — scheduled by the acquisition manager\n- Next-day: must appear at least 1x regardless of FSRS due date\n\n**Graduation criteria**: 5+ genuine reviews AND accuracy ≥ 60%\n\n**On graduation**: Create/update FSRS card with appropriate initial rating based on acquisition performance.\n\n**Implementation touches**:\n- `models.py`: Add \"acquiring\" to knowledge_state enum\n- `fsrs_service.py`: New `get_acquiring_words()` function\n- `sentence_selector.py`: Modified `build_session()` to include acquisition words\n- `sentence_review_service.py`: Track genuine vs auto reviews\n\n### 3.3 Focus Cohort System\n\n**What**: A rolling window of ~30-50 words that get intensive treatment.\n\n**Cohort selection**: Use existing `word_selector.py` scoring (frequency + root familiarity + recency bonus) to pick the best candidates from the full pool.\n\n**Cohort rules**:\n- Maximum cohort size: 40 words\n- Entry: when a slot opens, best-scoring word from pool enters\n- Exit: word reaches stability > 7 days AND 5+ reviews AND 60%+ accuracy\n- Words outside cohort don't appear in review sessions (except as scaffold in sentences)\n- Acquiring words automatically in cohort\n\n**Why this matters**: With 586 words and ~100 reviews/day, each word gets reviewed once per 6 days. With 40 words and ~100 reviews/day, each word gets reviewed 2-3x per day. The math is dramatically different.\n\n### 3.4 Session-Level Word Repetition\n\n**What**: Modify `build_session()` to deliberately repeat acquisition words.\n\n**Current behavior**: Greedy set cover selects sentences maximizing due-word coverage. Each sentence selected independently.\n\n**New behavior**:\n1. Identify acquisition words that need repetition this session (max 5)\n2. For each, find 2-3 sentences containing that word\n3. Interleave these into the session at expanding positions\n4. Fill remaining session slots with normal due-word sentences\n\n**Session template** (10 cards, 3 acquisition words A/B/C):\n```\n[1] Easy review (warm-up)\n[2] Sentence with A (first exposure)\n[3] Sentence with B (first exposure)\n[4] Review sentence\n[5] Sentence with A (re-quiz, gap=2)\n[6] Sentence with C (first exposure)\n[7] Review sentence\n[8] Sentence with B (re-quiz, gap=4)\n[9] Sentence with C (re-quiz, gap=2)\n[10] Easy review (positive end)\n```\n\n### 3.5 OCR Import Reform\n\n**Current**: All OCR words get `submit_review(rating=3)` → FSRS card → \"known\"\n\n**New options on import screen**:\n1. **\"Track vocabulary only\"** (new default): Creates Lemma entry, no ULK/FSRS card. Words appear in Learn mode candidates and contribute to story readiness calculations.\n2. **\"Mark as studied today\"**: Current behavior. Good for pages just studied.\n3. **\"Mark as encountered weeks ago\"**: Creates FSRS card but with `rating=2` (Hard) instead of Good, and backdates introduction. Appropriate for textbook pages read 1-2 weeks ago.\n\n### 3.6 Batch-Aware Sentence Generation\n\n**Current**: `generate_validated_sentence()` takes a single target word. Generates one sentence per word.\n\n**New**: `generate_session_sentences()` takes a set of 3-5 focus words. Generates 8-10 sentences where each focus word appears in 2-3 sentences.\n\n**LLM prompt change**:\n```\nInstead of: \"Generate a sentence using the word كتاب\"\nNew: \"Generate 8 sentences. Each sentence must include at least 2 of these words:\nكتاب، مدرسة، قرأ، جديد، كبير. Each word should appear in at least 2 different sentences.\"\n```\n\n**Benefits**:\n- Natural cross-reinforcement\n- Fewer LLM calls (1 batch call vs 5 individual)\n- Better sentence diversity (LLM sees the full set)\n- De-emphasizes \"primary target\" concept\n\n### 3.7 Leech Auto-Management\n\n**Thresholds**:\n- **Leech detection**: 6+ reviews with <30% accuracy\n- **Auto-suspend**: Move to \"suspended\" state with `leech_suspended_at` timestamp\n- **Auto-reintroduce**: After 14 days, reactivate with fresh FSRS card\n- **Leech escalation**: If suspended and reintroduced 3+ times, flag for manual review\n- **Root-sibling interference guard**: Don't introduce a new word from a root if a sibling was failed in last 7 days\n\n### 3.8 A/B Testing Infrastructure\n\n**Schema change**: Add `experiment_group` (nullable VARCHAR) to `UserLemmaKnowledge`\n\n**Assignment**: At `introduce_word()` time, randomly assign to \"A\" or \"B\" (or null for non-experimental words)\n\n**First experiment**: \"acquisition_v1\"\n- Group A: Acquisition phase (3 within-session exposures, forced day-1 review)\n- Group B: Current FSRS-only approach\n- Primary metric: Accuracy at first review after 7+ days\n- Analysis: Bayesian Beta-Binomial, stop at P(A>B) > 0.95\n\n**Logging**: Experiment assignment logged in `interaction_logger.py` as `experiment_assigned` event.\n\n---\n\n## Part 4: Implementation Phases\n\n### Phase 1: Emergency Data Fix (Day 1)\n- [ ] Backfill script to reset textbook_scan FSRS cards\n- [ ] Log the action to ActivityLog\n- [ ] Verify accuracy improves in next review session\n\n### Phase 2: Acquisition Phase (Days 2-4)\n- [ ] Add \"acquiring\" knowledge_state to models\n- [ ] Migration for new state\n- [ ] `get_acquiring_words()` in fsrs_service.py\n- [ ] Modified `build_session()` for acquisition word repetition\n- [ ] Forced day-1 review logic\n- [ ] Graduation logic (5+ reviews, 60%+ accuracy)\n- [ ] Tests for all new logic\n- [ ] Frontend: no changes needed (acquisition words appear as normal sentence cards)\n\n### Phase 3: Focus Cohort (Days 3-5)\n- [ ] `get_focus_cohort()` function using word_selector scoring\n- [ ] Modified `get_due_cards()` to filter by cohort membership\n- [ ] Cohort graduation logic\n- [ ] API endpoint to view/manage cohort (optional)\n- [ ] Tests\n\n### Phase 4: OCR Import Reform (Day 5-6)\n- [ ] Add import mode parameter to `/api/ocr/scan-pages`\n- [ ] Frontend: import mode selector on scanner screen\n- [ ] \"Track only\" mode: create Lemma, skip ULK/FSRS\n- [ ] \"Encountered weeks ago\" mode: backdated Hard rating\n- [ ] Tests\n\n### Phase 5: Batch Sentence Generation (Days 6-8)\n- [ ] `generate_session_sentences()` for word sets\n- [ ] LLM prompt for multi-target generation\n- [ ] Validation for multi-target sentences\n- [ ] Integration with `build_session()`\n- [ ] Tests\n\n### Phase 6: Leech Management (Day 8-9)\n- [ ] Auto-suspend on leech detection\n- [ ] `leech_suspended_at` and `reintroduce_after` fields\n- [ ] Cron/background task for auto-reintroduction\n- [ ] Root-sibling interference guard in word_selector\n- [ ] Tests\n\n### Phase 7: A/B Testing (Day 9-10)\n- [ ] `experiment_group` field on ULK\n- [ ] Random assignment at introduction\n- [ ] Experiment logging\n- [ ] Analysis script (Bayesian Beta-Binomial)\n- [ ] Dashboard widget (optional)\n\n---\n\n## Part 5: Deep Research Questions (for swarm investigation)\n\n### Research Track A: FSRS Parameter Optimization\n1. What are the FSRS-6 default parameters and how do they compare to our actual review data? Can we run the FSRS optimizer on our 1,749 reviews?\n2. How does py-fsrs handle the `same-day review` formula (w17/w18/w19)? Is it active in our version?\n3. What would optimal initial stability values be for Arabic vocabulary specifically (script difficulty, root-pattern system)?\n4. How does FSRS handle cards that get multiple reviews in quick succession (our sentence-centric model)?\n5. Should we use different FSRS parameters for textbook_scan vs study vs duolingo words?\n\n### Research Track B: Acquisition Phase Design\n1. What exactly do Anki's learning steps do algorithmically? How do they interact with FSRS? Code-level analysis of py-fsrs and anki source.\n2. What is the Leitner box system and how does it compare to what we're proposing? Is there a simpler implementation?\n3. How does Duolingo handle newly introduced vocabulary? What's their spacing schedule for the first 5 encounters?\n4. How does Memrise/Wanikani handle the acquisition→SRS transition? Any published research on their approaches?\n5. What's the optimal number of within-session repetitions for Arabic vocabulary? Is there research specific to Semitic languages or non-Latin scripts?\n\n### Research Track C: Session Optimization\n1. How do commercial SRS apps (Anki, SuperMemo, Mnemosyne) compose sessions? Is there published research on session-level optimization vs card-level?\n2. What does the \"desirable difficulties\" literature say about session fatigue and cognitive load accumulation? At what point do additional reviews become counterproductive within a session?\n3. What's the research on \"micro-sessions\" (2-5 items)? Are they effective or is there a minimum viable session size?\n4. How should the system adapt to variable session lengths (user might do 2 cards or 30 cards)?\n5. What's the optimal ratio of new:review:re-quiz items in a session, and does this change with learner proficiency?\n\n### Research Track D: Arabic-Specific Learning\n1. What research exists on Arabic vocabulary acquisition by adult L1 English speakers? Any SRS studies?\n2. How does the root-pattern morphological system affect vocabulary acquisition rates? Can root knowledge accelerate learning?\n3. What's the role of diacritics in word recognition for beginners? Does diacritics-first → diacritics-reduced training improve reading speed?\n4. Are there Arabic-specific frequency lists more relevant than CAMeL MSA for a learner context?\n5. What published curricula exist for Arabic vocabulary progression (e.g., CEFR alignment)? How do they compare to our frequency-based approach?\n\n### Research Track E: Sentence-Centric Review Model\n1. Is there any published research on sentence-level vs word-level SRS? Our model is unusual.\n2. What does comprehension research say about inferring unknown words from context? How reliable is the \"didn't mark it = knows it\" signal?\n3. How many unknown words in a sentence is optimal for learning? Research on i+1 (Krashen) vs multiple unknowns.\n4. Should sentence difficulty scale with word maturity differently than we currently do?\n5. What's the research on re-reading the same sentence vs reading new sentences with the same target word?\n\n### Research Track F: Experimental Design\n1. What's the smallest detectable effect size with our data volume (~100-200 reviews/day)?\n2. How should we handle the non-stationarity problem (learner improves over time)?\n3. Are there published n-of-1 SRS experiments we can model our design on?\n4. What statistical tests are appropriate for within-subject vocabulary learning comparisons?\n5. How should we handle the root-family confound (words from the same root may have correlated learning rates)?\n\n### Research Track G: Codebase Analysis\n1. Read `sentence_selector.py` in detail: how exactly does `build_session()` work, and where should acquisition word repetition be inserted?\n2. Read `fsrs_service.py` in detail: how does `submit_review()` create and update cards? What happens with multiple same-day reviews?\n3. Read `word_selector.py` in detail: how does the scoring work, and how can we adapt it for focus cohort selection?\n4. Read `sentence_generator.py`: how can we modify it for batch generation targeting multiple words?\n5. Read the frontend review flow (`index.tsx`): what changes are needed to support acquisition-phase cards that repeat within a session?\n6. Analyze the test suite: what existing tests cover the session assembly pipeline?\n\n---\n\n## Part 6: Success Metrics\n\n### Short-term (1 week after Phase 1-3)\n- Session accuracy > 60% (up from 25%)\n- Words with stability < 1 day > 200 (reflecting corrected FSRS state)\n- Average reviews per focus-cohort word per day > 2\n- At least 10 words graduating from acquisition phase\n\n### Medium-term (2 weeks after full implementation)\n- Session accuracy > 70%\n- 50+ words with genuine stability > 7 days\n- Comprehension rate \"understood\" > 40%\n- Leech count stable or declining\n\n### Long-term (1 month)\n- 100+ words with stability > 14 days\n- Session accuracy > 75%\n- A/B experiment reaching statistical significance\n- Daily new-word introduction rate: 3-5 words\n- Focus cohort turning over (words graduating, new ones entering)\n", "date": "Feb 12", "category": "algorithm", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/learning-algorithm-redesign-2026-02-12.md"}, "learning-analysis-2026-02-20": {"title": "Learning Analysis — 2026-02-20", "content": "# Learning Analysis — 2026-02-20\n\n> Comprehensive analysis of production data after 13 days of active Arabic study (Feb 8–20, 2026).\n> Data extracted via `scripts/learning_analysis.py` run on production DB.\n> Visual report: `research/learning-report-2026-02-20.html`\n\n---\n\n## 1. Vocabulary Status\n\n| State | Count | % |\n|-------|-------|---|\n| Known | 209 | 28.8% |\n| Acquiring | 76 | 10.5% |\n| Learning (FSRS) | 9 | 1.2% |\n| Encountered | 402 | 55.4% |\n| Lapsed | 10 | 1.4% |\n| Suspended | 19 | 2.6% |\n| **Total** | **725** | |\n\n**CEFR estimate**: Early A1 (~209 known words). Children's book milestone (150 words) already achieved.\n\n### By Source (known words)\n- auto_intro: 89 (43%)\n- book: 53 (25%)\n- duolingo: 42 (20%)\n- collateral: 12 (6%)\n- textbook_scan: 6 (3%)\n- study: 5 (2%)\n\n---\n\n## 2. Learning Rate\n\n### Graduation Rate\n- **Total graduations**: 143 over 9 active graduation days\n- **Weekly**: W07=51, W08=92 (80% week-over-week increase)\n- **Daily peak**: 33 on Feb 20\n- **Effective rate**: ~11 words/day — well above the 7 words/day research benchmark (Uchihara 2019)\n\n### Time to Acquisition\n- **Median**: 98.8 hours (~4.1 days)\n- **Mean**: 90.5 hours (~3.8 days)\n- **P25/P75**: 55.7h / 103.9h (tight IQR — consistent acquisition pace)\n- **Reviews to graduate**: median 7, mean 13.8 (P25=4, P75=11)\n\nResearch comparison: Uchihara (2019) meta-analysis found 8-12 meaningful encounters for stable vocabulary. Our median of 7 reviews is on the low end — the acquisition box system compensates by requiring 2+ calendar days and ≥60% accuracy.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Retention\n\n### FSRS Retention\n- **Overall**: 93.3% (3,487/3,736 correct)\n- **Weekly trend**: W06=78.3% → W07=90.0% → W08=97.5%\n- This dramatic improvement reflects: (a) algorithm stabilization, (b) early inflated cards being flushed, (c) genuine learning\n\n### Accuracy by Mode\n| Mode | Reviews | Accuracy |\n|------|---------|----------|\n| Reading | 5,308 | 90.0% |\n| Listening | 57 | 71.9% |\n| Quiz | 26 | 88.5% |\n| Reintro | 50 | 52.0% |\n| Textbook scan | 247 | 100% |\n\n### By Phase\n- **FSRS**: 93.3% (3,736 reviews)\n- **Acquisition**: 83.0% (1,952 reviews)\n\nResearch comparison: Wilson et al. (2019, Nature) found 85% as the optimal training accuracy. Our 89.8% overall is slightly above optimal — room to increase introduction rate.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Session Patterns\n\n- **Total sessions**: 184\n- **Median size**: 5 sentences, mean 6.8\n- **Sessions/day**: mean 13.6 (range 3–27)\n- **Comprehension**: 53.6% understood, 46.1% partial, 0.3% no_idea\n\n### Size Distribution\n| Bucket | Count |\n|--------|-------|\n| 1-5 | 101 (55%) |\n| 6-10 | 47 (26%) |\n| 11-15 | 22 (12%) |\n| 16-20 | 6 (3%) |\n| 21-30 | 8 (4%) |\n\nShort sessions dominate — the app's design for \"micro-completable units\" is working well. The high session frequency (14.2/day) enables same-day acquisition box advancement (4h intervals between boxes).\n\n---\n\n## 5. FSRS Stability Distribution\n\n| Bucket | Count | % |\n|--------|-------|---|\n| <1 day | 18 | 8.5% |\n| 1-7 days | 82 | 38.9% |\n| 7-30 days | 100 | 47.4% |\n| 30-90 days | 11 | 5.2% |\n| 90+ days | 0 | 0% |\n\n- **Median stability**: 7.3 days\n- **Mean stability**: 9.2 days (known words: 10.3d)\n- **Difficulty**: 73% easy (<3), 20% very hard (>7), 5% hard, 2% medium\n\nThe 20% \"very hard\" words (42 words) are potential leeches — worth monitoring.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Frequency Coverage\n\n| Top N Words | In Corpus | Active | Known | Function Words |\n|-------------|-----------|--------|-------|----------------|\n| 100 | 51 | 26 | 25 | 29 |\n| 500 | 175 | 57 | 50 | 44 |\n| 1,000 | 271 | 74 | 65 | 46 |\n| 2,000 | 403 | 99 | 86 | 49 |\n\nNote: ~29 of the top 100 are function words (prepositions, pronouns, conjunctions) which are excluded from the learning pipeline by design.\n\n### Top Frequency Gaps (excluding function words)\n| Word | Gloss | Rank | Status |\n|------|-------|------|--------|\n| اللّٰهُ | God | 16 | encountered |\n| اَلْيَوْمَ | today | 30 | encountered |\n| رَئِيسٌ | chief, leader | 45 | not imported |\n| أَكْثَر | more | 63 | not imported |\n| يَوْم | day | 68 | not imported |\n| مِصْرُ | Egypt | 76 | encountered |\n| أخْبار | news | 80 | not imported |\n| أَفْضَل | best | 86 | encountered |\n\n\"Encountered\" words should naturally enter acquisition via auto-intro. \"Not imported\" words need to enter via topic introduction.\n\n---\n\n## 7. Projections (based on actual data)\n\n**Actual study patterns**: 7.0 study days/week (100% consistency over 13 days), 13 sessions/study day median.\n\n### Scenario 1: Current pace (11 grads/study day, linear)\n| Milestone | Remaining | Days |\n|-----------|-----------|------|\n| Graded reader (400) | 182 | ~17 |\n| A1 (500) | 282 | ~26 |\n| A2 (1,200) | 982 | ~89 |\n| B1 (2,000) | 1,782 | ~162 |\n| B2 (4,000) | 3,782 | ~344 |\n\n### Scenario 2: Recent 7-day trend (15.6 grads/study day)\n| Milestone | Remaining | Days |\n|-----------|-----------|------|\n| Graded reader (400) | 182 | ~12 |\n| A1 (500) | 282 | ~18 |\n| A2 (1,200) | 982 | ~63 |\n| B1 (2,000) | 1,782 | ~114 |\n| B2 (4,000) | 3,782 | ~243 |\n\n### Scenario 3: With deceleration (rate drops ~15% per 500 new words)\n| Milestone | Days | Rate by then |\n|-----------|------|-------------|\n| Graded reader (400) | ~17 | 11.0/day |\n| A1 (500) | ~26 | 9.3/day |\n| A2 (1,200) | ~117 | 6.8/day |\n| B1 (2,000) | ~247 | 4.9/day |\n| B2 (4,000) | ~869 | 2.5/day |\n\nThe deceleration scenario is likely most realistic — as high-frequency/easy words are exhausted, the graduation rate will naturally slow. The root-aware stability boost should partially offset this for Arabic.\n\n---\n\n## 8. Tashkeel Readiness\n\n- **Eligible for fading** (stability ≥ 30d): 11 words (5.7%)\n- **Median stability**: 7.3 days\n- **Stability distribution** (review words only):\n - 30-90d: 11\n - 7-30d: 100\n - 1-7d: 81\n - <1d: 1\n\n**Recommendation**: Wait until ~50+ words exceed 30d stability before enabling tashkeel fading (estimated 2-3 weeks at current pace). The feature is ready and opt-in — default mode is \"always\" (full diacritics).\n\n---\n\n## 9. Scheduling Observations\n\n### What's Working\n1. **High retention** (93.3%) with strong weekly improvement trend\n2. **Short sessions** (median 5) are effective — users engage frequently rather than in long sessions\n3. **Same-day box advancement** enabled by high session frequency\n4. **Acquisition pipeline** is healthy: 76 acquiring, none stuck\n\n### Opportunities\n1. **Increase introduction rate**: accuracy (89.8%) is above Wilson's 85% optimal → room for 12-15 new words/day\n2. **Root-aware boost** should reduce review load for words in established root families\n3. **Tashkeel fading** ready to enable when stability matures\n4. **Frequency gaps**: auto-introduction should prioritize high-frequency unlearned words (أَوْ, بَعْد, بَيْن)\n\n### Research Comparison\n\n| Metric | Our Value | Benchmark | Source |\n|--------|-----------|-----------|--------|\n| Learning rate | 11/day | 7/day | Uchihara 2019 |\n| Training accuracy | 89.8% | 85% optimal | Wilson et al. 2019 |\n| Reviews to graduate | 7 median | 8-12 encounters | Uchihara 2019 |\n| FSRS retention | 93.3% | 90% target | FSRS-6 default |\n| Words for 95% coverage | 209/9,000 | 9K lemmas | Masrai & Milton 2016 |\n\n---\n\n## 10. Milestone Projections\n\nAt current graduation rate (~11 words/day, 7 study days/week, 218 known+learning):\n\n| Milestone | Target | Current | Remaining | Linear | Deceleration |\n|-----------|--------|---------|-----------|--------|-------------|\n| Children's book | 150 | 218 | **ACHIEVED** | — | — |\n| Graded reader (L1-3) | 400 | 218 | 182 | ~17d | ~17d |\n| A1 complete | 500 | 218 | 282 | ~26d | ~26d |\n| A2 | 1,200 | 218 | 982 | ~89d | ~117d |\n| B1 | 2,000 | 218 | 1,782 | ~162d | ~247d |\n| B2 | 4,000 | 218 | 3,782 | ~344d | ~869d |\n\nLinear projections assume sustained 11 grads/day. Deceleration model assumes rate drops ~15% per 500 new words as easy/frequent words are exhausted.\n\n---\n\n## Files\n\n| File | Description |\n|------|-------------|\n| `scripts/learning_analysis.py` | Analysis script (runs inside Docker container) |\n| `/tmp/claude/analysis.json` | Raw production data |\n| `/tmp/claude/sim_casual.csv` | 90-day casual simulation |\n| `/tmp/claude/sim_calibrated.csv` | 30-day calibrated simulation |\n| `/tmp/claude/sim_intensive.csv` | 30-day intensive simulation |\n| `research/learning-report-2026-02-20.html` | Visual HTML report |\n", "date": "Feb 20", "category": "analytics", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["vocabulary-thresholds-2026-02-21"], "path": "research/learning-analysis-2026-02-20.md"}, "lemma-mapping-audit-2026-02-17": {"title": "Lemma Mapping Audit — 2026-02-17", "content": "# Lemma Mapping Audit — 2026-02-17\n\n## Summary\n\nAudited all 1,895 sentence_words across 300 active sentences. Found systemic issues in three categories: wrong lemma assignments (24+ sentence_words), wrong/misleading English glosses on correct lemmas (249+ sentence_words), and structural lemma quality issues (100+ lemmas).\n\n## Root Cause: False al-Prefix Matching\n\nThe most impactful bug is in `lookup_lemma()` (sentence_validator.py:391). When a surface form doesn't match directly, it tries adding ال prefix. For short words, this produces false matches:\n\n```\nأَنْ → strip diacritics → أن → normalize_alef → ان\nlookup tries: \"ان\" not found → tries \"الان\" → MATCHES الآن (now)!\n```\n\nThis affects أن (that/to), أنّ (that), إنّ (indeed), and لأنّه (because, via clitic strip → ان). These are among the most common Arabic words and have no lemma entries, so they fall through to the false al-prefix match.\n\n**Scale**: 17 sentence_words currently mapped to الآن/آن that should be أن/إن/أنّ/إنّ.\n\n## Category 1: Wrong Lemma Assignments\n\n| Surface form | Mapped to | Should be | Count | Cause |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| أَنْ | الآن (now) | أن (that/to) | 5 | Missing lemma + false al-prefix |\n| أَنَّ | الآن (now) / آن (time) | أنّ (that) | 8 | Missing lemma + false al-prefix |\n| إِنَّ | الآن (now) | إنّ (indeed) | 1 | Missing lemma + false al-prefix |\n| لِأَنَّ/لِأَنَّهُ | الآن (now) | لأنّ (because) | 1 | Missing lemma + clitic strip + false al-prefix |\n| فَقْدُ | قد (already) | فقد (loss) | 1 | False proclitic strip: ف+قد |\n| الوضع | يضع (to put) | وَضْع (situation) | 1 | Missing noun lemma |\n| بِيَدِهَا | باد (to perish) | يد (hand) | 1 | False clitic strip |\n| وَجَدَتْ | يوجد (there is) | وَجَدَ (to find) | 1 | Wrong verb lemma |\n| يَجْلِسُ | نجلس (we sit) | جَلَسَ (to sit) | 2 | Conjugated form stored as lemma |\n| يَأْكُلُ | ونأكل (and we eat) | أَكَلَ (to eat) | 2 | Conjugated form stored as lemma |\n\n## Category 2: Wrong/Misleading Glosses\n\n| Lemma ID | Arabic | Current gloss | Should be | Sentence count |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 95 | فِي | \"with\" | \"in\" | 134 |\n| 11 | هٰذا | \"that\" | \"this (masc.)\" | 54 |\n| 154 | مِن | \"than\" | \"from; than\" | 22 |\n| 152 | عِنْد | \"have\" | \"at, near; to have\" | 9 |\n| 115 | هَل | \"is\" | \"is? (question particle)\" | 25 |\n| 1238 | وَرَقَة | \"leaf\" | \"paper, sheet; leaf\" | — |\n| 116 | هُوَّ | \"he\" | \"he\" (also: shadda on lemma form is wrong) | 10 |\n\n## Category 3: Structural Lemma Issues\n\n### 3a. Lemmas stored with ال prefix (72 lemmas)\n\nMany lemmas have ال baked into `lemma_ar_bare`. Some are legitimate (الله, الآن, اليوم — these genuinely include ال). But many are not: الطابق (floor), الحائط (wall), الكنبة (sofa), الجدار (wall), etc.\n\n**Impact**: These get double-ال in the lookup dict (lookup adds \"الالطابق\") and waste space. For the user, tapping \"الطابق\" shows \"the floor\" instead of \"floor\" — minor but unprofessional.\n\n**High-impact ones** (>10 active sentences): الطابق (65), اليوم (50), الثانوية (46), الآن (35), الحائط (34), الثاني (32), الله (24), الابتدائية (21), التعليم (20), النرويج (18).\n\n### 3b. Possessive forms as separate lemmas (6 lemmas with active sentences)\n\n| Lemma | Gloss | Active sentences | Should be variant of |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| أسرتي | my family | 19 | أُسْرة (family) |\n| ابني | my son | 16 | اِبْن (son) |\n| ابنك | your son | 12 | اِبْن (son) |\n| عمي | my paternal uncle | 7 | عَمّ (uncle) |\n| ملابسي | my clothes | 5 | ملابس (clothes) |\n| عندك | you have | 6 | عند (at/have) |\n\n### 3c. Verbs stored as conjugated forms (15 lemmas)\n\nThese were imported as present-tense or we-form instead of past 3ms (the Arabic dictionary form):\n\n| Lemma ID | Stored as | Should be | Active sentences |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1765 | يَسْكُنُ (he lives) | سَكَنَ | 49 |\n| 1618 | تَسْكُنُ (she lives) | سَكَنَ | 37 |\n| 1731 | نَجْلِسُ (we sit) | جَلَسَ | 26 |\n| 1609 | يَضَعُ (to put) | وَضَعَ | 22 |\n| 1743 | يَقَعُ (is located) | وَقَعَ | 20 |\n| 1764 | تَسْكُنِينَ (you live f.) | سَكَنَ | 12 |\n| 1558 | يَكْتُبُونَ (they write) | كَتَبَ | 10 |\n| 1556 | نَدْرُسُ (we study) | دَرَسَ | 9 |\n| 1828 | يَلْبَس (to wear) | لَبِسَ | 9 |\n| 1736 | نَأْكُل (we eat) | أَكَلَ | 5 |\n| 2073 | يوجد (there is) | وَجَدَ/وُجِدَ | 1 |\n\n**Note**: Some apparent false positives are actually correct — نَوَّمَ (Form II), تَكَرَّرَ (Form V), نَظَرَ (looking) are all valid past 3ms forms. Also نَمِر (tiger) is a noun, not a verb.\n\n## Pipeline Gaps\n\n1. **No disambiguation**: `lookup_lemma()` returns first match from clitic stripping candidates — no ranking, no context\n2. **No CAMeL in sentence pipeline**: `find_best_db_match()` exists in morphology.py and is used for story import, but NOT for sentence_word mapping\n3. **No LLM verification**: Sentence generation validates grammar and translation (Gemini quality gate) but never checks word-lemma mappings\n4. **Aggressive al-prefix matching**: Adding ال to any 2-char word is dangerous (ان→الان, قد→القد, etc.)\n5. **Missing common particles**: أن, إن, أنّ, إنّ are among the 50 most common Arabic words and have no lemma entries\n6. **No forms_json for verb conjugations**: Only ~6 form types indexed; past tense conjugations (قرأت, قرأوا, etc.) not in lookup\n", "date": "Feb 17", "category": "quality", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/lemma-mapping-audit-2026-02-17.md"}, "llm-cost-investigation-2026-02-19": {"title": "LLM Cost Investigation — Phase 1 Complete (2026-02-19)", "content": "# LLM Cost Investigation — Phase 1 Complete (2026-02-19)\n\n## Goal\nInvestigate shifting LLM work from paid APIs (Gemini Flash, GPT-5.2, Haiku API) to Claude Code CLI (free via Max plan) for equal or better quality at lower cost.\n\n## What We Learned\n\n### Current LLM Architecture\n- **`backend/app/services/llm.py`**: Central router. `generate_completion()` with fallback chain: Gemini Flash → GPT-5.2 → Haiku → Opus. Most callers set `model_override` to use one model only.\n- **`backend/app/services/claude_code.py`**: Local-only wrapper for `claude -p`. Two modes: `generate_structured()` (single-turn JSON) and `generate_with_tools()` (multi-turn agentic with Read/Bash).\n- **Claude Code does NOT work in Docker** — only on local machine. Server cron jobs can't use it without installing Node.js + claude CLI on server.\n\n### All LLM Task Sites (18 callers tagged with task_type)\n\n| Task Type | Caller | Model | Volume | Notes |\n|-----------|--------|-------|--------|-------|\n| `sentence_gen` | llm.py `generate_sentence()` | Gemini Flash | High (cron) | Single target word |\n| `sentence_gen_batch` | llm.py `generate_sentences_batch()` | Gemini Flash | High (cron) | Multiple sentences per call |\n| `sentence_gen_multi` | llm.py `generate_sentences_multi_target()` | Gemini Flash | High (cron) | 2+ target words per sentence |\n| `quality_review` | llm.py `review_sentences_quality()` | Haiku (API) | High (per sentence batch) | Fail-closed gate |\n| `enrichment_forms` | lemma_enrichment.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Medium (post-import) | forms_json for vocab expansion |\n| `enrichment_etymology` | lemma_enrichment.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Medium (post-import) | etymology_json |\n| `memory_hooks` | memory_hooks.py | Haiku (API) | Low (per acquiring word) | Mnemonics, cognates |\n| `variant_detection` | variant_detection.py | Gemini Flash | Low (post-import) | Confirm morphological variants |\n| `import_quality` | import_quality.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Low (post-import) | Classify standard/proper/junk |\n| `flag_evaluation` | flag_evaluator.py | GPT-5.2 | Negligible (9 total) | Auto-fix flagged content |\n| `story_gen` | story_service.py | Opus (API) | Rare | Full stories |\n| `story_title` | story_service.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Rare | Title generation |\n| `story_word_import` | story_service.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Rare | Translate unknown words |\n| `mapping_verification` | sentence_validator.py | Gemini Flash | Off (VERIFY_MAPPINGS_LLM=0) | Homograph disambiguation |\n| `morphology` | morphology.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | One-time | Root meaning backfill |\n| `grammar_tag` | grammar_tagger.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | One-time | Grammar pattern tagging |\n| `book_import` | book_import_service.py | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Rare | OCR cleanup + translation |\n| `chat` | chat.py router | Gemini Flash (fallback) | Rare | User Q&A |\n\n### Cost Analysis (Feb 8-19, server logs)\n\n**Gemini 3 Flash pricing**: $0.50/M input tokens, $3.00/M output tokens\n\n| Model | Successful Calls | Input Tokens (est) | Est. Total Cost |\n|-------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|\n| Gemini Flash | 12,388 | ~10.5M | $10-15 |\n| GPT-5.2 | 1,791 | ~861K | $3-5 |\n| Haiku (API) | 963 | ~239K | $0.34 |\n| OCR (vision) | 230 | unknown (images) | unknown |\n| **Total** | **15,142** | | **~$14-20** |\n\n**GPT-5.2 was a one-time spike** (Feb 11-12 redesign scripts), not ongoing. Multiple scripts (`verify_sentences.py`, `generate_sentences.py`, `pregenerate_material.py`, `analyze_word_distribution.py`) default to `--model openai` — should be changed to `gemini`.\n\n**Ongoing daily cost** (post-redesign, excluding one-time scripts): ~$1-1.50/day, almost entirely Gemini Flash.\n\n**By task category** (Gemini Flash only, inferred from prompt length):\n- Enrichment (<500 chars): 6,263 calls — $0.08 (cheap but high volume)\n- Sentence gen (2k-8k chars): 2,979 calls — $0.66\n- Multi-target sentence gen (8k+): 1,294 calls — $1.46\n- Quality review/forms (500-2k): 1,852 calls — $0.16\n\n### Claude Code CLI Capabilities\n- **`generate_structured()`**: Single-turn, `--json-schema` validation, models: opus/sonnet/haiku\n- **`generate_with_tools()`**: Multi-turn agentic with Read/Bash, self-validation loops, $0.50 budget cap\n- **`dump_vocabulary_for_claude()`**: Exports vocab as `vocab_prompt.txt` + `vocab_lookup.tsv` for Claude sessions\n- **Existing scripts**: `generate_story_claude.py`, `generate_sentences_claude.py`, `audit_sentences_claude.py`\n- **Local only** — `is_available()` checks for `claude` in PATH, returns False in Docker\n\n### Server Health\n- Gemini Flash + Haiku both work fine on server (96%+ success rate)\n- `update_material.py` cron was crashing since Feb 17 18:30 — **fixed and deployed** (None stability bug)\n- Cron runs every 6h: `rotate_stale_sentences.py` then `update_material.py --limit 50`\n- Cron script at `/opt/alif-update-material.sh`, log at `/var/log/alif-update-material.log`\n\n### Bugs Found & Fixed\n1. **`update_material.py:531`**: `card.get(\"stability\", 0)` → `(card.get(\"stability\") or 0)` — None stability crashes audio eligibility check\n2. **Script defaults**: Several scripts default to `--model openai` instead of `--model gemini` (not yet changed)\n\n### What Was Committed\n- `6e5ad70` — task_type logging on all 18 callers, audit script, cron fix\n\n## Phase 2: Quality Benchmarks — COMPLETE (2026-02-19)\n\n### Quick Wins (done)\n- Changed 4 script defaults from `--model openai` to `--model gemini` (verify_sentences, pregenerate_material, generate_sentences, analyze_word_distribution)\n- Fixed `audit_llm_usage.py` cost estimates: Gemini Flash $0.075→$0.50/M input, added proper output pricing\n\n### Step 3: Benchmark Results\n\nBenchmark script: `backend/scripts/benchmark_claude_code.py`\n\n#### Sentence Generation (5 words × 3 sentences each)\n\n| Model | Validator Pass% | Total Time | Per Word | Cost |\n|-------|----------------|-----------|----------|------|\n| Gemini Flash (API) | 73.3% | 14.2s | 2.8s | ~$0.001 |\n| **Sonnet (CLI, per-word)** | **86.7%** | 160.4s | 32.1s | Free |\n| Sonnet (CLI, batched) | 73.3% | 79.9s | 16.0s | Free |\n\n**Key finding**: Sonnet per-word has the best vocabulary compliance. Batching cuts time 50% but quality dropped to Gemini level — the batch prompt may need tuning. Sonnet is ~11x slower than Gemini but free and higher quality.\n\n**Invalid sentence analysis**: Most failures are conjugated forms not in forms_json (ذَهَبْتُ, مَكْتُوبَةٌ, وَجَدَتِ). This is a validator limitation, not a model quality issue — both Gemini and Sonnet produce natural Arabic.\n\n#### Quality Gate (10 known-good sentences)\n\n| Model | Approval% | Time | Notes |\n|-------|-----------|------|-------|\n| Gemini Flash (API) | 80% | 5.9s | |\n| **Haiku (API)** | **90%** | 8.1s | Current production — best balance |\n| Sonnet (CLI) | 90% | 41.7s | Same quality as Haiku API, 5x slower |\n| Haiku (CLI) | 60% | 16.0s | Unexpectedly strict — possible prompt/format issue |\n\n**Key finding**: Haiku API remains the best quality gate. Sonnet matches but is much slower. Haiku CLI behaves differently (more rejections) — likely a `--json-schema` vs `response_format` formatting difference worth investigating.\n\n#### Forms Generation (8 words)\n\n| Model | Match% | Avg Time |\n|-------|--------|----------|\n| Gemini Flash | 100% | 1.3s |\n| Sonnet (CLI) | 100% | 12.4s |\n| Haiku (CLI) | 100% | 6.9s |\n\nAll models produce identical quality forms. Gemini is 5-10x faster and this task is cheap ($0.001/word), so no reason to switch.\n\n#### Memory Hooks (5 words)\n\n| Model | Avg Time | Cognates/word | Mnemonic Quality |\n|-------|----------|---------------|------------------|\n| Gemini Flash | 3.4s | 2-3 | Good sound-alikes |\n| **Sonnet (CLI)** | 29.9s | **3-4** | **Best — creative, more cognates** |\n| Haiku (CLI) | 12.2s | 1-4 | Decent but less creative |\n\n**Key finding**: Sonnet produces noticeably better mnemonics with more cognates. Worth using for hooks since they're generated once per word (low volume) and quality matters most.\n\n### Recommendations\n\nBased on benchmarks, here's the migration priority:\n\n| Task | Current → Recommended | Rationale |\n|------|----------------------|-----------|\n| Sentence gen | Gemini → **Keep Gemini** | 2.8s vs 32s, pass rate difference is mostly validator false negatives. Gemini is fast+cheap. |\n| Quality gate | Haiku API → **Keep Haiku API** | Best balance of accuracy (90%) and speed. CLI Haiku too strict. |\n| Forms gen | Gemini → **Keep Gemini** | All models 100% match. Gemini is 5-10x faster. |\n| Memory hooks | Haiku API → **Sonnet CLI** | Sonnet quality is noticeably better. Low volume (once per word). |\n| Story gen | Opus API → **Opus CLI** | Already proven. Save API costs. |\n| Flag eval | GPT-5.2 → **Gemini** | Only 9 flags total. GPT-5.2 is overkill. |\n\n**Biggest savings opportunity**: Not model switching (costs are already low at ~$1.50/day) but **reducing false validator rejections** — expanding forms_json with more conjugation patterns would improve pass rates for ALL models equally.\n\n### Phase 3: Implementation Complete\n\nAll background LLM tasks switched to Claude CLI (free via Max plan):\n\n| Task | Old Model | New Model | Status |\n|------|-----------|-----------|--------|\n| Sentence gen (cron) | Gemini Flash | **Claude Sonnet CLI** | Done — `update_material.py --model claude_sonnet` |\n| Sentence gen (on-demand) | Gemini Flash | **Gemini Flash** (kept) | No change — latency-critical |\n| Quality gate | Haiku API ($) | **Haiku CLI** (free) | Done — `llm.py review_sentences_quality()` |\n| Enrichment (forms) | Gemini Flash | **Haiku CLI** (free) | Done — `lemma_enrichment.py` |\n| Enrichment (etymology) | Gemini Flash | **Haiku CLI** (free) | Done — `lemma_enrichment.py` |\n| Memory hooks | Haiku API ($) | **Haiku CLI** (free) | Done — `memory_hooks.py` |\n| Pre-gen warm cache | Gemini Flash | **Gemini Flash** (kept) | No change — runs during user session |\n| Story gen | Opus API | Opus API (kept) | TODO — switch to Opus CLI |\n| Flag eval | GPT-5.2 | GPT-5.2 (kept) | Negligible volume (9 total) |\n\n**Key architecture decisions:**\n- `llm.py` has `_generate_via_claude_cli()` — shells out to `claude -p` with `--output-format json`\n- Model overrides `claude_sonnet` and `claude_haiku` route through CLI, all others through LiteLLM API\n- On-demand generation (user-facing) stays on Gemini for ~1s latency\n- Background/cron tasks use Claude CLI — ~15-30s latency is fine for batch jobs\n- Claude CLI authenticated on server via `claude setup-token` (Max plan)\n\n### Remaining Work\n\n1. **Expand forms_json validator** — add past_1s, present_3fs, present_3p, passive forms to reduce false negatives\n2. **Switch stories to Opus CLI** — already have `generate_story_claude.py`\n3. **Agentic enrichment prototype** — one session for forms+etymology+hooks per batch\n4. **Monitor post-deploy** — verify Claude CLI stability on server cron\n\n## Key Files\n- `backend/app/services/llm.py` — LLM router with Claude CLI integration\n- `backend/app/services/claude_code.py` — Claude Code CLI wrapper (standalone scripts)\n- `backend/scripts/benchmark_claude_code.py` — Benchmark script\n- `backend/scripts/audit_llm_usage.py` — Cost audit script\n- `backend/scripts/update_material.py` — Cron sentence generation (default: claude_sonnet)\n- `research/experiment-log.md` — Updated with findings\n- `IDEAS.md` — Updated with new ideas\n", "date": "Feb 19", "category": "cost", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/llm-cost-investigation-2026-02-19.md"}, "norwegian-arabic-curriculum-2026-02-21": {"title": "Norwegian High School Arabic Curriculum — Benchmarking", "content": "# Norwegian High School Arabic Curriculum — Benchmarking\n\n## The Only Textbook Series\n\nTwo volumes by **John Erik Sætren** (PhD Arabic, UiB) and **Afrah Mohammed Ghalion** (Arabic teacher, Stavanger katedralskole), published by Fagbokforlaget:\n\n### Volume I: \"Lær arabisk!\" (Arabisk for vg2)\n- **Published**: 2014, 308 pages\n- **Structure**: 12 chapters across 4 themes\n - Theme 1 (ch 1-3): The Arabic language — script, dialects vs. MSA, cultural overview\n - Theme 2 (ch 4-6): Self, family & friends — greetings, names, origins, age, family, professions, colors\n - Theme 3 (ch 7-9): Home & school — rooms, directions, school life, transport, days of the week, telling time\n - Theme 4 (ch 10-12): After school — leisure, food/restaurants, shopping/prices\n- **Target**: Students with zero prior Arabic knowledge (Nivå I / fremmedspråk nivå I)\n\n### Volume II: \"Arabisk for vg3\"\n- **Published**: 2016, 250 pages\n- **Structure**: 5 thematic units\n - Final theme: specialization topics (politics, literature, extracting info from advanced texts)\n- **Target**: Students who completed Nivå I (Nivå II / fremmedspråk nivå II)\n\nBoth include vocabulary lists per chapter (ordlister), exercises, basic grammar, and companion web resources with audio and interactive exercises.\n\n## Level Achieved\n\n### UDIR official position on Arabic and CEFR\n\nThe Norwegian Directorate for Education (UDIR) explicitly distinguishes:\n- **European languages** (French, Spanish, German, etc.): Nivå I ≈ CEFR A1, Nivå II ≈ CEFR A2\n- **Non-European languages** (Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Urdu): **CEFR does not apply**. Students \"may not reach A1\" after completing Nivå I.\n\nThis means after **two full years** of Arabic instruction (vg2 + vg3, ~190 teaching hours each at 2-3 hours/week), a Norwegian high school student is realistically at **sub-A1 to A1**. The writing system overhead and morphological complexity eat into what would otherwise be vocabulary and grammar time.\n\n### Why Arabic is harder to benchmark\n- Script learning (letter forms, connected writing) consumes early months\n- Root-and-pattern morphology is fundamentally different from European inflection\n- No cognates with Norwegian (unlike French/Spanish/German)\n- Diglossia: MSA taught in school vs. dialects in media\n\n## Vocabulary Size (Estimated)\n\nNo published word counts for the textbooks exist. Cross-referenced estimates:\n\n| Source | Estimate |\n|--------|----------|\n| CEFR A1 Arabic (eArabicLearning) | 500-700 words |\n| Typical A1 textbook (12 chapters × ~40 words/chapter) | ~480 words |\n| Volume I topics (survival: greetings, family, school, food, shopping) | ~400-600 words |\n| Volume II adds 5 thematic units | ~300-500 additional words |\n| **Total estimate after both volumes** | **~700-1000 words** |\n\nFor reference, CEFR benchmarks for Arabic vocabulary:\n- A1: 500-700 words\n- A2: 800-1,200 words\n- B1: 1,500-2,000 words\n- B2: 2,500-3,500 words\n\n## Relevance for Alif\n\nAs of 2026-02-21, Alif tracks ~500+ lemmas in active learning (acquiring + FSRS), meaning the app already covers roughly the equivalent of the entire Volume I vocabulary, achieved in weeks rather than a school year.\n\nThis benchmark is useful for:\n- **Framing progress**: \"You know as many words as a Norwegian high school graduate in Arabic\" is a motivating milestone\n- **Vocabulary selection**: The textbook themes (family, school, food, shopping, leisure) represent the canonical A1 survival vocabulary — useful for ensuring Alif's word introduction covers these domains\n- **Setting expectations**: Even formal instruction only reaches sub-A1 to A1 after 2 years — the difficulty of Arabic for Nordic speakers is real\n\n## Sources\n- [UDIR - Fremmedspråk nivå I](https://www.udir.no/laring-og-trivsel/lareplanverket/kjennetegn/kjennetegn-pa-maloppnaelse--fremmedsprak-niva-i/)\n- [UDIR - Fremmedspråk nivå II](https://www.udir.no/laring-og-trivsel/lareplanverket/kjennetegn/kjennetegn-pa-maloppnaelse-fremmedsprak-niva-ii/)\n- [Lær arabisk! — Fagbokforlaget](https://fagbokforlaget.no/produkt/9788245013573-laer-arabisk)\n- [Arabisk for vg3 — Fagbokforlaget](https://fagbokforlaget.no/products/9788245013580-arabisk-for-vg3)\n- [VG2 Arabic curriculum overview](https://arabiskfr.portfolio.no/read/c62f8ff0-3da6-41dc-8504-93b6bb3f2a40)\n- [Arabic Vocabulary by CEFR Level](https://earabiclearning.com/blog/2026/01/arabic-vocabulary-by-level/)\n", "date": "Feb 21", "category": "education", "status": "active", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/norwegian-arabic-curriculum-2026-02-21.md"}, "per-word-contextual-translations": {"title": "Per-Word Contextual Translations in Sentence Review", "content": "# Per-Word Contextual Translations in Sentence Review\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-13\n**Status**: Idea — revisit during sentence generation redesign\n**Triggered by**: Non-clickable words in review (words with no lemma_id and no function word gloss)\n\n## Current State\n\n### How word glosses work today\n\nThe LLM sentence generation returns only sentence-level data:\n```python\nclass SentenceResult(BaseModel):\n arabic: str # Full diacritized Arabic sentence\n english: str # Full English translation\n transliteration: str # ALA-LC transliteration\n```\n\nPer-word data is produced **deterministically after generation** by `sentence_validator.py`:\n- Tokenize the Arabic text\n- Strip diacritics + clitics from each token\n- Match against the lemma database (`build_lemma_lookup()`)\n- Store in `sentence_words` table: position, surface_form, lemma_id, is_target_word\n\nThe `sentence_words` table has **no `gloss_en` column**. (In contrast, `story_words` does have one.)\n\n### How glosses reach the frontend\n\nWhen building review sessions, `sentence_selector.py` resolves glosses at query time:\n\n```python\ngloss = lemma.gloss_en if lemma else FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES.get(bare)\n```\n\nThree fallback levels:\n1. **Lemma in DB** → use `Lemma.gloss_en` (dictionary definition)\n2. **In FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES** → hardcoded dict with ~130 entries (prepositions, pronouns, particles, etc.)\n3. **Neither** → `gloss_en: null` sent to frontend\n\n### The gap\n\nWords that fall through all three levels:\n- No `lemma_id` because the backfill (`lookup_lemma_id()`) couldn't match the surface form\n- Not in `FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES` because they're content words, not function words\n- Result: the word is either not tappable or shows an empty info card\n\nExamples of how this happens:\n- Complex clitic combinations the stripping logic doesn't handle\n- Proper nouns not in the lemma database\n- Variant forms not covered by `forms_json`\n- Words from older sentences before the lemma database was comprehensive\n\n## Function Word Tracking\n\nFunction words exist in a gray area:\n- **~113 words** in `FUNCTION_WORDS` set (used for validation gating)\n- **~130 entries** in `FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES` dict (used for frontend glosses)\n- **Some have lemma entries** in the DB (from imports) — these are explicitly skipped for FSRS credit in `sentence_review_service.py`\n- **Some don't** — they rely entirely on the hardcoded glosses\n- A cleanup script (`cleanup_glosses.py`) periodically deletes accidental ULK records for function words\n\nThis means function words work either way, but there's no single source of truth. Worth considering whether all function words should have proper lemma entries for consistency.\n\n## The Idea: LLM-Generated Contextual Translations\n\n### Motivation\n\nInstead of just copying dictionary definitions to `sentence_words`, ask the LLM to provide per-word **contextual** translations during sentence generation. This solves two problems:\n\n1. **Coverage**: Every word gets a gloss, regardless of whether it's in the lemma DB\n2. **Context-specificity**: A word like عين means \"eye\" in one sentence and \"spring (water)\" in another. The dictionary gloss lists both; the contextual gloss gives the right one.\n\n### What changes\n\n**LLM response model** — add per-word breakdown:\n```python\nclass SentenceResult(BaseModel):\n arabic: str\n english: str\n transliteration: str\n word_glosses: dict[str, str] # surface_form → contextual English gloss\n```\n\n**Database** — add column:\n```python\nclass SentenceWord(Base):\n # ... existing columns ...\n gloss_en = Column(Text, nullable=True) # Contextual gloss from LLM\n```\n\n**Sentence selector** — prefer stored contextual gloss:\n```python\ngloss = sw.gloss_en or (lemma.gloss_en if lemma else FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES.get(bare))\n```\n\n### Trade-offs\n\n| Pro | Con |\n|-----|-----|\n| 100% word coverage — no more null glosses | ~20% more output tokens per generation |\n| Context-specific meanings | Need to match LLM word keys to tokenized surface forms |\n| Learning value: see how a word is used HERE | Existing ~300 active sentences need backfill |\n| Generated once, stored forever | LLM might return inconsistent word segmentation |\n| No dependency on lemma DB completeness | Quality varies — LLM might gloss particles unhelpfully |\n\n### Matching challenge\n\nThe LLM's word segmentation may not match our tokenizer's output. For example:\n- LLM might return \"بالكتاب\" as one key; our tokenizer might strip it to \"كتاب\"\n- LLM might split clitics differently than our `_strip_clitics()`\n\nSolution options:\n- Fuzzy matching by stripped form\n- Ask LLM to use diacritized forms (matches our stored surface_form)\n- Accept partial coverage — unmatched words fall back to lemma gloss\n\n### Interim alternative: Lemma-based backfill\n\nA simpler approach that covers most cases without LLM changes:\n\n1. Add `gloss_en` column to `SentenceWord`\n2. At creation time, populate from `Lemma.gloss_en` + `FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES`\n3. Backfill existing rows via SQL join\n\nThis covers ~95% of words (anything with a lemma_id or in the function word dict) but doesn't provide contextual meanings and doesn't help words with no lemma match.\n\nCould be implemented as a stepping stone before the full LLM approach.\n\n## Implementation Timing\n\nRevisit when sentence generation is being redesigned. The prompt changes fit naturally into a generation overhaul. The `gloss_en` column on `SentenceWord` is needed either way (lemma-based or LLM-based) and could be added independently.\n\n## Related Files\n\n- `backend/app/services/llm.py` — LLM prompts and response models\n- `backend/app/services/sentence_generator.py` — generation orchestration\n- `backend/app/services/sentence_validator.py` — tokenization, `FUNCTION_WORD_GLOSSES`, `map_tokens_to_lemmas()`\n- `backend/app/services/sentence_selector.py` — session building, gloss resolution (lines 630, 1089, 1181)\n- `backend/app/services/material_generator.py` — sentence storage, `SentenceWord` creation\n- `backend/app/models.py` — `SentenceWord` model (no gloss_en), `StoryWord` model (has gloss_en)\n- `backend/app/routers/review.py` — recap endpoint gloss resolution (line 421)\n", "date": null, "category": "quality", "status": "todo", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/per-word-contextual-translations.md"}, "research-knowledge-system-2026-02-22": {"title": "Designing a Personal Research Knowledge System", "content": "# Designing a Personal Research Knowledge System\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-22\n**Purpose**: Given the landscape of agent memory systems (see [agent-knowledge-systems-2026-02-21.md](agent-knowledge-systems-2026-02-21.md)), design a system for a human researcher interested in educational theory, Norwegian politics, cognitive science, and idea history — where diverse sources (news articles, opinion pieces, academic papers, personal questions) are ingested into a growing knowledge base with claim chains, source attribution, contradiction detection, and rich human/agent interfaces.\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [The Use Case Concretely](#1-use-case)\n2. [What Exists Today: Tool Landscape](#2-tool-landscape)\n3. [UI/UX Patterns Worth Stealing](#3-ux-patterns)\n4.
