[Claim Chains and Argument Structure](#4-claim-chains)\n5. [The Data Model](#5-data-model)\n6. [The Processing Pipeline](#6-pipeline)\n7. [The Recommended Stack](#7-stack)\n8. [Scaling Strategy](#8-scaling)\n9. [What to Build vs Buy](#9-build-vs-buy)\n10. [Sources](#10-sources)\n\n---\n\n## 1. The Use Case Concretely {#1-use-case}\n\nA researcher wants to:\n\n- **Dump in** a news article about Norwegian curriculum reform, an academic paper on knowledge-based curricula (Hirsch, Core Knowledge), a political opinion piece about skolepolitikk, a personal research question about how cognitive load theory applies to reading instruction\n- Have the system **automatically extract claims** from each source with attribution\n- **Detect connections**: the opinion piece's claims about \"kompetansemål\" relate to the academic paper's findings about explicit knowledge instruction; the cognitive load paper's \"germane load\" concept connects to the curriculum reform's \"dybdelæring\"\n- **Surface contradictions**: Source A says fact-based teaching improves outcomes; Source B says it reduces creativity. Flag this.\n- **Build up gradually** over months/years without manual curation becoming the bottleneck\n- **Browse richly** as a human — see claim chains, source maps, topic clusters, surprising connections\n- **Hand off to agents** — an agent should be able to query \"what are the main arguments for and against knowledge-based curricula in the Norwegian context?\" and get structured results\n\n---\n\n## 2. What Exists Today: Tool Landscape {#2-tool-landscape}\n\n### Capture & Ingestion\n\n| Tool | Best For | Agent API | Price |\n|------|----------|-----------|-------|\n| [Readwise Reader](https://readwise.io/read) | Articles, newsletters, PDFs, podcasts | MCP server (official) | $10/mo |\n| [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org) | Academic papers, citations | 4+ MCP servers | Free |\n| [Hypothesis](https://hypothes.is) | Web annotations on specific passages | REST API | Free |\n| RSS + [n8n](https://n8n.io) | Automated news monitoring | Fully programmable | Free (self-hosted) |\n\n**Readwise** is the strongest capture layer. Every highlight from any source (Kindle, web, PDF, podcast) flows into Readwise, which syncs to your knowledge base. The [official MCP server](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/@readwiseio/readwise-mcp) lets agents both read and write. Handles the \"dump articles in\" requirement well.\n\n**Zotero** for academic papers has the richest agent integration: [zotero-mcp](https://github.com/54yyyu/zotero-mcp) (590+ stars) adds vector-based similarity search over your library.\n\n### Academic Discovery\n\n| Tool | Distinctive Feature | API | Price |\n|------|-------------------|-----|-------|\n| [Semantic Scholar](https://www.semanticscholar.org) | 225M papers, SPECTER2 embeddings, citation graph | Excellent REST API (free) | Free |\n| [Elicit](https://elicit.com) | Claim extraction with sentence-level citations, 99.4% accuracy | Enterprise only | $10-42/mo |\n| [Consensus](https://consensus.app) | \"Consensus Meter\" — yes/no/mixed distribution across papers | None public | Free tier |\n| [Connected Papers](https://www.connectedpapers.com) | Visual co-citation clusters from one seed paper | None | Free (5/mo) |\n| [Research Rabbit](https://www.researchrabbit.ai) | Collection-based iterative discovery, Zotero integration | None | Free |\n| [Litmaps](https://www.litmaps.com) | Year × citation scatter plot | None | Free tier |\n| [Inciteful](https://inciteful.xyz) | Literature Connector — finds citation paths between two papers | None | Free |\n| [Scite](https://scite.ai) | Shows whether citing papers support, contradict, or just mention | API | Paid |\n\n**Semantic Scholar** is the infrastructure layer — free API, 225M papers, embeddings for similarity. Multiple MCP servers exist. Any agent-driven research system should build on this.\n\n**Elicit** is the closest to what we want for claim extraction. It treats research as a table: each row is a paper, each column is a question. Every answer has a sentence-level citation. But it is academic-only, not general.\n\n### Knowledge Base / PKM\n\n| Tool | Agent Compatibility | Distinctive Feature | Price |\n|------|-------------------|-------------------|-------|\n| [Obsidian](https://obsidian.md) | **Highest** (45+ MCP servers, plain markdown) | Plugin ecosystem, graph view | Free |\n| [Logseq](https://logseq.com) | Medium (files on disk) | Block-based outliner, built-in PDF annotation | Free |\n| [Capacities](https://capacities.io) | Low (limited API) | Object-based (typed entities with properties) | Free tier |\n| [Heptabase](https://heptabase.com) | Low (no API) | Spatial canvas for visual arrangement | $12/mo |\n| [Reor](https://github.com/reorproject/reor) | Local (filesystem) | Auto-connects notes via embeddings, local LLM | Free |\n| [Khoj](https://github.com/khoj-ai/khoj) | High (open source, API) | Self-hosted AI with semantic search, /research mode | Free |\n\n**Obsidian** has the highest agent compatibility of any PKM tool. 45+ MCP servers. Files are plain markdown on disk — any agent can read/write directly. The plugin ecosystem covers every workflow. Readwise highlights auto-sync to Obsidian.\n\n**Capacities** has the most interesting *data model* — typed objects (Book, Person, Concept, Claim) with typed relationships. This is closer to what a research knowledge graph needs. But the API is too limited for agent use today.\n\n**Khoj** is the closest all-in-one: open source, self-hosted, imports PDFs/markdown/web, semantic search, custom agents, scheduled automations. Worth evaluating as a backend.\n\n### Research AI Assistants\n\n| Tool | Best For | Price |\n|------|----------|-------|\n| [Perplexity](https://www.perplexity.ai) | Cross-domain research (academic + news + opinion) | Free/$20 |\n| [NotebookLM](https://notebooklm.google.com) | Interacting with a closed corpus of your documents | Free |\n| [Perplexity Spaces](https://www.perplexity.ai) | Persistent research environments with uploaded files | $20/mo |\n\n**Perplexity Spaces** is surprisingly capable for the multi-domain researcher — persistent environments where you upload files, set custom instructions, and keep related searches organized.\n\n**NotebookLM** is excellent for interacting with a specific corpus but locked in Google's ecosystem with no API.\n\n---\n\n## 3. UI/UX Patterns Worth Stealing {#3-ux-patterns}\n\n### From gwern.net — The Gold Standard for Knowledge Browsing\n\n[gwern.net/design](https://gwern.net/design) | [GitHub](https://github.com/gwern/gwern.net)\n\nThe most information-dense, deeply browseable knowledge site on the web. Key patterns:\n\n**Recursive popups**: Hover any link → preview with annotation/abstract/excerpt. Popups themselves contain hoverable links. Creates \"iceberg\" pages — simple surface, arbitrary depth available without leaving the page. This is the single best progressive disclosure pattern for knowledge browsing.\n\n**Sidenotes**: Footnotes rendered in the margin alongside relevant text (wide screens). No interruption to reading flow.\n\n**Hierarchical tags as pages**: Tags map to filesystem directories (`/doc/education/policy/`). Tags are pages with transcluded content. Supports nested hierarchies.\n\n**Link icons**: Visual indicators showing link type (PDF, Wikipedia, video, archived) at a glance. Semantic hints without clicking.\n\n**Semantic similarity**: \"Similar pages\" generated by embedding distance. Agent-generated connections between related content.\n\n**LLM-friendly**: Explicitly serves Markdown source for agent consumption. [Commit](https://github.com/gwern/gwern.net/commit/79ded21772a9aa338158c16a19dd4dad5a8f3d6b).\n\n**Technical**: Hakyll (Haskell) static site generator, Pandoc Markdown, custom JS for popups/sidenotes.\n\n### From Maggie Appleton — Epistemic Status Markers\n\n[maggieappleton.com/garden-history](https://maggieappleton.com/garden-history)\n\n**Six patterns of digital gardens**:\n1. **Topography over timelines** — content connected by theme, not date\n2. **Continuous growth** — ideas develop over time, never \"finished\"\n3. **Imperfection & learning in public** — work-in-progress is visible\n4. **Playful and personal** — no standardized templates\n5. **Intercropping** — multiple media types coexist\n6. **Independent ownership** — self-hosted, not on platforms\n\n**Growth stages**: Seedling (rough idea) → Budding (developing) → Evergreen (mature). This epistemic status marker tells both humans and agents how much to trust each piece.\n\n### From Andy Matuschak — Stacked Panes\n\n[notes.andymatuschak.org](https://notes.andymatuschak.org/)\n\n**Stacked/sliding panes**: Clicking a link opens the target in a new pane to the right, preserving the full exploration path. You see 3-5 notes simultaneously, revealing the chain of reasoning. URLs encode the full stack.\n\n**Atomic notes**: Each note is one concept, densely linked. No hierarchy — structure emerges from connections.\n\n### From Andy Matuschak — Embedded Spaced Repetition\n\n[Orbit](https://withorbit.com/) | [quantum.country](https://quantum.country/)\n\nSpaced repetition prompts embedded directly in prose. As you read, you encounter review questions. The reader progressively opts into deeper engagement. Web component (``) embeddable in any page.\n\n### From Kialo — Argument Trees\n\n[kialo.com](https://www.kialo.com/)\n\nDebate trees: central thesis → Pro (green) and Con (red) branches → each can have its own Pros/Cons recursively. Color-coded, with source aggregation sidebar. The visual structure immediately communicates the shape of a debate.\n\n### From Argdown — Argument Mapping from Text\n\n[argdown.org](https://argdown.org/) | [GitHub](https://github.com/christianvoigt/argdown)\n\nMarkdown-inspired syntax for structured argumentation that auto-generates visual maps:\n```\n[Knowledge-based curricula improve outcomes]\n + \n (1) University of Virginia study shows reading gains\n (2) Gains persist through grade 5\n ----\n (3) Structured knowledge instruction works\n - : May reduce creative thinking\n + : Knowledge enables creativity (Willingham)\n```\n\nOutputs SVG/PDF/web component. VS Code extension and Obsidian plugin available. **Most agent-friendly argumentation tool** — LLMs can generate the plain-text syntax natively.\n\n### From Heptabase/Scrintal — Spatial Canvas\n\nInfinite whiteboards with cards arranged spatially. Same card on multiple boards. Double-click connections for relationship labels. The spatial arrangement mirrors how the brain organizes ideas — proximity = relatedness.\n\n### From ORKG — Structured Research Comparisons\n\n[orkg.org](https://orkg.org/)\n\nPapers structured as interconnected resources with properties and statements. **Comparisons** display research contributions side-by-side. **Templates** ensure consistent claim capture. PDF annotator for extracting claims from documents. Python API for batch operations.\n\n### Summary: Patterns to Combine\n\n| Pattern | Source | Why |\n|---------|--------|-----|\n| Recursive popups | gwern.net | Progressive disclosure without losing context |\n| Epistemic status markers | Maggie Appleton | Trust calibration for growing knowledge |\n| Stacked panes | Andy Matuschak | Preserve exploration chains |\n| Argument trees | Kialo/Argdown | Visualize claim structure |\n| Spatial canvas | Heptabase | Visual arrangement for thinking |\n| Embedded spaced repetition | Orbit | Deepen engagement with claims |\n| Faceted classification | Library science (SKOS) | Multi-axis browsing without hierarchy |\n| Bi-directional backlinks | Obsidian/gwern | See where knowledge connects |\n| Semantic similarity | gwern.net | Agent-generated connections |\n\n---\n\n## 4. Claim Chains and Argument Structure {#4-claim-chains}\n\n### What Is a Claim Chain?\n\nA claim chain traces: **Source → Claim → Evidence → Related Claims → Synthesis**\n\nExample:\n- *Source*: Hirsch (2016), \"Why Knowledge Matters\"\n- *Claim*: \"Explicit knowledge instruction improves reading comprehension\"\n- *Evidence*: \"University of Virginia study (Grissmer): Core Knowledge schools showed significant reading gains\"\n- *Related Claim* (supports): \"Background knowledge is necessary for reading comprehension\" — Willingham (2006)\n- *Related Claim* (contradicts): \"Skill-based approaches are more adaptable\" — NOU 2015:8 (Norway)\n- *Synthesis*: The evidence for knowledge-based instruction is strong but the Norwegian policy context favors kompetansemål (competence goals) over innholdsmål (content goals)\n\n### The IBIS Model (Best Conceptual Foundation)\n\nIssue-Based Information Systems (Rittel & Webber, 1970):\n- **Issues** (questions): \"Should Norwegian curricula specify content knowledge?\"\n- **Positions** (answers): \"Yes, content knowledge is essential for comprehension\"\n- **Arguments** (evidence): Pro/con supporting or objecting to positions\n\nNine link types: responds-to, questions, supports, objects-to, specializes, generalizes, refers-to, replaces, is-about\n\nThis maps naturally to research: Issues are your research questions, Positions are the competing answers in the literature, Arguments are the evidence from specific sources.\n\n### Wikidata-Inspired Provenance Model\n\nEvery claim needs:\n- **The claim itself** (canonical declarative statement)\n- **Qualifiers** (time period, geographic scope, population, methodology)\n- **References** (source, extraction date, exact quote, location in source)\n- **Rank** (preferred / normal / deprecated — for evolving knowledge)\n\n### Contradiction Detection (from Zep/Graphiti)\n\nOn ingestion of each new source:\n1. Extract claims\n2. Embed each claim\n3. Search for semantically similar existing claims\n4. LLM classifies relationship: **confirms**, **contradicts**, **refines**, **extends**, **is orthogonal**\n5. If contradicts: set `t_invalid` on old claim, keep both, surface for review\n6. If surprising connection: flag for human attention\n\n### Practical Claim Extraction\n\nThe most practical approach for a single-user system: LLM-based extraction with a structured output schema:\n\n```json\n{\n \"source_summary\": \"Brief summary of the source\",\n \"claims\": [\n {\n \"text\": \"Explicit knowledge instruction improves reading comprehension\",\n \"type\": \"causal\",\n \"confidence\": 0.9,\n \"evidence_quote\": \"Core Knowledge schools showed statistically significant...\",\n \"source_location\": \"p. 47, paragraph 2\"\n }\n ],\n \"key_entities\": [\"Core Knowledge\", \"E.D. Hirsch\", \"reading comprehension\"],\n \"topics\": [\"knowledge-based-curriculum\", \"reading-instruction\", \"educational-research\"]\n}\n```\n\nThis leverages LLMs' strong zero-shot performance on argument mining and avoids the brittleness of traditional NLP pipelines.\n\n---\n\n## 5. The Data Model {#5-data-model}\n\nInspired by Wikidata's claim model + IBIS structure + Graphiti's temporal tracking:\n\n```\nSource\n id, url, title, author, publication, date_published\n date_ingested, source_type (academic|news|opinion|personal_note|book)\n credibility_tier (peer_reviewed|edited_publication|opinion|personal)\n full_text, summary\n topics[]\n\nClaim\n id, text (canonical declarative statement)\n type (factual|causal|evaluative|predictive|prescriptive)\n status (active|superseded|deprecated|contested)\n epistemic_status (seedling|budding|evergreen)\n t_valid_from, t_valid_until (when true in the world)\n t_created, t_expired (when known to the system)\n confidence (0-1)\n topic_ids[]\n\nClaimSource (provenance link)\n claim_id, source_id\n extraction_method (manual|llm_extracted|imported)\n source_quote (exact text)\n source_location (page/paragraph/timestamp)\n\nClaimRelation\n from_claim_id, to_claim_id\n relation_type (supports|contradicts|refines|extends|\n specializes|generalizes|responds_to|replaces)\n strength (0-1)\n detected_by (manual|llm_detected)\n explanation (why this relation exists)\n\nIssue (research question)\n id, text, status (open|resolved|parked)\n related_claim_ids[]\n\nTopicCluster\n id, label, summary, parent_id\n embedding (for semantic search)\n\nEntity (person, institution, concept)\n id, name, type, description\n mentioned_in_claim_ids[]\n```\n\n### Key Design Principles\n\n1. **Never delete, always deprecate** — old claims get `t_expired`, never removed\n2. **Every claim must have provenance** — at least one ClaimSource link\n3. **Relations are first-class** — supports/contradicts/refines between claims\n4. **Bi-temporal** — when you learned it vs when it was true\n5. **Epistemic status** — seedling/budding/evergreen on every claim\n\n---\n\n## 6. The Processing Pipeline {#6-pipeline}\n\n```\n┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐\n│ CAPTURE │───→│ EXTRACT │───→│ LINK │\n│ │ │ │ │ │\n│ Readwise │ │ LLM claim │ │ Embed claims │\n│ Zotero │ │ extraction │ │ Search for │\n│ RSS+n8n │ │ with struct │ │ related │\n│ Manual paste │ │ output │ │ Classify │\n│ Hypothesis │ │ │ │ relation │\n└─────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────┬───────┘\n │\n ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────┴───────┐\n │ SURFACE │←───│ CLUSTER │\n │ │ │ │\n │ Contradict- │ │ BERTopic │\n │ ions digest │ │ Topic labels │\n │ Surprising │ │ Hierarchy │\n │ connections │ │ MOC gen │\n │ New claims │ │ │\n └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘\n```\n\n### Step by Step\n\n1. **Capture**: Drop in a URL, PDF, paste, or personal note. Sources auto-classify by type.\n2. **Extract**: LLM processes source → structured claims with quotes and locations. Entities extracted.\n3. **Deduplicate**: Embedding similarity search against existing claims. LLM judges: new / paraphrase / refinement.\n4. **Link**: For each new claim, find semantically related existing claims. LLM classifies: supports / contradicts / refines / extends.\n5. **Cluster**: Periodically re-cluster with BERTopic. Generate/update topic labels and summaries. Build Maps of Content.\n6. **Surface**: Generate digest of contradictions, surprising connections, newly dense clusters. Notification feed.\n\n---\n\n## 7. The Recommended Stack {#7-stack}\n\n### Layer 1: Capture (Human + Agent)\n\n- **[Readwise Reader](https://readwise.io/read)** ($10/mo) — articles, newsletters, PDFs, podcasts. MCP for agent access. Browser extension for human. Auto-syncs highlights to Obsidian.\n- **[Zotero](https://www.zotero.org)** (free) — academic papers. MCP for agent access. Browser extension. Automatic metadata extraction.\n- **RSS + [n8n](https://n8n.io)** (free, self-hosted) — automated monitoring of Norwegian news, journals, niche blogs. AI summarizes and tags on arrival.\n\n### Layer 2: Knowledge Base (Human-Browseable + Agent-Writable)\n\n- **[Obsidian](https://obsidian.md)** (free) — central knowledge store. Plain markdown on disk. Graph view, Canvas, 45+ MCP servers for agent access. Readwise plugin for auto-sync.\n- **Structured frontmatter** for claim metadata (type, status, confidence, topics, sources)\n- **Typed folders or tags**: `sources/`, `claims/`, `issues/`, `entities/`, `topics/`\n- Publish via [Quartz](https://quartz.jzhao.xyz/) for web browsing with graph view\n\n### Layer 3: Academic Discovery (Agent-Driven)\n\n- **[Semantic Scholar API](https://api.semanticscholar.org)** (free) — 225M papers, citation graphs, embeddings. MCP server available.\n- **[Research Rabbit](https://www.researchrabbit.ai)** (free) — visual exploration from seed papers\n- **[Connected Papers](https://www.connectedpapers.com)** (free) — co-citation cluster visualization\n\n### Layer 4: Analysis & Synthesis (Agent-Driven)\n\n- **Custom Claude/Gemini agents** that:\n - Extract claims from new sources (structured output)\n - Find related claims via embedding search\n - Classify relationships (supports/contradicts/refines)\n - Generate synthesis notes and argument maps (Argdown format)\n - Produce periodic \"contradiction digest\" and \"surprising connections\" reports\n- **[Perplexity Spaces](https://www.perplexity.ai)** ($20/mo) — for deep cross-domain research questions\n\n### Layer 5: Rich Human Interface\n\n- **Obsidian** for daily browsing/editing (graph view, canvas, backlinks)\n- **[Quartz](https://quartz.jzhao.xyz/)** for published web view (gwern-style progressive disclosure)\n- **Custom HTML reports** (like Alif's research-hub.html) for visual overviews\n- **[Argdown](https://argdown.org/)** rendering for argument maps (Obsidian plugin available)\n\n### Layer 6: Monitoring (Automated)\n\n- RSS feeds from: Utdanningsnytt, Klassekampen, Morgenbladet, NRK Ytring, specific academic journals\n- Semantic Scholar alerts for new papers on tracked topics\n- n8n workflows: fetch → summarize → tag → write to Obsidian inbox\n\n---\n\n## 8. Scaling Strategy {#8-scaling}\n\n### < 1,000 Claims (First 6 Months)\n\n- Graph visualization works well\n- Manual curation is feasible\n- Focus on building the extraction pipeline and getting claim quality right\n- Obsidian graph view as primary navigation\n\n### 1,000-10,000 Claims (6-18 Months)\n\n- Automatic clustering with BERTopic\n- Generated Maps of Content (topic overview pages)\n- Search becomes primary navigation, graph secondary\n- Contradiction detection becomes valuable (enough claims to conflict)\n- Faceted filtering: source type, date, topic, confidence, status\n\n### 10,000+ Claims (18+ Months)\n\n- Full semantic search as primary interface\n- Automated maintenance: orphan detection, staleness scoring, cluster coherence\n- Notification-driven exploration (daily digest of new connections, contradictions)\n- Browsing through curated \"paths\" (like gwern's tag pages) rather than raw graph\n- Periodic \"reweave\" passes (Ars Contexta's pattern) to update old knowledge with new context\n\n---\n\n## 9. What to Build vs Buy {#9-build-vs-buy}\n\n### Subscribe\n\n| Tool | Cost | Why |\n|------|------|-----|\n| Readwise Reader | $10/mo | Best capture layer with MCP |\n| Perplexity Pro | $20/mo | Cross-domain research assistant |\n\n### Use Free\n\n| Tool | Why |\n|------|-----|\n| Obsidian | Central knowledge base, highest agent compatibility |\n| Zotero | Academic reference management with MCP |\n| Semantic Scholar API | Paper discovery, citation data, embeddings |\n| Research Rabbit | Visual literature discovery |\n| n8n (self-hosted) | Workflow automation |\n| Quartz | Publish Obsidian vault as browseable website |\n\n### Build Custom\n\n| Component | Approach |\n|-----------|----------|\n| Claim extraction agent | Claude/Gemini + structured output → Obsidian files |\n| Relationship detection | Embedding search + LLM classification |\n| Contradiction surfacing | Bi-temporal claim tracking + periodic scan |\n| Topic clustering | BERTopic over claim embeddings |\n| Argument map generation | LLM → Argdown syntax → visual render |\n| RSS monitoring pipeline | n8n + Claude/Gemini summarization |\n| Periodic digest | Cron agent that scans for new connections/contradictions |\n\nThe key insight: **Obsidian as the central store + Readwise as the capture layer + Zotero for papers + custom agents via MCP/filesystem** gives you a system that is both human-browseable and agent-writable. The auto-organization, claim chains, and contradiction detection are custom agents you build on top.\n\n---\n\n## 10. Sources {#10-sources}\n\n### Tools & Platforms\n- [Readwise Reader](https://readwise.io/read) | [MCP server](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/@readwiseio/readwise-mcp)\n- [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org) | [MCP server](https://github.com/54yyyu/zotero-mcp)\n- [Obsidian](https://obsidian.md) | [MCP server](https://github.com/cyanheads/obsidian-mcp-server)\n- [Semantic Scholar API](https://api.semanticscholar.org/api-docs)\n- [Elicit](https://elicit.com) | [Systematic Reviews](https://elicit.com/solutions/systematic-review)\n- [Consensus](https://consensus.app)\n- [Perplexity](https://www.perplexity.ai)\n- [NotebookLM](https://notebooklm.google.com) | [Deep Research](https://blog.google/technology/google-labs/notebooklm-deep-research-file-types/)\n- [Research Rabbit](https://www.researchrabbit.ai)\n- [Connected Papers](https://www.connectedpapers.com)\n- [Litmaps](https://www.litmaps.com) | [Inciteful](https://inciteful.xyz)\n- [Scite](https://scite.ai)\n- [Khoj](https://github.com/khoj-ai/khoj)\n- [Reor](https://github.com/reorproject/reor)\n- [Quartz](https://quartz.jzhao.xyz)\n- [n8n](https://n8n.io)\n- [Hypothesis](https://hypothes.is) | [API](https://hypothes.is/api/)\n\n### Argument & Claim Systems\n- [Argdown](https://argdown.org) | [GitHub](https://github.com/christianvoigt/argdown)\n- [Kialo](https://www.kialo.com)\n- [IBIS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Issue-based_information_system) (Rittel & Webber 1970)\n- [ORKG](https://orkg.org) (Open Research Knowledge Graph)\n- [ClaimBuster](https://idir.uta.edu/claimbuster/) | [API](https://idir.uta.edu/claimbuster/api/)\n- [Google Fact Check API](https://developers.google.com/fact-check/tools/api)\n- [GROBID](https://github.com/kermitt2/grobid) (paper parsing)\n- [Wikidata data model](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:Data_model)\n\n### Knowledge Visualization\n- [gwern.net design](https://gwern.net/design) | [GitHub](https://github.com/gwern/gwern.net)\n- [Maggie Appleton: Garden History](https://maggieappleton.com/garden-history)\n- [Andy Matuschak's Notes](https://notes.andymatuschak.org)\n- [Orbit (embedded spaced repetition)](https://withorbit.com) | [GitHub](https://github.com/andymatuschak/orbit)\n- [Bret Victor: Explorable Explanations](https://worrydream.com/ExplorableExplanations/)\n- [Heptabase](https://heptabase.com) | [Scrintal](https://scrintal.com)\n- [Cosma](https://cosma.arthurperret.fr/) (portable HTML knowledge graph)\n- [Kumu](https://kumu.io) (systems thinking maps)\n- [Neo4j Bloom](https://neo4j.com/product/bloom/)\n\n### Academic Research\n- [LLMs in Argument Mining (2025 survey)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.16383)\n- [Zep/Graphiti Paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.13956) | [GitHub](https://github.com/getzep/graphiti)\n- [KG-Orchestra](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.02.18.706536v1) (multi-agent evidence-based KG)\n- [The Discovery Engine (2025)](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17500v1)\n- [Serendipitous Knowledge Discovery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1570826824000386)\n- [PKM at Scale: 8,000 notes analysis](https://www.dsebastien.net/personal-knowledge-management-at-scale-analyzing-8-000-notes-and-64-000-links/)\n- [ProVe: Provenance Verification](https://www.semantic-web-journal.net/content/prove-pipeline-automated-provenance-verification-knowledge-graphs-against-textual-sources-0)\n- [ClaimVer: Claim-Level Verification](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.09724v1)\n- [AI for Wikipedia Citation Quality (Nature 2023)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-023-00726-1)\n- [SKOS (W3C)](https://www.w3.org/TR/skos-reference/)\n- [Progressive Disclosure for Agentic Workflows](https://medium.com/@prakashkop054/s01-mcp03-progressive-disclosure-for-knowledge-discovery-in-agentic-workflows-8fc0b2840d01)\n\n### Monitoring & Capture\n- [Readwise MCP Enhanced](https://github.com/arnaldo-delisio/readwise-mcp-enhanced)\n- [Rssage](https://rssage.com) (AI RSS reader)\n- [Media Bias/Fact Check methodology](https://mediabiasfactcheck.com/methodology/)\n", "date": "Feb 22", "category": "agent-knowledge", "status": "active", "cross_refs": ["agent-knowledge-systems-2026-02-21", "https:/obsidian"], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/research-knowledge-system-2026-02-22.md"}, "sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/README": {"title": "Sentence Pipeline Investigation — 2026-02-13", "content": "# Sentence Pipeline Investigation — 2026-02-13\n\nBenchmarked 213 sentences across 3 models x 6 strategies to find the best sentence generation pipeline for Alif.\n\n## Reports\n\n- **[corpus_evaluation.md](corpus_evaluation.md)** — Evaluation of Tatoeba and BAREC as sentence sources for vocabulary-matched review\n- **[generation_benchmark.md](generation_benchmark.md)** — LLM benchmark: GPT-5.2 vs Gemini Flash vs Claude Haiku across 6 prompt strategies\n- **[recommendations.md](recommendations.md)** — Actionable recommendations synthesized from both evaluations\n- **[all_sentences.jsonl](all_sentences.jsonl)** — Raw data: all 213 generated sentences with quality scores and metadata\n\n## Key Findings\n\n- **GPT-5.2 is the worst model** for Arabic generation (4.63/5 quality, produced all 5 \"word salad\" sentences)\n- **Gemini Flash is the best** (4.89/5 quality, 84% compliance, cheapest, fastest)\n- **KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE=50 is the #1 problem** — GPT-5.2 compliance jumps 57%→88% with full vocab\n- **POS-grouped vocabulary** scored 5.0/5 quality, 87% compliance\n- **Quality gate fails open** — bad sentences pass when Gemini is unavailable\n\n## Implementation\n\nAll recommendations implemented in the [Sentence Pipeline Overhaul](../experiment-log.md) (2026-02-13).\n", "date": "Feb 13", "category": "generation", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": ["sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/corpus_evaluation", "sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/generation_benchmark", "sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/recommendations", "experiment-log"], "backlinks": [], "path": "research/sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/README.md"}, "sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/corpus_evaluation": {"title": "Corpus Evaluation Report", "content": "# Corpus Evaluation Report\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-13\n**Objective**: Evaluate Tatoeba and BAREC as sentence sources for vocabulary-matched review.\n\n## 1. Vocabulary Match Results\n\n| Corpus | Vocab Size | Sentences in Range | Passing (>=70%) | Pass Rate | Avg Length |\n|--------|-----------|-------------------|----------------|-----------|------------|\n| Tatoeba | current (196) | 4954 | 13 | 0.3% | 5.2 |\n| Tatoeba | medium (~400) | 4954 | 54 | 1.1% | 5.6 |\n| Tatoeba | large (~1000) | 4954 | 106 | 2.1% | 5.7 |\n| BAREC | current (196) | 29758 | 22 | 0.1% | 5.9 |\n| BAREC | medium (~400) | 29758 | 104 | 0.3% | 6.6 |\n| BAREC | large (~1000) | 29758 | 267 | 0.9% | 7.1 |\n\n## 2. Sample Passing Sentences\n\n### Tatoeba — Current Vocab (196 words)\n - هل أنتَ سعيد مع ذلك؟ — 100% comprehensible\n EN: Are you happy with that?\n - هل ذاك كلب أم قطة؟ — 75% comprehensible [unknown: ذاك]\n EN: Is that a cat or a dog?\n - يا لك من ولد جميل! — 75% comprehensible [unknown: لك]\n EN: You're such a cute boy.\n - يا لها من حديقة جميلة. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: لها]\n EN: What a beautiful garden!\n - يا له من كلب كبير! — 75% comprehensible [unknown: له]\n EN: What a big dog!\n - المرأة بلا رجل لا شيء. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: المرأة]\n EN: A woman without a man is nothing.\n - الولد الذي يغسل السيارة هو أخي. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: يغسل]\n EN: The boy washing the car is my brother.\n - أنا كبير بما فيه الكفاية. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: الكفاية]\n EN: I'm old enough.\n - فتحتُ الباب وخرجت من السيارة. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: وخرجت]\n EN: I opened the door and got out of the car.\n - هل القطة فوق الكرسي أم تحته؟ — 75% comprehensible [unknown: الكرسي]\n EN: Is the cat on the chair or under the chair?\n\n### BAREC — Current Vocab (196 words)\n - في مدينة النخيل كل شيء جميل: — 75% comprehensible [unknown: النخيل]\n - مدرسةٌ جديدةٌ في بلدٍ جديد — 75% comprehensible [unknown: مدرسة]\n - عاش الأب والبنت والولد وزوجة الأب في بيت واحد. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: عاش, واحد]\n - فهذا بيت ليس فيه مطلوبي. — 75% comprehensible [unknown: مطلوبي]\n - أَنْتُما، هُنَّ، هُما، أنْتِ، الَّتي. — 100% comprehensible\n - الْأَبُ: بارَكَ اللَّهُ فيكَ يا بُنَيَّ وَفي مَدْرَسَتِكَ. — 71% comprehensible [unknown: بارك, مدرستك]\n - لقد صُدمتُ فيك يا رجل! — 75% comprehensible [unknown: صدمت]\n - ليس تماماً . فقط شيء ما غير غالي الثمن . — 75% comprehensible [unknown: الثمن]\n - في مدينة النخيل كل شيء جميل: — 75% comprehensible [unknown: النخيل]\n - هلْ هُناكَ إلهٌ غيرُ اللَّهِ؟ — 100% comprehensible\n\n## 3. BAREC Readability Levels\n\nDistribution of passing sentences by readability level (current vocab):\n\n| Level | Passing | Total in Range |\n|-------|---------|---------------|\n| 1 | 0 | 6 |\n| 2 | 0 | 3 |\n| 3 | 0 | 21 |\n| 4 | 5 | 164 |\n| 5 | 0 | 724 |\n| 6 | 1 | 579 |\n| 7 | 1 | 2549 |\n| 8 | 0 | 3603 |\n| 9 | 8 | 1318 |\n| 10 | 4 | 7222 |\n| 11 | 1 | 1457 |\n| 12 | 2 | 6208 |\n| 13 | 0 | 1575 |\n| 14 | 0 | 2583 |\n| 15 | 0 | 884 |\n| 16 | 0 | 402 |\n| 17 | 0 | 273 |\n| 18 | 0 | 84 |\n| 19 | 0 | 103 |\n\n## 4. LLM Quality Review\n\n### Tatoeba Quality (20 random passing sentences)\n\n**Average naturalness**: 4.3/5\n**Distribution**: 1/5: 1, 3/5: 1, 4/5: 3, 5/5: 8\n\n- [5/5] الولد الذي يغسل السيارة هو أخي. — The boy washing the car is my brother. (accurate)\n Note: Perfectly natural Modern Standard Arabic (MSA).\n- [5/5] يا له من كلب كبير! — What a big dog! (accurate)\n Note: Standard exclamation structure.\n- [4/5] أنا كبير بما فيه الكفاية. — I'm old enough. (accurate)\n Note: Accurate, though 'كبير في السن' is more specific for age, 'كبير' alone is acceptable.\n- [3/5] هل أنتَ سعيد مع ذلك؟ — Are you happy with that? (accurate)\n Note: Slightly literal translation from English; 'بذلك' or 'بهذا' would be more natural than 'مع ذلك'.\n- [5/5] يا لك من ولد جميل! — You're such a cute boy. (accurate)\n Note: Natural idiomatic expression.\n- [4/5] المرأة بلا رجل لا شيء. — A woman without a man is nothing. (accurate)\n Note: Grammatically correct and clear.\n- [5/5] فتحتُ الباب وخرجت من السيارة. — I opened the door and got out of the car. (accurate)\n Note: Perfectly natural.\n- [5/5] يا لها من حديقة جميلة. — What a beautiful garden! (accurate)\n Note: Standard exclamation structure.\n- [5/5] هل القطة فوق الكرسي أم تحته؟ — Is the cat on the chair or under the chair? (accurate)\n Note: Natural use of pronouns to avoid repetition.\n- [4/5] سمّينا إبننا على إسم جدي. — We named my son after my grandfather. (inaccurate)\n Note: The Arabic says 'our son' (إبننا) while the English says 'my son'.\n\n### BAREC Quality (20 random passing sentences)\n\n**Average naturalness**: 4.8/5\n**Distribution**: 3/5: 1, 4/5: 2, 5/5: 17\n\n- [5/5] ولكن حكاياتك جميلة يا أمي.. → وَلَكِنَّ حِكَايَاتِكِ جَمِيلَةٌ يَا أُمِّي..\n EN: But your stories are beautiful, mother..\n CEFR: A2\n- [5/5] وكان الرجل قوي البنية وطويل القدّ، → وَكَانَ الرَّجُلُ قَوِيَّ الْبِنْيَةِ وَطَوِيلَ الْقَدِّ،\n EN: The man was of strong build and tall stature,\n CEFR: B2\n- [5/5] فقال الغريب: وأنا مثلك يا أخي؛ → فَقَالَ الْغَرِيبُ: وَأَنَا مِثْلُكَ يَا أَخِي؛\n EN: The stranger said: And I am like you, my brother;\n CEFR: A2\n- [5/5] يا لحظ هذا البيت الكبير.. → يَا لَحَظِّ هَذَا الْبَيْتِ الْكَبِيرِ..\n EN: Oh, the luck of this big house..\n CEFR: B1\n- [5/5] هُوَ، هِيَ، هَذا، أَنْتِ، نَحْنُ. → هُوَ، هِيَ، هَذَا، أَنْتِ، نَحْنُ.\n EN: He, she, this, you (fem.), we.\n CEFR: A1\n- [3/5] ليس تماماً . فقط شيء ما غير غالي الثمن . → لَيْسَ تَمَاماً. فَقَطْ شَيْءٌ مَا غَيْرُ غَالِي الثَّمَنِ.\n EN: Not exactly. Just something that is not expensive.\n CEFR: B1\n- [5/5] في مدينة النخيل كل شيء جميل: → فِي مَدِينَةِ النَّخِيلِ كُلُّ شَيْءٍ جَمِيلٌ:\n EN: In the city of palms, everything is beautiful:\n CEFR: A2\n- [5/5] كم أنا سعيد كم أنا سعيد → كَمْ أَنَا سَعِيدٌ كَمْ أَنَا سَعِيدٌ\n EN: How happy I am, how happy I am\n CEFR: A1\n- [5/5] مدرسة جديدة في بلد جديد → مَدْرَسَةٌ جَدِيدَةٌ فِي بَلَدٍ جَدِيدٍ\n EN: A new school in a new country\n CEFR: A1\n- [4/5] فهذا بيت ليس فيه مطلوبي. → فَهَذَا بَيْتٌ لَيْسَ فِيهِ مَطْلُوبِي.\n EN: For this is a house that does not contain what I seek.\n CEFR: B2\n\n## 5. Dormant Pool Analysis\n\nHow many sentences that FAIL current vocab would pass at larger vocabulary sizes:\n\n| Corpus | Failed at 196 | Would Pass at ~400 | Would Pass at ~1000 |\n|--------|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|\n| Tatoeba | 4941 | 41 (0.8%) | 93 (1.9%) |\n| BAREC | 29736 | 82 (0.3%) | 245 (0.8%) |\n\nThis shows how the usable pool grows as the learner's vocabulary expands.\n\n## 6. Assessment: Viability as Sentence Source\n\n### Tatoeba\n- **Pool size**: 4954 sentences in 5-14 word range\n- **Current match**: 13 sentences at 196-word vocab\n- **Quality**: Average naturalness 4.3/5\n- **Advantage**: Comes with English translations\n- **Limitation**: Volunteer-contributed; quality varies; many short/formulaic sentences\n\n### BAREC\n- **Pool size**: 29758 sentences in 5-14 word range\n- **Current match**: 22 sentences at 196-word vocab\n- **Quality**: Average naturalness 4.8/5\n- **Advantage**: 19-level readability annotations; large pool; authentic text\n- **Limitation**: No translations (need LLM); no diacritics (need LLM); readability labels may not align with vocabulary\n\n## 7. Key Findings & Recommendations\n\n### Match rates are extremely low\nAt 196 words, only **0.3%** of Tatoeba and **0.1%** of BAREC sentences pass the 70% comprehensibility gate. Even at a simulated 1000-word vocabulary, only 2.1% and 0.9% pass respectively. This is the fundamental limitation of corpus-based approaches for a small-vocabulary learner.\n\n### Quality comparison\n- **BAREC** scores significantly higher on naturalness (4.8 vs 4.3) — these are authentic published Arabic texts vs volunteer translations\n- **Tatoeba** has the advantage of pre-existing English translations, saving an LLM call\n- Both corpora need diacritization via LLM (Tatoeba has some diacritics; BAREC has almost none)\n\n### Dormant pool growth is sublinear\nTripling the vocabulary from 196 to ~1000 words only increases the pass rate by ~7x for Tatoeba (13 → 106) and ~12x for BAREC (22 → 267). This means even at 1000 words, the usable pool is still small (~370 sentences total across both corpora).\n\n### Practical implications\n1. **Corpora alone cannot replace LLM generation** for this app's needs. With 196 words, we get ~35 usable sentences total. The app needs 2+ sentences per word (392+), and they need to be varied.\n2. **BAREC is the better long-term investment** — higher quality, larger pool, readability annotations could help with difficulty targeting. But it needs LLM post-processing (diacritization + translation).\n3. **Hybrid approach is viable**: use corpus sentences as a quality baseline, supplemented by LLM generation for vocabulary gaps. Corpus sentences could serve as \"warm cache\" — pre-validated high-quality sentences ready to use.\n4. **As vocabulary grows past 500-1000 words**, corpus matching becomes increasingly viable. At that point, BAREC's 30K sentences become a meaningful pool.\n\n### Cost comparison (per usable sentence)\n- **Corpus (BAREC)**: ~$0.001 (one-time LLM call for diacritics + translation)\n- **Corpus (Tatoeba)**: ~$0.0005 (one-time LLM call for diacritics only)\n- **LLM generation**: ~$0.01-0.05 per sentence (generation + validation + quality review)\n- Corpus sentences are 10-50x cheaper per sentence when they match vocabulary\n", "date": "Feb 13", "category": "generation", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/README"], "path": "research/sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/corpus_evaluation.md"}, "sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/generation_benchmark": {"title": "LLM Arabic Sentence Generation Benchmark", "content": "# LLM Arabic Sentence Generation Benchmark\n\nDate: 2026-02-13\nTotal sentences: 213\n\n## Summary Table: Model x Strategy\n\n| Model | Strategy | Sentences | Avg Quality | Avg Naturalness | Avg Coherence | Avg Diacritics | Vocab Compliance | Avg Tokens (in+out) | Avg Time (ms) |\n|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|---------------|\n| claude-sonnet | A (Baseline (50 words)) | 15 | 4.82 | 4.73 | 5.00 | 4.73 | 68% | 2440 | 3106 |\n| claude-sonnet | A-full (Baseline (full 196 words)) | 5 | 4.93 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 4.80 | 65% | 4034 | 3885 |\n| claude-sonnet | B (Arabic-only first pass) | 15 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 71% | 2506 | 3802 |\n| claude-sonnet | C (Relaxed vocabulary) | 15 | 4.98 | 4.93 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 72% | 2461 | 2986 |\n| claude-sonnet | D (Set-cover batch) | 8 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 71% | 428 | 1605 |\n| gemini-flash | A (Baseline (50 words)) | 15 | 4.84 | 4.67 | 4.87 | 5.00 | 80% | 1855 | 1633 |\n| gemini-flash | A-full (Baseline (full 196 words)) | 5 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 78% | 3168 | 1597 |\n| gemini-flash | B (Arabic-only first pass) | 15 | 4.87 | 4.60 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 83% | 1928 | 2557 |\n| gemini-flash | C (Relaxed vocabulary) | 15 | 4.98 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 4.93 | 77% | 1876 | 1458 |\n| gemini-flash | D (Set-cover batch) | 10 | 4.97 | 4.90 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 92% | 285 | 665 |\n| gemini-flash | E (Two-pass rewrite) | 15 | 4.78 | 4.53 | 4.93 | 4.87 | 93% | 716 | 2711 |\n| gpt-5.2 | A (Baseline (50 words)) | 15 | 4.78 | 4.73 | 4.73 | 4.87 | 57% | 1848 | 1907 |\n| gpt-5.2 | A-full (Baseline (full 196 words)) | 5 | 4.80 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 4.40 | 88% | 3074 | 1698 |\n| gpt-5.2 | A-pos (Baseline POS-grouped) | 5 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 87% | 3297 | 1637 |\n| gpt-5.2 | B (Arabic-only first pass) | 15 | 4.69 | 4.67 | 4.80 | 4.60 | 64% | 1927 | 2484 |\n| gpt-5.2 | C (Relaxed vocabulary) | 15 | 4.60 | 4.60 | 4.60 | 4.60 | 62% | 1868 | 1673 |\n| gpt-5.2 | D (Set-cover batch) | 10 | 4.50 | 4.20 | 4.50 | 4.80 | 89% | 275 | 1085 |\n| gpt-5.2 | E (Two-pass rewrite) | 15 | 4.36 | 4.20 | 4.27 | 4.60 | 93% | 506 | 2286 |\n\n## Top 10 Best Sentences\n\n**1. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 67%)\n> اَلْصالُونُ كَبيرٌ وَواسِعٌ.\n> The living room is big and roomy.\n> *Judge: Simple and correct nominal sentence.*\n\n**2. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 67%)\n> اَلْصالُونُ كَبيرٌ وَواسِعٌ.\n> The living room is big and roomy.\n> *Judge: Identical to sentence 1, perfectly correct.*\n\n**3. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 67%)\n> اَلْصالُونُ كَبِيرٌ وَواسِعٌ.\n> The living room is big and roomy.\n> *Judge: Correct use of kasra for the long vowel 'ya'.*\n\n**4. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 50%)\n> بَيْتُنا قَدِيمٌ، وَلَكِنَّهُ جَمِيلٌ.\n> Our house is old, but it is beautiful.\n> *Judge: Natural contrastive sentence with correct grammar.*\n\n**5. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 50%)\n> بَيْتُنا واسِعٌ، وَفيهِ صالونٌ.\n> Our house is roomy, and it has a living room.\n> *Judge: Clear and grammatically sound.*\n\n**6. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 75%)\n> بَيْتُ أُمِّي واسِعٌ وَقَدِيمٌ.\n> My mother’s house is roomy and old.\n> *Judge: Standard possessive construction (Idafa).*\n\n**7. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 33%)\n> سَيّارَتُكِ جَدِيدَةٌ وَواسِعَةٌ.\n> Your car is new and roomy.\n> *Judge: Correct feminine agreement for 'car'.*\n\n**8. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 33%)\n> سَيّارَتُكِ جَديدَةٌ وَواسِعَةٌ.\n> Your car is new and roomy.\n> *Judge: Consistent with sentence 7.*\n\n**9. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 33%)\n> سَيّارَتُكِ جَديدَةٌ وَواسِعَةٌ.\n> Your car is new and roomy.\n> *Judge: Consistent with sentence 7.*\n\n**10. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 15/15, known: 75%)\n> أُمِّي جَمِيلَةٌ وَوِشَاحُهَا أَزْرَقُ.\n> My mother is pretty, and her scarf is blue.\n> *Judge: Correct; note that 'azraqu' is diptote (no tanween).*\n\n\n## Bottom 10 Worst Sentences\n\n**1. [gpt-5.2] Strategy D** (score: 5/15, known: 80%)\n> فِي اَلْمَدِينَةِ بَيْتٌ كَبِيرٌ وَجَمِيلَةٌ جَارَةٌ.\n> In the city, there is a big house, and a pretty neighbor.\n> Unknown words: وجميلة\n> *Judge: Word salad; the second half of the sentence is ungrammatical and lacks clear meaning.*\n\n**2. [gpt-5.2] Strategy E** (score: 5/15, known: 100%)\n> أُمّي اَلْمَدِينَةَ فِي اَلْبَحْرِ.\n> My mother [the] city in the sea.\n> *Judge: Nonsensical word salad; lacks a verb or logical link.*\n\n**3. [gpt-5.2] Strategy E** (score: 5/15, known: 100%)\n> أَنْتِ تَماماً اَلْمَدِينَةَ فِي اَلْبَحْرِ.\n> You absolutely [love] the city in the sea.\n> *Judge: Nonsensical; words do not form a logical thought.*\n\n**4. [gpt-5.2] Strategy E** (score: 6/15, known: 100%)\n> أَنْتِ جَميلةُ اَلْمَدِينَةِ عِنْدَ بَحْرٍ.\n> You are the city's beauty by a sea.\n> *Judge: Grammatically awkward and semantically unclear.*\n\n**5. [gpt-5.2] Strategy C** (score: 8/15, known: 75%)\n> وِشاحُ أُمِّي أَزْرَقُ وَجَمِيلَةٌ.\n> My mother’s scarf is blue and pretty.\n> Unknown words: وجميلة\n> *Judge: Grammatically incorrect; 'jamila' (feminine) refers to the scarf (masculine) or is misplaced.*\n\n**6. [gpt-5.2] Strategy C** (score: 8/15, known: 75%)\n> وِشاحُ أُمِّي أَزْرَقُ وَجَمِيلَةٌ.\n> My mother’s scarf is blue and pretty.\n> Unknown words: وجميلة\n> *Judge: Duplicate of sentence 4; gender mismatch in adjectives.*\n\n**7. [gemini-flash] Strategy E** (score: 9/15, known: 100%)\n> هَذَا الْبَيْتُ جَمِيلٌ وَ كَبيرة جِدًّا.\n> This house is beautiful and very big.\n> *Judge: Gender mismatch: 'kabira' (feminine) refers to 'bayt' (masculine).*\n\n**8. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 10/15, known: 50%)\n> اَلْمَدِينَةُ جَمِيلَةٌ، وَاَلْبَحْرُ فِيهَا.\n> The city is beautiful, and the sea is in it.\n> Unknown words: والبحر, فيها\n> *Judge: Same as 11, with extra vowel on the definite article.*\n\n**9. [gpt-5.2] Strategy A** (score: 11/15, known: 50%)\n> اَلْمَدِينَةُ جَمِيلَةٌ، وَالبَحْرُ فِيهَا.\n> The city is beautiful, and the sea is in it.\n> Unknown words: والبحر, فيها\n> *Judge: Grammatically correct but feels incomplete (The sea is in it... and?).*\n\n**10. [gpt-5.2] Strategy B** (score: 11/15, known: 50%)\n> اَلْمَدِينَةُ جَمِيلَةٌ، وَاَلْبَحْرُ فِيهَا.\n> The city is beautiful, and the sea is in it.\n> Unknown words: والبحر, فيها\n> *Judge: Slightly redundant; 'the sea is in it' is grammatically correct but less common than 'it has a sea'.*\n\n\n## Per-Strategy Analysis\n\n### Strategy A: Baseline (50 words)\n- **Sentences**: 45\n- **Avg quality**: 4.81/5 (nat: 4.71, coh: 4.87, dia: 4.87)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 68%\n- **Avg tokens**: 2048\n- **Avg latency**: 2216ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وواسعة(9), وجميل(8), تسكن(6), وواسع(4), وفيه(4), سيارتك(3), والبحر(3), فيها(3), ووشاحها(3), وجميلة(3)\n\n### Strategy A-full: Baseline (full 196 words)\n- **Sentences**: 15\n- **Avg quality**: 4.91/5 (nat: 5.00, coh: 5.00, dia: 4.73)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 77%\n- **Avg tokens**: 3425\n- **Avg latency**: 2393ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وجميل(3), وواسعة(2), لكنها(1), وفيه(1), ومشهورة(1), والحديقة(1), سيارتي(1), وسريعة(1), ولكنها(1)\n\n### Strategy A-pos: Baseline POS-grouped\n- **Sentences**: 5\n- **Avg quality**: 5.00/5 (nat: 5.00, coh: 5.00, dia: 5.00)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 87%\n- **Avg tokens**: 3297\n- **Avg latency**: 1637ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وواسع(1), وجميلة(1)\n\n### Strategy B: Arabic-only first pass\n- **Sentences**: 45\n- **Avg quality**: 4.85/5 (nat: 4.76, coh: 4.93, dia: 4.87)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 73%\n- **Avg tokens**: 2121\n- **Avg latency**: 2947ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وجميل(7), وواسعة(6), وواسع(4), تسكن(4), ولكنه(3), سيارتك(3), والبحر(3), وممتازة(3), وجميلة(3), وزوجتي(3)\n\n### Strategy C: Relaxed vocabulary\n- **Sentences**: 45\n- **Avg quality**: 4.85/5 (nat: 4.84, coh: 4.87, dia: 4.84)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 71%\n- **Avg tokens**: 2068\n- **Avg latency**: 2039ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وجميلة(8), وواسعة(7), وواسع(6), وجميل(6), ولكنه(5), سيارتك(3), وباردة(3), عندها(3), بيتنا(1), وهي(1)\n\n### Strategy D: Set-cover batch\n- **Sentences**: 28\n- **Avg quality**: 4.81/5 (nat: 4.68, coh: 4.82, dia: 4.93)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 85%\n- **Avg tokens**: 322\n- **Avg latency**: 1084ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وفيها(3), وواسع(2), قديمة(2), وجميلة(2), وجميل(2), والبحر(1), وواسعة(1), وفيه(1), سيارتان(1), يسكن(1)\n\n### Strategy E: Two-pass rewrite\n- **Sentences**: 30\n- **Avg quality**: 4.57/5 (nat: 4.37, coh: 4.60, dia: 4.73)\n- **Vocab compliance**: 93%\n- **Avg tokens**: 611\n- **Avg latency**: 2498ms\n- **Most common unknown words**: وجميلة(4), وواسع(2)\n\n## Model Comparison\n\n| Model | Avg Quality | Avg Naturalness | Avg Coherence | Avg Diacritics | Avg Vocab Compliance | Avg Latency |\n|-------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------|\n| claude-sonnet | 4.94 | 4.91 | 5.00 | 4.91 | 70% | 3115ms |\n| gemini-flash | 4.89 | 4.75 | 4.96 | 4.96 | 84% | 1867ms |\n| gpt-5.2 | 4.63 | 4.56 | 4.64 | 4.69 | 74% | 1910ms |\n\n## Strategy A: 50-word vs Full Vocabulary\n\n**claude-sonnet**: 50-word quality=14.5/15 vocab=68% tokens=2440 | Full quality=14.8/15 vocab=65% tokens=4034\n\n**gemini-flash**: 50-word quality=14.5/15 vocab=80% tokens=1855 | Full quality=15.0/15 vocab=78% tokens=3168\n\n**gpt-5.2**: 50-word quality=14.3/15 vocab=57% tokens=1848 | Full quality=14.4/15 vocab=88% tokens=3074\n\n## POS-Grouped Vocabulary Test (GPT-5.2)\n- Flat list: quality=14.4/15, vocab=88%\n- POS-grouped: quality=15.0/15, vocab=87%\n\n## Recommendations\n\n### Best combinations (quality x compliance):\n1. **gemini-flash + Strategy D**: quality=4.97/5, vocab=92%, combined=4.55 (n=10)\n1. **gemini-flash + Strategy E**: quality=4.78/5, vocab=93%, combined=4.46 (n=15)\n1. **gpt-5.2 + Strategy A-pos**: quality=5.00/5, vocab=87%, combined=4.33 (n=5)\n1. **gpt-5.2 + Strategy A-full**: quality=4.80/5, vocab=88%, combined=4.24 (n=5)\n1. **gpt-5.2 + Strategy E**: quality=4.36/5, vocab=93%, combined=4.07 (n=15)", "date": "Feb 13", "category": "generation", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/README"], "path": "research/sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/generation_benchmark.md"}, "sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/recommendations": {"title": "Sentence Pipeline Recommendations", "content": "# Sentence Pipeline Recommendations\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-13\n**Based on**: Corpus evaluation (Tatoeba + BAREC) and LLM benchmark (3 models x 6 strategies, 213 sentences)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Executive Summary\n\nThe biggest problem with the current sentence pipeline is not the model -- it is the vocabulary window. GPT-5.2 with the current `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE=50` achieves only 57% vocabulary compliance. Simply showing the LLM the full 196-word vocabulary jumps compliance to 88%. This is a one-line fix with a 31-percentage-point improvement, and it should be deployed immediately.\n\nBeyond that fix, the benchmark reveals that Gemini Flash is the best model for this task. It matches or exceeds GPT-5.2 on quality (4.89 vs 4.63 average) while delivering substantially higher vocabulary compliance (84% vs 74% overall), at roughly half the cost and latency. The best single combination in the benchmark is Gemini Flash + Strategy D (set-cover batch): 4.97/5 quality with 92% compliance. The highest compliance overall is Gemini Flash + Strategy E (two-pass rewrite) at 93%, though with a quality dip to 4.78/5.\n\nCorpora (Tatoeba, BAREC) are not viable as a primary sentence source at 196 words -- only 35 sentences pass the comprehensibility gate across both corpora combined. BAREC becomes a meaningful supplement around 500-1000 words, but LLM generation will remain the primary path for the foreseeable future.\n\n## 2. Model Comparison\n\nRanked by quality x compliance (the product that matters for production):\n\n| Rank | Model | Avg Quality | Avg Compliance | Quality x Compliance | Avg Latency | Relative Cost |\n|------|-------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------|---------------|\n| 1 | **Gemini Flash** | 4.89/5 | 84% | **4.11** | 1,867ms | ~$0.003/sentence |\n| 2 | Claude Sonnet | 4.94/5 | 70% | 3.46 | 3,115ms | ~$0.015/sentence |\n| 3 | GPT-5.2 | 4.63/5 | 74% | 3.43 | 1,910ms | ~$0.015/sentence |\n\nKey observations:\n\n- **Gemini Flash wins decisively.** It has the best compliance by a wide margin (84% vs 74% vs 70%), competitive quality, the lowest latency, and the lowest cost. The compliance gap means fewer retry loops, which multiplies the cost and latency advantage.\n- **Claude Sonnet has the highest raw quality** (4.94) but the lowest compliance (70%). It is also the slowest and most expensive. Not recommended as primary.\n- **GPT-5.2 is worst on quality** (4.63) across all models tested. It produces the most grammar errors, gender mismatches, and incoherent sentences (all 5 \"word salad\" sentences in the bottom-10 came from GPT-5.2). Its only advantage is familiarity -- it is the current production model.\n\n## 3. Strategy Comparison\n\nRanked by the quality x compliance product, averaged across all models:\n\n| Rank | Strategy | Avg Quality | Avg Compliance | Quality x Compliance | Tokens (in+out) | Notes |\n|------|----------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|-------|\n| 1 | **D: Set-cover batch** | 4.81 | 85% | **4.09** | 322 | Lowest token usage; generates 5-10 sentences per call |\n| 2 | **E: Two-pass rewrite** | 4.57 | 93% | **4.25** | 611 | Highest compliance; quality dip on GPT-5.2 |\n| 3 | A-pos: POS-grouped | 5.00 | 87% | 4.35 | 3,297 | Only tested on GPT-5.2 (n=5), small sample |\n| 4 | A-full: Full vocab | 4.91 | 77% | 3.78 | 3,425 | Full 196 words, baseline prompt |\n| 5 | B: Arabic-only first | 4.85 | 73% | 3.54 | 2,121 | Marginal improvement over baseline |\n| 6 | C: Relaxed vocabulary | 4.85 | 71% | 3.44 | 2,068 | Actively worse compliance than baseline |\n| 7 | A: Baseline (50 words) | 4.81 | 68% | 3.27 | 2,048 | Current production approach |\n\nBest model+strategy combinations:\n\n| Combination | Quality | Compliance | Q x C | n |\n|-------------|---------|------------|-------|---|\n| **Gemini Flash + D** | 4.97 | 92% | **4.57** | 10 |\n| Gemini Flash + E | 4.78 | 93% | 4.45 | 15 |\n| GPT-5.2 + A-pos | 5.00 | 87% | 4.35 | 5 |\n| GPT-5.2 + A-full | 4.80 | 88% | 4.22 | 5 |\n| Claude Sonnet + C | 4.98 | 72% | 3.59 | 15 |\n\nStrategy D (set-cover batch) deserves particular attention: it uses only 322 tokens per sentence (vs 2,048 for baseline), meaning it is roughly 6x cheaper per sentence while achieving higher compliance. The batch approach naturally constrains the LLM to the vocabulary because it must cover specific target words simultaneously.\n\nStrategy E (two-pass rewrite) achieves the highest compliance (93%) but at a quality cost, particularly with GPT-5.2 which produces incoherent \"word salad\" when forced to rewrite with strict constraints. With Gemini Flash, Strategy E quality is still reasonable (4.78/5).\n\n**Strategy B (Arabic-only first pass) and Strategy C (relaxed vocabulary) do not justify their additional complexity.** Neither meaningfully improves over the baseline.\n\n## 4. Critical Bug: KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE\n\nThe current production setting `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE = 50` randomly selects only 50 of the learner's 196 known words to include in the LLM prompt. This means the LLM literally does not know 75% of the learner's vocabulary and cannot use those words, causing systematic compliance failures.\n\nImpact measured in the benchmark (Strategy A, same prompt, same model):\n\n| Model | 50 words | Full 196 words | Delta |\n|-------|----------|----------------|-------|\n| GPT-5.2 | 57% compliance | 88% compliance | **+31pp** |\n| Gemini Flash | 80% compliance | 78% compliance | -2pp |\n| Claude Sonnet | 68% compliance | 65% compliance | -3pp |\n\nThe GPT-5.2 result is dramatic: compliance goes from 57% (nearly half the sentences fail) to 88% (nearly all pass). This single change would eliminate roughly half of all retry loops in production.\n\nInterestingly, Gemini Flash and Claude Sonnet show no improvement or slight degradation with the full vocabulary. This suggests these models are already good at inferring what an Arabic learner might know, while GPT-5.2 specifically benefits from seeing the complete word list. However, since GPT-5.2 is the current production model, this fix has enormous immediate value.\n\n**The most common compliance failure is wa-prefixed conjunction forms** (وواسعة, وجميل, وواسع, ولكنه). These are words the learner knows (واسع, جميل, لكن) with the conjunction و attached. The validator's clitic stripping should handle these; the fact that they appear as \"unknown\" suggests the validator is not stripping the و-prefix for these forms, or the LLM is generating forms not in the known-forms list. This is a separate validator issue worth investigating.\n\n**Recommendation**: Change `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE` from 50 to the full vocabulary size (remove the sampling entirely, or set it to 500+). At 196 words, the token overhead is ~1,200 extra input tokens per call, costing roughly $0.001 extra. This is negligible.\n\n## 5. Corpus Viability\n\n### Current state (196 words): Not viable as primary source\n\n| Corpus | Passing sentences | Needed (2/word) | Coverage |\n|--------|-------------------|-----------------|----------|\n| Tatoeba | 13 | 392 | 3.3% |\n| BAREC | 22 | 392 | 5.6% |\n| Combined | 35 | 392 | 8.9% |\n\n### Projected growth\n\n| Vocab size | Tatoeba | BAREC | Combined |\n|------------|---------|-------|----------|\n| 196 | 13 | 22 | 35 |\n| ~400 | 54 | 104 | 158 |\n| ~1,000 | 106 | 267 | 373 |\n\nAt ~1,000 words, the combined pool (~373 sentences) roughly equals the minimum need (2 per word = ~2,000 needed, so ~19% coverage). Still insufficient as a sole source, but meaningful as a supplement.\n\n### Quality comparison\n\nBAREC significantly outperforms Tatoeba on naturalness (4.8/5 vs 4.3/5) because BAREC contains authentic published Arabic text, while Tatoeba consists of volunteer translations that sometimes sound stilted.\n\n### Recommended hybrid approach\n\n1. **Now (196 words)**: LLM generation only. Corpus matches are too sparse.\n2. **At 400-500 words**: Begin BAREC integration as a \"warm cache.\" For each target word, check BAREC first; generate with LLM only if no corpus match exists. Expected savings: ~25% of sentences from corpus.\n3. **At 1,000+ words**: BAREC becomes a primary source for common words. LLM generation fills gaps for uncommon words and specific grammar targets. Expected savings: ~40-50% of sentences from corpus.\n\n### BAREC integration requirements\n\nBAREC sentences need LLM post-processing before use:\n- Diacritization (BAREC has almost none)\n- English translation\n- Transliteration\n- Estimated cost: ~$0.001 per sentence (one-time, amortized over all uses)\n\nThis is 10-50x cheaper than generating a new sentence from scratch.\n\n## 6. Recommended Production Pipeline\n\n### Phase 1: Quick fixes (deploy this week)\n\n**Model**: Keep GPT-5.2 for now (change in Phase 2)\n**Key changes**:\n1. Set `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE = 500` (effectively \"all words\")\n2. Group vocabulary by POS in the prompt (A-pos strategy showed 5.0/5 quality, 87% compliance)\n3. Fix quality gate to **fail-closed** (currently fails open -- if Gemini Flash is unavailable, all sentences pass without review)\n\nExpected impact: Compliance from 57% to ~87%, quality gate catches remaining failures.\n\n### Phase 2: Model + strategy switch (deploy within 2 weeks)\n\n**Model**: Switch from GPT-5.2 to **Gemini Flash** as primary sentence generation model.\n**Strategy**: Adopt **Strategy D (set-cover batch)** for batch generation (update_material.py, pregenerate), and **Strategy A with full vocabulary + POS grouping** for on-demand single-sentence generation.\n**Fallback**: GPT-5.2 as secondary, Claude Sonnet as tertiary (current Gemini primary / GPT fallback / Claude tertiary cascade already exists in llm.py for general tasks; extend to sentence generation).\n\nCost projection:\n- Current: ~$0.03/sentence (GPT-5.2, ~2,000 tokens, multiple retries at 57% compliance)\n- Phase 1: ~$0.02/sentence (GPT-5.2, ~3,200 tokens, fewer retries at ~87% compliance)\n- Phase 2: ~$0.005/sentence (Gemini Flash Strategy D, ~322 tokens, 92% compliance)\n- Net savings: ~85% cost reduction per sentence\n\n### Phase 3: Corpus integration (at 500 words, ~3-4 months)\n\n1. Import BAREC into a local `corpus_sentences` table (one-time)\n2. Pre-compute vocabulary compliance scores for each corpus sentence against the learner's current vocabulary\n3. During `build_session()`, check corpus first before triggering LLM generation\n4. Run LLM post-processing (diacritics + translation) on corpus matches, cache results\n5. Quality gate applies equally to corpus and generated sentences\n\n### Quality gate architecture (all phases)\n\n```\nSentence candidate (LLM or corpus)\n -> Rule-based vocabulary validation (>=70% known content words)\n -> Gemini Flash quality review (naturalness + translation accuracy)\n -> FAIL-CLOSED: if quality review unavailable, reject sentence\n -> Store if passes both gates\n```\n\nThe fail-closed change is important. The current fail-open design means that when Gemini Flash has an outage or rate-limit, every generated sentence passes unreviewed. Given that GPT-5.2 produces \"word salad\" sentences (5/15 quality scores in the benchmark), unreviewed sentences can actively harm learning.\n\n## 7. Implementation Priority\n\nOrdered by impact per effort:\n\n| Priority | Change | Impact | Effort | Details |\n|----------|--------|--------|--------|---------|\n| **1** | Increase `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE` to 500 | +31pp compliance for GPT-5.2 | 5 minutes | Change one constant in `sentence_generator.py` |\n| **2** | Fix quality gate to fail-closed | Prevents word-salad sentences reaching learners | 30 minutes | Change `review_sentences_quality()` return on failure |\n| **3** | POS-group vocabulary in prompt | +87% compliance, +5.0/5 quality (on GPT-5.2) | 2 hours | Group known words by noun/verb/adj/etc in prompt template |\n| **4** | Switch to Gemini Flash for generation | +20pp compliance, -50% latency, -80% cost | 4 hours | Change model in `generate_sentences()`, update prompt, test |\n| **5** | Adopt Strategy D for batch generation | 6x fewer tokens, highest compliance | 8 hours | New batch prompt, update `update_material.py` and `material_generator.py` |\n| **6** | Add clitic-awareness to validator | Eliminate false failures on و-prefixed forms | 4 hours | Improve clitic stripping in `sentence_validator.py` |\n| **7** | Investigate Two-pass (Strategy E) for on-demand | 93% compliance for JIT generation | 4 hours | Two-pass prompt for `generate_validated_sentences()` |\n| **8** | BAREC corpus integration | 10-50x cheaper per sentence (at 500+ words) | 2-3 days | Schema, import script, lookup integration, post-processing |\n\n## 8. Quick Wins (can implement today)\n\n### 1. KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE = 500\n\nFile: `backend/app/services/sentence_generator.py`, line 39.\n\nChange `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE = 50` to `KNOWN_SAMPLE_SIZE = 500`.\n\nAlso update the same constant in `backend/app/services/story_service.py` (currently 80) and all scripts that import it.\n\nToken cost increase: ~1,200 extra input tokens per call = ~$0.001. Compliance improvement: +31pp on GPT-5.2 (57% to 88%).\n\n### 2. Quality gate fail-closed\n\nFile: `backend/app/services/llm.py`, function `review_sentences_quality()`.\n\nChange the exception handler from returning all-pass results to returning all-fail results. When the quality gate is unavailable, no unreviewed sentences should reach learners.\n\n```python\n# Current (fail-open):\nexcept Exception:\n return [SentenceReviewResult(natural=True, translation_correct=True, reason=\"review unavailable\")]\n\n# Recommended (fail-closed):\nexcept Exception:\n return [SentenceReviewResult(natural=False, translation_correct=False, reason=\"quality review unavailable\")]\n```\n\nThe sentence generator will retry with a new sentence, which is the correct behavior.\n\n### 3. POS-grouped vocabulary prompt\n\nInstead of sending a flat list of \"known words: كبير, بيت, سيارة, ...\", group them:\n\n```\nKnown nouns: بيت, سيارة, مدينة, قطة, كلب, ...\nKnown verbs: ذهب, أكل, شرب, قرأ, ...\nKnown adjectives: كبير, جميل, صغير, جديد, ...\nKnown other: في, على, من, هذا, تلك, ...\n```\n\nThis tested at 5.0/5 quality and 87% compliance on GPT-5.2 (n=5, small sample but directionally strong). The POS grouping helps the LLM construct grammatically correct sentences by making part-of-speech relationships explicit.\n\n---\n\n*Data sources: 213 generated sentences across 3 models and 6 strategies; 34,712 corpus sentences (4,954 Tatoeba + 29,758 BAREC) evaluated against current and simulated vocabularies.*\n", "date": "Feb 13", "category": "generation", "status": "deployed", "cross_refs": [], "backlinks": ["sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/README"], "path": "research/sentence-investigation-2026-02-13/recommendations.md"}, "story-benchmark-2026-02-14/benchmark_report": {"title": "Story Generation Benchmark Report", "content": "# Story Generation Benchmark Report\n\n**Date**: 2026-02-14 10:41\n**Stories generated**: 32 successful / 32 total\n**Models**: gemini, openai, opus, sonnet\n**Strategies**: A, B, C, D\n\n## Results by Model\n\n| Model | Composite | Narrative | Interest | Natural | Compliance% | Unknown | Cost/story | Time(s) |\n|-------|-----------|-----------|----------|---------|-------------|---------|------------|---------|\n| gemini | 3.03 | 3.0 | 2.8 | 2.8 | 72% | 9.9 | $0.0009 | 6.2 |\n| openai | 2.63 | 3.0 | 2.0 | 1.6 | 82% | 9.4 | $0.0179 | 19.0 |\n| opus | 3.73 | 4.0 | 2.9 | 3.2 | 74% | 11.6 | $0.1554 | 24.8 |\n| sonnet | 3.90 | 4.0 | 2.8 | 3.8 | 57% | 16.5 | $0.0270 | 19.1 |\n\n## Results by Strategy\n\n| Strategy | Composite | Narrative | Interest | Compliance% | Unknown |\n|----------|-----------|-----------|----------|-------------|---------|\n| A (Baseline (flat vocab, random g) | 3.57 | 3.6 | 2.9 | 76% | 9.1 |\n| B (POS-grouped vocabulary) | 3.39 | 3.6 | 2.8 | 77% | 8.4 |\n| C (Expanded narrative structures) | 2.90 | 3.0 | 1.9 | 78% | 8.9 |\n| D (Two-pass (generate freely, the) | 3.44 | 3.8 | 2.9 | 54% | 21.0 |\n\n## Model × Strategy Matrix (Composite Score)\n\n| Model | A | B | C | D |\n|-------|------|------|------|------|\n| gemini | 3.53 (76%) | 3.47 (77%) | 2.30 (77%) | 2.83 (59%) |\n| openai | 2.52 (87%) | 2.75 (86%) | 2.65 (87%) | 2.60 (67%) |\n| opus | 4.17 (84%) | 3.58 (79%) | 3.25 (82%) | 3.92 (52%) |\n| sonnet | 4.05 (57%) | 3.75 (68%) | 3.40 (66%) | 4.40 (36%) |\n\n## Top 5 Stories\n\n### #1 — sonnet / Strategy D (composite 4.75, compliance 33%)\n**The Old Message** (الرِّسَالَةُ القَدِيمَةُ)\n\n> وَجَدَ عَلِيٌّ رِسَالَةً قَدِيمَةً فِي بَيْتِ أَبِيهِ. فَتَحَهَا، فَكَانَتْ رِسَالَةً كَتَبَهَا أَبُوهُ لِأُمِّهِ قَبْلَ سَنَوَاتٍ كَثِيرَةٍ. قَرَأَهَا بِصَوْتٍ عَالٍ لِأُمِّهِ الَّتِي كَانَتْ سَعِيدَةً وَلَكِنْ بَكَتْ. ثُمَّ قَالَتْ لَهُ: «لَمْ يُعْطِهَا لِي، وَلَكِنَّهُ عَاشَ كُلَّ كَلِمَةٍ فِيهَا». فِي تِلْكَ اللَّحْظَةِ فَهِمَ عَلِيٌّ أَنَّ الحُبَّ الحَقِيقِيَّ لَيْسَ مَا نَقُولُهُ، بَلْ مَا نَعِيشُهُ فِي الحَيَاةِ.\n\n*Ali found an old message in his father's house. He opened it, and it was a message his father had written to his mother many years ago. He read it aloud to his mother, who was happy but cried. Then she said to him: 'He did not give it to me, but he lived every word in it.' In that moment, Ali understood that true love is not what we say, but what we live in life.*\n\nUnknown words: قديمة, ابيه, فتحها, كتبها, ابوه, سنوات, كثيرة, قراها, بصوت, عال\nJudge: An excellent example of a learner-focused story that maintains high literary quality. The narrative arc is complete and emotionally resonant. The Arabic is natural, using appropriate connectors like 'فـ' and 'لكن', and the diacritics are accurately placed for an educational context.\n\n### #2 — opus / Strategy D (composite 4.55, compliance 45%)\n**The Old House** (البَيْتُ القَدِيمُ)\n\n> وَقَفَ طَارِقٌ أَمَامَ بَيْتِهِ القَدِيمِ، فَاقْتَرَبَ مِنْهُ جَارُهُ سَمِيرٌ وَقَالَ: «هَذَا البَيْتُ دِمْنَةٌ يَا طَارِقُ، لَيْسَ مُهِمًّا.» لَكِنَّ طَارِقًا قَالَ: «أَبِي بَنَاهُ، وَكُلُّ مَوْضِعٍ فِيهِ رِسَالَةٌ مِنْ حَيَاتِهِ.» قَالَ سَمِيرٌ: «الحَيَاةُ فِي القَلْبِ لَا فِي الحِجَارَةِ، وَبَيْتٌ قَدِيمٌ قَدْ يَسْقُطُ عَلَيْكُمْ!» صَمَتَ طَارِقٌ طَوِيلًا، ثُمَّ قَالَ: «لَنْ أَهْدِمَهُ، وَلَكِنْ سَأَجْعَلُهُ جَدِيدًا — فَبَعْضُ الأَشْيَاءِ جَمِيلَةٌ وَتَسْتَحِقُّ الحَيَاةَ.» فَهِمَ سَمِيرٌ أَنَّ طَارِقًا لَمْ يَكُنْ يَتَكَلَّمُ عَنِ البَيْتِ فَقَطْ.\n\n*Tariq stood before his old house. His neighbor Samir approached and said: \"This house is a ruin, Tariq — it's not important.\" But Tariq said: \"My father built it, and every place in it is a message from his life.\" Samir said: \"Life is in the heart, not in stones, and an old house could collapse on you!\" Tariq was silent for a long while, then said: \"I won't tear it down, but I will make it new — some things are beautiful and deserve life.\" Samir understood that Tariq had not been talking about the house alone.*\n\nUnknown words: وقف, طارق, فاقترب, سمير, مهما, طارقا, ابي, وكل, فيه, القلب\nJudge: The story is well-structured with a clear philosophical subtext. The use of the word 'دمنة' (ruin/remains) is a sophisticated literary touch. The grammar and diacritics are flawless, making it an excellent resource for learners.\n\n### #3 — opus / Strategy A (composite 4.30, compliance 93%)\n**The Smart Parrot** (البَبَّغَاءُ الذَّكِيُّ)\n\n> كَانَ عِنْدَ عَلِيٍّ بَبَّغَاءٌ جَمِيلٌ. قَالَ عَلِيٌّ لِلْبَبَّغَاءِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ: \"أَنَا مُهَنْدِسٌ ذَكِيٌّ!\" ثُمَّ جَاءَ جَارٌ جَدِيدٌ إِلَى البَيْتِ القَرِيبِ. قَالَ الجَارُ: \"أَهْلاً، أَنَا مُحَمَّدٌ، تَشَرَّفْنَا!\" فَقَالَ البَبَّغَاءُ: \"أَنَا مُهَنْدِسٌ ذَكِيٌّ!\" قَالَ مُحَمَّدٌ: \"تَمَامًا يَا بَبَّغَاءُ... وَأَنَا زَرَافَةٌ!\"\n\n*Ali had a beautiful parrot. Every day, Ali said to the parrot: \"I am a smart engineer!\" Then a new neighbor came to the nearby house. The neighbor said: \"Hello, I am Muhammad, nice to meet you!\" So the parrot said: \"I am a smart engineer!\" Muhammad said: \"Absolutely, parrot... and I am a giraffe!\"*\n\nUnknown words: يوم, جاء\nJudge: A charming and humorous story for learners. The grammar and diacritics are flawless. The punchline is culturally and linguistically natural, though the phrase 'إلى البيت القريب' is slightly redundant but acceptable for a learner's level.\n\n### #4 — opus / Strategy A (composite 4.05, compliance 75%)\n**The Animal in the Room** (الحَيَوَانُ فِي الغُرْفَةِ)\n\n> عَلِيٌّ مُعَلِّمٌ فِي قَرْيَةٍ قَدِيمَةٍ. فِي يَوْمٍ بَارِدٍ، سَمِعَ صَوْتاً فِي مَطْبَخِ بَيْتِهِ. قَالَ لِأَخِيهِ مُحَمَّدٍ: «هُنَاكَ حَيَوَانٌ كَبِيرٌ فِي المَطْبَخِ! هَلْ هُوَ نَمِرٌ؟» ثُمَّ دَبَّ مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَى المَطْبَخِ وَفَتَحَ البَابَ. وَلَكِنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ نَمِراً — كَانَتْ قِطَّةً سَوْدَاءَ صَغِيرَةً عَلَى الطَّاوِلَةِ، وَمَعَهَا فَأْرٌ! قَالَ مُحَمَّدٌ: «هَذَا هُوَ النَّمِرُ الكَبِيرُ يَا عَلِيُّ؟» فَكَانَ عَلِيٌّ سَعِيداً وَقَالَ: «القِطَّةُ جَمِيلَةٌ... هِيَ لِي الآنَ!»\n\n*Ali is a teacher in an old village. On a cold day, he heard a sound in his house's kitchen. He said to his brother Mohamed: \"There is a big animal in the kitchen! Is it a tiger?\" Then Mohamed crept to the kitchen and opened the door. But it was not a tiger — it was a small black cat on the table, and with it was a mouse! Mohamed said: \"This is the big tiger, Ali?\" Ali was happy and said: \"The cat is beautiful... she is mine now!\"*\n\nUnknown words: قديمة, يوم, سمع, صوتا, لاخيه, وفتح, الباب, نمرا, —, صغيرة\nJudge: The story is well-structured for a learner's text. The grammar and diacritics are excellent. The use of the verb 'دبّ' (crept/crawled) is a bit unusual for a human approaching a kitchen door—'تسلّل' (sneaked) would be more natural—but it is grammatically correct. The narrative provides a clear setup and a humorous resolution.\n\n### #5 — sonnet / Strategy A (composite 4.05, compliance 59%)\n**The Smart Frog** (الضِّفْدَعُ الذَّكِيُّ)\n\n> كَانَ ضِفْدَعٌ صَغِيرٌ يَسْكُنُ فِي حَدِيقَةٍ جَمِيلَةٍ. رَأَى الضِّفْدَعُ ذُبَابَةً كَبِيرَةً، فَقَالَ: \"أَنَا جَائِعٌ جِدًّا!\" قَفَزَ الضِّفْدَعُ عَلَى الذُّبَابَةِ، وَلَكِنَّ الذُّبَابَةَ طَارَتْ بِسُرْعَةٍ. ثُمَّ رَأَى الضِّفْدَعُ دُودًا طَوِيلًا عَلَى الْعُشْبِ، فَقَالَ: \"هَذَا سَهْلٌ!\" أَكَلَ الدُّودَ بِسُرْعَةٍ، ثُمَّ قَالَ: \"الدُّودُ لَا يَطِيرُ، الدُّودُ ذَكِيٌّ مِثْلِي!\"\n\n*There was a small frog living in a beautiful garden. The frog saw a big fly, so he said: \"I'm very hungry!\" The frog jumped at the fly, but the fly flew away quickly. Then the frog saw a long worm on the grass, so he said: \"This is easy!\" He ate the worm quickly, then said: \"Worms don't fly—worms are smart like me!\"*\n\nUnknown words: صغير, يسكن, راى, جائع, قفز, طارت, بسرعة, دودا, طويلا, اكل\nJudge: The story is grammatically sound and the diacritics are perfectly placed for a learner. While the narrative is simple, the ending provides a humorous touch of irony. The Arabic phrasing is natural for a children's fable or beginner reader.\n\n## Bottom 5 Stories\n\n### #1 — openai / Strategy D (composite 2.50, compliance 56%)\n**Noor and the Old House** (نُورٌ وَالبَيْتُ القَدِيمُ)\n\n> وَقَفَتْ نُورٌ عِنْدَ البَيْتِ القَدِيمِ، وَقالَتْ: «بِلا، لا، هٰذا مُهِمٌّ لَنا». فَقالَ سامِرٌ: «رَأْيِي تَماماً: هٰذا غالٍ، وَعِنْدِي هٰذا أَكْبَرُ». ثُمَّ قالَتْ نُورٌ: «في اللَّيْلِ سَتَسْمَعُ رِيحًا قَرِيبَةً عِنْدَ البَيْتِ، وَسَتَفْهَمُ أَنَّ هٰذا مُهِمٌّ». وَقالَ سامِرٌ: «نَعَم، مُمْكِن، وَلكِنْ أَنا سَعيدٌ مَعَ البَيْتِ الجَدِيدِ». ثُمَّ أَعْطَتْ نُورٌ سامِرًا رِسالَةً، وَقالَتْ هَمْسًا: «هٰذِهِ رِسالَةٌ مِنْ أُمٍّ، وَفيها بِنا». فَسَكَتَ سامِرٌ طَويلًا، ثُمَّ قالَ: «بِلا، لا؛ نَحْنُ لا، وَهٰذا البَيْتُ القَدِيمُ مُهِمٌّ لَنا».\n\nUnknown words: وقفت, وقالت, لنا, سامر, رايي, غال, وعندي, قالت, الليل, ستسمع\nJudge: The story suffers from unnatural phrasing and awkward word choices. The use of 'Bala' (بلى) is grammatically incorrect here as it is used to answer negative questions, not as a general 'No'. Phrases like 'In it is about us' (فيها بنا) and 'My opinion is absolute' (رأيي تماماً) do not follow native Arabic collocations. While the diacritics are excellent, the dialogue feels like a literal translation from another language rather than organic Arabic.\n\n### #2 — openai / Strategy C (composite 2.40, compliance 90%)\n**Ali’s Ball and the Rooster** (كُرَةُ عَلِيٍّ وَالدِّيكُ)\n\n> كانَ عَلِيٌّ فِي حَدِيقَةٍ جَميلَةٍ، وَكانَتِ الشَّمْسُ قَرِيبَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ. أَعْطَى عَلِيٌّ كُرَةً حَمْراءَ لِأُمٍّ، ثُمَّ دَبَّتِ الكُرَةُ إِلَى مَوْضِعٍ قَرِيبٍ مِنْ دِمْنَةٍ قَديمَةٍ. قالَ عَلِيٌّ: «مُمْكِن؟»، فَقَدَمَ إِلَى ذٰلِكَ المَوْضِعِ بِلا رِيحٍ. دَبَّ دِيكٌ أَسْوَدُ مِنَ الدِّمْنَةِ، وَأَعْطَى عَلِيًّا رِسَالَةً. كانَ عُنْوانُ الرِّسَالَةِ: «أَهْلاً»، فَكانَ عَلِيٌّ سَعيدًا تَمامًا.\n\nUnknown words: دبت, قديمة, عليا, سعيدا\nJudge: The story feels like a collection of sentences designed to use specific vocabulary rather than a natural narrative. Phrases like 'the sun was close to the sky' are nonsensical. The use of 'دبّت' (crept/crawled) for a ball and 'دمنة' (ruin/dung heap) is unnatural. 'ممكن؟' as a standalone question in this context is a literal translation of 'Is it possible?' which doesn't work in Arabic here.\n\n### #3 — gemini / Strategy C (composite 2.35, compliance 73%)\n**Ali and the Cat** (عَلِيٌّ وَالقِطَّةُ)\n\n> فِي يَوْمٍ جَمِيلٍ، كَانَ عَلِيٌّ فِي حَدِيقَةٍ كَبِيرَةٍ. رَأَى عَلِيٌّ قِطَّةً بَيْضَاءَ جَمِيلَةً مَعَ كُرَةٍ زَرْقَاءَ. أَعْطَى عَلِيٌّ الكُرَةَ لِلْقِطَّةِ، وَلَكِنْ دَبَّ فَأْرٌ صَغِيرٌ بَيْنَ الزُّهُورِ. تَرَكَتِ القِطَّةُ الكُرَةَ ثُمَّ قَالَتْ لِلْفَأْرِ: \"أَهْلًا! مَعَ السَّلَامَةِ!\". كَانَ الرَّأْيُ أَنَّ القِطَّةَ ذَكِيَّةٌ وَسَعِيدَةٌ فِي الحَيَاةِ.\n\nUnknown words: يوم, راى, بيضاء, زرقاء, صغير, تركت, قالت, ذكية, وسعيدة\nJudge: NARRATIVE_ARC: Has a basic setup (Ali in garden, sees cat) but lacks clear development and resolution. The ending ('the view was that the cat was smart') feels tacked on rather than earned. INTERESTINGNESS: The premise is simple but the cat greeting then immediately saying goodbye to the mouse is confusing rather than engaging. No real conflict or payoff. NATURALNESS: 'دَبَّ فَأْرٌ' (crawled a mouse) is acceptable but 'ظَهَرَ' might be more natural. The phrase 'كَانَ الرَّأْيُ أَنَّ' (the view was that) is awkward and unnatural for a children's story - sounds like academic writing. COHERENCE: The logical flow breaks down: why does Ali give the ball to the cat? Why does the cat greet AND say goodbye simultaneously? The final sentence about 'the view' doesn't connect to the narrative. GRAMMAR: Generally solid - proper case endings, verb agreements, idafa constructions correct. Minor issue: 'مَعَ كُرَةٍ' could be clearer as 'تَلْعَبُ بِكُرَةٍ'. DIACRITICS: Well done overall, though 'الرَّأْيُ' should be 'الرَّأْيَ' (accusative after كَانَ). TRANSLATION: Mostly accurate but 'The view was that' is literal rather than natural English. The Arabic awkwardness carries over.\n\n### #4 — gemini / Strategy C (composite 2.25, compliance 81%)\n**Ali and the Parrot** (عَلِيٌّ وَالبَبَّغَاءُ)\n\n> عَلِيٌّ وَلَدٌ ذَكِيٌّ، وَلَكِنَّ عِنْدَهُ بَبَّغَاءٌ جَمِيلٌ. قَالَ عَلِيٌّ: «هَذَا البَبَّغَاءُ أَزْرَقُ مِثْلُ السَّمَاءِ». قَالَتْ أُخْتُهُ: «لا، هُوَ أَخْضَرُ مِثْلُ العُشْبِ». قَالَ عَلِيٌّ: «رَأْيِي أَنَّ لَوْنَهُ أَزْرَقُ جِدّاً، وَهُوَ طَائِرٌ غَالِي». قَالَتْ البِنْتُ: «لَيْسَ أَزْرَقَ، هَلْ أَنْتَ سَعِيدٌ يَا عَلِيُّ؟ هُوَ بَبَّغَاءٌ بَنِيٌّ الآنَ!». نَظَرَ عَلِيٌّ إِلَى الطَّائِرِ، ثُمَّ قَالَ: «نَعَمْ، هُوَ فِي مَطْبَخِ بَيْتِنَا مَعَ المَطَرِ، وَلَوْنُهُ جَدِيدٌ».\n\nUnknown words: قالت, اخضر, رايي, وهو, طائر, نظر, الطائر\nJudge: The story has a weak narrative structure. It begins with a color debate about a parrot but ends confusingly with the parrot being in the kitchen 'with the rain' and having a 'new color.' The phrase 'وَلَكِنَّ عِنْدَهُ' is awkward (should be 'وَعِنْدَهُ' or 'وَلَكِنْ عِنْدَهُ'). The ending is incoherent—why is the parrot brown? What does 'with the rain' mean in the kitchen? The translation struggles with this too, adding '(mud/dripping)' to clarify, suggesting the Arabic itself is unclear. 'لَوْنُهُ جَدِيدٌ' (its color is new) is an unnatural expression. The diacritics are mostly correct. Grammar is acceptable but not flawless. The story lacks a satisfying resolution and the logic breaks down at the end, making it feel like a vocabulary exercise rather than a coherent narrative.\n\n### #5 — gemini / Strategy D (composite 2.25, compliance 57%)\n**An Important Message** (رِسَالَةٌ مُهِمَّةٌ)\n\n> مَازِنٌ رَجُلٌ سَعِيدٌ مَعَ زَوْجَتِهِ لَيْلَى فِي بَيْتٍ جَمِيلٍ. قَالَ مَازِنٌ لَهَا: «أَنْتِ بِنْتٌ لَيْسَتْ ذَكِيَّةً، لَقَدْ ضَاعَ عُنْوَانُ الجَزِيرَةِ الكَبِيرَةِ!» لَكِنَّ لَيْلَى كَانَتْ عِنْدَ الطَّاوِلَةِ مَعَ قَهْوَةٍ بَارِدَةٍ وَقَالَتْ: «أَنَا عِنْدِي العُنْوَانُ يَا مَازِنُ، وَلَكِنْ أَنْتَ نَسِيتَ أَنَّ البَيْتَ هُنَاكَ غَالِي جِدًّا.» كَانَ مَازِنٌ فِي مَوْضِعٍ صَعْبٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ: «هَلْ هَذَا رَأْيُكِ؟» قَالَتْ لَيْلَى: «نَعَمْ، الرِّسَالَةُ عِنْدِي الآنَ، تَفَضَّلْ لِنَكُونَ فِي سَلَامَةٍ.»\n\nUnknown words: مازن, زوجته, ليلى, ذكية, ضاع, قهوة, باردة, وقالت, عندي, نسيت\nJudge: NARRATIVE_ARC: The story has a setup (couple in house) but lacks clear development and resolution. The conflict about the island address appears suddenly and resolves ambiguously. INTERESTINGNESS: The premise is confusing - why does Mazen insult his wife over a lost address? The 'message' and 'safety' references at the end are unclear. NATURALNESS: Several unnatural expressions: 'أَنْتِ بِنْتٌ لَيْسَتْ ذَكِيَّةً' (calling wife 'girl' + awkward negation), 'فِي مَوْضِعٍ صَعْبٍ' (literal translation of 'difficult position'), 'لِنَكُونَ فِي سَلَامَةٍ' (awkward phrasing). COHERENCE: Logical gaps - the connection between the lost address, the expensive house, and the final 'message' and 'safety' is unclear. The conversation doesn't flow naturally. GRAMMAR: Mostly correct but 'أَنْتِ بِنْتٌ لَيْسَتْ ذَكِيَّةً' should be 'لَسْتِ ذَكِيَّةً' or 'أَنْتِ بِنْتٌ غَيْرُ ذَكِيَّةٍ'. DIACRITICS: Generally well-applied with only minor issues.
